key_numbers.sub: solve rate barely moved (within noise)

key_numbers.label: More variety, same training cost
key_numbers.sub: solve rate, a clean real jump

key_numbers.label: Kept climbing, no plateau
key_numbers.sub: solve rate through 1,156 distinct examples

key_numbers.label: One rung deeper worked weakly
key_numbers.sub: harder-puzzle coverage, but first-try stayed flat at 3%

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate keeps rising as more distinct solved problems are banked
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of distinct solved problems banked, from 39 up to 1,156. The upper line is the share solved within 16 tries; the lower line is the share solved on the very first try. Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steadily to the last point with no bend toward flat, so the model keeps gaining and there is still headroom past 1,156 distinct examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Variety beats extra training at the same cost
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the share of unseen puzzles solved within 16 tries: 40 examples trained briefly, the same 40 trained sixteen times harder, and a larger, more varied set at that same heavy training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The middle bar barely clears the left one (more training, no real gain), but the right bar more than doubles it at equal cost, so variety is the lever.

charts.chart_plain_title: One rung deeper: extra examples lift coverage but not first-try success
charts.chart_read: For harder, deeper puzzles, three models each show two bars, solved-within-16-tries and solved-on-first-try. Left is the untrained model, middle is trained only on shallower puzzles, right adds 320 deeper examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding deeper examples nearly triples the 16-try coverage (7% to 18%), but the first-try bar stays flat at 3%, so the skill is present yet not reliable enough to deploy.

qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.verdict_tag: It runs any recipe but can't reverse-engineer one
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.concept_primer: Picture a cook who follows any written recipe flawlessly. But set a finished dish before that cook and they cannot deduce which steps produced it, nor mentally re-cook a guessed recipe to check it against the plate. This model executes; it cannot infer.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_question: When a small model fails at chaining several data transformations together, is the hard part running the long chain of steps, or figuring out which steps produced the result?
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_answer: It's figuring out the steps. Handed the exact sequence of operations, this 4-billion-parameter model writes correct code almost every time, even four steps deep, with zero execution deficit. Left to infer that sequence from before-and-after examples, it solves almost nothing past two steps, spotting each added operation only about twice as well as guessing among 63 candidates.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.why_it_matters: Don't fine-tune a small model to discover multi-step transformations from examples, its weakest skill. Let cheap external tools enumerate and propose candidate pipelines, and use the model for what it does near-perfectly: turning a stated plan into working code.

key_numbers.label: Solve rate: told the steps versus left to guess them
key_numbers.sub: four-step pipelines, guessed from examples vs stated outright

key_numbers.label: How fast the odds of solving collapse per added step
key_numbers.sub: each composed operation, regardless of operation type

key_numbers.label: Power to identify each added step beyond the first
key_numbers.sub: vs random guessing among 63 candidate operations

key_numbers.label: Recognizing a single operation on its own
key_numbers.sub: but it cannot chain these recognitions together

charts.chart_plain_title: Told the plan the model wins; asked to guess it, it fails
charts.chart_read: Six task types along the bottom (chain length two to four, each with clean or information-destroying steps); bar height is share of tasks solved. Three bars each: from bare examples, from the stated plan, and with intermediate states shown. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stated-plan bars stay pinned near the top through chain length four, while both guessing bars sit on the floor. The wall is finding the plan, not running it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate crashes after two chained steps, whatever the step type
charts.chart_read: Bottom axis is chain length (one to five steps); height is share of tasks solved. Four lines mark how many information-destroying steps are present, from zero to three. Upper-left is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line falls off a cliff between one step and two and hits zero by three, overlapping regardless of step type, so depth alone kills it, walling at length two.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even pre-cut steps and yes-or-no checks fade with depth
charts.chart_read: Six task types along the bottom (chain length two to four, clean or information-destroying steps). One bar per type is solving when steps are pre-separated; the other is accuracy telling the true pipeline from a decoy. Higher is better; half is a coin-flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: Pre-separated solving slides from half toward zero as chains lengthen, and true-versus-decoy accuracy drops to a coin-flip at the deepest lossy cell. Even isolated pieces and simple checks break down.

charts.chart_plain_title: The old three-step wins were secretly shallower tasks
charts.chart_read: Two task groups along the bottom: tasks labeled three-steps-deep that secretly collapse to something shallower, versus genuinely three-step tasks. Bar height is how often an external split-and-search method solved them. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The search cleared every secretly-shallow task but only one in six genuinely deep ones, so nearly all the celebrated three-step success was a measurement artifact.

qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.verdict_tag: Most missed coding problems needed more varied tries
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Think of asking one coder to solve the same problem again and again while dialing their creativity from cautious to wild. Each setting surfaces solutions the others miss. Pooling all the moods, rather than any single one, cracks problems that a few careful tries left unsolved.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_question: When a small model flunks a coding problem on its first few tries, is it truly incapable, or did it just not explore enough options?
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_answer: Mostly the second. Of 24 Python problems a 4-billion-parameter model missed on four tries, spending more and more varied sampling recovered 15, lifting the share solved from 70% to nearly 89%. Mixing three creativity settings beat any single one, 13 recovered versus 10, because each cracked different problems. Nine stayed unsolved: genuine capability gaps.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: Before training a small model to be more creative, exhaust cheap tricks at answer time: sample more, and pool several creativity settings instead of tuning just one. But budget for a hard core of problems no sampling fixes.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved after heavy sampling
key_numbers.sub: share of 80 coding tasks with at least one correct answer, before vs after wide sampling

key_numbers.label: First-pass misses rescued
key_numbers.sub: problems missed on four tries that later got a correct answer

key_numbers.label: Mixing settings vs best single setting
key_numbers.sub: misses recovered by pooling three creativity settings versus the best one alone

key_numbers.label: Recovered by patching failing code
key_numbers.sub: and its two apparent fixes both passed the visible check but were secretly wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage climbs from 70% to 89% as sampling widens
charts.chart_read: Bars, left to right: the four-try base pool, three separate creativity settings, their pooled union, that union plus a code-repair pass, and a larger union. Bar height is the share of tasks solved on hidden checks; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every increase in sampling budget lifts the bar, though gains shrink; the repair pass adds nothing over the union, pointing to varied sampling as the real lever.

charts.chart_plain_title: Pooling several settings recovers more missed problems than any one
charts.chart_read: Bars count how many of the 24 first-pass misses each strategy rescued: three single creativity settings, their union, the union plus repair, and a larger union. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The union (13) beats the best single setting (10), and the largest budget reaches 15 - the settings recover different problems, so their gains stack.

charts.chart_plain_title: More compute buys more solved problems, with shrinking returns
charts.chart_read: The line plots share of tasks solved (vertical) against total compute spent generating candidate answers (horizontal). Points climb up and to the right as budget grows; up-and-to-the-left would be more efficient.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage keeps rising with spend but flattens; this no-training curve is the bar any future trained model must beat at equal cost.

qwen35_4b_early_text_hypothesis_forking.verdict_tag: A 24-way early-hint test has cleared its model-free checks
qwen35_4b_early_text_hypothesis_forking.concept_primer: Imagine giving the same puzzle to twenty-four independent scratchpads. Each starts with a different concrete guess about the first transformation, may reject that guess, and must still write the complete two-step rule. Public examples—not the hidden answer—choose one result.
qwen35_4b_early_text_hypothesis_forking.plain_question: Does giving each possible first transformation before reasoning produce better complete rules than giving it late or simply buying more ordinary attempts?
qwen35_4b_early_text_hypothesis_forking.plain_answer: The experiment design and model-free checks now pass, but the model has not run. Review expanded the first draft from twelve operation names to twenty-four fully specified operations, added two fair late-hint comparisons, and proved that all 576 possible two-step rules are covered by the public-data control. The next gate tests whether the model can actually use these hints rather than merely repeat them.
qwen35_4b_early_text_hypothesis_forking.why_it_matters: The previous internal-state experiment could change a candidate name without showing that later reasoning used it. This test moves a plain-text candidate to the start of reasoning and demands a full computed rule, while matched ordinary attempts and exhaustive search prevent extra branching from receiving free credit.

key_numbers.label: Fully specified first-step candidates
key_numbers.sub: parameters are included, not left for the model to guess

key_numbers.label: Two-step rules checked per task
key_numbers.sub: the complete public search space

key_numbers.label: Fresh procedural tasks frozen
key_numbers.sub: 48 qualification and 96 untouched confirmation

key_numbers.label: Model calls so far
key_numbers.sub: this is design and model-free validation only

charts.chart_plain_title: Every diagnostic input distinguishes all twenty-four candidates
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one public diagnostic list. Its height is the number of fully specified operations that produce a distinct expected result; all four reach the required twenty-four.
charts.chart_takeaway: The upcoming model check cannot pass by exploiting two candidates that happen to mean the same thing on a diagnostic input.

qwen35_4b_error_localization.verdict_tag: Its confidence dips exactly where it first slipped
qwen35_4b_error_localization.concept_primer: Picture reciting a memorized phone number: your voice naturally steadies as you get going, but wavers exactly on the digit you misremember. Correct for that natural steadying, and the leftover waver pinpoints the wrong digit. That waver is the model's confidence dip.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_question: When a small model makes a mistake partway through a multi-step calculation, can its own confidence tell you exactly which step first went wrong?
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_answer: Yes, and the dip lands on the exact step, not just late in the chain. Confidence naturally climbs the deeper the model goes, so "least sure" could just mean "last step." Correcting for that, the least-confident step is the true first slip 56% of the time, versus 36% for guessing the usual trouble spot and 19% for blind chance. When it slips several times, though, only 27%.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.why_it_matters: Log how confidently your small model commits to each reasoning step. When an answer has one error, redo from its least-confident step: cheaper than restarting and just as effective. Don't trust the signal when several steps look shaky.

key_numbers.label: First wrong step pinpointed
key_numbers.sub: the least-confident step is the true first slip, on single-error chains (vs 19% for blind chance)

key_numbers.label: Beats guessing by position
key_numbers.sub: how often the first slip is found: position guess vs the model's confidence

key_numbers.label: When it slips several times
key_numbers.sub: confidence catches the FIRST error only about 1 in 4 times

key_numbers.label: Cheaper repair
key_numbers.sub: redo from the flagged step vs redo the whole chain

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence bottoms out exactly at the first mistake
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is each step's distance from the first error: negative before it, zero the error itself, positive after. The vertical axis is confidence with the natural climb-with-position removed; lower means less sure.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line sits high two steps before the slip, plunges to its lowest point precisely at the first error, then recovers: the dip marks the origin, not just late steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence pinpoints the slip better than guessing by position
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way to guess which step is the first slip on single-error chains; bar height is how often it's right, so taller is better. Confidence-based methods sit beside guessing-by-position, always-guess-last, and blind chance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two confidence bars (56% and 64%) tower over guessing by position (36%) and chance (19%), while always-last is near zero: confidence carries real information about where the error is.

qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.verdict_tag: Show the worked steps and runnable fixes transfer
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.concept_primer: Instead of eyeballing a broken formula and guessing a patch, the model rewrites it as a small runnable program, which the computer then test-drives on real example cases to see if it truly works — like a mechanic rebuilding an engine and road-testing it, not just trusting the manual.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_question: Can a small model learn to fix broken little programs by rewriting them, so the fixes still work on kinds of problems it never saw during training?
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_answer: Yes, but with a catch. Shown worked-through reasoning in the prompt, the model fixed unseen problem types about three-quarters of the time, versus one-in-three when the prompt showed no steps. Strip out or scramble those steps and most of the gain vanishes. One two-condition problem type stayed stubborn, rising from never solved to roughly one in three only after adding extra practice examples.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.why_it_matters: When you post-train a small model to emit runnable fixes, keep matching worked-through steps in every prompt — missing or scrambled steps erase most of the gain. And cover combined two-condition patterns in training, or they quietly fail.

key_numbers.label: Fixes that worked on unseen problems
key_numbers.sub: with no worked steps versus with matching worked steps

key_numbers.label: The stubborn two-condition problem
key_numbers.sub: solved after adding that pattern to training practice

key_numbers.label: Two of three unseen problem types
key_numbers.sub: near-perfect transfer to families never trained on

key_numbers.label: Worked steps scrambled on purpose
key_numbers.sub: shuffling the steps wrecks accuracy, proving they carry real signal

charts.chart_plain_title: Success on unseen problems across four training-and-prompt setups
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one setup; bar height is the share of unseen problems fully solved. Within a cluster, one bar is a single best guess, the other picks the best of a few tries. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two setups that include matching worked steps tower over the two without — about three-quarters of unseen problems solved versus one-in-three — and best-of-few barely helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success on each unseen problem type, before and after extra practice
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved when picking the best of a few tries. The two bars compare the first model with one given extra practice on two-condition problems. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stubborn two-condition type climbs from never solved to about a third after extra practice, while the other two types stay near-perfect.

charts.chart_plain_title: What removing or scrambling the worked steps does to each problem type
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved. The three bars are a prompt with matching worked steps, no steps, or scrambled steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing the steps drops the branching type from near-perfect to zero, and even scrambled steps cap it at a fifth — the steps do real work.

qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.verdict_tag: A cheap shape check beats running every program
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.concept_primer: Picture a returns desk that opens a package for full inspection only when the box comes back lighter than it shipped, a weight drop hinting something inside went missing. Here the quick answer having fewer columns than the input table is that lighter box, triggering the slow repair programs.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_question: Can a cheap rule decide when a small model should run its slow, expensive extra program attempts on a table task, instead of always running them?
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_answer: Yes, and here is the twist: running all five programs on every task scored lower (56%) than the cheap rule (58%), because blanket spending overwrote one answer the quick pass already had right. The rule fires only when the quick answer has fewer columns than the input, flagging exactly the hard cases, beating the quick answer's 42% and tying a perfect-hindsight ceiling at half the compute.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.why_it_matters: Before training a learned judge, check whether your expensive fallback's wins cluster behind one cheap, visible signal; a hand-written trigger may match the best achievable accuracy. And beware: running every tool everywhere can clobber correct answers, not just waste compute.

key_numbers.label: Quick answer alone vs. the routed rule
key_numbers.sub: exactly correct answers on 50 table-transformation tasks

key_numbers.label: Running all five programs on every task
key_numbers.sub: below the rule's 58%, at double the compute

key_numbers.label: Compute per task, run-everything vs. rule
key_numbers.sub: the rule spends about half while scoring higher

key_numbers.label: Wrong answers fixed vs. broken by the rule
key_numbers.sub: captured every available fix with no losses

charts.chart_plain_title: Cheap rule ties perfect hindsight and beats running everything
charts.chart_read: Bars show exactly-correct accuracy on 50 tasks for four policies: quick answer only (42%), running all five programs everywhere (56%), the cheap shape rule (58%), and a perfect-hindsight policy that runs programs only when they help (58%). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cheap rule reaches the perfect-hindsight ceiling and edges past run-everything, while the quick answer trails far below, so selective spending beats blanket spending.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent per task by each of the four policies
charts.chart_read: Bars show average tokens per task for the same four policies: quick answer cheapest at 1,478, run-everything priciest at 17,801, the shape rule in between at 9,017, perfect-hindsight lowest among spenders at 3,959. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule costs about half of run-everything yet scores higher, and the perfect-hindsight bar shows even more compute could still be shaved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixed budgets never catch the selective rule
charts.chart_read: A rising line tracks accuracy as 0 to 5 programs are forced on every task, climbing from 42% to 56%; a flat reference line marks the shape rule at 58%. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The always-run line climbs then stalls at 56%, always under the rule's 58%, so spending the whole budget blindly cannot match spending it selectively.

charts.chart_plain_title: All the gains come from just two task families
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family (five tasks each) compare quick answer against the shape rule across ten families. Higher is better. Two families jump from 0 to 80%; every other family is unchanged.
charts.chart_takeaway: All eight rescues cluster in the merge/split and one synthetic family, and the rule never dents a family the quick answer already solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying one program first barely helps
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per program strategy compare running that strategy alone against a version that escalates only when its answer disagrees with the quick answer. Higher is better; all bars sit between 42% and 50%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Escalating after one program adds at most two points, and no single-strategy approach reaches the shape rule's 58%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Requiring programs to agree lowers accuracy at every budget
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at three portfolio sizes compare committing to the first plausible program against requiring two or three programs to agree. Higher is better; the agreement bars are shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: Demanding agreement drops accuracy 8 to 12 points versus trusting the first plausible program, so consensus voting throws away correct lone answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Looser trigger rules pay far more compute for the same accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show total tokens over 50 tasks for six trigger rules, from the tightest fewer-columns rule (231,365) to always-run (890,030). Shorter is better; the five selective rules all score 29 of 50, or 58%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The five selective triggers tie at 58% accuracy while the loosest costs about three and a half times the tightest, so trigger precision is what saves compute.

qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.verdict_tag: Skip the converter, just ask the model
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.concept_primer: Imagine fixing a messy spreadsheet two ways: a converter that can only chain moves from a fixed menu of table operations, or a person shown one before-and-after example who simply retypes the corrected table. The fixed menu cannot express most oddball reshapes.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_question: To reshape messy tables, is it better to build a converter out of fixed operations, or to just ask a small model to rewrite the table itself?
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_answer: Just ask the model. Directly generating the reshaped table got 55% of 250 table tasks exactly right, versus only 18% for the fixed-operation converter. The model even nailed 103 reshapes the converter could not even express, while the converter uniquely solved just 10. Direct generation is far from perfect, with 45% still failing, but the converter is a narrow backup, not the main route.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.why_it_matters: For small-model table wrangling, do not build a fixed-operation converter first. A plain "here is a before-and-after example, now rewrite this table" prompt, with a generous output length, wins on most real reshapes; keep any converter only as a small backup.

key_numbers.label: Reshapes only one approach could solve

key_numbers.label: Adding the converter as a backup
key_numbers.sub: combining both adds only 9 more of 250 cases

key_numbers.sub: it solves most tables even the converter can handle (35 of 45)

Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct-ask bar towers over both converter bars, beating the specialized converter by roughly three to one; adding the converter on top barely helps.

charts.chart_read: Bars split the 250 tasks into four outcome groups: solved by both methods, by asking directly only, by the converter only, or by neither. Taller bars for the direct-only and both groups favor the model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'directly only' bar of 103 dwarfs the 'converter only' bar of 10, so the model handles about ten times more cases the converter cannot than the reverse.

qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.verdict_tag: Writing self-checked code loses to just asking
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.concept_primer: The job is reshaping a messy table into a clean one. Instead of doing it directly, the model writes a small reshaping script, tests it on the one visible example, then runs it on hidden rows — like a spreadsheet macro that passes one row but breaks on the rest.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_question: If a small model writes a little reshaping program and tests it on the one example it can see, does that beat just asking it for the answer directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_answer: No. Asking directly reshaped 55% of tables correctly; the write-and-test-a-program route managed just 15%. Even a magic chooser that always picked the right route each time would reach only 59% — four points above asking directly. And the self-check leaks: about 1 in 5 programs that passed the one visible example were still wrong on the hidden data.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.why_it_matters: For small-model table reshaping, direct generation beats having the model write and self-check code. A single passing example is a leaky correctness gate — about a fifth of programs that clear it are still wrong — so never ship on that signal alone.

charts.chart_takeaway: The direct-only bar towers over script-only, 110 versus 10 — asking directly uniquely solves eleven times as many tables as the script route does.

charts.chart_takeaway: On the easy cases direct answering and the perfect chooser hit 100% while the script route already trailed at 63% and 88%; on the harder sample the script route collapses to 12% — the easy cases had flattered it.

qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.verdict_tag: A fixed toolbox cannot build most answers
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.concept_primer: Imagine cleaning up messy spreadsheets using only a fixed drawer of moves: split a column, flip rows into columns, fold repeated headers. If the one move a task needs is not in the drawer, no cleverness rebuilds the right answer. This checks whether the frozen drawer can reach it at all.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_question: If you lock in a small, fixed set of spreadsheet-reshaping moves, can you actually rebuild the correct clean table for most real cleanup tasks?
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_answer: No. Across 250 real spreadsheet-cleanup tasks, the frozen set of moves could rebuild the correct table for only 18% of them. And the weakness is not picking the right sequence: simply grabbing the first move-sequence that fits already solves 17%, almost touching that 18% ceiling. The moves themselves are missing, and showing more worked examples does not budge it.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.why_it_matters: Before training a model to write or choose reshaping programs, first check the correct program is even buildable from your moves. If it is not for most tasks, no training rescues you; widen the set of moves and re-test first.

charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near one-fifth, and taking the first fitting sequence nearly matches the best-case bar, so the toolbox itself, not the choosing, is the limit.

charts.chart_takeaway: The fresh-table line is dead flat across one to five examples, meaning failures come from missing moves in the toolbox, not from too few examples.

charts.chart_takeaway: The unsolved bar towers over the solved one, 41 versus 9, showing the toolbox handles a narrow slice and misses most task types entirely.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.verdict_tag: Trust the first working program, not a vote
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.concept_primer: Think of fixing a messy spreadsheet: the model writes a few small transform-the-table programs, then checks each against one worked example whose answer is known. The question is whether making several programs agree beats simply running the first one that passes the example.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_question: When a small model writes several little programs to reshape a table, does making them agree beat just running the first one that passes a checkable example?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_answer: No. The simplest rule won: run the first program that passes a single worked example. It solved 52% of tables versus 44% when the model just answered directly, rescuing 23 tables it had otherwise botched while breaking only 4. Requiring several programs to agree was more careful but far too timid, recovering fewer mistakes and topping out at 49%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.why_it_matters: For structured tasks where you can write code and check it against a known example, have the small model generate programs and deploy the first one that passes. Don't gate on ensemble agreement — it trades away most of the wins.

key_numbers.label: Answering directly
key_numbers.sub: tables solved out of 250

key_numbers.label: First working program

key_numbers.label: Requiring agreement
key_numbers.sub: the more cautious voting rule fell short

key_numbers.label: Best any rule could reach

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.verdict_tag: Writing code helps only as a backup plan
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.concept_primer: The task: reshape a messy table into a target layout, given one solved example. The model can name the answer directly, or write a reshaping program, test it against that example, and patch bugs until it matches — like fixing a spreadsheet macro on a sample before the real run.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_question: If a small model writes and debugs a table-reshaping program against a worked example before running it, does that beat just guessing the final answer directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_answer: No. Guessing the answer directly won outright, solving 55% of unseen tables versus only 25% for the debugged program. But the program is a useful complement, not a replacement: it rescued 18 tables the direct guess botched, so running both and keeping whichever works lifts success to 62%. And the repair rounds earned their keep, raising program accuracy from 16% to 25% while breaking nothing.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.why_it_matters: Don't replace direct output with a self-debugging code path; run both and keep whichever succeeds. And never treat "passed the worked example" as proof — one in five example-passing programs was silently wrong on the real table.

charts.chart_takeaway: The stricter instruction doubled program correctness and cut misleading passes from about seven in ten to four in ten, so telling the model not to memorize helped.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.verdict_tag: Trust the first passing program, not a vote
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.concept_primer: To reshape a messy table, the model writes several small programs and runs each against the one worked example it can check, like trying a ring of keys against a test lock. Programs that pass become candidates; a commit rule picks which to trust.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_question: When a small model reshapes messy tables, does having it write and test several small programs beat just asking it for the finished table directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_answer: Yes, but modestly, and which passing program you trust matters more than the programs. Asking directly for the finished table got 42% right. The rule the team locked in, commit only when two programs agree, reached just 48%. A simpler rule they also wrote down beforehand, trust the first program that passes the check, hit 56%, nearly the 58% best-possible ceiling. Consensus quietly discarded correct lone hits.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.why_it_matters: When candidate programs self-check against a worked example, commit the first that passes rather than requiring two to agree, since one correct program is usually right. Gains cluster in a few table shapes, so route effort, do not run every strategy everywhere.

charts.chart_takeaway: The portfolio burns about twelve times more tokens (890,030 versus 73,911) for a modest accuracy gain, real added coverage but not yet an efficient policy.

qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.verdict_tag: Matching the shown examples wins; stress-tests backfire
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.concept_primer: Reshaping a messy table has two routes: hand-write the finished answer, or write a tiny program that does the reshaping. Run that program on the worked examples you can already see; if it reproduces them exactly, it has earned the right to handle the hidden test.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_question: When a small model can either write a table's answer directly or write a program to produce it, which should you trust, and do extra sanity checks help decide?
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_answer: Trust the program the moment it reproduces the visible worked examples. Doing that lifted exact-match accuracy from 55% to 62% across 250 table tasks, rescuing 18 answers the direct route got wrong while losing none it got right. The fancier idea of stress-testing the program against fresh made-up inputs did not help and slightly lowered accuracy.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.why_it_matters: When a generated program reproduces the examples you can see, deploy it as a fallback and stop there. Skip synthetic double-checks: the model's own answers on invented inputs repeat the same mistakes, so they veto good programs more than bad ones.

qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.verdict_tag: Self-invented recipes beat prompting but unlock nothing new
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.concept_primer: A small model studies a few already-solved table-reshaping puzzles and writes itself two named recipes: one pivots columns into rows, the other merges repeated rows. It freezes them, then follows a recipe to write fresh code for brand-new tables it has never seen.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_question: If a small model invents its own reusable recipes for reshaping messy data tables, does it then solve more new tables than just asking it directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_answer: Barely, and nothing genuinely new. The self-written recipes lift exactly-solved tables from 42% to 46%, edging out plain prompting. But a ready-made library of solved programs already handled 56 to 58%, and every table the recipes cracked, that library already covered. Net new tables unlocked: exactly zero. Discovery just re-derived a slice of what the model could already reach.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.why_it_matters: Before trusting a model to invent reusable strategies, check what a curated library of solved programs already covers — here self-discovery only re-derived existing wins. If you do commit a discovered recipe, gate it on a table-shape match, which made every commit correct.

charts.chart_takeaway: The invented-recipe bars land just above asking-directly at 42-46%, but the two library bars jump to 56-58% — clever self-made recipes still trail the existing toolbox.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where approaches split: three revealing table families
charts.chart_read: Three task families along the bottom; within each, grouped bars compare asking directly, the invented recipes, and the ready-made library. Bar height is the share of that family's five tables solved exactly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On merge-and-split tables the recipes recover cases direct generation misses, yet the library solves those plus a whole family the recipes never crack.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much each invented recipe solves on its own
charts.chart_read: The two invented recipes along the bottom; paired bars show the share of 50 tables where the recipe passes the shown example versus matches the hidden answer. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each recipe solves only about one-in-eight to one-in-five tables alone — narrow, family-specific reach, not a general-purpose reshaping skill.

qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.verdict_tag: A stop signal is not an answer slot
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.concept_primer: A reasoning model works in two phases: a private scratchpad where it thinks, then a final answer, split by a hidden "done thinking" marker. This test jammed that marker in early to cut thinking short, like telling a colleague mid-sentence "time's up" without handing them an answer form.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_question: If a small model keeps thinking past its budget and never wraps up on its own, can you just insert a stop-thinking marker to make it commit to an answer?
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_answer: No. Inserting the model's own "done thinking" marker cut the reasoning off but almost never flipped it into answer mode. Across three thinking budgets, only 13% to 19% of forced stops produced anything readable, and just 1 of 48 was correct, versus the 90% readability the test demanded. Usually the model simply restarted its analysis instead of committing.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.why_it_matters: If you cap a small model's thinking to save tokens, don't trust the stop signal alone to produce an answer. Give it an explicit answer slot to fill, and verify output is readable, not just that thinking stopped.

key_numbers.label: Forced stops that produced a readable answer
key_numbers.sub: across three thinking budgets, vs the 90% the test required

key_numbers.label: Forced stops that were actually correct
key_numbers.sub: about 2%, unchanged at every budget

key_numbers.label: Still rambling when the answer limit hit
key_numbers.sub: hit the short answer cap without committing, vs 5% allowed

key_numbers.label: Traces that never finished thinking on their own
key_numbers.sub: every trace hit the token ceiling, so forcing was always needed

charts.chart_plain_title: Readable and correct answers versus rambling, at three thinking budgets
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by the thinking budget before the forced stop (256, 512, 1024 tokens); height is the share of 48 traces. Three bars per group: readable-answer rate, correct-answer rate, and still-rambling-at-the-cap rate. Higher readable and correct is better; lower rambling is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Readable bars sit near one-fifth, correct bars hug zero, while rambling bars tower above 85% at every budget, so the stop signal never produced a clean answer.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.verdict_tag: It knew the answers but never wrote them
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.concept_primer: Each answer gets a fixed thinking budget, like a timed exam. When the clock runs out mid-thought, the model re-explains from scratch instead of writing its answer on the answer line, so correct reasoning is never scored. Training taught it to commit an answer when cut off.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_question: When a small model keeps failing a benchmark, is it because it cannot reason well enough, or because it never actually commits to a final answer?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_answer: Mostly the second. On a blind benchmark the model scored about 14 percent, with six task types near zero, but it had usually reasoned correctly. It simply hit its thinking limit, restarted explaining instead of writing the answer line, and scored blank. Teaching it to commit an answer from a cut-off chain lifted scores to 36 and 45 percent, and the fix carried to tasks it never practiced.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.why_it_matters: Before assuming a small model lacks a skill, measure how often it emits no readable answer within its thinking budget. Training it to commit an answer from its own cut-off reasoning can unlock skill it already has, and where you place that training signal beat adding more data.

key_numbers.sub: still improved from the shared finishing fix

charts.chart_read: Two groups, one per fresh test seed. In each, the left bar is the untrained base model and the right bar is the model after breadth training; height is the blind benchmark score, higher is better. Both bars share seed and decoding.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both seeds roughly triple, from about 14 percent to 36 and 45 percent, clearing the tiny 3-point success bar many times over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Gains reached puzzle types the model never practiced
charts.chart_read: Four groups: the average of ten trained families, two families unseen of training (brinework, spindle), and one given zero training examples (stallwright). Left bar base, right bar after training; height is success rate on unseen items, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: unseen and zero-data families rose about as much as trained ones, so the fix is a shared finishing skill, not memorized per-task tricks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Unreadable answers collapsed after the finishing fix
charts.chart_read: Five puzzle families along the bottom. Each pair counts, out of 100 attempts, how many produced no readable final answer: left bar base model, right bar after training. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Base bars sit near 100 (93 to 100 of 100); after training they drop to single digits, direct evidence the failure was finishing, not reasoning.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.concept_primer: A small model has one fixed pool of internal wiring, like a blanket sized for a single sleeper. Pull it to cover fast, shallow tasks and the slow, deep-reasoning tasks go cold; pull it the other way and the fast tasks do. The blanket never grows.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_question: When a small model plateaus, can feeding it harder and more varied practice make it excel at both quick tasks and slow, step-by-step reasoning at once?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_answer: No, not both at once. Piling on more, harder, or more varied self-generated practice all stalled at the same ceiling, and even hand-written expert solutions the model could not discover on its own failed to move it. A new trick, self-training the model's own shortest correct reasoning into its weights, finally cracked the slow-reasoning tier, but only by ceding ground on the fast one.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.why_it_matters: Do not chase one small-model fine-tune that aces everything; ship two specialists, one tuned for speed, one for deep reasoning. And skip broad data harvesting: a single rich task family taught nearly the whole gain that broad variety did.

key_numbers.sub: aggregate hidden-task score: untrained base vs the best trained arm

key_numbers.label: Best gain on fast tasks
key_numbers.sub: the speed-optimal arm's average lift over the untrained model on quick tasks

key_numbers.label: Slow tier finally cracked

key_numbers.label: Hard failures that are just too slow

charts.chart_plain_title: Each training recipe wins one difficulty tier, never both
charts.chart_read: Three trained recipes sit along the bottom. For each, paired bars show its average gain over the untrained model on fast tasks (one color) and slow reasoning tasks (other color). A line marks the success bar at +32 points; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One recipe clears the fast bar but misses slow; a second clears slow but misses fast; the blended-dose middle recipe misses both, so the tiers trade against each other.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which slow-task types the new trick lifted, and the one it did not
charts.chart_read: One bar per slow-task type, rule-induction, optimization search, knowing-when-to-abstain, triage, and deep repair, showing the reasoning specialist's average gain over the untrained model across three runs. Taller is better; zero means no change.
charts.chart_takeaway: Abstention and triage jumped most (+77 and +63 points); induction and optimization rose solidly; deep repair sat just below zero, the one skill still unmoved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most hard problems are solvable but too slow, not impossible
charts.chart_read: Two bars split the hardest problems: the share the model cannot solve even in six attempts, versus the share it can solve but too slowly to fit its answer budget. A bigger right-hand bar means more failures are fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly two-thirds are solvable-but-slow, a time-budget problem rather than a capability wall, exactly what compressing the model's own solutions targets; the rest is a genuine gap.

qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.verdict_tag: Small models grade their own code only after
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.concept_primer: Picture a coder who dashes off eight rough versions of a function. Asked to grade them on sight, they nod along and mark almost everything correct. Given a minute to trace each line, they turn sharp — reliably catching the broken ones.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_question: If a small model writes eight tries at a coding problem, can it reliably tell which ones actually work — without running the code?
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_answer: Only after it thinks. Judging on sight, the model rubber-stamps 91% of its tries as correct while just 77% truly pass — barely a check, mostly agreeing with itself. Given room to reason first, it becomes a real critic: letting it pick its best of eight lifts success from 77% to 86%, closing three-quarters of the gap to a flawless picker's 89% ceiling.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.why_it_matters: Before training a separate model to rank candidate answers, first have the model reason through and grade its own — zero extra training, it beats blind picking, and it works from the task text alone with no hidden tests.

key_numbers.label: Best of eight tries, blind pick vs a thinking self-check
key_numbers.sub: against a flawless picker's 89% ceiling

key_numbers.label: Share of the gap to the ceiling that thinking closes
key_numbers.sub: snap-judging vs thinking self-check

key_numbers.label: Answers the snap-judge calls correct
key_numbers.sub: rubber-stamping, not real judgment

key_numbers.label: How reliably it ranks a good answer above a bad one
key_numbers.sub: snap-judging vs thinking

charts.chart_plain_title: How good the picked answer is, by how it was chosen
charts.chart_read: Four accuracy bars for the coding tasks, left to right: picking one of eight tries at random, the snap-judge's pick, the thinking-judge's pick, and a flawless picker that peeks at hidden tests. Taller is better; the last bar is the ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The thinking-judge bar nearly reaches the flawless ceiling and towers over random and snap-judge picks — reasoning before judging recovers most of the achievable gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Skill at telling its own right answers from wrong ones
charts.chart_read: Two skill scores — grading accuracy that corrects for guessing, and how reliably a correct answer outranks a wrong one — each shown for snap-judging (lighter) versus thinking (darker). Higher is better; 50% is pure guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both scores jump sharply with thinking — grading from 63% to 83% — turning a near-coin-flip judge into a dependable critic of its own code.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each judge calls an answer correct, versus the truth
charts.chart_read: Three bars: the share of candidates the snap-judge calls correct, the share the thinking-judge calls correct, and the true pass rate as the honest target. Landing closer to the true rate is better; far above it means rubber-stamping.
charts.chart_takeaway: The snap-judge sits well above the truth at 91% against a real 77%; thinking pulls its approvals down toward the actual rate — a sign of genuine judgment.

qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.verdict_tag: A fix-it pass catches noise, not real mistakes
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.concept_primer: The model writes a program two ways: a dense one-line formula, or an editable step-by-step diagram. A separate fix-it pass proofreads that diagram, but it practiced only on randomly scrambled versions, so it catches obvious damage while missing a clean-looking step that quietly computes the wrong answer.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_question: If a small model writes code as an editable step-by-step diagram, then runs a learned fix-it pass over its failures, does that beat a plain one-liner?
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_answer: No. The plain one-line formula fully solved 29% of brand-new tasks; the step-by-step diagram managed only 22%, and the fix-it pass recovered part of that gap to 24% — still behind. That pass genuinely works: on randomly scrambled diagrams it lifted success from 3% to 27%, an eightfold jump. But the model's own errors are confident, consistent wrong logic, not obvious damage, so repair rarely helps.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.why_it_matters: Do not assume a fix-it loop that aces synthetically broken inputs fixes a model's real errors — it patches random damage, not systematic wrong logic. Test repair on the model's own failures, and check whether a fancier program format earns its complexity.

key_numbers.label: Plain formula vs diagram-plus-repair on new tasks

key_numbers.label: Fix-it pass on randomly scrambled diagrams
key_numbers.sub: fully solved before vs after repair, an eightfold jump (4 to 32 of 120)

key_numbers.label: Repair's lift on the real pipeline
key_numbers.sub: diagram alone to diagram-plus-repair, still under the 29% baseline

key_numbers.label: Familiar-style tasks
key_numbers.sub: every approach solves seen-style tasks perfectly (60 of 60)

charts.chart_plain_title: On new tasks, the plain formula still beats the diagram pipeline
charts.chart_read: Three bars give the share of 120 brand-new tasks whose every hidden test passes: the plain one-line formula, the step-by-step diagram alone, and the diagram plus the fix-it pass. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain formula is tallest at 29%; the fix-it pass lifts the diagram from 22% to 24% but never closes the gap — the extra machinery does not pay off.

charts.chart_plain_title: The fix-it pass really does repair randomly broken diagrams
charts.chart_read: Two bars show the share of 120 tasks whose every hidden test passes on diagrams damaged by random scrambling: before the fix-it pass runs, and after it. Taller is better; the leap measures raw repair skill.
charts.chart_takeaway: Success jumps from 3% to 27%, an eightfold rise, proving the repair skill is real — yet it barely helps the full pipeline because real mistakes differ from random damage.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families each approach wins and loses
charts.chart_read: Six task families along the bottom; three bars each give the share solved on every hidden test — plain formula, diagram, and diagram-plus-repair. Taller is better; families are ordered by how much the approaches diverge.
charts.chart_takeaway: Gains and losses cancel: the diagram unlocks one family the formula fails completely (83% vs 0%) but collapses to zero on families the formula solves perfectly.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.verdict_tag: Deciding when to stop cannot rescue wrong picks
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.concept_primer: Picture choosing an answer by popularity vote: the system writes several candidate programs, feeds them the same sample inputs, groups programs whose outputs match, and commits to the biggest group. But agreement is not correctness, and there is no answer key. The model only decides whether to run one more test.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_question: If a small model could smartly decide when to stop running test inputs on candidate programs, would it pick correct code more often while wasting less effort?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_answer: No. Every strategy landed at the same 16.7% correct-pick rate — running no tests, running all eight, and even a strategy allowed to peek at the right answer to time its stop. The trained model did learn to quit sooner, using under four tests instead of eight, but its choice stayed wrong: grouping programs by matching outputs never reveals which group is actually correct, and a correct program was available for every task.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.why_it_matters: When candidate code is chosen by which programs agree on unlabeled test inputs, spend effort on grounding the choice — a trustworthy answer key or a stronger selector — not on tuning when to stop. More tests cannot fix a blind chooser.

key_numbers.label: Correct code picked, no matter the strategy
key_numbers.sub: identical from zero tests up to eight, and even for a strategy that could peek at the right answer

key_numbers.label: A correct program was available
key_numbers.sub: so the failure was choosing it, not missing it

key_numbers.label: Tests spent by the trained model
key_numbers.sub: cheaper stopping, but zero accuracy gain

key_numbers.label: Coding tasks in the pilot
key_numbers.sub: small scale — read as a diagnostic, not a benchmark score

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct-pick rate stays flat no matter how the model decides to stop
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across five stopping strategies. In each pair, the full-height bar shows a correct program existed among the candidates (always 100%); the shorter bar shows how often the chosen program was actually correct. Higher is better; the short bars never rise.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every strategy's correct-pick bar sits at the same low height while availability stays full — the chooser almost never lands on the correct program that was there all along.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many test runs each stopping strategy used
charts.chart_read: One bar per strategy showing the average number of test inputs run before committing; shorter means cheaper. The trained model's bar sits well below the run-all-eight bar, but correct-pick rate from the other chart is flat across all of them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained model learned to stop early and spend fewer test runs, yet since accuracy never changes, that saving buys nothing useful.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.verdict_tag: One yes-or-no check beats averaging the whole answer
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.concept_primer: A model writes a function one token at a time. You could gauge its confidence from how smoothly it "typed" hundreds of characters, but doubt in one spot blurs into the average. Better to ask point-blank, "Is this right?", and read how firmly it answers that one word.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_question: When a small model writes several candidate programs and no tests are available to run them, how can it tell which one is actually correct?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_answer: Ask it directly. Averaging the model's confidence across every code token barely helps, nudging correct picks from 77% (blind guessing) to just 79%. But handing the model its own code and reading its confidence in one yes-or-no verdict jumps that to 84% — near the 87% ceiling of a flawless picker. How you ask matters more than whether you ask.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.why_it_matters: When no tests exist to run generated code, don't rank candidates by average per-token confidence — long answers dilute the signal. Feed each solution back, ask the model to judge it yes-or-no, and pick by its confidence in that verdict word.

key_numbers.label: Picking the right program
key_numbers.sub: averaging confidence over every code token vs. reading one yes-or-no self-verdict

key_numbers.label: Blind guessing baseline
key_numbers.sub: expected accuracy from picking a candidate at random

key_numbers.label: Best possible picker
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if a correct candidate were always chosen when one existed

key_numbers.label: Ranking a correct solution above a wrong one
key_numbers.sub: the self-verdict vs token-averaging, where chance is 50%

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each method picks correct code when no tests can run
charts.chart_read: Four bars, taller is better, showing how often each method's pick passes the hidden tests across all 164 coding tasks. Left to right: blind random pick (77%), averaging confidence over every code token (79%), one yes-or-no self-verdict (84%), and a flawless picker (87%).
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-verdict bar clears both averaging and guessing and nearly reaches the flawless-picker ceiling, showing one concentrated judgment carries more correctness signal than the diluted token-by-token average.

qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.verdict_tag: A taught routine sticks to exactly its practiced
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.concept_primer: Show the model only before-and-after examples and ask it to reverse-engineer the hidden chain of operations that produced them: a two-step chain, then a three-step one. The taught routine is a chef's tasting drill: shortlist likely ingredients, imagine the dish, taste-check against the real one, revise.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_question: If a small model can't reverse-engineer three-step transformations from examples, can you fix that by teaching it a step-by-step guess-and-check routine for finding them?
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_answer: No. Fine-tuning the model on 1,476 worked guess-and-check traces doubled its success at the two-step depth it practiced on (lists jumped from 37% to 70%), yet did nothing one step deeper: three-step success stayed near 5%, statistically flat and no better than an untaught model. The skill installed cleanly, then refused to stretch a single operation further.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.why_it_matters: Match training depth to deployment depth: a model drilled on N-step reasoning gets strong at exactly N steps and stays at the floor at N+1. Hand deeper cases to external search or more thinking budget, not a smarter prompt.

key_numbers.label: Two-step reverse-engineering success (list tasks)
key_numbers.sub: after teaching the routine, the success rate roughly doubles at the practiced depth

key_numbers.label: Same task one step deeper (three-step)
key_numbers.sub: not a real change; the wall holds one composition step further out

key_numbers.label: A borrowed prior skill aimed at a new two-step task
key_numbers.sub: the transferred skill interfered — worse than doing nothing

key_numbers.label: Three-step success when the thinking budget is doubled
key_numbers.sub: raw compute, not any taught routine, was the only lever that moved the deep case

charts.chart_plain_title: Teaching the routine doubles two-step success but adds nothing at three steps
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups: the practiced two-step depth and the deeper three-step depth. In each, an untaught model and the routine-trained model appear for list tasks and string tasks. Height is the share of tasks whose operation structure was proposed correctly — taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At two steps the trained bars roughly double the untaught ones; at three steps all four bars collapse to the floor together — the skill does not stretch across depth.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every way of teaching the routine fails the three-step test
charts.chart_read: One bar per approach to the three-step task: the untaught baseline, a prompt-only routine scaffold, a borrowed prior skill applied as-is, and full routine fine-tuning. Height is the share of three-step tasks solved with correct structure; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits within a few points of the low untaught baseline, and none clears the pre-set improvement bar — the three-step wall is untouched by how the routine is delivered.

charts.chart_plain_title: The borrowed skill masters its home task but breaks on new ones
charts.chart_read: Three task groups. First the borrowed skill on its own home task, paired against its known score. Then two new task types (list and string, two-step), each pairing that same skill against a plain untrained model. Height is accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On home turf the skill matches its known score (92% vs 91%); on the new task types it drops to near zero, even below the untrained model — it interferes rather than transfers.

qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.verdict_tag: Follows a new rule but cannot discover one
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.concept_primer: A cook follows any written recipe flawlessly, but can only reverse-engineer a dish back into a recipe when it is one they already know. Hand them a strange new dish to taste and rebuild, and they fail — the very recipe they would have cooked perfectly.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_question: Can a small model work out a brand-new rule just from examples, or can it only reuse patterns it already picked up during training?
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_answer: Only patterns it already has. Told a scrambled counting order outright, the model applied it almost perfectly — 97 percent right. But shown examples of that same order and asked to work it out, it scored 12 percent, no better than the 10 percent you would get by guessing. Examples surface familiar structure; they do not discover genuinely new structure.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.why_it_matters: Do not expect examples to teach a small model a genuinely new rule — adding more only hurt here. Examples re-surface structure it already carries. When a task needs a novel mapping, state the rule outright instead of demonstrating it.

key_numbers.label: Works out that same rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: about chance — pure guessing scores 10%

key_numbers.label: Works out a familiar rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: even known structure is only half-recovered

key_numbers.label: Effect of adding more examples
key_numbers.sub: working out the new rule gets worse, not better

charts.chart_plain_title: Applying a rule versus figuring it out, for familiar and new orders
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups: left, the rule is stated outright; right, it must be figured out from examples. In each, one color is the familiar 0-to-9 order, the other a scrambled new order. Taller is more accurate; 10 percent marks pure guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: When the rule is stated both bars stand near the top; when it must be inferred, the new-order bar collapses to guessing level — obeying is easy, discovering is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does adding more examples help the model figure out the rule?
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is examples shown (five then eight); vertical axis is how often the model works out the rule, higher is better. One line is the familiar order, the other the scrambled new order; both would climb if examples helped.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward as examples grow — familiar from 45 to 25 percent, new from 15 to 5 — so the limit is familiarity, not too little data.

qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.verdict_tag: Frozen toolbox barely reaches brand-new tasks
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.concept_primer: Picture a toolbox of generic Python operations locked shut before you see any problem. A new task counts as solved only if one tool, or a short two-step chain, exactly reproduces the required answer, like a preset universal remote where you hope one button already matches.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_question: If you assemble a fixed toolbox of generic coding operations before seeing any problems, how many brand-new coding tasks can it solve exactly, out of the box?
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_answer: No, hardly any. The locked toolbox solved only about 14% of brand-new tasks, roughly 1 in 7, versus 37% on the familiar tasks it was shaped around. Reshuffling which tasks are unseen barely moves it, around 18%. And nearly every win was a single tool matching by luck, not a real composed solution, far too thin to build a trainable system on top of.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.why_it_matters: Before training a small model to translate problems into a fixed operation library, first measure how much of the target tasks that library can even express on fresh problems. About 15% coverage, mostly trivial, leaves nothing broad to learn.

key_numbers.sub: 23 of 160 unseen tasks where some operation passed every test

key_numbers.label: Coverage drop moving to brand-new tasks
key_numbers.sub: familiar tuning tasks versus brand-new tasks

key_numbers.label: Wins that needed just one operation
key_numbers.sub: only 3 solved tasks combined two operations

key_numbers.label: Matches that look right but are wrong
key_numbers.sub: candidates passing the visible examples that still fail hidden tests

charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new bar falls to well under half the familiar bar, showing the toolbox does not carry over to unseen problems.

charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near the same low level, around a fifth, so the weak coverage is consistent, not an unlucky draw.

charts.chart_takeaway: No category clears about a fifth and number tasks nearly bottom out, so the gap spans every domain, not one weak spot.
