charts.chart_read: For each task group, one shade shows how often tasks with a quick-check match had no true solution, another how often individual matches fail hidden tests; lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new tasks over nine in ten quick-check passes still fail hidden tests, so the visible check gives false confidence.

qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.verdict_tag: Independently derived solutions never agreed, so nothing shipped
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.concept_primer: Imagine pulling several proven algorithms from a small library, reshaping each independently into a solution for a new coding task, then trusting only answers where those separate versions agree on a tricky test input you invented yourself, with no answer key. Here they almost never agreed.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_question: If you fetch several unrelated proven algorithms, reshape each to a new coding task, and trust wherever their independent versions agree, can that agreement pick correct answers without an answer key?
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_answer: No. Requiring independently reshaped solutions to agree on invented tricky inputs, the system committed to zero of 24 unsolved tasks — the separate versions never converged. Simply taking the first solution that passes the public tests got 6 right but 8 wrong, a coin-flip error rate. Worse, forcing source variety cut how many tasks were solvable at all, from 9 down to 7, so it beat neither the simple picker nor a same-topic control.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.why_it_matters: Diversifying retrieval sources does not manufacture trustworthy agreement. When your library lacks several genuinely relevant algorithms per task, forcing variety just cuts relevance. Invest in a larger verified library or stronger counterexample generation, not diversity for its own sake.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the agreement rule would ship
key_numbers.sub: Independently reshaped solutions never agreed on a tricky test input

key_numbers.label: Simple picker's accuracy
key_numbers.sub: Taking the first solution that passes the public tests, out of 24 unsolved tasks

key_numbers.label: What diversifying did to solvable tasks
key_numbers.sub: Diversified pool vs same-topic pool — forcing variety left fewer tasks solvable

key_numbers.label: Passing-looking answers that were secretly wrong
key_numbers.sub: Diversified pool candidates that passed the public tests but failed hidden ones

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each picking rule commits, and whether it was right
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is one picking rule; height counts how many of 24 problems it committed an answer to. Green is commits that were actually correct, red actually wrong. More green and less red is better; the rightmost pair is the best any picker could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both independent-agreement rules sit flat at zero — requiring separate solutions to agree never committed to anything, while the loose first-pass rule commits often but is wrong more than right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Diversifying the source pool lowered its quality
charts.chart_read: For two ways of pulling reference solutions, one bar shows the share of problems the pool could actually solve (higher is better), the other the share of passing-looking answers that were secretly wrong (lower is better). Compare the diversified independent pool against the same-topic pool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The diversified pool has the lower solve share and the taller wrong-answer bar — forcing variety made the source material less relevant, not more trustworthy.

qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.verdict_tag: Correcting its live mistakes made it stop verifying
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.concept_primer: An interactive repair agent runs a loop: look, edit, run the test, commit the fix. Its correction clips were nearly all edits and almost none of the run-the-test moments — like an apprentice who watches endless bolt-tightening but rarely sees the engine started and checked.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_question: If you correct a small agent at the exact moments it stumbles mid-task, one step at a time, does it become a better multi-step problem-solver?
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_answer: No — it backfired badly. Success on the practice tasks fell from about 60% to 35%, and on tasks never touched in training from about 69% to 35%. It kept editing fluently but stopped running its checks: the verify-and-commit step was only about 1 in 40 correction examples, so it washed out entirely. On one repair family it never verified once, scoring zero.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.why_it_matters: Before fine-tuning a looping agent, count how often each decision type appears in your training data. Rare but decisive steps — verify, commit, stop — get drowned out by common edits while parsing, formatting, and surface scores keep looking healthy.

key_numbers.label: Practice-task success
key_numbers.sub: score on task families used for the correction training

key_numbers.label: Untouched-task success
key_numbers.sub: families never used in training, tested for transfer

key_numbers.label: How rare the verify step was
key_numbers.sub: 55 of 2,270 correction examples were the verify-and-commit action

key_numbers.label: Checks run on one repair family
key_numbers.sub: the retrained model never ran its test there, scoring zero

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the retrained model gained or lost on each task group
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the retrained model's score minus the original model's, for trained task families, untouched task families, and basic single-step skills. The zero line means no change; bars above zero are better, below zero are worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both multi-turn task bars plunge far below zero, roughly a quarter to a third of the scale, while the basic-skill bar barely dips — the damage is to in-task decision-making, not underlying knowledge.

qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.verdict_tag: Training failed to teach picking the right tools
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.concept_primer: Picture a wall of 512 tools and a hidden machine built from exactly two of them. Shown only the numbers going in and the numbers coming out, the model must name both tools. Even allowed over a thousand best guesses, it never named the right pair.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_question: Given only a few input-output examples, can a small model learn to pick the right two building blocks from a library of 512 to rebuild a hidden program?
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_answer: No. Even after extra training, and even allowed to submit its 1,024 strongest paired guesses, it got exactly zero right — no better than the untrained model, and no better than a control with the tool labels scrambled. Training error dropped then leveled off, so the model learned the answer format fine; it simply never learned to choose the correct tools from examples.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.why_it_matters: At this size, blind brute-force ranking over a large tool library will not work; invest instead in structured semantic search or a better teaching signal. Separately, carefully designed test examples cut ambiguity far more than piling on random ones.

key_numbers.label: Right pair found, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: among 1,024 combined guesses; the scrambled-label control also scored zero

key_numbers.label: Library the model chose from
key_numbers.sub: must name both from a handful of examples alone

key_numbers.label: Candidate programs left after six probes
key_numbers.sub: designed examples vs random ones; fewer means less ambiguity

key_numbers.label: Picked the right program on hard cases
key_numbers.sub: designed probe examples vs random ones

charts.chart_plain_title: Right pair never found, whether untrained, trained, or label-scrambled
charts.chart_read: Bars group three conditions along the bottom: untrained model, trained model, and trained model with tool labels scrambled. Two bars each show a looser test (right pair anywhere in the 1,024 guesses) and a stricter test (the single top pick). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits flat at zero, so training added nothing — the failure is the task itself, not one bad setup or a loose scoring rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: Well-designed probe questions shrink the guessing space
charts.chart_read: Two bars: six random example cases on the left versus six deliberately designed questions on the right. Height is the average number of candidate programs still consistent with everything seen; lower is better, meaning less ambiguity.
charts.chart_takeaway: Designed probes leave roughly a quarter as many candidates as random ones, so which examples you show matters far more than how many.

charts.chart_plain_title: Designed probes pick the right program more often
charts.chart_read: Two bars: six random example cases versus six deliberately designed questions. Height is the share of times the chosen program passed every hidden test case; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Designed probes lift success from under a third to over four in ten — real progress, but selection still fails most of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error falls fast then levels off
charts.chart_read: The line traces training error down the vertical axis against training step, from step 1 to 80 across the bottom; lower means the model is fitting the answer format better. It drops sharply early, then bounces around a flat plateau.
charts.chart_takeaway: Loss plateaus rather than failing to converge, confirming the model learned the output format — so the zero score is a selection failure, not a training glitch.

qwen35_4b_jacobian_counterfactual_branch.verdict_tag: Balanced internal nudges fail to steer real reasoning
qwen35_4b_jacobian_counterfactual_branch.concept_primer: Imagine pausing one scratchpad halfway through, then making twelve balanced copies. Each copy gets a tiny internal nudge toward a different possible answer and is allowed to finish reasoning. Because every answer gets one nudge, no answer is secretly favored.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_counterfactual_branch.plain_question: Can balanced internal nudges make one half-finished reasoning path explore better futures than spending the same effort on ordinary independent attempts?
qwen35_4b_jacobian_counterfactual_branch.plain_answer: No. Across all three allowed strengths, the meaningful nudge made its assigned answer win only 4 of 48 times—exactly the one-in-twelve chance rate and identical to a generic nudge. The probabilities barely moved even at the strongest setting. Because the write itself failed, no expensive continuation or correctness run was opened.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_counterfactual_branch.why_it_matters: A direction that works when copied from a clean semantic example is not automatically a push-button control inside arbitrary free-form reasoning. The next test must restore an explicit semantic anchor and copy full coordinates, rather than merely pushing the last scratchpad token harder.

key_numbers.label: Assigned answer selected
key_numbers.sub: 8.3%, exactly chance, at every tested strength

key_numbers.label: Largest average probability lift
key_numbers.sub: versus +0.15 required for a usable write

key_numbers.label: Numeric control rows valid
key_numbers.sub: the null is behavioral, not a broken control

charts.chart_plain_title: Every nudge strength stays at chance
charts.chart_read: For each of three nudge strengths, compare the meaningful and generic bars with the tall required bar. Height is how often the branch's assigned answer became the model's answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Meaningful and generic nudges overlap at one-in-twelve while the required bar is 60%, so this internal write method is unusable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Meaningful and generic nudges were physically matched
charts.chart_read: At each of five internal layers, neighboring bars show the physical size of the two nudges. Matching bars rule out a weaker generic control.
charts.chart_takeaway: The control matched the intervention's strength, confirming that the chance result is not a simple size mismatch.

qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.verdict_tag: Rewrite a model's early concept, later steps obey
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.concept_primer: Picture a package on a sorting line with its destination written up front. Rewrite that early label and it not only drops into a different bin but gets a different zip code stamped on it downstream. A few early internal numbers are that label.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.plain_question: Inside a small model, is there a compact spot that stores which concept it is handling, and does editing that spot steer the later steps too?
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.plain_answer: Yes. Editing a handful of internal numbers at the early moment the model names its concept made it answer as a completely different concept on all 48 fresh test items, and a separately computed digit that depends on that concept flipped to match too. Same-size random edits changed nothing, and knowing only the output direction changed nothing: the effect needs the real concept numbers.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_transport_control_rep.why_it_matters: To steer or debug a small model, edit the early spot where it names a concept, not the layers near the output, since changes there propagate downstream. Always include same-strength random edits, or you cannot separate real steering from noise.

key_numbers.label: Direct answer after overwriting the concept
key_numbers.sub: fresh test items that output the swapped-in concept, up from none

key_numbers.label: Separate downstream digit after the same edit
key_numbers.sub: a later computation that reads the concept flipped to match too

key_numbers.label: Same-size random edits
key_numbers.sub: matched-strength random perturbations never moved the answer

key_numbers.label: Injecting the wrong concept
key_numbers.sub: produced that concept's answer, never the target, proving specificity

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each edit forced the swapped-in concept as the answer
charts.chart_read: Eight interventions across the bottom; bar height is the share of 48 items answering the swapped-in concept. Two bars per group: the model's direct answer and the separately computed digit. Taller is a stronger effect; controls should sit at zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three genuine concept edits reach 100% (the paired edit 96% on the digit), while the wrong-concept, output-direction, and both random controls sit flat at zero, a specific effect, not any-edit-works.

charts.chart_plain_title: How strongly the model preferred the new concept over the old
charts.chart_read: Five interventions across the bottom; bar height is the model's average preference for the new concept minus the original, shown for the direct answer and the downstream digit. Above zero favors the new concept; below zero favors the original.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the real concept edit crosses above zero, reversing a strong preference for the original into a strong preference for the new concept; the output-direction and both random controls stay firmly negative.

qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.verdict_tag: You can edit the word, not the meaning
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.concept_primer: Picture a speaker reading from a teleprompter. The edit rewrites the last word an instant before they say it, so they announce a new concept out loud. But their mental picture never changed, so any follow-up reasoning still runs on the original concept.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.plain_question: If you reach inside a small model and swap the concept it is about to say, does the rest of its reasoning follow along and recompute?
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.plain_answer: No. Editing one internal direction at a late layer flipped the concept the model said aloud on 18 of 24 tries, up from never, and far beating an ordinary readout-style edit that worked only 1 in 5 times. But when that concept had to feed a follow-up calculation, the edit changed nothing on all 24 items, at every layer. It moved the model's mouth, not its mind.
qwen35_4b_jacobian_value_transport.why_it_matters: If you steer a small model by editing its internal directions, don't assume the change reprograms its reasoning. Test whether downstream steps actually recompute from your edit — a direction can be readable, even writable, yet completely inert for real thinking.

key_numbers.label: Concept the model said out loud
key_numbers.sub: how often the edit flipped the spoken concept, at the best layer (18 of 24 items)

key_numbers.label: Follow-up answer using that concept
key_numbers.sub: times the same edit changed a downstream calculation, at every layer tested

key_numbers.label: An ordinary readout-style edit
key_numbers.sub: how often the plain edit flipped the spoken concept, versus 3 in 4 for the targeted one

key_numbers.label: Downstream lean as edit force grew eightfold
key_numbers.sub: the follow-up stayed pinned to the original concept while the spoken concept crossed over

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the edit changed the answer, by model layer
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is which layer was edited, 8 to 24; the vertical axis is how often the edit produced the target concept, so higher is a stronger edit. Three lines track the spoken concept, a follow-up calculation, and an ordinary readout-style edit.
charts.chart_takeaway: The spoken-concept line spikes to three-quarters only at the last layer, the readout edit reaches one-fifth, and the follow-up-calculation line never leaves zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: As the edit gets stronger, does the follow-up answer move?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is edit strength, rising eightfold left to right; the vertical axis is how far the output leans toward the new concept versus the original, where above zero favors the new. One line is the spoken concept, the other the follow-up calculation.
charts.chart_takeaway: The spoken-concept line climbs and crosses into new-concept territory; the follow-up line stays pinned far below and flat — more force never switched the reasoning.

qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.verdict_tag: Emitting two looked-up codes at once fails
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.concept_primer: Each problem ships with a glossary pairing every building-block operation with a short code, freshly reshuffled so codes can't be memorized. From a few input-output examples the model must deduce which two operations ran and copy both of their exact codes in one shot.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_question: Can a small model read a fresh, reshuffled glossary and directly output the exact two codes that identify a hidden two-step program?
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.plain_answer: No. Across every version — untrained, trained, and with the glossary's descriptions scrambled — the model got both codes exactly right zero percent of the time, even when allowed sixteen guesses. Training pushed single-code accuracy from near zero up to roughly one in eight, but scrambling the glossary barely changed that, proving it was pattern-matching familiar-looking codes, not actually reading the lookup.
qwen35_4b_joint_shortlister_ladder.why_it_matters: Don't ask a small model to both look up unfamiliar codes and commit to a multi-part answer in one generation. Split it: let a program-runner narrow the candidates, then have the model only rank a short list.

key_numbers.label: Both codes exactly right
key_numbers.sub: every version, even when allowed sixteen guesses

key_numbers.label: One code right, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: but scrambling the glossary barely changed it, so not real reading

key_numbers.label: Candidates left after the clues
key_numbers.sub: designed clues nearly halve the guesswork, yet recall stayed zero

key_numbers.label: Training error, start to end
key_numbers.sub: learned the answer format, not how to use the glossary

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct two-code answer never appeared, in any version
charts.chart_read: Four clusters, one per version: untrained, trained, trained on the harder designed cases, and trained with the glossary scrambled. Within each, bars show how often the correct both-code answer, or just the left or right code, showed up among the guesses. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The both-code bar sits flat at zero everywhere, and the single-code bars barely rise and stay nearly as high when the glossary is scrambled, so the small lift isn't genuine reading.

charts.chart_plain_title: Designed clues shrink the pool of possible answers, but the model can't use it
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many operations are in the glossary; vertical axis is how many answers still fit the shown clues, so fewer means less guessing. The two designed-clue lines sit below their random-clue counterparts across both problem templates. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Designed-clue lines run below random-clue lines at every glossary size, yet that smaller pool of possibilities never converted into a single correct model answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error dropped fast, then flattened partway down
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress in optimizer steps; vertical axis is the model's error while learning, where lower means a closer fit to the training answers. The single line falls steeply at first, then wanders in a middling band. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line plateaus in a mid-range band rather than near zero, matching a model that learned the answer format but not how to read the glossary.

qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.verdict_tag: Great at following rules, blind at finding them
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.concept_primer: Picture a stack of secret decoder rings, each swapping made-up words a fixed way. Handed the right ring, the model decodes flawlessly. But shown only a few finished translations and asked which rings produced them, it must play detective and infer the hidden rule. That detective step is the wall.
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_question: Can a small model figure out a hidden rule just by watching a few worked examples, the way it can follow a rule it is handed directly?
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.plain_answer: No. Handed the rule outright, the model applies it correctly 86% of the time. Asked to infer that same one-step rule from worked examples, it scores 0% — below even the roughly 6% that pure guessing would earn. Letting it reason step by step lifts inference only to a coin-flip 50%, still short of the 75% it reaches simply executing. It runs rules; it does not discover them.
qwen35_4b_language_proposal_wall.why_it_matters: If your system needs a small model to find the rule behind examples, expect failure — even where it flawlessly applies that rule once stated. State rules explicitly; if it must infer, budget thinking and verify, since inferred rules come out wrong about half the time.

key_numbers.label: Applying a rule it is handed
key_numbers.sub: correct on the simplest one-step rule, no thinking

key_numbers.label: Inferring the rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: same rule, single pass — below even guessing

key_numbers.label: Pure guessing
key_numbers.sub: the chance baseline, roughly 1 in 16

key_numbers.label: Inference once allowed to think
key_numbers.sub: only a coin flip, and still error-prone

charts.chart_plain_title: Following a handed rule versus figuring it out, with and without thinking
charts.chart_read: Four bars show accuracy on the simplest one-step rule; taller is better. The left two apply a rule the model was handed (first without thinking, then with); the right two infer the rule from examples (without, then with thinking).
charts.chart_takeaway: The apply bars tower over the infer bars; inference sits flat at zero without thinking and reaches only a coin flip with it — running rules is easy, discovering them is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Inferring a rule stays at chance no matter how simple
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is rule depth — how many secret rings must be chained; the vertical axis is single-pass accuracy, higher is better. The upper line applies a rule the model was handed; the lower line infers the rule from examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The inference line hugs the floor at every depth; applying a given rule starts high then sags as chaining more rings strains the model — inference never leaves the ground.

qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.verdict_tag: A reasoning wall for code, not language
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.concept_primer: The task is a relay: each made-up name points to the next, and you follow the baton several hops along. Written as plain sentences, this small model runs the relay flawlessly; written as a code-style lookup table, it fumbles the very first handoff.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_question: A small model seems to stall after chaining about three reasoning steps. When the very same follow-the-chain puzzle is written in plain English, does it still stall?
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.plain_answer: No. Written as ordinary sentences, the model follows a chain of invented names near-perfectly through four hops (94 to 100 percent correct), far above the roughly 4 percent a blind guess earns. The three-step ceiling is really about code-style formatting: the identical chain shown as a code lookup table drops it to 3 percent at a single hop, because the model copies the table instead of tracing it.
qwen35_4b_language_reasoning_wall.why_it_matters: If a small-model task needs multi-step reasoning, phrase it as plain-language sentences, not symbol tables or code. Identical logic in code-style formatting can flip the model into echoing code instead of reasoning, collapsing accuracy to near chance.

key_numbers.label: Plain English, three-hop chain
key_numbers.sub: near-perfect where a hard wall was expected

key_numbers.label: Same chain as a code table, one hop
key_numbers.sub: model dumps code instead of tracing it

key_numbers.label: Made-up relation words, three hops
key_numbers.sub: format helps, not familiar word meanings

key_numbers.label: Blind-guess rate
key_numbers.sub: chance baseline the test is hardened against

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by chain length: plain sentences, made-up words, code table
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length, one to six hops; vertical axis is percent correct out of 80 tries, higher is better. Three lines: plain-English sentences, made-up relation words, and the same chain as a code-style lookup table; about 4 percent is chance.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both language lines sit near the top through three hops while the code-table line starts near zero, showing format, not the number of steps, sets the wall.

qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.verdict_tag: Shallow answers hide inside; deep answers never form
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.concept_primer: The model sees input-output examples and must guess the hidden recipe of list operations behind them. Picture its internal activity as private scratch paper; a simple decoder reads whether the recipe's first step is written there, even when the model never says it aloud.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_question: When a small model can't produce the right chain of list operations, has it secretly figured out the answer inside, or does it genuinely not know it?
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.plain_answer: It depends on the number of steps. For a one-step recipe the first step is almost perfectly written on the internal scratch paper (99% readable), yet the model voices it only 44% of the time: it knows but stays silent. For the hardest three-step recipe the scratch paper is nearly blank: the readable signal (about 13%) barely clears the 14% noise floor, meaning the answer was never worked out.
qwen35_4b_latent_composition_probe.why_it_matters: Before betting on tricks that read or nudge a small model's internal state to fix failures, check the skill is actually in there. That only helps on shallow tasks; deep multi-step gaps need training or external tools to install the missing ability.

key_numbers.label: One-step recipe: written inside vs said aloud
key_numbers.sub: first step read from the internal state vs the model naming it

key_numbers.label: How readable the first step is, by depth
key_numbers.sub: decoder accuracy at one, two, and three steps

key_numbers.label: Three-step wall: real signal vs noise floor
key_numbers.sub: genuine internal signal barely clears the overfitting floor

key_numbers.label: Producing the full correct recipe
key_numbers.sub: one-step to three-step compositions

charts.chart_plain_title: What the model knows inside versus what it says, by recipe depth
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for one-, two-, and three-step recipes; height is first-step accuracy, higher is better. Four bars each: the first step read from inside the model, the model naming it, the model generating the full recipe, and a shuffled-label noise floor for reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: At one step the read-from-inside bar towers over what the model says or generates; by three steps all four bars collapse together near the noise floor, so hidden knowledge is present when shallow and gone when deep.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where inside the model the first step becomes readable
charts.chart_read: Lines track how readable the first step is at each layer, from the input embedding on the left to the final layer on the right, one line per recipe depth. Higher is better; a flat band near 10% marks random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The one-step line climbs to near-perfect by the middle layers and holds; the two- and three-step lines stay low and flat, so deeper recipes are barely worked out anywhere inside.

qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.verdict_tag: Self-training installed the planning it couldn't do alone
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.concept_primer: Give a small model a tiny language of list operations and a three-move goal, then ask it to pick operations one at a time. It acts like a chess player who instantly spots checkmate-in-one but is blind to the opening move of a three-move combination.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_question: Can a small model plan its own multi-step solution by picking one operation at a time, and can cheap self-training teach it to plan ahead?
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.plain_answer: No, not out of the box. One step from the goal it picks the right operation about eight times better than chance, but on the opening move three steps out it ranks correctly only at chance — recognition, not planning. As its own guide it solved 1 of 80 puzzles, no better than random's 2. Self-training on its verified solutions installed real planning: 18 of 80, nearly matching trying every operation.
qwen35_4b_latent_decomposition.why_it_matters: When a small model guides its own multi-step search, don't trust its early moves — confident-but-wrong first picks can drag it below random. Measure first-move accuracy, and self-train on verified full solutions to install transferable planning.

key_numbers.label: Right first move, base model
key_numbers.sub: three steps from the goal — at or below random guessing

key_numbers.label: Right final move, base model
key_numbers.sub: one step from the goal (23.7%) — sharp recognition, no lookahead

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved as its own guide
key_numbers.sub: before vs after self-training, same compute budget

key_numbers.label: Self-trained guide vs trying every operation
key_numbers.sub: near-parity at low budget

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the right next operation is the top pick, by step
charts.chart_read: Three groups mark distance to the goal: three, two, then one operation away. Bar height is how often the model's top-ranked operation was correct — taller is better, with random guessing near three percent. Colors compare the base model against two self-trained versions.
charts.chart_takeaway: Base bars sit at the floor on the opening move and rise only near the finish; self-trained bars tower over base at every step — practice installed real planning, not just finishing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Puzzles solved versus compute budget: guided search vs trying everything
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is compute budget — attempts allowed per puzzle; vertical is the share of 80 puzzles solved, higher is better. Lines sweep trying-every-operation and random proposals across budgets; two dots mark base-guided and self-trained-guided search at one matched low budget.
charts.chart_takeaway: At equal budget the base dot sits below the random line, while the self-trained dot leaps about seventeenfold to nearly meet trying-everything — a misleading guide turned useful.

qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.concept_primer: A code reviewer can reliably tell a working program from a broken one. Reward it purely for widening that gap, and it finds a shortcut: push down its own odds of writing either program. Soon it can barely produce working code at all.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_question: If a small coding model can reliably tell its own correct answers from its wrong ones, can you train it to prefer the correct ones and write better code?
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.plain_answer: No. The model already ranked its own correct answer above its wrong one 81% of the time — a sharp internal judge. But training it to favor the correct ones destroyed its writing: single-best-guess solving peaked near 5% then cratered toward zero. The dull alternative won — simply training twice as long on the correct examples alone tripled first-try solving to about 11%.
qwen35_4b_learn_from_failures.why_it_matters: If a small model already ranks its good outputs above its bad ones, don't spend compute on preference training to exploit that — it can silently collapse generation. First exhaust plain training on the verified-correct examples; the ceiling is often just undertraining.

key_numbers.label: How often it ranks its own correct answer above its wrong one
key_numbers.sub: a strong internal judge, before any training to exploit it

key_numbers.label: First-try solve rate as preference training continues
key_numbers.sub: generation collapses under preference optimization

key_numbers.label: First-try solve rate from just training twice as long
key_numbers.sub: the boring lever roughly tripled it

key_numbers.label: Best-of-16-tries solve rate from the winner
key_numbers.sub: highest sampling ceiling of any method tested

charts.chart_plain_title: Training longer on correct examples beats preference training on solve rate
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training method, from the untrained base through training longer on correct examples to preference-trained versions. One bar per cluster is the best-of-16-tries rate, the other the single-best-guess rate; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The train-twice-as-long cluster stands tallest on both bars; apart from one tiny early single-shot bump, every preference-trained cluster falls back to or below plain training — preferring correct over wrong added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Preference training's solve rate crashes the longer it runs
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how long preference training continues; the two lines are the single-best-guess and best-of-16 solve rates, and a flat line marks the train-longer winner. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Single-best-guess gives a tiny early bump while best-of-16 holds flat, then both plunge toward zero, ending far below the flat winner line — more preference optimization steadily destroys generation.

charts.chart_plain_title: A fix for the collapse helps a little but never recovers
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis strengthens a stabilizer meant to hold up the correct answers while pushing away wrong ones. The two rising lines are the preference-trained solve rates; two flat lines mark plain and longer training. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Stronger stabilizing lifts the preference lines slightly, confirming the diagnosis, but single-best-guess never even reaches plain training and neither line approaches the longer-training winner — the fix mitigates without ever recovering.

qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.verdict_tag: Wins the side tests, loses the main one
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.concept_primer: Picture Guess Who, but with thousands of candidate programs that all match the examples you have already seen. Each move you run one new input; the best input is the question that splits the survivors evenly, knocking out the most wrong programs at once.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_question: When a small model must choose which extra input to run to narrow thousands of candidate programs, can it learn to pick better than a simple rule?
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.plain_answer: It depends. On the main test set a simple even-splitting rule beat the trained picker after one extra input — 91 percent of programs fully correct versus 87 — and the best-possible choice reached 97 percent. The picker did edge ahead on two other test sets. But its inputs ruled out only about 8 wrong candidates on average, versus 20 for the ideal pick.
qwen35_4b_learned_active_trace_policy.why_it_matters: Choosing an informative test is harder than generating candidates. Before you train a picker model, benchmark a cheap hand-coded splitting rule first — it may already beat your model, and its edge can widen as more tests compound.

key_numbers.label: Right program is always among the candidates
key_numbers.sub: so every failure is choosing which test to run, not building the program

key_numbers.label: Main test, one extra input: simple rule vs learned picker
key_numbers.sub: share of programs fully correct; the simple rule won

key_numbers.label: Another test set, one extra input
key_numbers.sub: here the learned picker pulled ahead of the simple rule

key_numbers.label: Wrong candidates each chosen input rules out
key_numbers.sub: learned picker versus the ideal pick, main test average

charts.chart_plain_title: With one extra input, share of programs fully solved by each method
charts.chart_read: Three groups of bars, one per test set (the main set plus two others). Within each group, four bars compare picking a random input, a simple even-splitting rule, the learned picker, and the best-possible pick. Height is the share of programs fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned picker tops the simple rule on the two secondary sets but falls short on the main set; the best-possible pick leads everywhere, showing real headroom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Main test: share solved as more extra inputs are allowed
charts.chart_read: On the main test set, each line tracks the share of programs fully solved as the picker is allowed more extra inputs, from none to three. Four lines compare a random pick, a simple rule, the learned picker, and the best-possible pick. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned line starts below the simple rule at one input, then climbs to match it by three; the best-possible line stays clearly above both.

charts.chart_plain_title: A harder test set: share solved as more extra inputs are allowed
charts.chart_read: On the hardest test set, each line shows the share of programs fully solved as extra inputs increase from none to three, comparing a random pick, a simple rule, the learned picker, and the best-possible pick. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned picker leads the simple rule at one and two inputs, then slips behind it at three; the best-possible pick stays far ahead throughout.

charts.chart_read: Three pairs of bars, one per test set. Each pair compares the wrong candidates ruled out by a single input: the learned picker's choice versus the best-possible choice. Taller means the chosen input carried more information.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every test set the learned picker's bar sits under half the best-possible height — a real but only mild signal in its choices.

qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.verdict_tag: Choosing when to run code cuts cost
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.concept_primer: Picture a clerk reshaping a messy spreadsheet with one example row already solved. The clerk both eyeballs an answer and writes a formula, tests that formula against the visible row, then a supervisor decides which answer to hand in — never seeing the final grade.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_question: If a small model can both guess a table's new layout and write code to compute it, does learning when to trust the code make it more accurate?
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.plain_answer: Only its cost, not its accuracy. Answering directly solved none of twelve unseen tasks; letting the model write and run code recovered two — about one in six — and spoiled nothing it already had right. But even a flawless, all-knowing chooser topped out at those same two: the ceiling is the model's raw skill, not the decision. The trained controller's real win was reaching that ceiling with half the code runs and zero wasted repairs.
qwen35_4b_live_tool_dagger.why_it_matters: Before engineering a policy for when your model reaches for tools, measure the ceiling with a perfect-hindsight chooser. If it only ties a cheap rule, your bottleneck is tool capability — spend the policy on cutting wasted tool calls, not accuracy.

key_numbers.label: Direct answers alone
key_numbers.sub: unseen tasks solved without touching tools

key_numbers.label: With a code tool added
key_numbers.sub: tools recovered two cases, spoiled none

key_numbers.label: Cost to reach that same score
key_numbers.sub: trained controller versus always-run-code

key_numbers.label: Programs it chose to commit
key_numbers.sub: the controller never submitted a wrong program

charts.chart_plain_title: Unseen-task accuracy for six ways of choosing tools
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy's share of twelve unseen table tasks solved exactly; taller is better. The six: answer directly, always run code, a learned rule, a trained controller, a scrambled-label control, and a perfect-hindsight chooser.
charts.chart_takeaway: Direct-only and the scrambled control sit at zero; every genuine code-using strategy, even the perfect chooser, ties at one in six, so decision quality never lifts the ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: Practice-set versus unseen-task accuracy for each strategy
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per strategy: the lighter bar is the practice set, the darker the harder unseen families; height is share solved exactly, taller better. Same six strategies, from direct-only to the perfect-hindsight chooser.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every strategy falls sharply from practice to unseen; even the perfect chooser drops from about 58% to one in six, so the unseen families are simply harder.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent to reach the same score, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Five strategies that all solve two of twelve unseen tasks. For each, one bar counts programs written and run, the other counts repair attempts across twelve cases; lower is better since scores are equal.
charts.chart_takeaway: Always-run burns twelve program runs plus eighteen repairs; the trained controller needs six runs and zero repairs, the perfect chooser just two — routing cuts compute, not accuracy.

qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.verdict_tag: Shortest reasoning outranks the clever quality score
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.concept_primer: You hold the answer key. To choose which fully worked-out reasoning chains a small model learns from, score each by how much more confident the model grows in the correct answer after reading it — keeping the notes that make the right answer feel most inevitable.
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.plain_question: When a small model reasons for as long as it wants, can its own confidence in the correct answer flag which reasoning chains are worth training on?
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.plain_answer: Barely. That confidence signal does sort stronger reasoning above weaker about 60% of the time — better than a coin flip's 50%, and enough to lift a picked chain's success from 16% to 22%. But two dumb rules beat it: just keeping the shortest chain, or the most typical one, each rank correctly near 70%. The real training test is still pending.
qwen35_4b_long_horizon_answer_potential_.why_it_matters: Before building a confidence scorer to curate which reasoning a small model learns from, race it against trivial baselines — chain length and how typical the chain is. If those match or beat it, your fancy signal is mostly a proxy for length and habit.

key_numbers.label: Confidence score's ranking skill
key_numbers.sub: how often it ranks a winning chain above a losing one; 50% is a coin flip

key_numbers.sub: same ranking test, no scoring — and it beats the clever score

key_numbers.label: Top-picked chain lands the right answer

charts.chart_read: Three bars, one per stage, on a vertical log scale of candidate reasoning chains gathered: an earlier length-capped run near 2,000, this study's calibration near 9,000, and its full planned pool near 95,000. Taller means a bigger search, not better quality.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full pool towers about 46 times over the capped pilot, because uncapped, naturally finished reasoning needs a far larger haul to surface the rare high-quality chains.

qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_answer_s.verdict_tag: Strict answer seam failed; native commit token isolates one fresh repair
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_answer_s.concept_primer: Before asking whether concrete intermediate states help solve a puzzle, this test first checks whether the model can reliably enter a short, machine-readable answer mode. A bad output format must not be mistaken for a reasoning failure.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_answer_s.plain_question: Can any think/no-think and freeform/program-prefix answer style reliably emit a two-step program before the materialized-state test begins?
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_answer_s.plain_answer: No registered style qualified: all four scored zero strict parses out of 48, so mechanics stayed sealed. Removing only the final chat-end marker and newline made both no-think styles exact on all 48 rows; thinking still had extra close markers. That post-run clue motivates a fresh answer-stage commit-boundary test, not a retroactive pass.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_answer_s.why_it_matters: The firewall prevented a token-boundary mismatch from being mistaken for failed reasoning or a residual-state result. The next test can now change one registered thing—stop at the first tokenizer chat-end token during the answer stage—while preserving strict parsing and every matched control.

key_numbers.label: Authenticated outputs
key_numbers.sub: five durable calibration transactions

key_numbers.label: Strictly qualified styles

key_numbers.label: No-think exact after suffix removal
key_numbers.sub: both paired no-think styles; post-decision only

charts.chart_read: For each style, the strict bar is the registered rate. The frozen-parser diagnostic removes only final chat end plus newline; the separate expected-tail diagnostic takes the last segment after a thinking close and can hide extra close markers. Both are explanatory, not rescored results.
charts.chart_takeaway: All registered bars are zero. Suffix-only removal fully repairs no-think but not thinking, so the next fresh test should register the native answer-stage chat-end boundary and retain strict grammar.

qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.verdict_tag: Model-free design is ready for mechanics
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.concept_primer: Imagine trying every possible first move, writing down the concrete intermediate state each move creates, and asking the same solver to finish from each state. Public examples then choose among the finished candidates.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.plain_question: Can concrete intermediate states help a small model finish multi-step rules better than names alone or the same spending on ordinary attempts?
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.plain_answer: Not yet. The scientific design and every model-free construction check pass, but the model has not run. The frozen test contains 264 fresh functions, and all 38,596 planned prompt renderings fit their assigned context limits.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.why_it_matters: The controls prevent extra attempts, a cheaper exhaustive solver, or a broken answer format from masquerading as an improvement. Treat this as a trustworthy launch pad, not capability evidence.

key_numbers.label: Fresh functions frozen

charts.chart_read: The horizontal groups count tasks with one, two, three, or four publicly valid first moves; bar height is the number of frozen tasks. No height is better—the planned 8:8:4:4 balance repeats in every block.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first two bars match at 88 and the next two match at 44, confirming the planned ambiguity balance across 264 tasks.

qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.verdict_tag: Cheap materialized ranking fails; free generation has no valid answer interface
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.concept_primer: The previous test returned one batch of answers but lost them before saving. Its rules forbid drawing that same batch again, so this successor starts with entirely new puzzles and random draws while keeping the scientific comparison unchanged.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.plain_question: Can concrete intermediate states help the small model finish multi-step rules better than names alone or equally costly ordinary attempts?
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.plain_answer: The generation test cannot answer the question because every reasoning trace reached its length cap and the materialized arm parsed only 12 of 52 outputs, with zero successes. The separate one-token ranking test is a clean negative: materialized recall among viable first moves was 0.257, below names alone, shuffled states, listwise choice, and a surface-only rule.
qwen35_4b_materialized_residual_sibling_.why_it_matters: The full transaction record is finally durable, so the cheap ranking path can be retired confidently. But the free-generation null must stay narrow: it diagnoses a broken answer mode, not proof that concrete intermediate states are useless. A successor now qualifies the answer interface before mechanics.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is the average share of publicly viable first moves recovered among the top four choices. Compare materialized states with names alone, shuffled states, listwise choice, a surface-only rule, and random ranking.
charts.chart_takeaway: Materialized ranking beats random but remains below all five structured comparisons, so this cheap ranking route does not justify a larger search.

qwen35_4b_meta_induction.verdict_tag: Small models crack new rules only by reasoning
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.concept_primer: You see a few coded messages with their plain meanings, then must decode a new one — but the cipher is hidden. You can't guess it outright; you try each candidate rule against the examples until one fits. Writing the steps out is what makes it work.
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_question: If a small model can't spot the hidden rule behind a few examples, can training teach it that skill — and where does the learned skill actually live?
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.plain_answer: Yes, but only when it thinks out loud. The trained model cracks brand-new hidden rules perfectly when it writes out its reasoning, yet collapses to worse-than-guessing — about 1 in 100 — when forced to answer in a single step. The skill lives in the written steps, not the weights, and taught as a general try-and-check method it even transfers to rule types it never trained on.
qwen35_4b_meta_induction.why_it_matters: If your small model must infer structure from examples, let it reason in tokens — don't force one-shot answers or cram the skill into the weights. Teach a general try-and-check procedure, and mix in plain rule-following examples so training doesn't wreck existing skills.

key_numbers.label: Answering instantly versus reasoning aloud
key_numbers.sub: same trained model, cracking brand-new hidden rules

key_numbers.label: Cracking a rule type it never trained on
key_numbers.sub: via reasoning, as high as familiar types; guessing is 1 in 10

key_numbers.label: Cost of cramming the skill into the weights
key_numbers.sub: its ability to apply an already-stated rule collapses

key_numbers.label: Rule-finding forced into the weights alone
key_numbers.sub: rises above chance but plateaus far below its 72% ceiling

charts.chart_takeaway: The unseen bar (91%) stands as tall as the trained ones (88-96%) — the model learned a general infer-and-check method, not memorized rules.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same trained model: near-zero in one step, perfect when reasoning
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one setup's accuracy at inferring a unseen hidden rule. Bars span the untrained model and trained models, forced to answer in one step versus allowed to write out reasoning. The two rightmost share one trained model. Higher is better; guessing is 1 in 10.
charts.chart_takeaway: That trained model scores near zero (1%) when forced to answer instantly but a full 100% when it reasons — the written steps do all the work, not the weights.

charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how much answer-only training, from none to four thousand to eight thousand practice rounds. One line infers unseen rules of the trained type, another an untrained type; dashed lines mark the apply-a-stated-rule ceiling and pure guessing. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-type line rises to 40% but flattens far below the 72% ceiling, and the untrained-type line barely lifts — a narrow trick, not a general skill.

charts.chart_takeaway: Applying a stated rule collapses from 72% to 9% while rule-inference only climbs from 9% to 40% — the training buys a little induction by destroying execution.

qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.verdict_tag: Knows when it's guessing, but can't say so
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.concept_primer: The model answers with one digit, but underneath it spreads a fixed pile of betting chips across all ten possible digits. When it knows the answer, nearly every chip lands on one; when guessing, they scatter thin. That hidden pile is its true confidence — not anything it says aloud.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_question: When a small model is about to get an answer wrong, does it have any inner sense that it's just guessing — and can it actually tell you so?
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.plain_answer: Yes, but only in its numbers, never its words. The probability it quietly places on the digit it writes sorts right answers from wrong ones almost perfectly — 95 out of 100, versus 50 for a coin flip — and beats guessing from surface prompt features alone (61). Yet ask it outright "is your answer correct?" and it scores 46, no better than chance.
qwen35_4b_metacognitive_boundary.why_it_matters: For a confidence or abstain gate on a small model, read the probability it assigns to its own answer — one forward pass. Never trust a "how confident are you?" prompt or a self-check; both are noise here.

key_numbers.label: How well the answer-odds sort right from wrong
key_numbers.sub: near-perfect; 50 out of 100 would be a coin flip

key_numbers.label: How well its spoken self-check sorts right from wrong

key_numbers.label: How well surface prompt features sort right from wrong
key_numbers.sub: the answer-odds beat this too — genuine self-knowledge, not surface-reading

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on questions it chooses to answer, after abstaining
key_numbers.sub: dropping the answers it marks low-confidence

qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.verdict_tag: Hunting the model's own errors added nothing extra
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.concept_primer: Picture a driving instructor fixing a student's habits. One practice course drills tricky maneuvers chosen in advance; another watches the student's own bad turns and drills those exact spots. Here both courses produced flawless drivers, so the personalized one added nothing extra.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_question: When teaching a small model to repair broken programs, does building practice around its own real mistakes beat simply hand-picking the hard categories in advance?
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.plain_answer: No. Building practice cases from the model's actual wrong answers matched, but never beat, simply hand-picking the tricky categories in advance. Both lifted the hardest problems from 64% to a perfect 100% passing every hidden test, and the hand-picked set was even a touch cleaner on a single guess (100% versus 99%). The extra error-hunting loop earned nothing here.
qwen35_4b_model_in_loop_counterexamples.why_it_matters: Before investing in a loop that mines a model's own failures, first curate targeted hard examples and pair them with a checker that actually runs the output. On a narrow task that simpler recipe can fully solve the benchmark, making the fancier loop wasted effort.

qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.verdict_tag: The model never stopped thinking to answer
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.concept_primer: The plan: pause a small model partway through its own written scratch-work, read a hidden gauge of whether it is headed for the right answer, then edit just that gauge to rescue a failing attempt. Like reading a runner's pace mid-race and nudging them — but only if they cross the finish line.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.plain_question: Can we peek at a hidden "am I on track" signal partway through a small model's reasoning, then edit it to steer the final answer?
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.plain_answer: We could not even reach the test. The whole plan needs the model to finish reasoning and write an answer worth grading. But on all 48 attempts at simple two-step list puzzles, it rambled straight into its 160-token thinking limit and never concluded. Zero natural finishes. That is a setup-and-budget wall, not proof the hidden gauge is missing.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_jacobian_value_.why_it_matters: Before building any probe that reads or edits a small model's mid-reasoning confidence, first measure how often it actually finishes thinking and answers within your token budget. If it never stops on its own, nothing downstream is measurable.

key_numbers.label: Times the model finished thinking on its own
key_numbers.sub: no attempt ever closed its reasoning to give an answer

key_numbers.label: Attempts that hit the thinking limit
key_numbers.sub: every trace stopped at the fixed 160-token cap

key_numbers.label: Finish rate needed to proceed, versus seen
key_numbers.sub: the gate that blocked every later stage of the plan

key_numbers.label: Forward passes to establish this

charts.chart_plain_title: How every reasoning attempt ended
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of all 48 reasoning attempts that closed thinking naturally, gave a readable answer, gave a correct answer, or hit the token cap. Higher is better for the first three; the cap bar should be low.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars sit flat at zero while the cap bar fills the full height — every attempt exhausted its thinking budget before finishing, so no signal could be measured.

qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.verdict_tag: It never stops thinking to give an answer
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.concept_primer: This 4-billion-parameter model reasons on a private scratchpad, then is supposed to write an end-of-thinking mark and its answer. It behaved like a worker told to show all work who keeps filling pages rechecking, and never writes the final line.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.plain_question: If you let a small reasoning model think freely on simple list puzzles and wait for it to finish and hand you an answer, will it ever stop?
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.plain_answer: No. Across 48 tries on simple list-transformation puzzles, and at every thinking budget up to 1,024 tokens, the model closed its reasoning and produced an answer exactly zero times. It always burned the whole budget still analyzing. To be usable it needed to stop on its own at least 80 percent of the time, and it never once did. This was not a stuck repeating loop; it kept genuinely reworking the problem, just never committing.
qwen35_4b_native_thought_seam_budget_lad.why_it_matters: Do not trust a small thinking model to end its own reasoning. If your pipeline waits for a natural stop, you may get nothing back. Cap the thinking, force a commit at the cap, and measure your real self-stop rate before relying on it.

key_numbers.label: Self-stop rate it had to clear
key_numbers.sub: minimum voluntary-stop rate to be usable

key_numbers.sub: also tested at 256 and 512; all three failed identically

charts.chart_plain_title: Reasoning never closed at any budget; every trace hit the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three thinking-budget caps (256, 512, 1,024 tokens) along the bottom; height is the share of 48 traces. One color is the natural-close rate (higher is better), the other is the share that ran into the ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every natural-close bar sits flat at zero while every ceiling bar is full at 100 percent, so the model exhausted its room and never once stopped to answer.

qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.verdict_tag: Fixing its answers failed; retraining on them worked
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.concept_primer: Each puzzle hides a rule that reshapes one list of numbers into another. Shown a few examples, the model writes Python to reproduce it, then runs the code — an honest calculator, not a bigger brain — to see its real output beside the right one and revise.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_question: If a small model can run its own code and see exactly where it went wrong, can fixing mistakes that way beat just trying again?
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.plain_answer: No. Seeing its real error barely helped: the fix-it loop solved 29% of puzzles versus 34% for simply drawing five independent attempts and keeping the best, at equal compute; the error message itself added just two tasks over a control told only to try again. The model's test-time ceiling is whatever its own sampling can reach. Retraining on its verified solutions, though, lifted single-shot accuracy from 22% to 32%.
qwen35_4b_neurosymbolic_repl_substrate.why_it_matters: For a small model, don't spend test-time compute on run-and-fix correction loops; they rarely beat simply sampling more against a checker. Instead fine-tune it on answers it can already verify — that permanently banks the gain into single-shot deployment.

key_numbers.label: What the real error signal added
key_numbers.sub: vs a control told only to try again

key_numbers.label: Retraining on its own wins

charts.chart_plain_title: Run-and-fix loop versus just sampling more, at equal compute

charts.chart_takeaway: Sampling five and keeping the best (34%) is tallest; the fix-it loop (29%) barely tops one attempt and the retry control, which both sit at 26%, so reading the real error buys almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the fix-it loop lands on the pure-sampling curve
charts.chart_read: The rising line plots hidden-puzzle accuracy as independent attempts per task grow from one to five with the best kept; two dots mark the fix-it loop and the retry control at about four attempts each. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both loop dots sit on or below the sampling line at the same attempt budget, so those generations are better spent on fresh independent attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on its own answers: before and after, fresh tasks
charts.chart_read: Two measures on fresh unseen tasks — single best attempt and best-of-five coverage — each pairing the frozen model against the self-trained one. Higher is better; rising coverage means answer variety was kept.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-training lifts single-shot from 22% to 32% and coverage from 31% to 37% — both rise together, so accuracy is banked without collapsing onto one repeated answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repeating self-training: gains compound, then flatten
charts.chart_read: Three lines run across training rounds, round zero being the untrained model: single best attempt, best-of-five coverage, and accuracy on the hardest deepest puzzles. Higher is better; distance above round zero is accumulated gain.
charts.chart_takeaway: The top two lines climb but flatten by round three, while the hardest puzzles stay near 9% — the loop widens reach but never cracks the frontier.

qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.verdict_tag: Gentle nudge saves compute, hard shove breaks it
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.concept_primer: A small coding model writes several attempts per task. Some pass the one visible example test but secretly fail the hidden checks — convincing traps. This training mines pairs of a truly-correct solution versus such a trap and teaches the model to prefer the real one.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_question: Can a light preference-training pass teach a small coding model to solve more tasks without having to generate so many candidate answers?
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.plain_answer: It depends — and even the gentle version failed its own bar. A light touch matched the task-solving reach of generating twice as many candidate answers at roughly half the compute, and beat a shuffled-pair placebo. But it cut first-try correctness from 50% to 37.5%. Pushed harder, the model collapsed and stopped producing runnable code at all.
qwen35_4b_offline_hard_negative_coverage.why_it_matters: If you preference-tune a small model for broader coverage, watch first-answer quality and whether the output still runs. This objective quietly reshuffles probability mass — buying breadth by sacrificing the accuracy of any single generation.

charts.chart_takeaway: The gentle recipe's solved-bar edges above the untrained model while its first-answer bar sits lower, and the two aggressive recipes flatline at zero — coverage bought by trading first-try quality.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two aggressively trained bars collapse to zero runnable answers, while every light-touch recipe holds between three and four — overtraining destroys usable output.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.verdict_tag: Right answer always present, still hard to pick
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.concept_primer: Picture a recipe with two blanks, each filled from a spice rack of 512 interchangeable spices. That is a quarter-million possible pairings, and many taste identical on the handful of dishes you sampled, so the samples alone cannot reveal which pairing is truly the right one.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_question: When you build little programs by filling blanks from a growing toolbox of number-crunching functions, does scaling to hundreds of tools break your ability to find the right program?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.plain_answer: It depends on what you mean by break. The correct program always stays inside the pile you search, even with two blanks and 512 tools. But picking it out from a few examples alone drops to under half, versus always solved with a single blank, and the search balloons to over a quarter-million combinations per problem. The wall is ambiguity and compute cost, not reachability.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_scaling_str.why_it_matters: Do not brute-force two-blank program search over a large toolbox: cost grows with the square of the tool count. Train a small model to shortlist a few likely tools, then ask a couple of targeted examples to break the remaining ties.

key_numbers.sub: still true even with two blanks and 512 tools

charts.chart_takeaway: Single-blank programs stay near always-solved throughout, while two-blank programs sag toward half as the toolbox grows and identical-looking combinations pile up.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is toolbox size. The vertical axis is how often the correct two-blank program still falls inside a capped shortlist of combinations you agree to check; higher is better. Each line is a different shortlist size.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every capped shortlist catches the answer for small toolboxes, then collapses toward zero once the toolbox reaches the dozens, so a fixed shortlist stops working.

charts.chart_plain_title: Asking for a few extra examples to recover the right pick
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many extra targeted examples you request. The vertical axis is how often you then pick the exact right two-blank program at the largest toolbox; higher is better. One line is a practical question-picking rule, one is the best-possible questions.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb sharply from under half with no extra examples to the seventies or eighties after three, so a few well-chosen clues recover most of the lost accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: One two-blank program shape is far harder to pin down
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often examples pick the exact right two-blank program, at two toolbox sizes, for two program shapes: a scale-and-remainder arithmetic shape versus a compare-two-things-and-choose shape. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The compare-and-choose shape sits near zero to a quarter while the arithmetic shape stays two-thirds or better, because comparing reveals too little to separate the look-alikes.

qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.verdict_tag: Search finds any operation already in its toolbox
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.concept_primer: Several math operations — add up, keep the largest, keep the first — all take a list of numbers and return one number, so they look identical from outside, like kitchen gadgets that each take a bowl and hand back one item. Only running each on the same inputs reveals which one matches.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_question: When several math operations all turn a list of numbers into one number and look identical from the outside, can a program figure out which one was actually used?
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.plain_answer: Yes, but only if that operation sits on the program's list of candidates. Given the full set of seven operations, the program recovered the correct hidden one every time — matching against examples alone already solved about nine in ten cases, and a couple of extra probing inputs closed the rest to perfect. A shorter list that left the operation out recovered zero. Search was never the bottleneck; stocking the candidate was.
qwen35_4b_operator_inventory_search_pilo.why_it_matters: When a small model must identify which operation produced some outputs, spend effort widening the roster of candidate operations and adding a couple of probing test inputs — not on training the model to recover operations that plain example-matching already handles.

charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit flat at zero for the short list but leap to full and near-full height for the complete list — the missing operations become recoverable only once they are included.

charts.chart_read: Lines track how often the right operation gets picked as the number of extra probing test inputs rises from none up to three. Two lines use the full seven-operation list; a flat bottom line uses the short list. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-list lines climb from about nine in ten to a perfect score within one or two inputs, while the short-list line never leaves zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each operation gets picked correctly
charts.chart_read: One bar per operation shows how often the search picked it correctly using the full seven-operation list, covering both newly added operations and ones already stocked. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most bars reach near the top; greatest-common-divisor and first-element sit lowest because their outputs most often match another operation on the examples.

qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.verdict_tag: Helpful hints teach copying, not correctness
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.concept_primer: Picture an open-book coding exam where the same reference page is handed to the grader checking each keystroke. The page lifts the grader's confidence only where the code copies its exact words, and stays silent at the one keystroke that separates a right answer from a plausible wrong one.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_question: Before training a small model to copy its own best code, does handing the grader a helpful reference sharpen its judgment right where correct and near-miss wrong code split apart?
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.plain_answer: No. At the exact spots where correct code diverges from code that passes surface tests but is secretly wrong, the reference hint adds essentially nothing — scoring no better than a scrambled, meaningless hint. The hint only boosts the grader where the code echoes its own wording, or where it leaks the full answer outright. Neither teaches real correctness, so the planned training run was cancelled before it burned any compute.
qwen35_4b_opsd_pressure_locality_audit.why_it_matters: Before spending compute to train a model on its own best answers, verify the grader's edge lands on the real right-versus-wrong decisions, not surface word overlap. This cheap check, run before training, catches a hint that would teach mimicry.

Left to right: the real reference hint at decision points, a scrambled fake hint, an answer-leaking hint, and forks where the hint's words appeared in the code.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-hint bar sits at zero, level with the scrambled fake; only the word-overlap bar towers up, so the hint helps by matching surface text, not by teaching correctness.

charts.chart_read: Bars show how strongly each grader leans toward the correct branch over the wrong one at the decision points; taller means a more confident preference. Left to right: no hint at all, the real reference hint, a scrambled hint, and an answer-leaking hint.
charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars stand at nearly the same height, meaning the grader already favored the correct branch on its own, so the reference hint changes almost nothing.

charts.chart_read: Bars show how much extra weight the hinted grader adds to individual pieces of code; taller means more upweighting. Left to right: the real hint on correct pieces, the real hint on wrong pieces, a scrambled hint on correct pieces, and an answer-leaking hint on correct pieces.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-hint-on-correct-pieces bar is tallest, hinting at broad help, but that optimistic view collapses at the exact forks that actually separate right code from wrong.

qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.verdict_tag: Training a code judge helps at home
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.concept_primer: Several draft programs all pass the one test you can run, but the real grading tests stay hidden. A small model plays judge, betting which draft secretly passes. It learns by studying past programs already labeled by whether they cleared those hidden tests.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_question: When several candidate programs all pass the one test you can see, can a small model learn to pick the one that secretly passes the hidden tests?
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.plain_answer: Yes, but only on home turf. On the problem set it trained on, the trained judge picked a genuinely-correct program 81% of the time, up from 72% for the same model untrained and just 44% for grabbing the first candidate that passes the visible test. On a fresh, unfamiliar problem set, though, the untrained model judged better, 94% versus 90% — training sharpened its home instinct while dulling its general one.
qwen35_4b_oracle_distilled_semantic_veri.why_it_matters: If you fine-tune a small model to judge which code is correct, expect gains only on problems resembling your training set. An off-the-shelf model may already be the stronger judge elsewhere, so train on broad, varied code.

charts.chart_takeaway: On the familiar set barely half the visible-passing programs survive the hidden tests, versus about three-quarters on the fresh set, so choosing well matters more there.

qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.verdict_tag: The menu of questions beat more training
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.concept_primer: The model plays detective: a rule is hidden, so it runs a few test inputs and watches the outputs, each result ruling out suspects like narrowing options in Guess Who. The catch — researchers curated which test inputs it was even allowed to try.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_question: When a small model uncovers a hidden rule by running test inputs, does it help more to train it harder or to hand it better tests to choose from?
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.plain_answer: It's the menu. Just curating which eight test inputs the model chose from raised success from about 43% to 49% — a bigger jump than any training gave. Supervised coaching added a bit more (48% to 51%); preference- and reward-based training added nothing. A flawless picker allowed all 96 candidate tests hit 87%, so the bottleneck is generating good questions, not picking among them.
qwen35_4b_oracle_probe_synthesis_mdp.why_it_matters: When a small model gathers information by probing its environment, invest first in the quality of the moves you offer it, not in reward-based training. A better menu of test inputs — and ultimately generating them — beats coaching the picker.
