charts.chart_takeaway: On the hard rule type trained models stay near one in twenty, yet trying every question reaches nearly three-quarters — the needed questions exist but current methods rarely find them.

qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.verdict_tag: Simple copying nearly matched every fancier method
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.concept_primer: Picture "Guess Who?", but the hidden faces are secret math rules. The model never guesses the rule itself. It only picks which of eight test-inputs to try; a search engine runs that test and crosses off every rule that disagrees, for up to three rounds.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_question: Can a small model get better at solving puzzles by learning which diagnostic questions to ask, instead of trying to guess the answer outright?
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.plain_answer: Yes, but with sharp limits. Teaching the model to copy a flawless test-picker lifted its success from about a third of puzzles to 43%, versus roughly 31% for picking blindly. Layering on preference-based and reward-based training gained almost nothing more. And where the available tests simply can't tell two rules apart, even perfect choices topped out near 9%.
qwen35_4b_oracle_process_grpo.why_it_matters: For small-model agents that pick tools or diagnostic steps, plainly imitating good decisions can capture nearly all the gain; heavier reward-based training may add little. But no training beats an information ceiling: fix which tests are available first.

qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.verdict_tag: Longer thinking makes reasoning order matter more
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.concept_primer: Picture the model writing itself a recipe, then cooking from it. Blank filler pages help nothing; a different dish's recipe ruins the meal; and the same steps in scrambled order spoil it too. The longer the recipe, the more that scrambling hurts.
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_question: When you give a small model more space to reason before it answers, does that extra room dilute the value of thinking in a coherent order?
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.plain_answer: No, the opposite. Giving this small coding model more room to reason made genuine, ordered reasoning matter more, not less. Scrambling its own reasoning steps into random order cost about 10 points of solved tasks at the short budget but 15 points at the longest. Blank filler of the same length bought essentially nothing over not thinking at all, so raw extra compute was not the driver.
qwen35_4b_overthinking_content_ladder.why_it_matters: Don't buy accuracy by padding prompts or counting thinking tokens; empty length adds nothing. A longer thinking budget pays off only when it holds genuine coherent reasoning. Measure that by scrambling the steps, not by counting them.

key_numbers.label: Value of coherent order, short vs long budget
key_numbers.sub: extra tasks solved by real reasoning over the same reasoning scrambled, at the short vs the long budget

key_numbers.label: Blank filler vs no thinking at all
key_numbers.sub: same-length filler adds essentially no accuracy

key_numbers.label: Fed a different task's reasoning
key_numbers.sub: vs 84-87% with the task's own real reasoning; the model follows the wrong problem

key_numbers.label: Real-reasoning accuracy across all budgets
key_numbers.sub: roughly flat, no overthinking penalty

charts.chart_plain_title: Value of coherent reasoning order grows with the thinking budget
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is thinking budget in tokens (512, 1024, 2048). The vertical axis is the accuracy gap between real ordered reasoning and the exact same reasoning shuffled into random order. Higher means coherent order matters more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single line climbs from about 11 to 15 points, so a longer scrambled chain hurts more; extra thinking room strengthens rather than dilutes coherent reasoning.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each kind of thinking scores across three budgets
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is thinking budget in tokens; the vertical axis is the share of coding tasks solved. Five lines: real thinking on top, then shuffled tokens, filler dots and no thinking bunched together, and a foreign task's thinking near the floor. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real thinking sits alone near 85%, while filler, shuffle and no-thinking cluster around 72-76% and foreign reasoning craters near 3% -- only coherent, relevant reasoning lifts accuracy.

qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.verdict_tag: The specialist built for speed lost on speed
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.concept_primer: To fold two specialist models into one, each coaches the tasks it owns — a sprint coach and a marathon coach training a single runner. That only pays off if each is genuinely fastest in its own lane. So first you check.
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.plain_question: Two versions of the same small model each looked best at either quick or long, involved tasks — can you fuse them into one model good at both?
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.plain_answer: No. Retested on fresh, uncontaminated tasks, the version built for quick work actually lost at quick work by about two points, while the version built for long work won at both quick AND long tasks. One version quietly dominated everywhere, so there was no genuine trade to fuse — the whole merge was cancelled before any training ran.
qwen35_4b_pareto_policy_integration.why_it_matters: Before spending compute to merge or distill two specialists, prove on your own tasks that each is genuinely the stronger coach on the exact slice it will teach. A specialist label earned on another benchmark can silently invert.

key_numbers.label: Quick version's margin on quick tasks
key_numbers.sub: wrong direction — it lost in both test rounds

key_numbers.label: Long version's margin on long tasks
key_numbers.sub: the only specialty label that held up

key_numbers.label: Worst skill the quick version broke
key_numbers.sub: on a task type it was never trained on

key_numbers.label: Skills the long version quietly lost
key_numbers.sub: retention checks it slipped past, worst about -9%

charts.chart_plain_title: Each specialist versus its rival on its own tasks, by test round
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis lists four test rounds: two quick-task rounds, then two long-task rounds. The vertical axis is how much the version assigned to that task type beat the other version; zero is a tie, and above zero is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both quick-task bars dip below zero while both long-task bars rise above it: the quick version lost its own specialty in both rounds, the long version won its own in both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Pooled score and its cautious floor for each specialist
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis shows the two roles, quick version and long version. For each, one bar is its overall margin over the rival and a second is a cautious worst-case estimate of that margin; both bars above zero means a solid pass.
charts.chart_takeaway: The long version's two bars both sit above zero; the quick version's both fall below, so even its best-case estimate is negative and only one specialist qualified.

qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.verdict_tag: Extra reasoning made half-finished-program judging worse, not better
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.concept_primer: Picture a recipe that names only techniques in order — chop, sear, fold — with no ingredients and no amounts. Now judge whether some choice of missing ingredients could still turn it into the target dish. The model saw only those bare technique names.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_question: If you show a small AI only the skeleton of a half-finished program — the steps in order but none of the details — can it tell which skeletons can still be completed?
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.plain_answer: No. Shown a half-finished program skeleton, the four-billion-parameter model's guess at whether it could still be completed was barely above a coin flip — about 51% correct, where 50% is pure chance — and letting it reason step-by-step scored lower than a snap one-word yes/no. Yet an exact search reading the same skeletons pruned the work 262,144-fold, so the signal was there; the model just could not extract it.
qwen35_4b_partial_structure_search.why_it_matters: Before trusting a small model to prune a search, confirm its scores rank options within a single problem — a promising pooled score of 56% here hid within-problem chance. And check whether plain brute force is already fast enough to skip guidance entirely.

key_numbers.label: How well step-by-step reasoning ranked which skeletons could finish
key_numbers.sub: within-problem ranking accuracy near 51%, where 50% is pure chance

key_numbers.label: Reasoning versus a snap one-word answer
key_numbers.sub: within a problem, reasoning ranked skeletons worse than no reasoning at all

key_numbers.label: Search shrinkage an exact, non-model judge reached
key_numbers.sub: fewer completed skeletons per depth-5 task, keeping a solving path on all 12 test tasks

key_numbers.label: Plain brute-force search on 60 hard tasks
key_numbers.sub: 111.85 seconds on eight processor cores; found a solver for all 60, picked correctly for 56

charts.chart_plain_title: Every scoring method landed near a coin flip, and reasoning trailed the snap answer
charts.chart_read: Five scoring methods across the bottom: step-by-step reasoning, a snap no-reasoning answer, next operation likelihood, a surface-feature guess, and random. Two bars each — ranking accuracy (50% is chance) and how often the top four kept a completable skeleton. Higher is better on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: The five ranking-accuracy bars cluster near the 50% chance line, while the retention bars sit lower around a quarter; step-by-step reasoning sits at chance on ranking and below the snap answer on both measures — the reasoning added nothing and even hurt.

qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.verdict_tag: Cranking up randomness beat retraining for coverage
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.concept_primer: To cover more coding problems, let the model take several tries at each and count a win if any one passes — like buying several lottery tickets. More variety across tries covers more. You can retrain it to diversify, or just turn up its randomness dial.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_question: If you want a model to solve more coding problems across a handful of tries, is it worth retraining it to diversify, or just cranking up randomness?
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.plain_answer: No. Retraining the model to spread its tries across different problems actually lowered coverage: it solved about 44% of unseen coding problems within four tries, versus about 69% from simply raising the randomness dial at the same number of tries. It even trailed the plain, untouched model at 50%, and its first-try success sank too. The training collected its reward but never converted it into wider coverage.
qwen35_4b_passk_coverage_rl.why_it_matters: Before paying for reinforcement-style fine-tuning to broaden what a small model can solve, exhaust the free knob first: raise sampling randomness. Here a simple randomness bump beat a full training run outright, at zero extra training cost.

key_numbers.label: Problems covered: retrained model vs. randomness dial
key_numbers.sub: share of unseen coding problems solved within four tries

key_numbers.label: Retrained model vs. plain untouched model
key_numbers.sub: coverage — the training made it worse, not better

key_numbers.label: First-try success of the retrained model
key_numbers.sub: quality dropped alongside coverage

key_numbers.label: Training rounds that gave any usable signal
key_numbers.sub: most rounds were all-fail or all-pass, teaching nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of unseen problems solved by at least one of several tries
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per way of drafting; bar height is the share of unseen problems where at least one try passes, so taller is better. The retrained model is the third bar; the other three are the untouched model at different randomness levels or try counts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained bar is shortest — below even the plain untouched model — while the untouched model at highest randomness stands tallest.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-try accuracy and how different the tries are
charts.chart_read: For each drafting method, one bar shows how often the first try is correct and another shows how varied the tries are from each other; higher is better on both. The retrained model is the third group of bars.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained model has the lowest first-try quality of any bar, and it made its tries more alike than its own base — the opposite of the goal (only the sample-more arm ends up less varied still).

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful learning signal across the training rounds
charts.chart_read: Each point is one training round; the line shows how many of eight tries passed. Rounds sitting at zero (all failed) or at eight (all passed) teach nothing; only in-between rounds give a usable signal.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most points sit flat at zero or eight, so only a handful of rounds gave any teaching signal — explaining why the training stalled.

qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.verdict_tag: A graded head start solves no extra problems
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.concept_primer: Instead of writing eight complete programs and hoping one passes, the model writes four half-finished openings, secretly grades each by running the hidden answer tests, then finishes only the top-graded opening. Does a well-chosen head start beat brute-force full attempts?
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_question: When a small model writes code, does grading half-finished attempts and finishing only the most promising one solve more problems than just writing full solutions?
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_answer: No. Even when the half-finished drafts were graded with perfect knowledge of the hidden answer tests, finishing only the best-graded one solved the same share of problems as plain full-solution writing — 75% either way. Ungraded random drafts did worse, at 58%. The lone upside: the graded winner used about a quarter of the compute, but only because a flawless grader hand-picked it.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.why_it_matters: Don't build machinery to score and rank half-written code by promise: the good drafts reach no solutions plain writing misses. Spend compute finishing full attempts, not grading fragments, unless you already own a flawless grader.

key_numbers.label: First-try success rate
key_numbers.sub: each single attempt looked better, yet total problems solved never moved

key_numbers.label: Compute for the graded winner
key_numbers.sub: of what full writing burned, but only a perfect grader could pick it

key_numbers.label: Ungraded random head start
key_numbers.sub: worse than plain writing's 75%

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved by each search method
charts.chart_read: Five methods along the bottom: plain full writing and four graded-draft variants. Darker bars show the share of twelve problems solved at all; lighter bars the first-pick success rate. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every draft variant's solved share tops out level with plain writing and none rises above it, so the graded drafts add no new solutions even as first-pick success climbs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute used by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the total compute it burned, so shorter is cheaper. Plain full writing sits on the left, the four graded-draft variants follow.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best graded-draft bar is about four times shorter than plain writing, so it is cheaper, but only because the hidden answers were already known when picking that draft.

charts.chart_plain_title: How varied the generated programs are
charts.chart_read: Each method has two bars: how differently the programs behave when run, and how differently they are written. Taller means more varied. Neither direction is inherently better here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Graded-draft methods behave far more variously, yet that extra behavioral variety never converted into more problems solved.

qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.verdict_tag: The model computes the operation, not the number
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.concept_primer: A mechanic hears an engine and instantly diagnoses "needs an oil change" — real expertise, held in the head. But the exact oil grade? They read it off the cap. This model transforms lists of numbers, diagnosing each step's KIND internally yet reading its NUMBER off the examples.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_question: When a small model transforms a list of numbers step by step, does it hold each step's exact settings inside its weights, or just read them off the examples?
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_answer: It splits. The model genuinely computes the KIND of operation inside itself — reading its internal activity names the kind far better than the examples alone do (41% versus 27%, against 6% for blind guessing). But the specific number is not stored anywhere special: a trivial calculator run over the examples recovers it just as well (49% versus 53%). The model reads it off, same as anyone could.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.why_it_matters: Before mining a model's internals for hidden knowledge, test whether the fact is simply readable off the input-output examples. Derive those parts cheaply outside the model — faster and more accurate — and probe internals only for what it truly computes.

key_numbers.label: Reading the operation's KIND — the model's mind vs the raw examples
key_numbers.sub: the model's internal activity clearly wins; blind guessing is about 6%

key_numbers.label: Reading the operation's NUMBER — the model's mind vs the raw examples
key_numbers.sub: the no-model calculator ties or wins; blind guessing is about 30%

key_numbers.label: Solve rate when handed the full correct operation and number
key_numbers.sub: vs under 1% when told only the kind — the number is the real bottleneck

key_numbers.label: Delivering the number: cheap no-model pipeline vs reading the model
key_numbers.sub: you do not need the 4-billion-parameter model for the number

charts.chart_plain_title: Reading the operation's kind needs the model; the number does not
charts.chart_read: Three decode targets run along the bottom: the operation's kind, the full operation, and just its number. Bar height is decode accuracy — taller is better. One color reads the model's internal activity, the other a no-model classifier on the raw examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the kind, the model's bar towers over the examples bar; for the number, the examples bar matches or beats it — the kind is computed inside, the number just read off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Handing over the full operation unlocks solves; the model's guess barely helps
charts.chart_read: Six hint conditions run along the bottom: no hint, told only the kind, told the full operation, the model-decoded operation, the examples-decoded operation, and told a wrong number. Bar height is solve rate — one color a single best attempt, the other solved within six tries. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full operation spikes; kind-only and wrong-number sit near zero — and the cheap examples-based hint outdoes the model's own decoded guess.

qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.verdict_tag: Hint the exact step, not just its type
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.concept_primer: Picture reconstructing a lost recipe. The cook's instinct reveals the first step needs something sour — the category — but not the exact amount, two spoons of lime — the value. Given the category, they land it within a few tries; given the value, first try.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_question: A small model's hidden state already seems to know a task's right first step. Can you read it out and feed it into the prompt to solve harder tasks?
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_answer: Partly. Writing the concrete first step — exact value and all — into the prompt lifted the single-best-guess solve rate on two-step tasks sixfold, from 3% to 19%, where editing the model's internal state did nothing. But its internals reliably reveal only the step's category, and category alone merely helps it stumble onto answers across several tries — not solve first-shot. The exact value is the real unlock.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.why_it_matters: When a small model seems to internally know something, don't extract it by editing its internal state — decode the fact into the prompt instead. And decode the concrete value, not just the category; category-only hints merely widen the search without fixing the first answer.

key_numbers.label: Single best guess, two-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: with the exact first step — type and value — written into the prompt

key_numbers.label: Solved within six tries, two-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: same exact-step hint, allowing several attempts

key_numbers.label: Category-only hint, single best guess
key_numbers.sub: naming the step's type but not its value doesn't help the first try

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks, even with the exact hint
key_numbers.sub: no hint conjures a step the model never computed

charts.chart_plain_title: Which prompt hint actually lifts two-step solving
charts.chart_read: Six prompt conditions run along the bottom, from no hint through category-only, the exact step, the model's own decoded guess, to a deliberately wrong hint. One color is single-best-guess solves, the other solved-within-six-tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the exact-step hint (type plus value) raises single-best-guess solving; the category-only hint lifts just the six-try bar, and the wrong hint flattens both to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: The exact-step hint helps two-step tasks but not three-step
charts.chart_read: Five prompt conditions along the bottom; height is the single-best-guess solve rate, taller is better. One color is two-step tasks, the other three-step, showing how far each hint carries by task length.
charts.chart_takeaway: The exact-step hint lifts two-step tasks to 19% but leaves three-step tasks flat near zero — it can cash in latent knowledge but cannot create a step the model never computed.

qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.verdict_tag: The weak link is writing code
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.concept_primer: Think of an editor choosing the best essay from a stack of drafts a writer produced. On easy prompts a good draft always exists, so any editor succeeds. On hard prompts, if not one draft is actually correct, even a flawless editor cannot pick a winner.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_question: When a small coding model writes several attempts at a problem and a checker picks one to submit, is the weak link the picking or the writing?
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_answer: It's the writing. When a 4-billion-parameter model writes several code attempts per problem, choosing is not the bottleneck. On easier problems nearly every attempt is already correct, so the best result sits near 97%. On harder problems at least one attempt is correct only 60% of the time, and the trained checker captures that entire 60% — grabbing the first attempt that passes the visible examples reaches only 55%. No checker can invent programs that were never written.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.why_it_matters: Building a write-several-then-pick loop for a small model? Spend effort generating more and better attempts, not on a smarter checker. The checker already captures nearly every correct program that exists in the pool.

key_numbers.label: Chance a correct attempt exists, harder problems
key_numbers.sub: as you go from one attempt to many per problem, then it plateaus

key_numbers.label: Trained checker vs grabbing the first that passes visible tests, harder problems
key_numbers.sub: the checker keeps every correct attempt; the quick habit leaves some behind

key_numbers.label: Easier problems solved
key_numbers.sub: nearly every single attempt was already correct

key_numbers.label: Attempts needed on easier problems
key_numbers.sub: the loop stops early once an attempt passes the visible tests

charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, one per problem set. Within each: the ceiling of problems where a correct attempt existed, the result of grabbing the first attempt that passed the visible example tests, and the trained checker's choice. Taller means more problems solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained checker reaches the same height as the ceiling on both sets, losing nothing; the harder set's ceiling itself is simply lower.

charts.chart_plain_title: Chance a correct attempt exists as you write more attempts per problem
charts.chart_read: Two lines, the harder set and the easier set. The horizontal axis is how many attempts you write per problem; the vertical axis is the share of problems where at least one attempt is correct. Higher and rising is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The easier line starts near the top and barely moves; the harder line climbs from a third of problems to about six in ten, then flattens well below it.

charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, harder set and easier set. Bars show the average number of attempts written per problem: always writing the maximum, an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule, and a learned stopping rule. Fewer attempts is cheaper and better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned rule writes far fewer attempts than always writing the maximum, especially on the easier set — the same results for a fraction of the work.

charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one picking rule, from the best-possible pick down to simple habits like taking the first attempt that passed the visible tests or the answer most attempts agree on. Bar height is share of problems solved; taller is better. Shown for both sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every bar sits at the same height within each set — smarter picking rules do not beat simple ones; only a couple dip slightly on the harder set.

charts.chart_read: Three stopping rules side by side per set: an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule and two practical learned rules. Bar height is the share of problems still solved after stopping early; taller means less accuracy given up. Shown for both sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the easier set all three match; on the harder set the practical rules sit slightly below the ideal one — a small accuracy cost for stopping early.

charts.chart_read: Two lines, harder and easier set. The horizontal axis is the number of attempts allowed per problem; the vertical axis is how many were actually written before an attempt passed the visible tests and writing stopped. Lower means it stopped sooner.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines bend below the maximum at higher limits — writing stops early once an attempt passes the visible tests, and the easier set stops sooner than the harder one.

qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.verdict_tag: Recovery gain did not beat its incumbent
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.concept_primer: Two coding policies can resemble two mechanics who fix different failures. A fair test must show that one consistently outperforms the other, not merely that their strengths differ.
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.plain_question: Does giving tool commands enough room turn the reason-enhanced recovery model into a consistently stronger coding agent?
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.plain_answer: No. It led on the first unseen task block at 71%, but confirmed at 69%, exactly tied with the action-only model and short of the required lead. The external benchmark stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.why_it_matters: The larger command slot fixed a real interface problem, but changing one global model cannot combine both policies' strengths. The next test should fork both and let visible test evidence choose.

key_numbers.label: Development recovery
key_numbers.sub: reason-enhanced model passed every early gate

key_numbers.label: Confirmation recovery
key_numbers.sub: reason-enhanced and action-only models tied

key_numbers.label: Combined ceiling
key_numbers.sub: either local policy succeeded on both fresh blocks

key_numbers.label: Benchmark exposure
key_numbers.sub: stopped before external tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: The early lead disappears on confirmation
charts.chart_read: Each model or control is grouped along the horizontal axis, with recovery success vertically; taller is better. The two colors compare development with independent confirmation.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reason-enhanced bar leads during development but exactly matches action-only on confirmation, so it fails the required incumbent win.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal groups are the two unseen blocks, and bar height is recovery success. The third bar counts cases solved by either policy and is only a diagnostic ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The combined bar repeats at 79% on both blocks, showing stable complementary strengths that a visible test selector might exploit.

charts.chart_takeaway: The reason-enhanced bars stay near 8% while base stays near 30%, confirming that command truncation was a real interface bottleneck.

qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.verdict_tag: Safe blend beat both parents, gates stopped it
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.concept_primer: Two fine-tuned versions share one starting checkpoint: one only learned fix-it actions, the other also learned to explain its plan first. Rather than retrain, you average their learned weight changes on a dial and search for the mix that keeps the skill and drops the mistakes.
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.plain_question: If you average two versions of a model, one that just acts and one that also explains, can you dial in a mix that fixes its mistakes without breaking unrelated behavior?
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.plain_answer: Yes on skill, no on shipping. One dial setting recovered from broken code 97% of the time, about 12 points above the act-only version and 15 above a matched-training baseline, while barely moving unrelated behavior. But it tripped two automatic release checks, so the test halted before the transfer stage. On inspection, both checks were mis-specified, not the model.
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.why_it_matters: When two fine-tunes each get part of a job right, averaging their weights can capture both without retraining. But write release gates around the real goal, not a proxy like doing it in one step, or genuinely good models get blocked.

key_numbers.sub: the safe blend used about seven-tenths of the allowed drift; the explain-only extreme hit twice the cap

qwen35_4b_recovery_verifier_branch_tourn.concept_primer: Two mechanics only form a useful team when each can fix failures the other misses. If both get stuck on the same repair, choosing between them cannot create the missing solution.
qwen35_4b_recovery_verifier_branch_tourn.plain_question: Can visible tests combine two coding policies better than spending the same model budget on more attempts from either one?
qwen35_4b_recovery_verifier_branch_tourn.plain_answer: No. On four new problem types, both policies repaired 74%, and their combined best reached only 75%—exactly tied with two action-only attempts. The selector was correctly stopped before scoring.
qwen35_4b_recovery_verifier_branch_tourn.why_it_matters: The remaining gap needs a new kind of solution, not a better chooser. Training should target the shared validate-copy-commit pattern while replaying the reliable recovery behavior already learned.

key_numbers.sub: all required atomic resource reservations

key_numbers.sub: stopped before external tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Combining policies only ties more attempts
charts.chart_read: Each horizontal category is a policy or equal-budget control, and taller bars mean more repaired repositories. The final mixed-policy bar should exceed both more-attempt bars to justify selection.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mixed bar stops at 75%, exactly level with action-only sampling and only one point above either deterministic policy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Atomic reservations defeat both policies
charts.chart_read: Problem families run horizontally and recovery success runs vertically, where taller is better. The two colors compare the reason-enhanced and action-only policies family by family.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both policies fall to zero on atomic reservations and nearly overlap everywhere else, leaving no useful complementary coverage.

qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.verdict_tag: Leans toward correct code yet still ships bugs
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.concept_primer: The model writes several candidate programs by adapting retrieved algorithms. Wherever a correct and a buggy version diverge, it already leans the right way almost every time — yet, like a chess player who sees the best move but plays a worse one, it rarely assembles a working program (a workable one exists for only about a third of tasks).
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_question: When a small coding model writes several programs that all pass the quick sample checks a user can see, can cheap tricks reliably pick the truly correct one?
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_answer: No. All three cheap fixes failed. Ranking programs by the model's own confidence picked working code less often than just taking the first one that passed the visible sample tests (6 of 24 versus 8), and let more bugs slip through. Showing it one real failing test helped no more than scrambled nonsense — yet leaking the full answer clearly helped, so the test itself could see real signal.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.why_it_matters: Don't trust a small model's confidence, steadier decoding, or a single failing example to screen out buggy code — it already knows the right branch and still fails. Gather independent evidence: run several distinct solutions and commit only when they agree.

key_numbers.label: How often it already leaned toward the correct code
key_numbers.sub: measured at the exact points where a correct and a buggy program diverge

key_numbers.label: Working programs picked by trusting the model's confidence
key_numbers.sub: worse than the 8 of 24 from simply taking the first program that passed the visible tests

key_numbers.label: Buggy code that slipped through when trusting confidence
key_numbers.sub: share of committed programs that secretly failed hidden tests, up from taking the first passing one

key_numbers.label: Help from a real failing test versus scrambled nonsense
key_numbers.sub: a real failing test moved the model toward correct code no more than meaningless shuffled evidence

charts.chart_plain_title: Does a single failing test point the model at the correct code?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a type of hint fed to the model; height is how much more strongly it then leaned toward the correct program. Bars include a scrambled-nonsense hint and an answer-leaking hint for comparison. Taller means a stronger, more useful nudge.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real failing-test bar sits just below the scrambled-nonsense bar and far below the answer-leak bar, so a real failing test carries no genuine task-specific information.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trusting the model's confidence picks worse programs
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method over 24 tasks: working programs picked (want it tall) and buggy programs picked (want it short). Methods are taking the first program that passes the visible tests, ranking by the model's confidence, and a perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: Confidence ranking has a shorter working bar and a taller buggy bar than simply taking the first passing program, so it makes selection worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Steadier, more predictable writing does not solve more tasks
charts.chart_read: Across three settings from most predictable to most varied: how often at least one workable program appeared and how often the model's writing was correct on the first try, both over 24 tasks. Higher is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: No setting wins: the most predictable one gets slightly more first-try correct answers but fewer workable candidates — a wash with no clear winner.

qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.verdict_tag: Training on clean wins erases recovery skill
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.concept_primer: Imagine training a mechanic only on repairs that went perfectly the first time: inspect, fix, test, done. They nail that tidy four-step routine but never watch a stuck bolt get worked loose — so when a fix is rejected, they just resubmit the same broken move.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_question: If you train a small coding agent only on its clean, fully successful bug-fix runs, does it get broadly better at fixing bugs across new codebases?
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_answer: No. It became flawless on the six codebase types it trained on, solving every task in exactly four clean steps. But on four never-seen codebase types its success collapsed from 68% to 35% — worse than just running the untrained model twice, which scored 53%. Stripped of every failed attempt, it never learned what to do when a fix gets rejected.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.why_it_matters: When distilling agent trajectories, keep the failures and recoveries, not just the shortest winning path. Balance error-conditioned moves — failed-test-then-revise — not just how often each action appears, and test recovery on unseen tasks before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Practiced codebase types, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: success on the six trained families it memorized

key_numbers.label: Unseen codebase types, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: success on four never-trained families (a 33-point drop)

key_numbers.label: Retries a fix after a failed test
key_numbers.sub: untrained model re-patched 24 of 26 times; trained model 0 of 48

key_numbers.label: Beaten by running the untrained model twice
key_numbers.sub: same-compute baseline outscored it by 18 points

charts.chart_plain_title: Training perfected practiced codebases but broke unseen ones
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show the share of coding tasks solved. The left pair is the six practiced codebase types, the right pair four unseen types. The lighter bar is before training, the darker bar after clean-wins training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The practiced pair climbs to a perfect 100%, but the unseen pair drops by a third — the gain was memorized cloning, not transferable coding skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every training example kept all four coding steps equally
charts.chart_read: Bars count target training rows per step — inspect, patch, verify, commit — in a 20-repository setup check. All four sit at exactly 20 rows, showing compression kept each step in equal amounts. This is a data check, not a model result.
charts.chart_takeaway: Perfectly level bars confirm the compression balanced how often each step appears — yet equal counts still failed, because they never say when to recover after a failure.

qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.verdict_tag: Retrieval finds the fix but can't pick it
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.concept_primer: A small coding model that just failed a problem flips through a cookbook of 364 already-solved recipes, grabs the closest match, and rewrites it to fit the new problem. Some failures aren't beyond it — they were just missing the right recipe to start from.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_question: When a coding model gets stuck, can pulling a similar solved problem from a library and adapting it fix the miss — and can safe checks trust the result?
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_answer: It splits. Pulling a similar solved problem from a 364-entry library and rewriting it recovered 8 of 24 stuck tasks — a third, double the 4 that a random-library control found. But the only safe check, the visible sample tests, passed roughly two-thirds of wrong answers, so the best deployable picker committed 7 right fixes and 7 wrong ones — a coin flip on trust.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.why_it_matters: For small-model coding, the hard part isn't generating a fix once you retrieve a good hint — it's proof. Thin sample tests and code-only re-rankers can't separate right from wrong; invest in counterexample tests and cross-candidate agreement before committing.

key_numbers.label: Problems the model solved on its own
key_numbers.sub: 56 of 80 tasks

key_numbers.label: Stuck tasks rescued by adapting library code
key_numbers.sub: double the 4 a random library found

key_numbers.label: Best coverage if every good rescue were picked
key_numbers.sub: only with flawless picking

key_numbers.label: Best safe picker's committed fixes
key_numbers.sub: a coin flip — checks can't tell good from bad

charts.chart_plain_title: Meaning-matched lookup rescues the most stuck tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one lookup strategy; bar height is the share of the 24 stuck tasks it rescued into the candidate pool, so taller is better. Meaning-matched lookup is tallest; plain copying is shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The meaning-matched bar reaches a third of stuck tasks, roughly double the random-lookup bar — matching by meaning, not surface words, is what recovers misses.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every safe picker commits at least as many wrong answers as right
charts.chart_read: For each picking method, paired bars show correct fixes committed versus wrong ones, out of 24 stuck tasks; fewer wrong is better. Only the last method, a cheat that peeks at the hidden answers, has no wrong bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: For every real method the wrong bar is at least as tall as the correct bar — first-visible ties 7-to-7 while the other three commit even more wrong than right; the safe checks can't separate right from wrong, and only the peeking baseline avoids mistakes.

qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.verdict_tag: Being best overall isn't being best right here
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.concept_primer: Picture a lead actor who keeps fumbling one exact scene. Two understudies each do several takes of that same scene. You swap one in only if it clearly beats both the actor and the rival, and only after a fresh, separate batch of takes confirms it.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_question: When a small model stalls partway through a problem, can you tell which of two specialist helper models would actually finish it better from that exact spot?
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_answer: Only when it replicates, and here one helper didn't. The test actually ran: the deep specialist's local edge held up on a fresh, separate batch, but the quick specialist looked strong on one batch of stuck points and then went slightly negative on a second, so the check refused to trust it and the whole plan was called off. The insight is the reframe: a leaderboard tells you which teacher wins on average, not which one helps at a specific stuck moment, and here the stronger teacher's overall edge barely clears the bar. So each teacher must prove itself where the student fails, on fresh samples, or it sits out.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.why_it_matters: When merging specialist models into one small model, don't pick your teacher by leaderboard rank. Score each candidate on the exact states your model fails, copy its behavior only where it verifiably wins on separate samples, and skip the rest.

key_numbers.label: The stronger teacher's edge on medium tasks
key_numbers.sub: its error bar straddles the +32-point line it is judged against, so even the overall ranking is shaky

key_numbers.label: Forward tries per teacher at each stuck point
key_numbers.sub: one set of tries selects the winner, a separate disjoint set grades it, so the pick never grades itself

key_numbers.label: How the student model is built
key_numbers.sub: a blend of 40% quick specialist and 60% deep specialist; the strongest single checkpoint that exists and the bar every teacher must beat

key_numbers.label: Installation sanity check on identical prompts
key_numbers.sub: on eight fixed prompts the blended student behaved differently from the quick teacher every time and the deep teacher nearly every time, ruling out a silent do-nothing merge

charts.chart_plain_title: How many trial continuations feed picking versus checking each teacher
charts.chart_read: Two equal-height bars. The left bar counts trial continuations used to pick a teacher; the right counts a separate untouched set used to verify its edge. Each holds 4,608 continuations across two problem blocks. Equal size is the point.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pool that picks a teacher and the pool that confirms it are exactly the same size and fully separate, so a teacher can never be chosen and graded by the same evidence.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each training round splits its lessons across sources
charts.chart_read: Three bars showing where each round's 80 lesson-units come from: 30 from quick-teacher states, 30 from deep-teacher states, and 20 from replaying the original blend. Taller bars carry more of the training; the two teachers are matched.
charts.chart_takeaway: Three-quarters of each round teaches new behavior split evenly between the two teachers, while a quarter re-anchors the original skills as a guard against forgetting.

qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.verdict_tag: Perfect style-picking wins; no simple rule finds it
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.concept_primer: Writing code with a small model is like fishing with different lures: each sampling style hooks problems the others miss. Casting more with one favorite lure catches more fish, but only knowing which lure to tie on per spot reaches them all.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_question: When a small model writes code, is it better to keep several different sampling styles and pick the right one per problem, or just sample more from one style?
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_answer: In theory yes, in practice no. A flawless chooser that knew which style would crack each problem solved 75% of coding tasks at the cheapest run's cost, while just doubling tries from one style reached 67% at twice the cost. But every chooser we could actually build tied or lost to simply sampling more. The prize is real; the picker isn't.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.why_it_matters: Don't pour effort into brute-force sampling or scaling one style for coverage. The unclaimed win is a cheap predictor of which sampling style will add a new solution for a problem, before you spend the tokens.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved by a perfect style-picker
key_numbers.sub: of 24 coding tasks, at the cheapest run's cost

key_numbers.label: Doubling tries from one style
key_numbers.sub: more problems solved, but at twice the tokens

key_numbers.label: Best buildable chooser vs one fixed style
key_numbers.sub: the trained picker solved fewer on the 80-task pool

key_numbers.label: Compute for that perfect picker
key_numbers.sub: of sampling more, yet it solved the most

charts.chart_plain_title: Which style-picking approach solves the most coding problems
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of choosing generation styles; bar height is the share of 24 coding problems solved, so taller is better. The far-right bar is a perfect after-the-fact picker; the rest are approaches you could actually ship.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the far-right perfect-hindsight bar clearly rises above the pack; every buildable approach sits level with or below simply sampling more from one style.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving power of five effort recipes on a bigger problem pool
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a way of spending generation effort; height is the share of 80 problems solved, taller is better. The far-right bar keeps every candidate answer per problem, ignoring cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker dips below the plain fixed recipes, and the towering far-right bar reveals many solvable problems no practical approach reached.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute burned by each approach on the 24-problem set
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the total compute it spent across 24 problems, so shorter is better. Read it beside the first chart, which shows how many problems each one solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The perfect-picker bar is among the shortest, about half the compute of sampling more, yet it solved the most, so its payoff is cheap not costly.

qwen35_4b_semantic_anchor_coordinate_bra.verdict_tag: The late anchor writes names, but the consequence test is invalid
qwen35_4b_semantic_anchor_coordinate_bra.concept_primer: Imagine pausing a scratchpad, placing a candidate name in a dedicated slot, and copying the model's internal state for a different candidate into that slot. The important test is whether later computation follows the copied candidate—not merely whether the next word changes.
qwen35_4b_semantic_anchor_coordinate_bra.plain_question: Can a copied internal candidate state at a named hypothesis slot drive a separately computed consequence inside real reasoning?
qwen35_4b_semantic_anchor_coordinate_bra.plain_answer: This run cannot establish that. The internal edit strongly changed the model's choice among candidate names, but none of 440 consequence outputs began with a valid answer token. Even in a restricted twelve-choice readout, the edited state chose the intended consequence only 5 of 44 times, nearly the 4-of-44 control rate. A post-run audit also found that two rotating maps canceled, leaving the final name-to-label relation fixed across tasks.
qwen35_4b_semantic_anchor_coordinate_bra.why_it_matters: The numerical intervention controls all passed, so the failure is not hidden drift. But formatting failure and the fixed composed mapping make this an invalid consequence interface, not a clean negative about internal-state transport. The safe lesson is narrower: changing the next candidate name is not evidence that reasoning consumed it.

key_numbers.label: Unrestricted outputs parsed
key_numbers.sub: 6.36%, below the frozen 95% requirement

key_numbers.label: Consequence outputs parsed
key_numbers.sub: every first token was a formatting opener

key_numbers.label: Edited consequence choices
key_numbers.sub: versus 4/44 for source and generic controls

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing a candidate name did not carry through to its consequence
charts.chart_read: For each method, compare the dark bar for choosing the supplied candidate name with the light bar for choosing the label produced by actually applying that candidate's operation. These are restricted-choice diagnostics; the unrestricted output gate failed separately.
charts.chart_takeaway: Visible text, full-state copying, and coordinate copying all changed the immediate name choice, while their computed consequences stayed near the control rate.

qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.verdict_tag: The gap is before the failed test
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.concept_primer: A coding agent can know how to fix a clearly reported failure yet still patch too early when the requirement is ambiguous. The missing skill can be gathering the decisive evidence before writing code.
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.plain_question: Which malformed-input rules still challenge the transaction-trained model after a visible failure, and where in the coding loop does the remaining difficulty actually appear?
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.plain_answer: No rule produced a stable training target after a failed test: every one of 72 cases reached a correct patch. But before seeing test output, the model got zero of 54 ambiguous first patches fully right and never opened the visible tests first. The run formally stopped because answers also exceeded their length allowance too often.
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.why_it_matters: More post-failure practice would teach behavior the model already has. The promising strategy is to teach it to inspect the evidence that distinguishes two plausible requirements, then make its first patch follow what it found.

key_numbers.label: Correct after failure
key_numbers.sub: every case reached a fully correct patch

key_numbers.label: Ambiguous first patches
key_numbers.sub: fully correct without test output

key_numbers.label: Tests read before patching
key_numbers.sub: in rejected-patch trajectories

key_numbers.label: Answer-limit contacts
key_numbers.sub: both blocks exceeded the 5% stop

charts.chart_plain_title: Most rules were already repaired after a visible failure
charts.chart_read: Each group is one hidden rule, with one bar for each fresh block. Taller means the model ended with the correct behavior more often after seeing the failed test.
charts.chart_takeaway: Negative and non-integer handling were perfect in both blocks; blank-resource misses moved between data shapes and did not form a stable lesson.

charts.chart_plain_title: The sharp gap appears before the first patch
charts.chart_read: The first bar asks whether a correct patch ever appeared after test output. The other bars ask whether the model got an ambiguous first patch right or read the tests before making it.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model can act on decisive failure evidence, but it does not seek that evidence before committing to a guess.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both blocks exceeded the answer-length stop
charts.chart_read: The first two bars are the share of tool turns that filled the answer allowance; the last bar is the maximum allowed rate. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The formal result remains an instrument failure even though most long answers contained a usable tool call.

qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.verdict_tag: A perfectly repaired skill reached no dependent task
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.concept_primer: This model runs a recipe forward: given the steps and an input, it computes each result in turn. The reverse — naming the hidden steps from a finished dish — is a separate ability. Drilling forward recipes to perfection never touched it; the two sit in sealed compartments.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_question: If a small model keeps failing tasks because it cannot mentally trace a multi-step process, does training it to trace flawlessly finally fix those tasks?
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_answer: No. Training made the model trace a multi-step process almost flawlessly — even on longer chains and steps it never studied, so it learned a genuine skill, not memorized answers. Yet every task that supposedly needs tracing stayed frozen: naming the hidden steps from examples crept from 8% to 9%. Oddly, unrelated training that merely matched a task's answer format helped three times more.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.why_it_matters: When a small model fails a task, don't train the underlying skill and hope the fix spreads. Fine-tuning buys only the input-to-output mapping you train, plus format look-alikes. To add a truly missing ability, give the model a tool instead.

key_numbers.label: Tracing a four-step process
key_numbers.sub: frozen model vs after skill training, at a chain length it never trained on

key_numbers.label: Tracing steps it never studied
key_numbers.sub: proof it learned a real skill, not memorized answers

key_numbers.label: A task that supposedly needs tracing
key_numbers.sub: naming the hidden steps from examples — essentially unchanged

key_numbers.label: A format-matched task, trained on that format
key_numbers.sub: a threefold jump from matching answer shape, with no skill training

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step tracing collapses as the process gets longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps are chained (one to five); vertical axis is the share of traces the untrained model got exactly right. Two lines: answering after deliberate thinking, and answering immediately. Higher is better; far below the 80% pass mark counts as broken.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines plunge to about a third correct by four steps — thinking from a near-perfect 96%, immediate answers from a lower 84% — so the untrained model's tracing genuinely breaks at length, qualifying it for repair.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps tracing at every length, with or without lossy steps
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length (one to five); vertical axis is the share of exactly-correct traces. Four lines split deliberate thinking versus immediate answers, each with and without steps that throw away information. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Thinking lines mostly sit above their immediate-answer twins (the no-destructive pair briefly crosses at four steps), and information-losing steps hurt most without thinking — so slow deliberation is not the problem, chain length is.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each added step keeps less accuracy than the last
charts.chart_read: Three bars, one per added step (one-to-two, two-to-three, three-to-four), from the deliberate-thinking runs. Each bar's height is the share of accuracy kept versus the shorter chain. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Retention falls from 92% to 66% to 52% — each step costs more than the last, so errors compound rather than leaking at one fixed rate.

qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.verdict_tag: You cannot select a program never built
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.concept_primer: Picture a fill-in-the-blank code template, its gaps filled with pieces from a fixed toolbox; every combination runs and anything that passes the tests is kept. If a task needs a tool the toolbox lacks and the template never asks for it, the right program is never assembled.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_question: When a coding task suddenly needs an operation the template-based program builder has never seen, does the correct program still turn up among the candidates it generates?
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_answer: Only when the template explicitly names the new operation. On tasks needing a brand-new operation, hand-written templates that named and typed it kept the correct program every single time; auto-generated templates kept it just 17% of the time, and stripped-down templates never. And no amount of extra candidates per blank or follow-up test questions could recover a program the builder never proposed.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.why_it_matters: Before investing in a smarter candidate-picker or more test queries, make sure your template actually proposes the operations new tasks demand. No selector can rescue a correct program the generation step never produced.

key_numbers.label: Correct program kept when the template names the new operation
key_numbers.sub: hand-written template, tasks needing a brand-new operation

key_numbers.label: Correct program kept when the template is auto-generated
key_numbers.sub: same brand-new-operation tasks

key_numbers.label: Correct program kept when the template is stripped to output format only
key_numbers.sub: same brand-new-operation tasks

key_numbers.label: Final pick correct after two targeted test questions
key_numbers.sub: but only when the correct program was already among the candidates

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the correct program survive when the task shifts?
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type: a familiar task, the same task with a renamed operation, and a task needing a brand-new operation. Within each group, three bars for auto-generated, hand-written, and stripped templates. Bar height is the share of tasks where the correct program stayed a candidate; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Hand-written bars stay at full height across all three task types, while the auto-generated bar collapses to a sliver and the stripped bar to zero once the task needs a brand-new operation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying more options per blank does not rescue auto templates
charts.chart_read: On brand-new-operation tasks, the horizontal axis is how many fill-in options each blank may try (8, 16, then 28); the vertical axis is the share of tasks keeping the correct program. Three lines: hand-written, auto-generated, stripped templates. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the hand-written line rises to full coverage; the auto line stays flat near one in six and the stripped line at zero, because more options cannot propose an operation the template never names.

charts.chart_plain_title: Follow-up test questions help only when the right program exists
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many follow-up test questions are allowed (zero to three); the vertical axis is the share where the finally chosen program passes hidden tests. Two lines had the correct program available, one did not. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines with the correct program present climb to full correctness within two or three questions, while the line missing it stays flat no matter how many questions are asked.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families keep the correct program, by template style
charts.chart_read: A grid: each row is one of twelve task families (familiar, renamed, and brand-new-operation groups); each column is a template style (auto-generated, hand-written, stripped). Cell shade is the share of that family's tasks keeping the correct program; brighter is higher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written column is fully lit down every row, the auto column goes dark across most brand-new-operation families (one still keeps the program), and the stripped column is dark almost everywhere — the template, not the family, decides survival.

qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.verdict_tag: One nearly-perfect skill made the plan impossible
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.concept_primer: Picture four high-jumpers, each ordered to clear a bar ten centimeters above their own best. The gym ceiling caps everyone. Three have room, but the fourth already jumps just under the ceiling, so ten extra centimeters simply have nowhere to go.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_question: Can you train four separate skill specialists, then merge them into one small model that combines all their strengths on brand-new tasks?
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_answer: No — we never found out. Each of four required skills had to gain ten points before merging, but scores top out at 100. One tool-use skill already sat at 99.4%, so its target of 109.4% was impossible. The experiment stopped before a single training step. The real lesson: broad room on the overall task hid one corner already maxed out.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.why_it_matters: Before spending compute on a multi-specialist plan, check improvement room skill by skill, not just on the averaged task. A fixed points-gained target is unreachable once a skill nears its ceiling — size targets to the room actually left.

key_numbers.label: Starting score on the tool-use skill
key_numbers.sub: already pressed against the perfect ceiling of 100%

key_numbers.label: Score the rule demanded
key_numbers.sub: impossible, since the maximum is 100%

key_numbers.label: Room to improve versus room required
key_numbers.sub: the fixed gain bar dwarfed the headroom left

key_numbers.label: Specialists actually trained
key_numbers.sub: the plan halted before any training ran

charts.chart_plain_title: Required scores versus the 100 percent ceiling, per skill
charts.chart_read: Four skill domains sit along the bottom: discovery-and-repair, stateful control, tool use, and composition. Each shows three bars — current score, the score its rule required, and the 100% ceiling. A required bar staying under the ceiling means the target is reachable.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the tool-use skill's required bar pokes above the ceiling, so its target can never be met; the other three sit well below and stay feasible.

qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.verdict_tag: LoRA did not form the running state this test needed
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.concept_primer: Imagine tracking a piece moving across a board. One method keeps a single running note, updating and feeding it into each next turn. The other re-reads the opening position many times independently and pools its guesses. Same total effort — does chaining the note win?
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.plain_question: When a small model repeats the same computation to solve a step-by-step puzzle, does chaining its memory forward beat pooling many fresh, independent passes?
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.plain_answer: The first matched test did not answer that architecture question because the low-rank update failed to learn the required running state. Carrying memory improved overall accuracy by only 4.3 points, with uncertainty spanning a slight loss to a 9.4-point gain. More decisively, the model decoded the complete joint hidden state on just 0.46% of registered steps, far below the preregistered 40% floor, and swapping memory between examples did not make answers follow the donor. The three-run confirmation therefore stopped exactly as planned.
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.why_it_matters: This was a useful negative checkpoint rather than evidence that serial memory itself was ineffective. A later full-size pilot also showed almost no readable state, but it failed other promotion checks at the same time and did not share bit-identical state-module initialization with this run. A cleaner multi-run comparison is therefore required before blaming or clearing LoRA.

key_numbers.label: Carry minus parallel-pool accuracy
key_numbers.sub: paired 95% interval: -0.8 to +9.4 points

key_numbers.label: Joint hidden states decoded
key_numbers.sub: the preregistered state-formation floor was 40%

key_numbers.label: Unseen task-family gain
key_numbers.sub: paired 95% interval: +0.8 to +9.8 points, but without a readable full state

key_numbers.label: Answer gain from following donor memory
key_numbers.sub: paired 95% interval: -2.3 to +3.9 points

charts.chart_plain_title: Carry and parallel pooling received exactly the same repeated-compute budget
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars shows the total decoder-layer work at one, four, eight, or twelve middle-block passes. The Carry and pooled comparison bars overlap at every depth.
charts.chart_takeaway: The negative pilot cannot be explained by giving one arm less computation. Whether low-rank training caused the missing state remains the question for an initialization- and randomness-matched successor.

qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag_fullr.verdict_tag: Full-size pilot was blocked, not decisive
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag_fullr.concept_primer: The first test changed each large weight matrix through a narrow bottleneck, which is memory-efficient but limits the directions training can move. This successor gives every repeated-pass matrix a full-size change of its own while leaving the ordinary one-pass model untouched.
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag_fullr.plain_question: Was the failed running memory caused by the serial architecture, or did the low-rank training update prevent the model from building a sufficiently rich internal state?
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag_fullr.plain_answer: The run worked mechanically, but it did not settle the question. All 892 million full-size update weights trained and fit comfortably, yet macro task-mean joint-state accuracy was only 0.28% against a 40% requirement. At the same time, Carry scored 1.6 points below equal-compute pooling and failed both required query-kind checks. The frozen rules classify that combination as promotion blocked, not as an isolated capacity failure. The full-size construction also shifted the random initialization of the shared state modules relative to the LoRA run.
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag_fullr.why_it_matters: This valid diagnostic says the direct full-size recipe also failed; it does not prove that LoRA was innocent. The decisive follow-up must give LoRA and full rank exactly the same state-module start and stochastic training stream, prove the state readout can learn, and repeat the fixed final comparison across three seeds. Downstream answer and causal tests should wait until a readable state exists.

charts.chart_takeaway: Carry is uneven and averages 1.6 points below Bag, while the separate hidden-state score is almost zero. Because several gates failed together, this diagnoses a failed recipe but cannot isolate full-size capacity as the cause.

charts.chart_takeaway: The real optimizer and worst-depth gate fit comfortably, so the negative state result cannot be dismissed as an unexecuted or memory-infeasible full-rank proposal.

qwen35_4b_state_formation_analysis_recov.concept_primer: A frozen analysis is like a sealed calculator: repair the cable that feeds it, while leaving every button and formula untouched.
qwen35_4b_state_formation_analysis_recov.plain_question: Can the existing state-analysis run read its own saved files after a strict path check blocked them?
qwen35_4b_state_formation_analysis_recov.plain_answer: Yes, the narrow recovery check passed. It accepts only the one registered file location, still rejects unsafe shortcuts, and has not yet examined any model result.
qwen35_4b_state_formation_analysis_recov.why_it_matters: Practitioners can continue the original comparison without retraining or quietly changing how success is judged. The next evidence will still come from the frozen analysis.

charts.chart_takeaway: All four bars reach the pass mark, showing the file-path repair stayed narrow before any result was read.

qwen35_4b_state_formation_capacity_adjud.verdict_tag: Capacity test is ready, not yet run
qwen35_4b_state_formation_capacity_adjud.concept_primer: Imagine testing whether a compact repair kit can build the same working memory as a full workshop. Start both from identical materials, then open the larger workshop only if the compact kit fails.
qwen35_4b_state_formation_capacity_adjud.plain_question: Can a compact model update learn the deep running state needed for multi-step puzzles, or does it need a full-size update?
qwen35_4b_state_formation_capacity_adjud.plain_answer: Not run yet. The reviewed test starts with three compact-update training runs. If any required state check misses, six matched controls become mandatory; the final three runs open only if the full-size version also misses.
qwen35_4b_state_formation_capacity_adjud.why_it_matters: This staged design can separate a compact-update limitation from a broader training-recipe failure while avoiding expensive full-size runs when the smaller method already works.

key_numbers.label: First-stage training runs
key_numbers.sub: one compact-update run for each registered seed

key_numbers.label: After a first-stage miss
key_numbers.sub: adds six matched control runs

key_numbers.label: Maximum training ladder
key_numbers.sub: the final three open only after a full-size miss

charts.chart_takeaway: The bars rise from 3 to 9 to 12, so expensive full-size work stays locked unless earlier state checks miss.

qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.verdict_tag: A few harder examples double transfer, then stall
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.concept_primer: Picture the model as a code mechanic handed a broken tiny program plus a printout that, for each failing test, shows the answer it should have produced beside the wrong one it actually did. Its job: rewrite the program so every hidden test passes.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_question: If you mix a small batch of slightly-harder practice problems into a small model's training, does it learn a repair skill that carries to entirely new, deeper problems?
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_answer: Partly. Folding in just 60 slightly-harder "bridge" examples, a quarter of the training budget, more than doubled success on deeper, never-seen programs, from 20% to 44% fully repaired, with no loss on familiar skills. But it stalled fast: 80 examples did no better. And the skill is genuine reading, not memorization: feed a scrambled test printout and success sinks below giving no printout at all.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.why_it_matters: When teaching a small model to repair code, add a thin slice of slightly-harder examples and always show real execution results in the prompt. Expect a hard ceiling, and confirm it truly reads that feedback by scrambling the results and watching accuracy collapse.

key_numbers.sub: share fully repaired after adding 60 bridge examples, up from none

key_numbers.label: Bridge examples added
key_numbers.sub: a quarter of a fixed training budget

key_numbers.label: More examples, no payoff
key_numbers.sub: 60 versus 80 bridge examples on deeper programs

key_numbers.label: Correct vs scrambled printout
key_numbers.sub: a shuffled test printout hurts more than none at all

charts.chart_takeaway: Trained families jump to a perfect 100% and hold, while unseen families roughly double from 20% to 44% then flatten, so transfer is real but capped.
