charts.chart_read: Three bars, the same 60-bridge model on the same unseen programs, differing only in the test printout shown: correct, removed, or scrambled. Height is the share of programs whose fix passes every hidden test; taller means it leaned on that printout more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-printout bar towers over the rest, and the scrambled bar sinks below the removed one, proving the model genuinely reads the feedback rather than guessing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Bridge training helps some new families fully, others not at all
charts.chart_read: Six unseen program families along the bottom. Paired bars compare the no-bridge model against the 60-bridge model. Height is the share of programs whose fix passes every hidden test; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several families leap from zero to near-perfect, one stays frozen at zero, and one regresses, so the benefit is family-specific, never a uniform lift.

qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.verdict_tag: Naming coding tactics did not beat plain retries
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.concept_primer: Picture nudging a coder to "try a loop," then "try recursion," then "try math," hoping the varied attempts crack problems a quick pass missed. A small trained add-on learned to obey these tactic labels. But scrambling which label meant what worked exactly as well.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_question: If you train a small coding model to attempt problems under different labeled tactics, does it recover more failed problems than just taking extra plain tries?
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_answer: No. Feeding the model labeled coding tactics recovered 37.5% of the problems it first failed, slightly below plain extra sampling at the same cost, which recovered 41.7%. Tellingly, scrambling the labels so they meant nothing recovered just as many, proving the model was not truly switching tactics — it only gained from taking more shots. Only far pricier pooling of roughly triple the tries reached 62.5%.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.why_it_matters: Do not pay to fine-tune tactic or persona labels hoping for complementary attempts on hard problems. A small coder recovers the same failures from plain extra sampling at equal cost. Spend the budget on more samples, not clever labels.

key_numbers.label: Problems the model failed on its quick first pass
key_numbers.sub: about 1 in 3 coding tasks missed

key_numbers.label: Failures recovered by labeled tactics vs plain retries
key_numbers.sub: same cost, and tactic labels did not win

key_numbers.label: Recovery when the tactic labels were scrambled to mean nothing
key_numbers.sub: matched the plain retries, so the labels added nothing

key_numbers.label: Best recovery from brute-force pooled sampling
key_numbers.sub: the pricey option at roughly triple the tries

charts.chart_plain_title: Recovering failed problems: tactic labels vs plain extra tries
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling method; its height is the share of the 24 first-pass failures it eventually solves, and taller is better. The far-left bar is the cheap first pass, the two middle bars add tactic labels, and the two rightmost pool the most tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tactic-label bars sit no higher than plain extra retries, and the scrambled-label bar matches them; only the far costlier pooled bars climb clearly higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of all problems solved, by sampling method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling method; its height is the fraction of all 80 coding problems it solves, and taller is better. The far-left cheap first pass solves 70%; the tactic-label bars sit near 81-83%; the two rightmost pooled bars reach 86-89%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding tactic labels leaves total coverage in the same low-80s band as ordinary extra retries; clear gains come only from spending far more samples.

qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.verdict_tag: It picks the wrong steps, not wrong numbers
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.concept_primer: Picture a three-step recipe: the ordered list of operations, chop then boil then season, is the structure; the exact amount inside each step is the value. This tested a small model on multi-step list-transforming programs, asking whether failure comes from the order of steps or the numbers inside them.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_question: When a small model fails a three-step programming task, is it choosing the wrong sequence of operations, or the right sequence with the wrong numbers plugged in?
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_answer: The steps. Handed the correct sequence of operations, a cheap number-search finished every single task, so the numbers were never the bottleneck. Left alone, the model almost never even lands the right sequence, and crediting any-numbers-right adds nothing: its right-sequence rate exactly equals its solve rate. There is no hidden pool of right-recipe, wrong-number near-misses waiting to be unlocked.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.why_it_matters: Stop fixing deep multi-step failures with better number hints or value tuning; they will not move the wall. Instead supply the operation sequence from outside, via tools, enumeration, or a library of known structures, then let cheap number-fill finish.

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved: model alone vs handed the correct steps
key_numbers.sub: the whole gap is picking operations, not numbers

key_numbers.label: Extra tasks unlocked by getting the numbers right
key_numbers.sub: right-sequence rate equals solve rate; no wrong-number near-misses

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks cracked by 200 random sequences
key_numbers.sub: vs 100% with the true sequence, so the right structure genuinely matters

key_numbers.label: Best single first-try attempt, three-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: roughly 0.8% solved outright

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate stays near the floor until you hand over the correct steps
charts.chart_read: Four setups, each shown for two-step and three-step tasks; height is solve rate, taller is better. Left to right: single best attempt, best of eight attempts, best of eight credited if any numbers give the right sequence, and tasks solved when the true sequence is supplied.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars sit near the floor and barely differ, so any-numbers credit adds nothing, then jump to full height once the correct steps are supplied: steps are the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even 200 random step-sequences barely crack the three-step tasks
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis: how many random operation sequences were tried (8, 50, 200), each filled by number-search. Vertical axis: solve rate, higher is better. Upper line is two-step tasks, lower line three-step.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two-step tasks climb to 60% with enough random tries, but three-step tasks reach only about 11%, versus 100% with the true sequence, so the correct structure is genuinely required.

qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.verdict_tag: Brute-force search wins wider as tasks deepen
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.concept_primer: Picture a lock whose combination is a chain of dials, each with sixteen positions. The model tries to recall the right sequence from memory. Brute force instead tries every combination on the real lock and keeps only the ones that actually open it.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_question: When checking every possible answer gets too expensive, does the model's built-in sense of program structure finally beat blindly trying everything?
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_answer: No. Adding one extra dial — a sixteen-times-larger space of combinations — did not flip things. Exhaustive search stayed near-perfect at about 97 percent, while the model's knack for guessing the right combination from memory collapsed from 51 percent to 10 percent. The gap did not shrink toward a crossover; it widened, from 46 to 87 points. Structure fades faster than search slows.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.why_it_matters: If you can actually run candidate programs and check their output, spend compute on enumerate-and-test search plus a cheap output filter, not on training a small model to internalize program structure — that internal structure erodes as tasks get deeper.

key_numbers.label: Model guessed the program's shape right
key_numbers.sub: as tasks grow from three to four chained steps

key_numbers.label: Brute-force search solved rate
key_numbers.sub: 96.7% at four steps, 97.5% at three — near-perfect at both

key_numbers.label: How far the model trailed search
key_numbers.sub: the shortfall grows, not shrinks, with depth

key_numbers.label: Shapes left after a cheap output check
key_numbers.sub: an eight-value consistency filter prunes what search must actually run at four steps

charts.chart_plain_title: Model's shape-guessing collapses while brute-force search stays near-perfect
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is composition depth, three then four chained steps. The vertical axis is percent correct; higher is better. The upper line is brute-force search's solved rate; the lower line is how often the model guessed the right program shape from memory.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines spread apart instead of meeting: the model drops from 51 to 10 percent while search holds near 97, so no crossover ever appears.

charts.chart_plain_title: Program shapes brute-force must check explode with each added step
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is composition depth: three, four, five steps. The vertical axis counts program shapes to enumerate, sixteen raised to the depth — about four thousand, then sixty-five thousand, then a million. Fewer shapes favors search.
charts.chart_takeaway: The count multiplies sixteen-fold per step, only turning punishing near depth five — yet the model's structure has already collapsed at depth four, before search runs out of room.

qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.verdict_tag: Hand-written parts solve them; recycled ones solve none
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.concept_primer: Imagine a locksmith with nine stubborn locks. Cutting a fresh key shaped for each one opens all nine, but that only proves the locks are pickable. Reaching for keys already in the drawer, reshaped to fit, opened none, the real test of reuse.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_question: When a coding model gets stuck on hard problems, can a library of reusable code building-blocks, adapted from past solutions, express answers it could not find on its own?
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_answer: Only when a building-block was hand-carved for each specific problem. A shared library that reshapes old solutions to fit new ones solved zero of the nine stuck tasks, no better than nothing. Custom-written parts solved all nine, but that only proves the tasks are solvable, not that reuse works. Worse, over half the answers that passed the visible example tests were secretly wrong.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.why_it_matters: Do not expect a shared library of recycled solutions to expand what a small model can solve on genuinely new problems. And never mine training targets from visible example tests alone: here most answers that passed them were actually wrong.

key_numbers.label: Visible-passing answers that were secretly wrong
key_numbers.sub: 17 of 30 failed the hidden checks

key_numbers.label: Recycled answers that looked right but were wrong
key_numbers.sub: every reused answer that passed sample tests failed hidden ones

charts.chart_takeaway: The recycle bar is flat at zero while custom-built parts reach full height, so reusing past solutions solved nothing and hand-written parts solved everything.

charts.chart_takeaway: For recycled past solutions, 8 answers passed the sample tests but zero survived the full hidden checks.

charts.chart_takeaway: Expanded custom-built parts reached full coverage at about 15 tries per task, far cheaper than the pooled roughly 57.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.verdict_tag: Nudges fix confident mistakes, not coin-flip forks
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.concept_primer: A model's thinking has forks where one next word led to success, another to failure. This method nudges it toward the winner — like a GPS blocking a wrong turn. But at forks where both roads look equally good, nagging just makes the driver hesitate and run low on fuel.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_question: If you find the exact moments where a model's reasoning branches toward success versus failure, and nudge it toward the winning branch, does it get smarter?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_answer: No. Nudging at those forks made the model worse, not better — success on fresh tasks fell about 4 to 8 percent instead of clearing the 5-point gain hoped for. The tell: feeding it deliberately scrambled labels did nearly the same damage, so the harm came from the nudging procedure itself, not the success signal. The nudge only helps when the branch it discourages was already a runaway favorite.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.why_it_matters: Before training a model to prefer some thinking-tokens over others, confirm the token you push down is already its runaway favorite — a clear misstep, not a near-tie. At coin-flip forks the nudge just disrupts reasoning and burns the thinking budget.

key_numbers.label: Success after steering
key_numbers.sub: single best-answer success on fresh tasks, shorter thinking budget; a gain was the goal

key_numbers.label: Damage from scrambled labels
key_numbers.sub: shuffling which branch was a success lost almost as much, so the signal wasn't the cause

key_numbers.label: Thinking finishes on its own
key_numbers.sub: how often reasoning reaches a natural end within budget, longer budget — roughly halved

key_numbers.label: Headroom left unconverted
key_numbers.sub: single-shot success vs how often at least one of eight tries was right, same tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Steering and scrambled-label training both lag the untouched model
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show single best-answer success for three models — untouched base, steering-trained, and a scrambled-label control — with two bars each for a shorter and a longer thinking budget. Taller is better; base is tallest in both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both trained models sit below base by similar margins, and the scrambled control tracks the steering model — so the training procedure, not the success signal, caused the drop.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model finishes its thinking on its own
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each model reaches a natural end of its reasoning within the longer thinking budget, for untouched base, steering-trained, and scrambled control. Higher is better; both trained bars are far shorter than base.
charts.chart_takeaway: Base finishes on its own more than twice as often as either trained model — identically whether labels were real or scrambled — pinpointing disrupted reasoning flow as the damage.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.verdict_tag: A one-word nudge shifts the whole model
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.concept_primer: The goal was to fix the model's word choice at one confident wrong turn in its reasoning. But it's like retuning a single guitar string through a peg geared to all six: you can't shift that one note without detuning the whole chord.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_question: Can you make a small reasoning model smarter by gently editing the exact moments where it confidently takes a wrong turn in its thinking?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_answer: No. Gently pulling the better word up at 155 hand-picked wrong-turn moments was cleaner than shoving the bad word down, and it carried real signal, beating a scrambled-label control by about 14 points on fresh coding repairs. But it still lost to the untouched model, 54% versus 60% of repositories fixed, and the nudge leaked into unrelated words well past the safety limit.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.why_it_matters: Before trusting a targeted fine-tune to fix specific reasoning mistakes, measure how far it moves unrelated word-scores, not just its target, and always run a scrambled-label control. If drift clears your locality budget, more examples won't rescue it; shrink the dose.

key_numbers.label: Best edit vs untouched model on coding repairs

key_numbers.label: How far the gentlest edit overshot the safety limit
key_numbers.sub: even the cleanest arm moved unrelated words too much

key_numbers.label: Real labels beat scrambled labels on coding
key_numbers.sub: the outcome signal was real, just not enough to win

key_numbers.label: Editable moments harvested
key_numbers.sub: confident wrong turns pulled from the model's reasoning

charts.chart_takeaway: Pull-up's bar is about a third shorter than push-down's, yet all three, even scrambled labels, rise above the safety line, so no method stayed local.

charts.chart_read: Five agent versions along the bottom; bar height is the share of repository repairs passing hidden tests, higher is better: untouched model over eight serial turns, push down, pull up, pull up with scrambled labels, and the untouched model split into two shorter tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untouched eight-turn agent leads near 60%; the best edit reaches 54%, and splitting the calls into two short tries collapses to 31%, so long feedback loops mattered most.

charts.chart_read: Four evaluations along the bottom; bar height is the real pull-up minus the scrambled pull-up, so above zero means real labels helped: shorter and longer fresh reasoning, the practice gym, and the coding agent.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real labels beat scrambled by up to 14 points on coding and the practice gym, but dip negative at the longer reasoning budget, evidence of signal rather than a reliable gain.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.verdict_tag: A cheap recheck cuts thinking cost, not accuracy
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.concept_primer: Like a mechanic who tries a quick fix, takes one test drive, and only opens up the engine if the car still won't start. Here the quick fix is a fast draft answer, the test drive is the single example check shown in the task, and opening the engine is extra thinking.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_question: When a model can choose how long to think on each task, can a simple rule spend that effort more wisely than always thinking a fixed amount?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_answer: For cost, yes; for accuracy, no. Drafting cheaply and thinking harder only when the draft fails the one visible example matches a generous fixed budget's accuracy (88%) using about a fifth of the thinking (113 versus 507 tokens per task). But it never beats the best fixed budget (91%), because that quick check passes a wrong answer about 1 in 10 times.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.why_it_matters: Treat the thinking budget as an efficiency dial, not an accuracy dial: draft cheaply and spend more thinking only when a quick check fails. To actually raise accuracy, you need a commit signal better than a single example test.

key_numbers.label: Thinking spent per task
key_numbers.sub: best fixed budget vs the cheap draft-then-recheck rule, at near-equal accuracy

key_numbers.label: Accuracy: cheap rule vs best fixed budget
key_numbers.sub: about 3 points given up for a large cost cut

key_numbers.label: Quick check passes a wrong answer
key_numbers.sub: the visible example passes but the hidden tests still fail

key_numbers.label: Best-possible per-task ceiling
key_numbers.sub: a non-deployable chooser that knows the ideal thinking budget for each task

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average thinking tokens per task (further left is cheaper); the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved (higher is better). One line traces fixed budgets, another the recheck rule, and a lone dot marks the best-possible ceiling. Up-and-left wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recheck line sits far left of the fixed line at the same heights, so equal accuracy for a fraction of the thinking, but stops just below the fixed peak; the ceiling dot floats highest.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by strategy, from no thinking to the ideal ceiling
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy's share of tasks fully solved, taller is better, running left to right from no thinking through two fixed budgets and the two recheck rules to the non-deployable ideal ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recheck rules land at 88 to 89%, wedged between cheaper fixed budgets and the 93% ceiling; only the best fixed budget, at 91%, tops them.

charts.chart_read: Each bar shows the share of tasks a strategy commits to because the visible example passed, yet which fail the hidden tests; lower is better. Bars run from the best fixed budget to leaner, cheaper recheck rules.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rate climbs from 6% for the fixed budget to 11% for the leanest rule, so trusting thinner drafts leaks more errors, and this gap caps accuracy below the ceiling.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.verdict_tag: Capped thinking beats both silence and endless rambling
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.concept_primer: Before answering a coding task, this small model can fill a private scratchpad, working the logic out. With none, it just guesses. Given a page or two, it reasons and solves more. Given an unlimited pad, it talks itself out of the right answer.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_question: If you let a small coding model reason to itself before answering, does it actually solve more problems, and is more thinking always better?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_answer: Yes, but with a sweet spot. Turning on the model's built-in reasoning lifted the accuracy of the answer you would actually ship from 76% to 91% on basic Python tasks — a genuine capability gain, not just a wider pool of guesses to pick from. But more is not better: accuracy peaks around a few hundred to a thousand reasoning tokens, then falls. Unlimited thinking scored only 84%, worse than a firm cap.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.why_it_matters: Turn on native thinking mode for small coding models — it is real capability many setups leave switched off. But cap the thinking length: unlimited reasoning overthinks and loses accuracy. Sweep the budget once; the peak sat near a thousand tokens here.

key_numbers.sub: single best answer on 100 basic Python tasks

key_numbers.label: Unlimited thinking vs a firm cap
key_numbers.sub: free-running thinking scored below a mid-size budget

key_numbers.label: Best-of-eight ceiling barely moved
key_numbers.sub: only +5 points, versus +15 for the shipped answer

key_numbers.label: Gain on middling tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate by thinking budget: a mid-size cap wins
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped along the bottom by thinking budget — none, then 256, 512, 1024 and 2048-token caps, then unlimited. One color is the single shipped answer's solve rate, the other the best-of-eight ceiling. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Shipped-answer bars climb from 76% to a 91% peak at the 1024-token cap, then fall to 84% unlimited — a little thinking helps, too much hurts.

charts.chart_plain_title: The rise-then-fall shape holds across every accuracy measure
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is the thinking-token cap, from zero (off) to unlimited; vertical axis is solve rate, higher is better. Three lines: the single shipped answer, one random sampled attempt, and the answer picked by the one visible example test.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines rise into a 512-to-1024 cap then flatten or dip; the shared hump shows overthinking is a real cost, not one metric's noise.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps the hardest tasks the most
charts.chart_read: Tasks split into three difficulty groups along the bottom — already easy (51 tasks), middling (40), and never solved without thinking (9). Paired bars compare no thinking against a fixed 1024-token budget; taller shipped-answer solve rate is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gap widens with difficulty: +6 points on easy, +23 on middling, and a third of never-solved tasks (3 of 9) become solvable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does scrambling the reasoning erase the gain?
charts.chart_read: Two thinking budgets along the bottom (512 and 2048 tokens); three bars each — no thinking, the model's own thinking with word order scrambled, and its real thinking. Taller shipped-answer solve rate is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambled thinking recovers much of the lift here, hinting scaffold and compute matter — but the authors flag this as their shakiest result, likely understating real reasoning.

qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.verdict_tag: Only real reasoning helps, wrong reasoning wrecks answers
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.concept_primer: Treat the model's written thinking as a recipe it cooks from, not a warm-up that merely revs its engine. A blank card, or the ingredients listed in scrambled order, changes the meal not at all; a clear recipe helps; another dish's recipe cooks the wrong meal.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_question: When a small model writes out its reasoning before answering, does that help because it truly reasons, or just because generating extra text warms up its computation?
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_answer: It genuinely reasons; the boost is content, not compute. Blank filler of the same length, and the real thinking scrambled into nonsense, both scored like skipping thinking entirely, around 74 to 75 percent. Only coherent thinking lifted accuracy to 86 percent. And splicing in another task's reasoning crashed it to 4 percent, because the model solves whatever problem its notes describe.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.why_it_matters: Don't add reasoning tokens just to buy compute; padding and scrambled notes gain nothing here. Guard the scratchpad: a routing or retrieval bug that injects stale or off-topic reasoning is catastrophic, not harmless filler, because the model faithfully follows it.

key_numbers.label: No thinking vs coherent thinking
key_numbers.sub: share of Python tasks passing all tests, best of eight tries

key_numbers.label: Same length of blank or scrambled thinking
key_numbers.sub: both stay right at the 75% no-thinking baseline

key_numbers.label: Fed another task's reasoning
key_numbers.sub: a 71-point collapse; it solves the wrong problem

key_numbers.label: Share of the gain from coherent reasoning
key_numbers.sub: not compute, not scaffold, not token presence

charts.chart_plain_title: Coding pass rate for five kinds of thinking
charts.chart_read: Five bars, one per thinking condition along the bottom (no thinking, filler dots, shuffled thinking, real thinking, another task's thinking), all matched to the same thinking length. Height is the share of Python tasks fully passed across eight tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars sit together near three-quarters; only real thinking rises to about 86 percent, while foreign thinking collapses to a 4 percent stub, so content drives the result, not length.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each part of thinking actually adds to the score
charts.chart_read: Four bars, each the change in pass rate from adding one ingredient in turn: extra compute, having relevant words present, putting them in coherent order, and swapping in wrong-task content. Bars above zero help; bars below zero hurt.
charts.chart_takeaway: Extra compute and scrambled words hover at zero; coherent order alone lifts about 12 points, the entire gain, while wrong content plunges 71 points below baseline, toxic rather than inert.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coherent thinking's edge grows with a bigger thinking budget
charts.chart_read: The line tracks how much coherent thinking beats the same words scrambled (vertical) as the thinking-token budget rises left to right across three settings, from about 500 to 2000 tokens. Higher means coherence matters more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line climbs from roughly 10 to 15 points, so more room to think widens coherence's advantage rather than diluting real reasoning into mere compute.

qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.verdict_tag: Thinking sharpens recognition but cannot plan ahead
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.concept_primer: Picture a chess puzzle. One move from checkmate, extra thinking time reliably helps you spot the winning move. But asked to name the first move of a forced three-move mate, with no board to picture the middle positions, thinking out loud simply doesn't help.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_question: If you let a small model think out loud at length before answering, can it learn to plan several steps ahead toward a goal on its own?
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_answer: No. Asked to name the first of three moves toward a goal, the model stayed stuck at pure guessing — right about 1 time in 32 — no matter how long it thought, even with 2,048 tokens to think first. Yet recognizing a goal just one move away leapt from roughly 28% to 60% as thinking grew. So extra thinking amplifies spotting a near answer, not planning a distant one.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.why_it_matters: If your system needs genuine multi-step planning, a longer chain of thought won't manufacture it — here only training installed that ability. Spend thinking budget on recognition-style subtasks where the intermediate state is visible, and measure planning and recognition separately.

key_numbers.label: Planning the first of three moves
key_numbers.sub: next-move accuracy, no thinking vs longest scratchpad — not a real change, both near chance

key_numbers.label: Chance level
key_numbers.sub: guessing among the 32 possible moves

key_numbers.label: Recognizing a goal one move away
key_numbers.sub: next-move accuracy as thinking budget grows

key_numbers.label: What training did on that same first move
key_numbers.sub: training the model, not thinking, is what lifts planning

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps only when the goal is one move away
charts.chart_read: Three groups along the bottom mark how far the goal sits: three moves away (real planning), two away, and one away (recognition). Within each, bars are no thinking, medium, and large scratchpad. Height is next-move accuracy; taller is better, about 3% is chance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The one-away group climbs steeply toward 60% as thinking grows, while the three-away planning group stays flat near chance — thinking boosts recognition, not planning.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-move planning stays at chance as thinking grows
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is scratchpad size (0, 1,024, 2,048 tokens); the vertical axis is accuracy at picking the correct first move. One line tracks the model; a flat reference line marks the 1-in-32 chance rate. Rising well above it would be better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line barely lifts from about 3% to 8% and hugs the chance line at every budget — more thinking buys essentially no planning ability.

qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.verdict_tag: Scrambled thinking reads correctness as well as real
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.concept_primer: Picture clipping a meter onto the model's inner wiring: from a single snapshot taken the instant it finishes writing code, the meter guesses whether that code will actually run. The test is whether making the model think first makes that reading sharper.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_question: After a small model writes code, does its own internal state reveal whether that code is correct, and does making it think first make the signal clearer?
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_answer: Yes, but not for the reason you would expect. From one snapshot of internal activity, whether the model's own code is correct is readable well above a coin-flip, about 64 to 76 percent of the time. Thinking first sharpens that signal. But scrambling the thinking words into gibberish sharpens it just as much, so the boost comes from the act of thinking, not its logic.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.why_it_matters: If you plan to filter a small model's outputs by reading its internal confidence, the signal is real but weak: it appears only after the model generates thinking tokens, and it is never strong enough to trust as a standalone judge.

key_numbers.label: How readably internal state shows the code is correct

key_numbers.label: Reading correctness before versus after thinking
key_numbers.sub: no-thinking versus best scrambled thinking

key_numbers.label: Scrambled thinking versus real thinking

key_numbers.label: Catching code that fooled a quick test
key_numbers.sub: near coin-flip without thinking; weak signal after

charts.chart_plain_title: How readably do the model's internals show its own code is right?

charts.chart_takeaway: Every thinking bar rises above no-thinking, and the two scrambled-thinking bars are the tallest of all, so content-free thinking reads correctness best.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correctness readability at each layer of the network
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis walks through all 33 layers from input to output; the vertical axis is how readable correctness is, higher better. Five lines: no-thinking sits lowest, while real and scrambled thinking at both lengths ride above it across the whole depth.
charts.chart_takeaway: At nearly every layer the four thinking lines sit above no-thinking, and the scrambled lines match or top the real ones, so the ordering holds throughout the network.

charts.chart_plain_title: Can the probe catch code that fooled a quick test?
charts.chart_read: Five conditions along the bottom; bar height is how well the probe ranks truly-correct code above code that passes a quick visible test but fails hidden ones. Higher is better; 50 percent means no better than guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-thinking bar sits near 52 percent and is useless; thinking bars reach 60 to 68 percent, a weak but real filter that appears only after the model has thought.

qwen35_4b_tokenizer_eos_answer_commit_fa.verdict_tag: First-stop answer contract is registered; no model run is open
qwen35_4b_tokenizer_eos_answer_commit_fa.concept_primer: Qwen has two different end markers here: a chat-end token and a later model-end token. The prior test waited for the later one, so a correct short answer looked like it had extra text. This follow-up registers exactly where answer content must stop before trying the reasoning task again.
qwen35_4b_tokenizer_eos_answer_commit_fa.plain_question: Does stopping at the first chat-end token create a strict, usable program answer without loosening the parser?
qwen35_4b_tokenizer_eos_answer_commit_fa.plain_answer: No model result exists yet. A synthetic smoke now accepts only the exact answer followed by one terminal chat-end token. It rejects the old later boundary, early or repeated stops, missing stops, and any extra byte before commit.
qwen35_4b_tokenizer_eos_answer_commit_fa.why_it_matters: The design can distinguish a real token-boundary repair from arbitrary suffix stripping. Fresh tasks and a matched old-boundary control are still required before residual mechanics can open.

key_numbers.label: Registered answer boundaries
key_numbers.sub: tokenizer chat end versus HF model end

key_numbers.label: Synthetic boundary cases
key_numbers.sub: clean, old boundary, early, repeated, missing, and extra byte

key_numbers.label: Model-free tests passing
key_numbers.sub: protocol plus pinned runner template

key_numbers.label: Model calls
key_numbers.sub: construction and adversarial review still block live work

charts.chart_plain_title: Only one synthetic trace is both physically valid and exactly parsed
charts.chart_read: Each category is one frozen boundary case. The first bar says whether its stop-token geometry could be real; the second says whether every pre-commit token is exactly the requested answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the clean first-chat-end case passes exactness. Valid-looking old, early, or extra-byte traces still fail the strict answer grammar.

qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.concept_primer: This small model writes little programs by chaining list operations. It normally improves by restudying solutions it found itself — but it almost never discovers three-operation chains, so none enter its study pile. Here a plain brute-force search supplies those missing three-chain answers for it to copy.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_question: If a small model never discovers a three-step program on its own, can a mechanical search find those solutions and retraining teach it to produce them?
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_answer: Partly. A brute-force search found working three-step programs the model never produces itself, and retraining on them lifted three-step success from a hard zero to 5 of 40 fresh, never-seen tasks when it can reason across sixteen tries — a real, significant crossing. But in one shot, the production setting, it still solved none, versus 15% on two-step tasks. The knowledge crossed the wall, yet barely stuck in the weights.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.why_it_matters: To push a small model one composition-step deeper, use an external search to supply examples it cannot generate itself — but expect the new skill only with test-time reasoning, not one-shot production calls. Seed each depth separately; deeper installs weaker.

key_numbers.label: Three-step success, reasoning across sixteen tries
key_numbers.sub: novel unseen tasks unlocked, up from a hard zero

key_numbers.label: Three-step success, single best shot
key_numbers.sub: the skill never installed for one-shot deployment

key_numbers.label: Two-step success, reasoning across sixteen tries
key_numbers.sub: shallower chains install far more strongly

key_numbers.label: Hard programs the search found
key_numbers.sub: three-step tasks brute-forced on CPU, no model involved

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the tool-seeded training helps and where it doesn't
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by program depth — two, three, and four chained operations. Within each group: the untrained model's success across sixteen tries, the retrained model's success across sixteen tries, and the retrained model's single-shot production success. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At three operations the retrained multi-try bar jumps off zero while the untrained bar stays flat — but its single-shot bar is still zero, and four operations never moves. Knowledge crossed, not deployable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Three-step success climbs with more tries but stays low
charts.chart_read: Share of three-step tasks solved (vertical) as allowed tries rise from one to sixteen (horizontal). Flat bottom line: the untrained model, pinned at zero. Middle line: the retrained model. Top line: that same retrained model on easier two-step tasks. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained three-step line climbs steadily off zero to 5 of 40 while the untrained line stays flat at zero — real learning, but far below the two-step line's 42%.

qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.verdict_tag: Two cheap checks beat a fine-tuned model
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.concept_primer: For each table puzzle the model gives two answers: a quick guess, and a small program it writes and test-runs on one example whose answer is known. A referee commits just one, trusting the program only when it passes that test and disagrees with the guess.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_question: When a small model can either answer a table task directly or run a program it wrote, what is the best way to decide which answer to submit?
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_answer: Surprisingly, yes, and with almost no learning. Trusting the program only when it passes a worked example and disagrees with the quick answer lifted accuracy from 56% to 66%, exactly matching the best any picker could reach, and never overwriting a correct answer. Fine-tuning learned most of the signal but landed lower, at 64%. Letting the model commit its own program every time collapsed to 24%, because programs that pass the example are often still secretly wrong.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.why_it_matters: Before fine-tuning a selector, try cheap tool signals: does the generated program pass its worked example, and does it disagree with the direct answer? Those two checks captured every recoverable win here. Never let a small model commit its own code unchecked.

key_numbers.label: Two-check rule vs answering directly
key_numbers.sub: correct on 50 unseen table tasks: 28 of 50 up to 33 of 50

key_numbers.label: Best any picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: perfect-hindsight ceiling, which the simple rule tied

key_numbers.label: Fine-tuned model
key_numbers.sub: one task below the free rule and the ceiling

key_numbers.label: Letting the model commit its own program
key_numbers.sub: worse than never using it; overwrote 21 of 50 correct answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer accuracy for six ways of choosing which answer to submit
charts.chart_read: Each bar is test accuracy on unseen tasks for one commit policy, left to right: answer directly, commit the program blindly, fine-tune on scrambled labels, the fine-tuned policy, the two-check rule, and a perfect-hindsight ceiling. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-check rule reaches the perfect-hindsight ceiling, while committing the program blindly sits lowest, below even always answering directly. Knowing when to override beats raw effort.

charts.chart_plain_title: Wrong answers rescued versus right answers ruined, per policy
charts.chart_read: For five policies, two bars each out of 50 tasks: wrong direct answers the program rescued, and correct direct answers a bad program overwrote. More rescues with zero overwrites is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-check rule and fine-tuned policy rescue about 5 tasks with zero overwrites, while committing the program blindly destroys 10 to 21 correct answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the tool state hold a real, learnable choose signal?
charts.chart_read: Two training-loss curves over 80 steps: fitting the real pick-the-answer labels versus randomly shuffled labels. Lower loss means the model found a pattern; the shuffled line is the cannot-learn-noise baseline.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-label loss plunges toward zero while shuffled-label loss stays stuck high, confirming the tool state carries a genuine choose signal, not memorized noise.

qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.verdict_tag: More fresh tries beat a trained code fixer
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.concept_primer: A small coding model writes a program; it fails the tests. You can either train a specialist to patch the broken code, or throw the attempt away and write a few fresh ones. Here, the cheap fresh rewrites won.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_question: When a small coding model writes a program that fails its tests, is it better to train a dedicated fixer or just retry from scratch?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_answer: Retry from scratch. Given the same compute budget, a few more fresh attempts rescued 5 of 57 stuck problems, while a fixer trained to patch broken code rescued only 2 — and its repairs secretly failed the hidden tests nearly a third of the time. An untrained fixer helped slightly (3 of 57), but neither repair approach beat plain retries.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.why_it_matters: Before building a trained repair model, spend that budget on more diverse first-draft attempts — they recovered more failures here. And distrust code that passes the visible tests: repairs often quietly break on the hidden ones.

key_numbers.label: Solved by the four-tries baseline

key_numbers.label: Stuck tasks rescued: retry vs trained fixer
key_numbers.sub: more fresh attempts beat the trained fixer at equal cost

key_numbers.label: Trained fixes that secretly failed
key_numbers.sub: passed the visible tests but failed hidden ones (29%)

key_numbers.label: Untrained fixer rescues
key_numbers.sub: a small lift, still below simply retrying

charts.chart_read: Of 57 problems with no working solution, each bar counts how many one strategy rescued: an untrained fixer, a trained fixer, and simply making more fresh attempts. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: More-fresh-attempts is the tallest bar and the trained fixer the shortest, so retrying beat repair and training the fixer actually made it worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows the share of 150 problems solved. The leftmost is four fresh tries alone; the other three add extra effort in different ways. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All extra-effort bars edge above the four-tries baseline, but more fresh attempts sits highest, adding the most coverage overall.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each fixer's code passed visible but failed hidden checks
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often a fixer produced code that passed the visible checks yet still failed the hidden ones — a misfire. Untrained versus trained fixer. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained fixer's bar is taller, meaning training made it more likely to output code that looks correct but quietly fails.

qwen35_4b_transaction_invariant_recovery.verdict_tag: The structure transferred but one exception did not
qwen35_4b_transaction_invariant_recovery.concept_primer: A shop should reject a negative order as malformed, but simply decline an ordinary order when stock is too low. Both outcomes leave stock unchanged, yet they are different rules. The model learned the safe update pattern but confused these two kinds of failure.
qwen35_4b_transaction_invariant_recovery.plain_question: Can focused worked repairs teach one small coding model to validate a whole request, copy state, and commit changes atomically without erasing its existing test-and-revise loop?
qwen35_4b_transaction_invariant_recovery.plain_answer: Partly. Focused training lifted practice repairs from 52% to 82% and preserved the test-and-revise loop. But on different transaction APIs it reached 72% versus 70% before training, far below the required ten-point gain. Every target first patch copied state, checked the whole request, and updated atomically, yet all missed the separate negative-amount exception.
qwen35_4b_transaction_invariant_recovery.why_it_matters: A worked repair can install the broad shape of a safe program without installing the exact policy for each failure case. The next training set should begin from almost-correct failed tests and teach the smallest distinction-preserving correction, rather than add more generic success examples.

key_numbers.sub: strong local installation with the focused examples

key_numbers.label: Different-API repairs before and after
key_numbers.sub: only one additional success out of 64 cases

key_numbers.label: First target patches with the safe structure
key_numbers.sub: all copied, checked every resource, and updated atomically

key_numbers.label: External benchmark calls
key_numbers.sub: stopped after the different-API gain missed its bar

charts.chart_plain_title: A large practice gain shrinks to one new success
charts.chart_read: The two groups compare practice families with different transaction APIs. Within each, bars show the starting model, equal extra recovery training, and focused transaction training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Focused training dominates on practice tasks but barely moves the different APIs, so the learned rule shape did not become reliable task success.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every first patch learned three parts and missed one
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of 16 target first patches containing one required behavior. Full-height bars mean every patch had it; the missing bar is the exception policy.
charts.chart_takeaway: All patches copy state, check the whole request, and update atomically, but none reject negative amounts correctly—the precise residual for the next curriculum.

qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.verdict_tag: Let the model pick recipes, not write code
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.concept_primer: Think of cleaning a messy spreadsheet or pulling an ID out of ragged text. Instead of asking the model to type the code, the system pre-writes every valid short recipe, shows a few input-to-output examples, and the model just ranks which recipe fits. A trusted program then runs the winner exactly.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_question: If you hand a small model a menu of ready-made data-transformation recipes instead of asking it to write the code itself, can it reliably pick the right one?
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_answer: Yes. After a light round of tuning, the model chose a recipe that produced the correct output on all 48 test tasks, matching a perfect answer key and beating the untuned model's 92%. It recovered the harder multi-step chains the untuned model flubbed, 19 of 21 up to 21 of 21. Honest caveat: simply matching recipes against the shown examples already scored about 90%, so the menu was fairly easy.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.why_it_matters: For structured tasks, let the model score valid pre-built options instead of generating code from scratch: this erases syntax errors, and a tiny tune can push the choice to perfect. But seed the menu with hard look-alike decoys, or high accuracy is an illusion.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned-model bar rises to the same full height as the perfect answer key, while the untuned model's bar stops short, missing four tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Right answers on single-step versus multi-step recipes
charts.chart_read: Each method shows two bars: one for single-step tasks and one for tasks needing several chained steps; height is the share solved correctly, taller is better. Only the tuned model reaches full height on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the tuned model tops out on both bars; the untuned model's multi-step bar dips below its single-step one, showing chaining was the weak spot.

charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often a method chose the exact intended recipe, not just any recipe that happens to produce the right output; height is the share, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned model's bar towers to full height while every other bar sits near or below half, meaning the others often pass with look-alike recipes.

qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.verdict_tag: Let the model sketch, let the solver fill
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole corrected mini-program, the model writes a fill-in-the-blank template — a number goes here, a word there, a yes/no test here — and an automated search tries every valid way to fill the blanks, keeping only completions that pass the visible examples.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_question: When a small model has to fix a broken little program, does it work better to write the whole fix, or to sketch the shape and let a solver finish it?
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_answer: It depends on difficulty. On the hardest problems, sketching the shape and letting a verified search fill the blanks lifted correct fixes from 33% to 78%, and a safe blend of both methods reached 88%. But on easy problems, forcing sketches backfired — 100% dropped to 75% — because too many blank-fillings look equally right. So trust the model's direct fix whenever it already passes every visible test.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.why_it_matters: Use small models for what they do reliably — spotting the output shape and coarse control flow — and hand deep detail-composition to a typed search. Guard it: only override the direct fix when its whole-program attempt fails your visible tests.

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems fixed: writing the whole program vs sketch-plus-solver
key_numbers.sub: 40 of 120 versus 94 of 120 hard repairs solved

key_numbers.label: Best result on the hardest problems
key_numbers.sub: safe blend that keeps the direct fix when it passes, else uses the solver

key_numbers.label: How often the correct program was somewhere in the solver's candidates
key_numbers.sub: so remaining misses are picking the wrong candidate, not missing coverage

key_numbers.label: Easy problems fixed when forcing sketches instead of direct fixes

charts.chart_plain_title: Passing hidden tests: writing the whole fix vs sketching for a solver

qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.verdict_tag: Design frozen; model runs are pending
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.concept_primer: The previous training recipe nearly learned to finish fresh puzzles, but a few answers ran out of space before the model stopped reasoning. This follow-up teaches the exact transition from reasoning to a final answer while keeping the amount of practice equal across comparisons.
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.plain_question: Does putting extra teaching weight on the moment reasoning ends help the model finish reliably, beyond simply adding targeted lessons or more replay practice?
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.plain_answer: The comparison is ready, but the model has not run yet. Three versions will start from the same near-miss model and receive 320 examples apiece. One gets replay only, one gets 80 fresh execution and rule-finding lessons, and the third gets those exact same lessons with extra weight only on the two-piece closing marker.
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.why_it_matters: This separates three explanations that earlier work could not: benefit from another short training pass, benefit from task-targeted lessons, and benefit from teaching the model when to stop reasoning. Equal example counts and equal processed text make the comparison unusually clean.

qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.verdict_tag: Synthetic skills installed locally but displaced broad behavior
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.concept_primer: The curriculum teaches abstract procedures with invented symbols that do not resemble the benchmark. The crucial test is not whether the model learns those exercises, but whether the same update improves every unrelated family without erasing behavior it already had.
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.plain_question: Can carefully checked synthetic lessons add general procedures that improve every unseen benchmark family while preserving a strong starting policy?
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.plain_answer: Not with either tested training geometry. Continuing from the strong policy raised fresh synthetic accuracy from 50% to 69% and beat the untrained base overall, but it scored 31% aggregate versus 45% for the strong starting policy and fell below base on three of ten families. A second mixture trained from base with broad replay also reached 69% local accuracy, yet only 85% of its answers were well formed and it hit the response limit four times, so its broad test stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.why_it_matters: The lessons clearly changed the model, but local learning was not a universal gain. The result narrows the search toward gentler joint training that keeps mature broad behavior active during every update, with a replay-only comparison to separate designed-skill transfer from ordinary continued practice.

qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.verdict_tag: Low-density doses failed the local gate
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.concept_primer: The previous designed mix may have been too dense, and its replay comparison also saw more tokens. This follow-up changes only a tiny number of rows while holding total training exposure exactly equal, so the later difference can be tied much more cleanly to curriculum content.
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.plain_question: Can a tiny dose of abstract skill lessons improve the replay-refreshed model without erasing any of its broad gains?
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.plain_answer: No. Neither 40 nor 80 abstract lessons passed the fresh local screen. The stronger 80-lesson stream matched the inherited anchor at 54% accuracy and parsed 62% of answers, but still hit the answer cap 10 times; broad evaluation stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.why_it_matters: Exact exposure removes the prior compute ambiguity: these gentle lesson doses were too small to install reliable concise execution. The next attempt must bridge the dose gap or target answer commitment directly, while keeping a replay-only comparison and fresh evaluation seeds.

qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.verdict_tag: 160 lessons nearly pass; benchmark stays sealed
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.concept_primer: Very small doses of abstract lessons did not install reliable execution, while a much larger earlier dose learned locally but displaced broad behavior. This experiment tests two doses between those endpoints while keeping training exposure exactly equal to replay-only practice.
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.plain_question: Can a middle-sized dose of abstract skill lessons cross the local installation threshold without sacrificing the replay practice that protects broad capability?
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.plain_answer: The 160-lesson stream improved fresh local accuracy from 17 to 19 of 26 cases, raised readable answers from 18 to 23, and cut answer-limit contacts from nine to three. It still missed the readability and length gates by one case each. Adding 80 more lessons erased the accuracy gain, so no model advanced to broad evaluation.
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.why_it_matters: The middle dose moved the right behaviors and came close, while the larger dose moved backward. That narrows the next intervention to concise answer commitment at the 160-lesson mix instead of adding more generic lessons or weakening the gate.

qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.verdict_tag: Designed mix loses; replay-only sets a stronger anchor
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.concept_primer: This experiment kept the model practicing broad tasks while adding a smaller set of abstract lessons. A replay-only comparison received the same number of updates, revealing whether any gain came from the designed lessons or simply from continuing broad practice.
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.plain_question: Can gentle joint practice add designed general skills without erasing the broad behavior the model already learned?
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.plain_answer: No at this dose. The designed mix passed its fresh synthetic screen but scored about 42% overall, below both the 44% mature policy and the 49% replay-only comparison. It also fell below base on one family. Replay-only was the useful surprise: it improved eight of ten families, tied the other two, and regressed none.
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.why_it_matters: Broad replay was not merely protecting old behavior; it was still adding capability. Future designed curricula must beat another replay continuation, not just the older starting policy. The next search should begin from this stronger replay-refreshed model, use far fewer designed rows, and match token exposure as well as update count.

qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.verdict_tag: Spreading practice evenly beats chasing mistakes
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.concept_primer: Picture sixty tutoring sessions to split across ten tricky exam topics. Piling them onto the topic you flunk worst leaves shakier topics with too few sessions, and those quietly collapse on test day; splitting the sessions evenly covers every topic.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_question: You have a fixed budget of extra training examples to fix a model's weak spots. Should you spread them evenly, or pile them onto its worst failures?
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_answer: Spread evenly. Giving each of ten problem types the same six extra correction examples let the model fully fix 98% of hard cases. Piling those same examples onto whichever types it failed most reached only 85%, and starved one type all the way to zero. Chasing the loudest failures robbed quieter ones that needed just a little help.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.why_it_matters: With a small budget of corrective training examples, spread it evenly across every skill you care about. Concentrating it on the worst-scoring failures can silently starve borderline skills until they collapse entirely.

qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.concept_primer: A training experiment needs room to improve. If the starting model already fixes every practice case, even a perfect trained model cannot prove that the new lessons helped.
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.plain_question: Can a tiny set of almost-correct failed-test revisions teach that exact distinction without erasing the coding loop already learned?
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.plain_answer: The test could not answer the question. Before the newly trained model was allowed to run, the starting model and the equal-size old-lesson control each fixed all 48 practice cases. The required gains were therefore impossible, so the run stopped before transfer or the external benchmark.
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.why_it_matters: Turning the old failure into a clear instruction plus an almost-correct program also made it easy. Future curricula must first prove that the starting model still fails the exact new practice distribution; failure forensics alone do not guarantee useful training headroom.

qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.verdict_tag: Reusing solved code rescues a few stuck problems
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.concept_primer: A programmer keeps a cookbook of solutions that provably worked on past problems. Stuck on a new one, they pull the closest-matching entry and tweak it to fit. Here a small coding model does exactly that with a library of its own verified solutions.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_question: When a small coding model keeps failing certain problems, does handing it a similar problem it already solved and letting it adapt that code help it finally pass?
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_answer: Yes, but modestly. Fetching the most similar already-solved problem and adapting its code cracked 3 of the 8 problems that repeated tries kept missing, lifting overall success from 67% to 79%. Matching by meaning clearly beat fetching random code (rescued none) and mislabeled code (rescued one). But only 2 wins truly needed smart matching, and most adaptations that passed the shown tests still failed the hidden ones.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.why_it_matters: When a small model plateaus, a searchable library of its own verified solutions plus adaptation is a cheap way to claw back a few failures. But pair it with a stronger checker: thin public tests wave wrong answers through.

qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.verdict_tag: Trying tiny fixes beats retraining the model
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.concept_primer: A small model's wrong program is usually one slip from correct: a swapped operation, a missing "not." Picture a spell-checker that only knows single-edit fixes. It generates every nearby variant, runs each against the example cases, and keeps whichever one now passes.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_question: When a small model writes a program that is almost right but fails, can automatically trying tiny edited versions and testing them fix more than just asking again?
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_answer: Yes, on the hardest unseen tasks. Testing small edits of the model's own near-miss program and keeping whichever passes the example cases raised fully-correct answers from 39% to 52%, while re-sampling the model and re-ranking its guesses gave nothing over its first try. But it stalled near 58%: some wrong programs need a rewrite, not a tweak.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.why_it_matters: For small-model code repair, wrap generation in a cheap edit-and-run loop before spending on more training. Accept a fix only when it passes every example the model missed, which halves the edits that break working code.

charts.chart_takeaway: Both edit-testing bars jump from about 39% to roughly 52%, nearly reaching the 58% best-possible bar: verified edits, not more sampling, carry the gain.

charts.chart_takeaway: Editing lifts one family from zero to nine solved, but two families stay flat at zero even for the best-possible edit: no such fix exists in the menu.

charts.chart_takeaway: Moving to strict acceptance drops improved tasks from 49 to 33 but halves damaged tasks from 8 to 4: the safer setting to deploy.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.verdict_tag: Fixing the memory crunch just revealed endless looping
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.concept_primer: A server holds each active prompt's growing memory in a fixed pool of fast storage — a parking garage with set spaces. Raising the context limit lets each car park taller but adds no spaces; admit more full prompts than fit, and cars get towed and re-parked, wasting work.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_question: If you give a small model a huge context and a huge thinking budget, is that enough to make it actually reason over long inputs?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_answer: No. Two hidden limits bit first. Configuring the server for 64 simultaneous long prompts demanded about 2.4 million tokens of fast memory from a pool holding roughly 1 million, forcing constant re-reading. Cutting to 19 prompts fixed that overflow — but the model then looped instead of finishing: 37 of 48 attempts fell into exact repeating cycles, and none stopped on their own.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.why_it_matters: Before serving long-context batches, don't just raise the length limit. Measure your live fast-memory pool and cap concurrent prompts so worst-case demand fits with room to spare. And separately, guard against models that loop forever instead of stopping.

key_numbers.label: Simultaneous long prompts allowed
key_numbers.sub: cut so worst-case memory fit the fixed cache

key_numbers.label: Worst-case memory demand
key_numbers.sub: against a cache holding about 1 million tokens

key_numbers.label: Runs that ended by looping
key_numbers.sub: exact repeated-token loops; the gate allows at most 1 in 4

key_numbers.label: Runs that stopped on their own
key_numbers.sub: every sample instead ran into the length limit

charts.chart_plain_title: Fitting fewer prompts brings memory demand under the cache limit
charts.chart_read: Two setups on the horizontal axis: the over-committed 64-prompt config and the fitted 19-prompt config. For each, one bar is worst-case memory demand and the other is memory actually available, in cache tokens; demand below available is safe, and lower demand is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At 64 prompts, demand towers to about 2.4 million against a roughly 1-million cache — guaranteed overflow and re-reading. At 19, demand drops just under the cache.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even with memory fixed, every stop-quality check failed
charts.chart_read: Three failure types along the horizontal axis: prompts that never resolved, prompts stuck repeating themselves, and prompts that maxed out the answer length. Each shows its observed share of the 48 samples beside its allowed ceiling; staying below the ceiling passes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every observed bar overshoots its ceiling — repeating loops hit about 77% against a 25% cap — so the model kept rambling instead of finishing, and nothing could be scored.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.verdict_tag: More thinking room only deepened the loops
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.concept_primer: Picture the model working through a problem on scratch paper before it answers. On these tasks it kept rewriting the same few lines instead of concluding. Hand it a bigger pad and it just fills more pages with the identical loop.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_question: If a small model runs out of room to think before answering, does giving it more room help it finish, or does it just keep looping?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_answer: No, it just loops harder. At both budgets, all 48 reasoning attempts ran out of thinking room and had to be force-stopped, none finishing on its own. Widening the budget from 49,152 to 61,440 tokens pushed the share stuck endlessly repeating the same lines from 79% up to 83%. More space feeds the loop rather than breaking it.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.why_it_matters: Before enlarging a small reasoning model's thinking budget, measure how often its runs end by repeating themselves. If they loop, more budget just burns compute; the real fix is anti-repetition decoding or training, not more room.
