key_numbers.label: Stuck endlessly repeating the same lines
key_numbers.sub: share of samples in exact loops as the budget rose from 49,152 to 61,440 tokens

key_numbers.label: Loop rate still allowed to pass
key_numbers.sub: registered ceiling; the actual rate ran past three times higher

key_numbers.label: Speed gain from the engineering fix
key_numbers.sub: pre-recording the exact GPU batch shapes vs letting the engine guess them

charts.chart_plain_title: Spelling out batch sizes covered every width the model used
charts.chart_read: Four inference setups along the bottom: two thinking budgets, each run once with the engine guessing fast-path batch sizes and once with them spelled out. Per setup, one bar is the width actually used, the other the largest width pre-built; matching them is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Guessing pre-built only up to widths 16 and 8, below the 19 and 15 actually used; spelling them out matched exactly, closing the slow uncovered gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: More thinking room raised the share of looping runs
charts.chart_read: Two thinking budgets side by side, each over 48 attempts. Three colored shares per budget: attempts stuck repeating a block, attempts hitting the limit for other reasons, and attempts running out of final-answer space. Lower is better on all three.
charts.chart_takeaway: Moving to the bigger budget nudged the looping share up from 79% to 83% — extra room deepened the loop instead of resolving it.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.verdict_tag: Perfect shortcut syntax masked wrong rewrites
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.concept_primer: A "shortcut" here is a named stand-in for a fixed recipe of basic steps — like writing "make a roux" instead of its five exact stirring instructions. It counts as right only if spelling it back out reproduces those exact steps, unchanged.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_question: If you hand a small model ready-made shortcuts and the exact step-by-step plan, can it simply re-write that plan using the right shortcuts?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_answer: No. Even handed the finished plan and asked only to re-express it — no problem to actually solve — the model got it exactly right just one time in four, short of the three-in-four bar. Every output looked flawless: valid syntax, a shortcut always called, nothing cut off. It named the right first shortcut, then invented extra ones that quietly expanded into different steps.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.why_it_matters: When a small model drives a tool or shortcut interface, never treat clean formatting and high shortcut-usage rates as passing. Verify that every call, expanded literally, reproduces the intended steps — score meaning, not surface.

key_numbers.label: Rewrites that matched the plan exactly
key_numbers.sub: the gate needed at least 3 of 4 to continue

key_numbers.label: Outputs that were cleanly formatted and used a shortcut
key_numbers.sub: with deliberate thinking turned off, every sample parsed and called a shortcut

key_numbers.label: Failed rewrites that kept the right shortcut but added wrong ones
key_numbers.sub: correct leading shortcut, then extra shortcuts that meant something else

key_numbers.label: How far the wrong rewrites over-expanded
key_numbers.sub: the plan had exactly five steps; every wrong answer inflated past that

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct rewrites never reached the bar needed to continue
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups, one per attempt: thinking budgeted to 768 tokens, then thinking turned off. The tall bar counts how many of four test cases were rewritten exactly right; the flat reference marks the three needed to pass. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both attempts fell short — two, then one, of four — so the run halted at the interface check before the real shortcut question was ever tested.

charts.chart_plain_title: Turning off thinking fixed formatting but not correctness
charts.chart_read: Two groups over sixteen samples each: thinking at 768 tokens, then thinking off. One bar counts cleanly parsed samples, the other counts samples cut off mid-answer. Thinking off pushed clean parses to all sixteen and cut-offs to zero. More parsed, fewer cut off is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Turning thinking off made every sample parse cleanly and eliminated all cut-offs, yet exact expansion still failed on thirteen of sixteen — clean output was not correct output.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.verdict_tag: More thinking room only bought longer loops
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.concept_primer: Same model, two jobs on identical coding puzzles. Copy out a blueprint it is handed: quick, done. Or reverse-engineer that blueprint from only a photo of the finished part: it cannot, so it circles the same guess forever. A bigger budget is a longer track, not a finish line.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_question: If a small model keeps rambling and never finishes a hard coding puzzle, can you fix it just by handing it far more room to think?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_answer: No. The model was not short on thinking space, it was stuck repeating itself, and more space only bought longer loops. Handed the blueprint, all 16 tasks finished cleanly. Asked to invent the program from examples alone, every attempt ran off the end of its allowance at every budget, and pure repetition climbed from 9 percent of traces to 83 percent as the room grew.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.why_it_matters: Calibrate a thinking budget on the exact task you will ship, not a look-alike: copying a plan and inventing one behave nothing alike. When a model loops, add loop detection and hard stops rather than buying more tokens.

key_numbers.label: Copy-the-plan tasks solved cleanly
key_numbers.sub: every supplied-plan record finished, above the 12 needed to pass

key_numbers.label: Invent-from-examples tries that never stopped
key_numbers.sub: 131 of 144 samples ran off the end at the ~16,000-token budget

key_numbers.label: Attempts stuck repeating the same tokens
key_numbers.sub: as thinking room grew from about 16,000 to 61,000 tokens

key_numbers.label: Thinking room scaled up
key_numbers.sub: roughly quadrupled, yet every attempt still hit the ceiling

charts.chart_plain_title: How invent-the-program attempts failed as thinking room grew
charts.chart_read: The bottom groups three thinking budgets (about 16k, 32k, 49k tokens) on the invent-from-examples task. Within each group: share that never resolved, share stuck in exact repeating loops, share whose answer hit its length cap. Lower is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: As the budget grows, the never-resolved share shrinks while the repeating-loop share balloons: the failure just changes shape, and no budget ever lets attempts finish.

charts.chart_plain_title: Handed the plan, the model cleared every task
charts.chart_read: One group, the supplied-plan check. The taller bar counts records that produced at least one exactly correct rewrite using the library (16 of 16); the shorter reference bar marks the 12 needed to pass. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All 16 records cleared, above the 12 needed: the model and its interface work fine when handed the plan, isolating failure to inventing the program itself.

qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.verdict_tag: A tiny slice of text broke unrelated behavior
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.concept_primer: A coding agent loops: read the code, edit, run the tests, commit. Trained only on flawless runs, it never meets a failed test — like a driver who practiced on empty roads and freezes at the first skid. So it replays its own fixes from real broken states.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_question: If you teach a small coding agent to recover by replaying moments it once fixed, does it learn to recover — and what does that cost?
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_answer: Yes — replaying real failures taught recovery, lifting success from about half of cases to more than nine in ten. But the best version also added a little "explain your fix" text, and that supposedly tiny slice actually carried most of the training force, disturbing unrelated knowledge past a safety limit. The run stopped before it could even test whether recovery transfers to new code.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.why_it_matters: Don't gauge a training slice's impact by its share of the words. A handful of tokens the model finds unnatural can dominate the update and quietly corrupt unrelated skills. Measure the actual size of the update, not the word count.

key_numbers.label: Recovery success, untrained vs best recipe
key_numbers.sub: share of 60 controlled failure cases the agent fixed

key_numbers.label: The "tiny" explanation dose
key_numbers.sub: nominally 5% plan text, yet its update magnitude ran 42.1 versus 1.8 for the no-explanation arm — far more than 5% of the real training pressure

key_numbers.label: Damage to unrelated knowledge
key_numbers.sub: unrelated-output drift of 30% versus the 15% ceiling — this stopped the run

key_numbers.label: New code families tested for transfer
key_numbers.sub: run halted at the safety gate before any unseen family was touched

charts.chart_plain_title: Replaying real failures lifts recovery from half to nine in ten
charts.chart_read: Four bars, left to right: the untrained agent, one trained only on clean successful runs, one trained on real failure moments, and one that also explains its fix. Bar height is the share of 60 controlled recovery cases solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each step up adds recovery skill, and the explain-your-fix bar is highest at more than nine in ten — but this is trained-family practice, not transfer to new code.

charts.chart_plain_title: The winning recipe breaks the safety limit for unrelated knowledge
charts.chart_read: Three bars show how much each trained version disturbs the model's unrelated outputs; a flat reference line marks the allowed ceiling. Both action-only recipes sit well under it; the version that adds explanation text spikes far above. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding a nominally small amount of explanation text roughly triples the disturbance and clears the safety line, so the run halted before any transfer test ran.

qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.verdict_tag: Confidence can rank answers but can't clean training
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.concept_primer: This small model teaches itself: it writes many candidate programs, keeps the ones it is surest are right, and retrains on those. Like a cook who tastes to serve tonight's best plate (fine), but writes the cookbook by taste alone — subtly-wrong recipes slip in and the apprentice learns the flaws.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_question: If a small model grades its own generated code by how confident it feels instead of actually running it, can it still build a training set and teach itself to improve?
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_answer: No. When training on answers checked by actually running the code lifted single-shot accuracy from 8% to 24%, confidence-filtered data — fifteen times purer than a random grab of the model's own outputs — landed right on the no-filter floor at 12%, adding nothing. Self-training tolerates almost no wrong examples, and the confident mistakes a filter lets through are plausible near-misses that teach the worst habits.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.why_it_matters: In a self-training loop, keep a real code-runner cleaning the training data — confidence leaks plausible-but-wrong examples that poison learning at small data sizes. Use confidence only to rank candidates, filtering by rank within difficulty tiers, never by a fixed score cutoff.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from execution-checked training data
key_numbers.sub: depth-2 single-shot, thinking mode — the only filter that moved the needle

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from confidence-filtered data
key_numbers.sub: identical to an unfiltered random sample — a statistically insignificant nudge, not a real gain

key_numbers.label: Purity edge that bought nothing
key_numbers.sub: about 15x cleaner than random, yet trained the model no better

key_numbers.label: Self-grading right from wrong on one task
key_numbers.sub: blind until the model was allowed to think before scoring, which fixed it

charts.chart_plain_title: Only execution-checked training data improved the model; confidence-picked matched no filter
charts.chart_read: One bar per training filter, showing depth-2 single-shot accuracy in thinking mode after fine-tuning on 70 self-generated solutions drawn from one shared pool: no training, execution-verified (all correct), confidence-picked (43% correct), and unfiltered random (3% correct). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the fully-verified bar rises, 8% to 24%; the confidence-picked bar sits at 12%, dead level with random despite being fifteen times cleaner — near-purity bought nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: The one-token self-score is blind unless the model thinks first
charts.chart_read: Two judging methods side by side: an instant one-token self-score versus one given room to reason first. Dark bars measure how reliably it separates right from wrong answers on the SAME task (higher is better); light bars are a flattering score inflated by mixing easy and hard tasks together.
charts.chart_takeaway: Instant scoring sits at a coin flip within a task; thinking first lifts it to reliable. The flattering pooled bar hides this failure — always check the within-task bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does self-training break the judge? Ranking holds, scores creep up
charts.chart_read: Three models each grade the identical set of 777 candidates: untrained, trained on verified data, and trained on its OWN approvals. Dark bars: how well it separates right from wrong answers (higher is better, near-flat across all three). Light bars: confidence wrongly given to incorrect answers (lower is better, creeping up after training).
charts.chart_takeaway: The judge's ranking survives even after training on its own approvals, but its confidence on wrong answers drifts upward — so filter by rank each round, never by a fixed score cutoff.

charts.chart_plain_title: Side effect: instant-answer variety collapses onto the trained answer-family
charts.chart_read: Bars show depth-1 answer variety in instant no-think mode after each fine-tune — the share of tasks where at least one of 16 tries is correct (higher is better). Left to right: base, execution-verified, confidence-stratified, confidence-global, and unfiltered random.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most trained arms roughly halve base variety (72% down to 32-40%) as correct answers get crammed onto one banked program family; the light-dose stratified arm (76%) is spared, and reasoning mode stays untouched.

qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.verdict_tag: Learning from your own wins narrows your range
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.concept_primer: Picture a coder who studies only their own past solutions that worked. They grow fluent at familiar patterns but stop exploring alternatives, so their several fresh attempts start to look alike. On a new problem where the usual move fails, they have no backup.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_question: If a small coding model retrains only on the solutions it wrote that actually passed the tests, does it get better at solving brand-new problems it has not seen?
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_answer: No. After a four-billion-parameter model retrained on its own test-passing code, its success on unseen problems barely moved, going from about 65% to about 65% (a hair lower). Retraining made its several attempts look more alike, shrinking variety. The simplest lever won instead: just generating more attempts at solving time reached about 69%, beating every retraining approach.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.why_it_matters: For small coding models, spend compute on generating more diverse attempts and filtering them by actually running the code, not on fine-tuning the model on its own successes, which quietly collapses the variety that makes multiple tries pay off.

key_numbers.label: Success on unseen problems, before vs after retraining
key_numbers.sub: share where at least one of several attempts passes the hidden tests; a hair lower after retraining

key_numbers.label: Best lever was simply generating more attempts
key_numbers.sub: sampling more candidate solutions at solving time, for matched compute

key_numbers.label: Where retraining did nudge up: practiced problems
key_numbers.sub: but the small gain did not carry over to new problems

key_numbers.label: Candidate solutions offered per problem
key_numbers.sub: fewer after retraining on its own wins, so the pool of attempts shrank

charts.chart_plain_title: New-problem success: retraining approaches versus just sampling more
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the share of unseen problems where at least one of several attempts passes the hidden tests, so taller is better. Bars are the frozen model, retrain-on-verified-wins, retrain-on-unfiltered-code, retrain-on-reference-answers, and simply sampling more attempts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The sample-more bar is the tallest, standing above every retraining bar, so extra attempts beat teaching the model on its own work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Retraining helps on practiced problems but not on new ones
charts.chart_read: Two bars per group, frozen model versus retrained model, across three sets: practiced problems, unseen problems, and a different problem set; taller is better. Height is the share solved by at least one attempt.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained bar edges up only on practiced problems and sits slightly lower on both unseen sets, so the small gain does not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Retraining leaves the model offering fewer candidate attempts
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the average number of candidate solutions the model produces per unseen problem, and more candidates give more chances to land a working one. Rightmost is the sample-more approach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two self-training bars sit below the frozen model's, so retraining on its own code shrinks the pool of attempts, while sampling more lifts it far higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: No answer-picking rule rescues the retrained model
charts.chart_read: Two bars per group, frozen versus retrained model, with one group per rule for picking the single answer to submit; height is final accuracy on unseen problems, so taller is better. Rules range from first-that-runs to a best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every group the frozen model's bar is at least as tall, so switching the answer-picking rule cannot rescue the retrained model.

charts.chart_plain_title: A tiny 20-problem preview looked promising, then reversed
charts.chart_read: Two bars per group, frozen versus retrained model, comparing a small 20-problem preview against the full 150-problem test; height is the share solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained bar leads on the small preview but falls slightly behind on the full test, showing the early promise was just noise.

qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.verdict_tag: A cheap test makes slow self-checking pointless
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.concept_primer: Picking the best of eight code drafts is like hiring: run each past one cheap screening test, and the handful that survive are so close in quality that a quick gut-feel judgment ranks them as well as slow, careful deliberation.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_question: When a small model writes eight candidate programs and you must pick one to ship, is it worth having it slowly reason through which one is correct?
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_answer: No. When you can run even a single example test on each candidate, that filter alone lifts the share of shipped programs that fully work from 77% to 85%. Adding a free, instant self-confidence rating reaches 87% — matching a careful step-by-step self-check that costs five times the compute. Slow deliberation only earns its keep when no test can be run.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.why_it_matters: When your task ships an example test, spend compute running it first, then break ties among survivors with the model's free instant confidence score. Reserve slow step-by-step self-verification for settings where you cannot execute the code at all.

key_numbers.label: Random pick vs best selector
key_numbers.sub: share of chosen programs that pass every hidden test

key_numbers.label: Cost of slow self-checking
key_numbers.sub: for zero extra accuracy once a test exists

key_numbers.label: Programs that pass the example but still fail
key_numbers.sub: 6.6% of test-passers fail the hidden tests

key_numbers.label: Gap to a perfect picker closed
key_numbers.sub: of the distance from a random pick to a flawless one

charts.chart_plain_title: Which selector picks a working solution most often?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method for picking one of eight programs; height is the share of picks that pass every hidden test (taller is better), running from random pick at 77% up to a non-deployable perfect picker at 89%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The test-plus-free-confidence bar reaches 87%, level with the five-times-costlier deliberate check and just below the 89% perfect ceiling; cheap methods capture almost all the gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute cost per task for each picking method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is estimated compute per task for one method; shorter is cheaper. The two deliberate self-check bars tower near 4,960 while the test and quick-confidence options sit down at 960.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate check costs about five times the cheap combo yet, from the accuracy chart, buys no extra accuracy — the visual signature of wasted spend.

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking the survivors: quick versus deliberate self-check
charts.chart_read: Two bars show how reliably each self-check sorts a truly-correct survivor above a secretly-broken one; higher is better, and a coin flip would score 50%. Deliberate scores 76%, quick 70%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free quick check trails the costly one only slightly, because after the test filters candidates few broken survivors remain to sort apart.

qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.verdict_tag: Drilling two-step problems never unlocks three-step ones
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.concept_primer: Self-training here means fine-tuning a model only on problems it already solved by itself. Picture a climber drilling every hold she can reach: practice makes those moves rock-solid, but she never touches the higher hold, so there is nothing to learn from and her reach never extends.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_question: If you fine-tune a small model on the multi-step problems it can already solve, does it gain the ability to solve problems needing one more step?
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_answer: No. Fine-tuning the model on the two-step solutions it could already produce tripled its two-step success on fresh tasks, from 12% to 36%. But its three-step success stayed at exactly zero, unchanged from before: both models solved none of the three-step tasks within sixteen tries. Becoming fluent at one difficulty tier taught it nothing about the tier just above.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.why_it_matters: Self-training on a model's own correct outputs sharpens skills it already demonstrates; it cannot invent a harder skill it never produces. To reach a new difficulty tier, first generate correct examples at that tier with search or tools, then fine-tune on those.

key_numbers.label: Two-step tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: fresh tasks solved within sixteen tries (tripled)

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the unlock that never happened

key_numbers.label: Four-step tasks solved, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: unchanged — no accidental leap either

key_numbers.label: Training examples used
key_numbers.sub: all one- or two-step problems the model already solved

charts.chart_plain_title: Two-step success triples after training; three-step stays at zero
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the untrained model against the self-trained model at three difficulties — tasks needing two, three, or four chained steps. Bar height is the share of fresh tasks solved within sixteen tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the two-step bar grows, from 12% to 36%. The three- and four-step bars are identical between models, three-step pinned flat at zero — training never reached the next rung.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts lift two-step solving but never three-step
charts.chart_read: Lines track the cumulative share of tasks solved as attempts rise from one to sixteen. Two flat lines sit at zero: three-step tasks for both the untrained and self-trained models. One rising line is two-step for the self-trained model. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-step line climbs steadily to 36% while both three-step lines stay glued to zero across all sixteen tries — extra sampling never surfaces a single three-step solution.

qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.verdict_tag: Learning to judge its own code barely helped
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.concept_primer: The model writes a tiny program to solve each puzzle, then runs every draft in a sandbox to watch what it computes. A second model grades those drafts by their consequences, trying to pick the one whose output actually answers the puzzle.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_question: If a model can run each of its draft programs and watch what they compute, can it learn to pick the correct one and get better at writing them?
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_answer: Barely. Learning to grade drafts by their run results nudged picking accuracy only from about 10% to 11%, far short of the 37% reachable by always choosing the best available draft. It reliably picked programs that ran, just not ones that were correct. Yet feeding even those imperfect picks back still lifted directly-written program accuracy from 18% to 23%.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.why_it_matters: When a small model self-improves by running its own candidate answers, the bottleneck isn't generating candidates or reusing good ones — it's the judge. Invest in teaching it to compare rival candidates, not to polish its own writing.

key_numbers.label: Best it could pick if flawless
key_numbers.sub: how often a correct draft was among the options

key_numbers.label: Writing correct programs directly
key_numbers.sub: gain from reusing its imperfect picks

key_numbers.label: Ceiling when shown the answers
key_numbers.sub: same task with full correct-answer supervision

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing correct programs directly, across training methods
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a family of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved by writing one program with no retries. The four bars are the frozen starting model, two ways of reusing its own drafts, and a model simply shown the correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bars hover right around the starting model — sometimes a touch above, often a touch below — while the shown-answers bar towers far above, so self-teaching helps only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles when allowed to try several drafts
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a family of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved when the model may generate and test several drafts and keep a working one. Bars compare the starting model against two ways of reusing its own drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reusing drafts helps most here — the hardest puzzle family rises from 35% to 52% solved — even though the drafts were picked imperfectly.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the chosen program is actually correct
charts.chart_read: Bars group practice puzzles and fresh ones; taller means the chosen draft is correct more often. Three bars per group: the model's raw hunch, its learned grader, and the score if it always grabbed its best available draft.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned grader barely differs from the raw hunch while the best-possible bar sits far higher, showing weak judgment is the real bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Being pickier about which drafts to reuse
charts.chart_read: Two puzzle families; taller bars mean more solved by direct writing afterward. Bars compare reusing every draft against keeping only the higher-scoring drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Keeping only higher-scoring drafts raises fresh-puzzle accuracy, showing the quality of the reused drafts matters more than their quantity.

charts.chart_plain_title: Direct solving per puzzle family, self-teaching vs shown answers
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a puzzle family; taller bars mean more solved by writing directly. Four bars: the starting model, two self-teaching methods, and a model shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching bars edge only a few points above the start, dwarfed by the shown-answers bars reaching 52 to 82%.

qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.verdict_tag: Right on each row, not one reliable rule
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.concept_primer: Spreadsheets can watch you fill a few cells and infer one rule for the whole column. Here, thousands of candidate rules fit the shown examples, and a small model's yes/no votes on invented rows had to pick the single rule that holds on every future row.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_question: A model can usually reformat one text example correctly. Can its yes/no votes be distilled into a single fixed rule that reliably handles every future row?
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_answer: No. Using the model's votes to choose a rule worked on 25% of tasks — barely above the 22.5% you get from scrambled, meaningless votes, and it never beat the best rule the candidate pool could offer. The model answered individual unseen rows correctly about 80% of the time, yet almost never turned that skill into one dependable rule.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.why_it_matters: A model being right row by row does not mean you can compile it into one reusable rule. If you need a rule that holds on every input, test whole-task consistency, not sampled rows — the per-row skill collapses.

charts.chart_takeaway: The model-vote bar sits level with the scrambled-vote bar and the best-available ceiling, all far below the tall direct-answering bar.

charts.chart_read: Three bars on the same forty tasks; height is exact-match rate, taller is better. Left: model answering single rows; middle: model getting every row of a task right; right: one picked rule getting every row right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall single-row bar collapses when every row must match, and the picked-rule bar collapses furthest, showing consistency is the weak point.

charts.chart_read: Three bars, height is share of tasks, taller is better. Left: tasks where some rule fits the shown examples; middle: tasks where a fitting rule also handles unseen rows; right: picking the shortest fitting rule.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task has a rule fitting the examples, yet only about a third of those rules generalize, so fitting examples is easy but generalizing is not.

charts.chart_read: Task kinds along the bottom; paired bars show the best rule available versus the plain shortest-rule pick; taller is better. The two largest kinds, date-and-time and numbers, appear first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The date-and-time and number bars are lowest despite being the biggest groups, dragging overall rule coverage down below simpler kinds.

charts.chart_plain_title: Results hold steady across three run sizes
charts.chart_read: Three run sizes along the bottom; grouped bars show each method's share of tasks solved on unseen rows, taller is better. Bars include model votes, scrambled votes, the best rule available, shortest-rule picking, and direct answering.
charts.chart_takeaway: Across all three sizes the model-vote bars stay pinned near the scrambled-vote and best-available bars, while direct answering stays far higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible rule coverage depends on the task sample
charts.chart_read: Three sample sizes along the bottom; paired bars show the best rule available versus the plain shortest-rule pick; taller is better. Only the largest sample gives a stable reading.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar swings widely between small samples, so only the full-size run gives a trustworthy estimate of how far rules can reach.

qwen_active_example_acquisition.verdict_tag: One well-chosen example helps; random extras backfire
qwen_active_example_acquisition.concept_primer: A model reformatting messy data learns a rule from four worked examples and may request more. The smart version asks only for the one example it keeps second-guessing, where its answer flips depending on how the question is phrased, instead of grabbing three at random.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_question: If a small model can request extra worked examples before answering, does letting it pick the one it is most unsure about beat just handing it random ones?
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_answer: Barely, and the real lesson is the downside. Letting the model request the single example it was most unsure about lifted fully-solved tasks from 20 to 21 out of 30. But three random extras dropped it to 19, and feeding it examples with scrambled answers sank it to 18. Choosing well matters far more than adding more.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.why_it_matters: When steering a small model with examples, spend effort finding the one case it is most unsure about and verify its answer is correct. Padding prompts with random extras can quietly lower accuracy, especially on date- and number-formatting tasks.

key_numbers.label: Three random extra examples
key_numbers.sub: fewer solved than adding nothing

key_numbers.label: Examples with scrambled answers
