key_numbers.label: Best any tested pick could reach
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if the ideal extra example were always chosen

charts.chart_plain_title: How each way of picking extra examples affects tasks solved
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of unseen test tasks fully solved, one bar per strategy for choosing extra examples; taller is better. Leftmost adds only the four given examples; rightmost is the best pick that was tried.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two actively-chosen bars rise above the no-extras baseline while random and scrambled-answer bars fall below it, so how you pick beats how many you add.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as more extra examples are added, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Each line is a strategy for choosing extra examples; the horizontal axis is how many were added (none, one, or three), the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved; up is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the active-choice line stays above its no-extras starting point at both amounts, while the random and diversity lines dip below, so extras help only when chosen well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Date-and-time tasks: the largest hard group
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the 13 date-and-time tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through one and three actively chosen, then random and scrambled-answer extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One actively chosen example lifts this hard group the most, while random or scrambled-answer extras push it down to the lowest bars.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting every row right versus getting single rows right
charts.chart_read: Two bars per strategy: the lighter bar is the share of individual test rows answered correctly, the darker bar the share of tasks where every row is right; the darker bar is stricter, and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Whole-task bars always trail single-row bars, meaning one wrong row sinks a task, and active choosing narrows that gap the most.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types still have room to improve
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: accuracy with the four given examples versus the best pick of extras that was tried; each bar is the share of tasks fully solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Six of nine task types are already fully solved, so extras can only move the date-time and number groups, and one type stays stuck at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number-formatting tasks: smart picks help, scrambled answers hurt
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the six number-formatting tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through random, ordered, diverse, and actively chosen, then scrambled-answer and best-pick extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Active and diverse picks reach the best-achieved level, while examples with scrambled answers drag accuracy below the no-extras start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do different self-check styles agree on which example to add?
charts.chart_read: Three bars per hard task, one for each style of self-check the model uses to gauge its own uncertainty; each bar is the share of that task's rows solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three self-check styles mostly agree, but on the hardest tasks one style swings the result by a noticeable chunk in either direction.

qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.verdict_tag: Answering rows together never helped, sometimes hurt
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.concept_primer: Picture reformatting one column of a spreadsheet. You can hand the model each cell alone, or show it every cell at once, hoping it locks onto a single rule and applies it uniformly. Shared context should breed consistency, but one slip now spoils the whole column.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_question: If a model reformats a batch of similar entries all together instead of one by one, does it get the whole set right more often?
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_answer: No. Answering every entry in one combined pass got the whole task right 45 percent of the time, versus 50 percent when each entry was handled alone, a five-point loss. Bundling never rescued a task the solo approach had failed, and it spoiled two tasks that had been fully solved. Asking the model to reason first or format strictly did not close the gap.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.why_it_matters: To cut calls by packing similar rows into one generation, know the tax: one wrong element fails the whole batch, and shared context can corrupt rows the model would have nailed alone. Keep independent rows independent.

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully right, one entry at a time
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks with every entry correct, handled solo

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully right, all entries together
key_numbers.sub: best bundled approach, a five-point drop

key_numbers.label: Failed tasks the bundling rescued
key_numbers.sub: never turned a loss into a win

key_numbers.label: Solved tasks the bundling broke
key_numbers.sub: correct handled solo, wrong once bundled

charts.chart_plain_title: Bundling rows six ways versus handling them one at a time
charts.chart_read: Six methods run along the bottom, from one-at-a-time up through ever-larger bundles to all-at-once with order shuffled or a rule hint; taller is better. Dark bars show how often the whole task was fully correct, light bars how often a single entry was correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: The leftmost one-at-a-time bar is tallest on whole-task correctness, so no bundling method beats handling entries separately, though bigger bundles beat smaller ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stricter bundling instructions still fall short
charts.chart_read: Four methods along the bottom, taller is better: handling entries one at a time versus three ways of bundling them with tougher instructions. Dark bars are whole-task correctness; light bars are single-entry correctness.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bundled bar sits at or below the one-at-a-time bar on whole-task correctness, so tighter instructions never close the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: For each task, did bundling help, tie, or hurt?
charts.chart_read: Three bars count, out of forty tasks, how many bundling improved, left unchanged, or worsened versus one at a time. A tall wins bar would be good; a tall loses bar is bad.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wins bar is empty, thirty-eight tasks tie, and two tasks lose, so bundling only ever hurt or made no difference.

qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.verdict_tag: Right answers get written, but rarely chosen
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.concept_primer: For each word problem the model drafts a couple dozen tiny programs, and usually one computes the right answer. With no answer key it must guess which draft to trust — and it guesses far worse than a version allowed to peek at the answer.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_question: Can a small helper that never sees the answer learn to pick the correct program out of the many a language model drafts for a word problem?
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_answer: Barely. On fresh problems the model drafts a correct program among its candidates 81% of the time but ranks it first only 67% of the time. A learned scorer that never sees the answer nudged that to just 70% — while a control that peeks at the answer reached 82%. The wall is choosing, not writing.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.why_it_matters: When a small model produces correct outputs but ranks them poorly, don't scale the generator. Train a judge that scores whole finished programs by actually running them, so selection catches up to what the model can already write.

key_numbers.label: Correct program present vs actually picked first
key_numbers.sub: a right program is in the candidate pile far more often than it gets chosen (fresh problems)

key_numbers.label: Best the no-answer scorer reached
key_numbers.sub: learned value guidance barely beats plain search on fresh problems

key_numbers.label: Control that peeks at the answer
key_numbers.sub: upper bound that verifies candidates against the known answer

key_numbers.label: Textbook-matching steps vs still-salvageable steps
key_numbers.sub: only about 1 in 23 partial steps follows the canonical program, yet most can still reach the right answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned scorer versus peeking at the answer, two task sets
charts.chart_read: Bars show task accuracy (taller is better) for four ways of picking the final program — top guess, log-probability beam, best learned scorer, and a control that checks candidates against the answer — grouped as everyday paired tasks and harder multi-step tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-scorer bar sits just above plain search, but the answer-checking bar towers far above both, so the learned scorer captures little of the available gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually written but not picked
charts.chart_read: For four task sets, the short bar is how often the single top pick is right and the tall bar is how often any drafted candidate was right; taller is better, and the gap between them is missed opportunity.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every tall bar sits 14 to 20 points above its short partner, meaning a correct program was drafted far more often than it was actually chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much to trust the learned score before it backfires
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy (higher is better) on fresh paired tasks as the learned score is given more weight in the final choice, for two score types; the left is light weight, the right edge is heavy weight.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines peak at light weight then dip at the far right, showing that leaning too hard on the learned score makes choices worse, not better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How cleanly each kind of step-label can be told apart
charts.chart_read: Bars show how reliably a learned score separates good partial steps from bad ones (taller is better) for four training signals, from a strict exact-match label to richer graded scores.
charts.chart_takeaway: The exact-match bar is tall and clean while the graded-score bars are much shorter, so nuance is exactly what the lightweight scorer struggles to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples let the program-writer actually learn
charts.chart_read: Lines show how accurately the model turns prompts into runnable programs (higher is better) as training proceeds, comparing a small-data pilot against the main run that used four times the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pilot line stays flat near the floor while the main-run line climbs to about 72%, showing data volume, not tuning, unlocked the program-writer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exactly rebuilding the intended program stays low everywhere
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method reproduces the exact intended program (taller is better) across four task sets, comparing plain search, the best learned scorer, and the answer-checking version.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even the answer-checking bars only reach the mid-fifties, and exact recovery drops to about a third on the hardest set, so rebuilding the canonical program is hard even with answer checking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most steps aren't the textbook step, yet still reach the answer
charts.chart_read: For three data-collection runs, the short bar is the share of partial steps matching the single textbook program and the tall bar is the share that can still reach a correct answer; taller means more workable steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recoverable bar towers over the textbook bar in every run — about 72% versus 4% in the main run — so textbook-only supervision misses most good steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training longer doesn't sharpen the learned scores
charts.chart_read: Lines track how reliably each of four learned scores separates good steps from bad (higher is better) across eight training rounds; the four lines sit at clearly different heights.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line is essentially flat from the first round onward, so each score's quality is capped by its design, not by how long it trains.

qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.verdict_tag: It couldn't tell working fixes from broken ones
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.concept_primer: Picture a program that might have a bug, plus 64 suggested rewrites, each with a receipt showing exactly what it did when run. The test: can a small model read those receipts and reliably pick the rewrite that actually works?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_question: If you show a small model each candidate fix for a program plus what it actually did when run, can it reliably pick the one that works?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_answer: No. Doing nothing already solved about 44% of tasks, and always choosing a correct candidate could reach 90%. Yet no trained picker captured any of that headroom; the best merely tied doing nothing. Left unchecked, the model rewrote almost every program, including ones already correct, breaking up to 97% and crashing accuracy to about 1%.
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.why_it_matters: A model reading candidate code and its run trace still can't score which fix is correct. Before wiring a model fix-picker into a repair loop, gate it hard: unchecked, it breaks far more code than it repairs.

key_numbers.label: Doing nothing vs. an ideal picker
key_numbers.sub: tasks solved with no repair versus always choosing a correct candidate; a realistic shortlist tops out at 75%

key_numbers.label: Repair headroom the trained pickers captured
key_numbers.sub: the reachable improvement the learned selectors recovered — the best only tied doing nothing

key_numbers.label: Correct programs wrecked when unchecked
key_numbers.sub: already-correct programs the unrestrained model overwrote with wrong fixes

key_numbers.label: Accuracy when the model edited freely
key_numbers.sub: collapse once the model was allowed to swap in its chosen fixes

charts.chart_plain_title: No trained picker beats leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Each bar is accuracy (taller is better) for one way of choosing a fix: doing nothing, the best trained picker, two free-editing pickers, then two cheat pickers that already know the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best trained picker sits exactly level with doing nothing, the free-editing pickers collapse to the floor, and only the cheat pickers rise far above.

charts.chart_plain_title: A safety fallback stops the damage but adds no gains
charts.chart_read: For each picker variant, one bar is accuracy (taller better) and one is how often it broke a working program (shorter better). Each pair shows a free-editing version beside a fallback version that keeps the original when unsure.
charts.chart_takeaway: Free-editing versions show near-maxed breakage bars; the fallback versions zero out damage but their accuracy only climbs back to the do-nothing level, never past it.

charts.chart_plain_title: A working fix sits in the pile on every test set
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: doing nothing, a cheat picker limited to the shortlist, and a cheat picker over the full pile. Taller cheat bars mean more available improvement. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On every test set the cheat-picker bars tower over doing-nothing, proving correct fixes are present and the problem is choosing them, not lacking them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Free-editing pickers rewrite almost every program
charts.chart_read: Per approach, one bar is how often it changed the original program and one is how often that change broke a working one. Both lower is safer. Cheat pickers and fallback variants sit low; free-editing variants sit high.
charts.chart_takeaway: Free-editing bars nearly max out on both measures — they touch almost everything and wreck most working programs — unlike the cheat pickers, which edit only where a fix helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Genuine fix-readers end up no better than scrambled controls
charts.chart_read: Bars show each training approach's final usefulness score, one bar per repeat run; higher is better. Simple baselines sit high; the real fix-reading models sit low, near controls fed scrambled labels or corrupted run traces.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real fix-reading bars land down near the deliberately-broken control bars on both runs, showing they learned nothing the run traces should have provided.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the untouched program is already right swings by data batch
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the untouched program is already correct, split by test set. Each colored series is one batch of source problems; higher just means fewer things left to fix.
charts.chart_takeaway: One batch sits near the top on every test set while another drops to zero, so the room for repair concentrates in particular batches of problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: On unfamiliar problems, the fallback is all that prevents collapse
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on a batch of problems the model never trained on; taller is better. They span doing nothing, a cheat picker, and free-editing versus fallback versions of each trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: With doing-nothing already near the top, the free-editing bars crash to the floor while the fallback bars climb back near do-nothing but never pass it.

qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Finding the fix is easy; picking it isn't
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.concept_primer: A word problem becomes a 24-step arithmetic recipe, and the machine often fumbles a step. A search spins off about 1,300 near-miss versions, each showing its own worked-out running totals. A checker reads only those totals — never the answer — and picks the version that hangs together best.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_question: When a small model garbles a short arithmetic program, can another model pick the right fix from many candidates without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Yes. A small checker that reads each candidate's worked-out steps — never the true answer — lifted correctly-running programs from 30% to 54%, and to 56% when it cross-checks two wordings of one task. The twist: a picker handed the answer key hits 88%, so the right fix is usually in the pile; the checker just fails to spot it. Selection, not search, is the wall.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: When repairing shaky small-model outputs, spend on the selector, not just on generating more candidates — the correct fix usually already exists. First measure your answer-key ceiling: a big gap means selection, not coverage, is where to invest.

key_numbers.label: Correct-running programs, before and after the checker
key_numbers.sub: fresh paired programs, base compiler vs the trace-reading checker

key_numbers.label: If it could see the answer key
key_numbers.sub: ceiling — how often a correct fix is present in the candidate pile

key_numbers.label: With a two-wording cross-check
key_numbers.sub: reranking two phrasings of the same task for agreement

key_numbers.label: Candidates weighed per program
key_numbers.sub: local repair options enumerated for each 24-step prompt

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs that run right: no repair, checker's pick, answer-key ceiling
charts.chart_read: Three fresh test sets along the bottom (standard wording, reworded, paired wordings). Each has three bars: no-repair base (lowest), the trace-reading checker's pick (middle), and an answer-key picker (highest). Taller means more programs run exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The checker's bar roughly doubles the base on every set yet sits far below the answer-key bar — real gains, but most of the reachable headroom stays unclaimed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Paired wordings: where per-guess judging helps and where it hurts
charts.chart_read: Four measures along the bottom (runs exactly right, matching step prefix, both wordings correct, agreement between the two wordings), each with four bars: base, single-guess checker, pair-aware reranker, and answer-key ceiling. Higher is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-guess checker lifts most bars but drops wording-agreement below base (71% to 58%); reranking the pair jointly restores agreement to 87% and lifts both-correct to 52%.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training programs, higher repair accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two rising lines — checker accuracy on the fresh standard set and the fresh paired set — plotted against the number of training programs (128, then 192, then 512 across the bottom). Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steadily, from about 41% at 128 programs to 50-54% at 512, so accuracy was still rising with more training data — not yet plateaued.

qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.verdict_tag: Light step-by-step coaching helps; a heavy dose backfires
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.concept_primer: The model reads a recipe of add, subtract, and multiply steps, each result wrapping around at 97, then runs the whole 24-step chain. Coaching it during practice to keep every running total correct is a gentle reminder that helps it not lose its place.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_question: When you train a small model to run long chains of arithmetic, does making it keep each running total right help it get the final answer right?
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. A light, steady dose of show-your-work coaching, kept on through the hardest problems, lifted correct answers on 24-step chains from 25% to 33% and nearly doubled agreement between two wordings of the same problem, from 41% to 71%. But heavy coaching switched off for the long chains backfired at 19%, worse than no coaching at all.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.why_it_matters: If you supervise a model's intermediate steps, keep the pressure light and constant rather than strong-then-removed. And always save versions during training and pick the best one: here the final training step was reliably worse.

key_numbers.label: Hardest chains solved: no coaching vs light coaching
key_numbers.sub: exact answers on 24-step programs, fresh retest

key_numbers.label: Agreement across two wordings of one problem
key_numbers.sub: no coaching vs light coaching kept on through long chains

key_numbers.label: Heavy coaching then switched off
key_numbers.sub: worse than the 25% from no coaching at all

key_numbers.label: Best model appeared before training ended
key_numbers.sub: the final saved version was reliably worse everywhere

charts.chart_plain_title: Light coaching kept on beats no coaching on long chains
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one coaching recipe. One bar shows how often it solves long chains correctly; the other shows how often plainly worded and reworded versions produce the same running totals. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two light-coaching-kept-on recipes stand tallest on both measures, while the switched-off-late recipe sinks below giving no coaching at all.

charts.chart_plain_title: The best version shows up mid-training, not at the end
charts.chart_read: Each recipe gets a pair of bars: its best-scoring saved version versus its final end-of-training version, both measured on solving long chains correctly. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every final-version bar falls well below its best-saved counterpart, so simply ending training hands you a noticeably worse model.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching mainly protects the reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by wording — plainly stated, reworded, and both-must-match — with one colored bar per coaching recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes barely differ on plainly worded problems but split widely on reworded ones, where light coaching stays high and switched-off-late collapses.

qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.verdict_tag: Random seed, not recipe, decides whether it works
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.concept_primer: The model writes a fixed 24-step recipe of typed number operations, and a built-in calculator runs it — grading not just the final answer but every intermediate value. Re-running the same training from three different random starting points reveals whether it learns reliably.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_question: Can a small model be trained to reliably write correct 24-step number programs, and does the training schedule you pick actually decide whether it succeeds?
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_answer: No. The best schedule averaged 43% correct on plainly worded problems, yet the identical training swung from total failure to 81% just by changing the random starting number — so no schedule earns credit for the wins. Both growing programs from short to long and jumping straight to full length collapsed unpredictably. The seed, not the recipe, decided the outcome.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.why_it_matters: Before crediting a training trick for a small-model gain, re-run it across several random starting points. One lucky start can look like a breakthrough while the identical recipe flatlines on the next, faking a real effect.

key_numbers.label: Best recipe's swing across three random starts
key_numbers.sub: identical training, plainly worded 24-step problems

key_numbers.label: Best average accuracy of any recipe
key_numbers.sub: top schedule, hiding a 41-point spread across starts

key_numbers.label: Jumping straight to full length
key_numbers.sub: weakest schedule on plainly worded problems

key_numbers.label: Long opening run of steps recovered
key_numbers.sub: across all recipes, even when the full program fails

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training recipe runs 24-step programs best, on average
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars along four kinds of test question (plain wording, unfamiliar wording, matched question pairs, unfamiliar pairs); each recipe is a colored bar; height is the share of 24-step programs run correctly. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The grow-from-short recipe is tallest on most groups, yet even its best plain-wording bar sits below half, while training everything at once barely clears the floor.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same recipe's score swings wildly by random starting point
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by random starting point (three of them), each recipe a colored bar; height is share of plainly worded 24-step programs run correctly. Taller is better; equal heights within one color would mean a dependable recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best recipe goes from zero at one starting point to 81% at another, so a single run tells you almost nothing about the recipe.

charts.chart_plain_title: The early steps are right; the whole program is where it breaks
charts.chart_read: For each recipe, two bars: how often the opening run of steps is correct versus how often the entire 24-step program is correct. Height is accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe gets a long opening run of steps right far more often than the full program, so the failure is finishing consistently, not knowing individual steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs work at 16 steps, then fall off a cliff at 24
charts.chart_read: Each run (recipe and starting point) has two bars: accuracy on 16-step programs versus 24-step programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Almost every run nails the 16-step programs but crashes on the 24-step ones, so the trouble is tied to that extra length, not a general inability.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training looks like a clean success even when the final test collapses
charts.chart_read: Lines track, step by step during training, how often the model's intermediate work is right; one line per random starting point. Rising toward the top means training is going well.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines climb to near-perfect and look interchangeable, yet their final test scores differ hugely, so a smooth training curve gives no warning of a bad outcome.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded questions are far easier than the plain ones
charts.chart_read: Each run has two bars: accuracy on the plain question wording versus on reworded versions of the same programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most runs handle reworded questions far better than plain ones — some near 100% reworded while plain wording is near zero — revealing sensitivity to phrasing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting one wording right rarely means getting both
charts.chart_read: For each run, two bars: how often a single question is answered correctly versus how often both rewordings of the same program are answered. Height is the rate; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The both-versions bar sits well below the single-question bar in nearly every run, so success on a program usually doesn't survive a change of wording.

qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.verdict_tag: Right answer's always there, picker can't find it
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.concept_primer: A fixed model writes a tiny program to solve each problem. The system then spins off about 111 slightly edited variant programs and trains a small judge to pick the best one purely by watching each program run, never seeing the correct answer.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_question: If a model generates many candidate programs and a correct one is almost always among them, can a small trained judge reliably pick that winner out?
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_answer: Barely, and it backfires on hard cases. A correct program sat in the candidate pile 91 to 100 percent of the time, yet the trained picker nudged easy prompts only from 69 to 70 percent and actively hurt longer ones, dropping them from 41 to 35 percent. The ceiling was right there; the picker just could not reach it. Selection, not availability, is the bottleneck.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.why_it_matters: When you generate many candidates and rerank, the hard part is not producing a correct one; it is supervision. Grading a picker only on the final answer is too weak, since many wrongly reasoned programs land on it. Reward correct intermediate steps instead.

key_numbers.label: A correct program was almost always available
key_numbers.sub: share of prompts where at least one generated candidate reached the right answer

key_numbers.label: Easy prompts, before then after the picker
key_numbers.sub: accuracy without versus with the trained judge

key_numbers.label: Longer prompts, before then after the picker
key_numbers.sub: the judge actively hurt accuracy on longer problems

key_numbers.label: Candidate programs generated per prompt
key_numbers.sub: roughly 28 to 30 of them reached the correct answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained picker barely beats the plain system, far below the best possible pick
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars, one cluster per test set. Each cluster shows three heights: the model's plain first pick, the trained picker, and the best candidate that actually exists. Taller means more answers correct; the best-possible bar towers over the other two everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker's bar sits almost on top of the plain first pick in every cluster, while the best-possible bar stays near the top, so nearly all reachable gain goes untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: On longer problems the picker sinks below the plain system
charts.chart_read: Lines track how often the answer is correct as problems grow from six to ten steps, left to right. One line each for the plain first pick, the trained picker, and the best available candidate. Higher is better; the best-possible line stays flat near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Past the shortest problems the picker line dips under the plain first pick, even though a correct candidate is nearly always available, showing the picker actively hurts on harder problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of the reachable improvement the picker actually captured
charts.chart_read: One bar per test set showing how much of the gap between the plain first pick and the best available candidate the picker closed. Above zero is good; below zero means it did worse than picking nothing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars reach at most a thin positive sliver on the shortest splits and turn negative on the paired split and every longer problem, so the picker captured almost none of the available headroom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Picker helps most task types a little, hurts yes-or-no questions
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task type: the plain first pick, the trained picker, and the best available candidate. Higher is better. The best-possible bar sits near the top for every type.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker nudges four task types up slightly, leaves one unchanged, and knocks the yes-or-no type down about nine points, where answers collapse to few values.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scoring candidates by simulation loses to simply trusting the system
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: the plain first pick, a shortcut that scores candidates by simulating them run, and the trained picker. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simulation shortcut's bar sits below the plain first pick on every test set except the hardest reworded one, so scoring by simulation alone underperforms doing nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded questions widen the accuracy gap as problems lengthen
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as problems grow from six to ten steps, split into plainly worded and reworded versions, for both the plain system and the trained picker. Higher is better; reworded lines sit lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded lines fall well below plainly worded ones at every length, and the picker fails to close that reworded gap on the longer problems, dragging accuracy lower on both.

qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.verdict_tag: A few multi-step examples fix ordering mistakes
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.concept_primer: Think of the model as writing a numbered recipe that a robot cook then follows. Trained only on one-action recipes, it fumbles the order of an eight-step dish. Mixing in a handful of two- and three-action recipes teaches it to sequence long recipes correctly.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_question: Does showing the model a few short multi-step examples help it correctly turn long procedures into working programs, instead of putting the steps in the wrong order?
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Adding a few two- and three-step examples lifted correct answers on unseen six-step problems from 72% to 83%, and on eight-step problems from 78% to 89%. But adding only two-step examples did nothing (72% stayed 72%) — you must reach at least one step past your shortest hard case. Every program already followed the format's rules, so this fixed step ordering, not formatting.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.why_it_matters: When training a small model to write programs or tool-call sequences, forcing a valid output format is not enough for correct results. Add a few genuine multi-step training examples, reaching deeper than your shortest failing case, to teach correct step order.

key_numbers.label: Correct on six-step problems
key_numbers.sub: after adding two- and three-step training examples

key_numbers.label: Correct on eight-step problems
key_numbers.sub: same training mix, deeper unseen problems

key_numbers.label: Adding only two-step examples
key_numbers.sub: two-step examples alone gave zero gain at six steps

key_numbers.label: Programs with wrong step order
key_numbers.sub: share of valid programs that sequenced steps wrongly

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer unseen problems solved correctly, by training mix
charts.chart_read: Two groups, six-step and eight-step unseen problems; each has three bars: trained on single-step only, on a one-and-two-step mix, and on a one-two-and-three-step mix. Height is share solved correctly by the external checker; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the one-two-and-three-step mix rises in both groups (to 83% and 89%); the other two mixes stall near 72% (atomic-only edges up to 78% at eight steps), so shallow practice alone does not help.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy holds up as problems get longer
charts.chart_read: Three lines, one per training mix (single-step only, one-and-two-step mix, one-two-and-three-step mix), track the share of correct answers as problem length grows from two to eight steps; higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the three-step-mix line stays high and climbs back to 89% at eight steps, while both other lines sag to about 72% by six steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where valid programs go wrong on the longest problems
charts.chart_read: On the longest problems, bars split every valid program into exactly correct, wrong step order or choice, and correct alternate route, grouped by training mix. A taller first bar and shorter middle bar are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three-step mix has the biggest exactly-correct share (83%) and nearly halves wrong-order errors (25% to 14%), so it fixed real reasoning, not neatness.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guided generation versus letting the model run free
charts.chart_read: For eight-step problems, bars compare three ways of producing the program — format-guided decoding, retry-until-valid, and free generation — for two training mixes (single-step only and the three-step mix); taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Guided decoding is tallest for both mixes; the single best result pairs the three-step mix with guided decoding at 89%.

qwen_constrained_abi_parser.verdict_tag: Blocking bad steps beats retrying until valid
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.concept_primer: Picture a small AI turning a plain-English request into a short recipe of steps a computer then runs. As it writes, a guardrail blocks any half-finished step that would break the recipe's grammar, so every finished recipe comes out at least well-formed.
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_question: When a small model turns a plain-English request into a runnable recipe of steps, does forcing each step to follow strict grammar rules make the result correct more often?
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_answer: Yes, mostly. On the hardest six-step requests, blocking any invalid step as the model writes lifted correctly-running recipes from 60% to 75%, and it won on all five training runs. It also beat merely re-rolling until valid, which reached only about 63%, and writing no steps at all, which got 0%. The catch: the guardrail also makes some wrong recipes look well-formed.
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.why_it_matters: When your small model writes machine-runnable steps, block anything that breaks your format as it writes, rather than re-rolling failed outputs. The guardrail rescues cases re-rolling misses, and its edge grows when requests are worded unfamiliarly.

key_numbers.label: Correct on the hardest requests
key_numbers.sub: free writing vs grammar guardrail

key_numbers.label: How often the guardrail won
key_numbers.sub: every training run improved, by 15 points on average

key_numbers.label: When the request is reworded
key_numbers.sub: free writing falls off; the guardrail holds steady

key_numbers.label: Just re-rolling until valid
key_numbers.sub: the cheap alternative, barely above plain writing

charts.chart_plain_title: Hardest requests: the guardrail gets the most recipes running correctly
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way to turn the request into steps; bar height is the share of runs whose steps produced the right final answer. Taller is better. The rightmost bar is the perfect-input ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bar clears free writing and cheap re-rolling; writing no steps scores nothing, and only the perfect-input ceiling stands higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail leads at every recipe length
charts.chart_read: Left to right is how many steps the recipe needs, so further right is harder; height is the share of recipes running to the right answer, higher is better. Each line is one way of writing the steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line sits on top at every length, staying above free writing and cheap re-rolling as recipes grow longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: When the request is reworded, only the guardrail holds up
charts.chart_read: Left to right is recipe length; height is the share running to the right answer, higher is better. Lines show each writing method when the request uses unfamiliar phrasing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line stays high near 75% while free writing drops to about 48% and the parse-first method collapses far below it.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail's trade-off: all well-formed, but not all correct
charts.chart_read: Three measures: how often the recipe is well-formed, how often it runs to the right answer, and how often well-formed recipes are actually right. Two bars each, free writing versus guardrail. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail makes every recipe well-formed and lifts right-answers to 75%, but that last measure also dips to 75%, so some forced-valid recipes are still wrong.

qwen_context_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: The right answer was there but unpickable
qwen_context_trace_verifier.concept_primer: A small model turns each question into a tiny step-by-step calculator program, then spawns about a hundred edited variants of it. A separate judge watches each one run and tries to pick the best, but never sees the correct answer, like a talent scout choosing without the scoreboard.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_question: If a model rewrites a question as a tiny program, can a separate judge pick the best version just by watching each one run, without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Mostly no. A correct program was almost always sitting in the candidate pile, for 91 to 100 percent of questions, yet the judge barely helped: it nudged easy questions from about 69 to 70 percent and actually made the hardest ones worse, dropping them from 41 to 35 percent. The wall is choosing the right program, not finding one.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: When dozens of candidate programs land on the same final answer, "is the answer right" is too weak a signal to train a picker. Supervise on the intermediate calculation steps, not just the final number.

key_numbers.label: How often a correct program was even available
key_numbers.sub: share of questions with a right answer somewhere in the candidate pile

key_numbers.label: Best-case gain on easy questions
key_numbers.sub: the judge's pick vs the model's own default program

key_numbers.label: What happened on the hardest questions
key_numbers.sub: the judge made accuracy worse, not better

key_numbers.label: Reachable ceiling vs what the judge captured
key_numbers.sub: how much correct-program headroom went unused

charts.chart_plain_title: Judge barely beats the model, though a correct answer almost always existed
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by difficulty, easier on the left to harder on the right. Height is the share of questions answered correctly, so taller is better. Three bars per group: the model's own first choice, the trained judge, and a best-possible bar showing what perfect selection would reach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible bar sits near 98 to 100 percent everywhere while the judge's bar hugs the model's own bar, so correct answers exist but the judge rarely selects them.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the room to improve the judge actually captured
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one difficulty level. Height shows how much of the gap between the model's own accuracy and best-possible the judge captured; the zero line means no change, above zero is better, below zero means it made things worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars are only slightly positive on easy questions and drop below zero on paired and longer questions, so the judge helps a little then backfires.

charts.chart_plain_title: By task type: small gains everywhere except one, which got worse
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type, from math and dates to true-or-false. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better, with bars for the model's own choice, the trained judge, and best-possible selection.
charts.chart_takeaway: The judge's bar edges above the model for most tasks but drops sharply on true-or-false, where few possible answers make competing programs hard to tell apart.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training signal actually changed the judge's choices
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by the rule used to teach the judge what counts as a good program. Height is the share of mixed questions answered correctly, taller is better, with the model's own choice next to the trained judge.
charts.chart_takeaway: Three of five training rules leave the judge identical to the model; only rewarding a correct final answer nudged it above, and only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: A rule-based scorer trails the model at every program length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the program takes, longer to the right. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better. Three lines track a rule-based scorer, the model's own choice, and the trained judge.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule-based scorer line sits well below the other two at every length, ruling it out as a standalone way to pick the best program.

qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.verdict_tag: Clever test-picking tied random guessing; direct answers won
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.concept_primer: A small model sees a few input-output examples, drafts several little rule-programs, then invents tricky inputs where the programs disagree and grades itself on them to crown a winner — like a cook-off decided by ever-harder taste tests, pointless when every dish is already spoiled.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_question: If a small model writes little rule-programs from a few examples, can inventing tricky test cases help it pick the one that truly works?
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_answer: No. Answering each row directly solved three-quarters of tasks completely, while the best rule-program the method could pick solved only about four in ten — and a perfect picker that peeks at the answers did no better. The clever edge-case grading tied random cases and even meaningless shuffled labels. The real problem: usually no correct program existed to pick.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.why_it_matters: Before building elaborate selection or verification machinery, measure whether a correct candidate even exists among what your model generates. If a perfect picker still loses to the plain baseline, invest in better generation, not smarter ranking.

key_numbers.label: Answering rows directly vs. writing a rule
key_numbers.sub: whole tasks fully correct: direct answers vs. the best program the method could pick

key_numbers.label: A perfect picker that peeks at the answers
key_numbers.sub: even flawless hindsight selection loses to direct answers

key_numbers.label: Tasks the program approach improved
key_numbers.sub: no task got better; ungated programs broke up to 9

key_numbers.label: Clever probes vs. random vs. shuffled nonsense
key_numbers.sub: three selection strategies scored identically

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task success: direct answering vs. program methods
charts.chart_read: Bars give whole-task success (every unseen row correct) for seven methods; taller is better. Direct row-by-row and the two fallback-gated program methods reach 75%; batch is 58%; the counterexample program and a perfect-hindsight picker sit at 42%; visible-only is lowest at 38%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every program-only bar, including a picker that peeks at answers, sits far below direct answering; gated methods only tie by falling back.

charts.chart_plain_title: Clever test-picking scores no better than random or nonsense
charts.chart_read: Bars show whole-task success for five ways of choosing a program; taller is better. Counterexample-guided probes, random probes, shuffled nonsense labels, and a perfect-hindsight picker all land at 42%; using visible examples alone is 38%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four bars sit at identical height — the clever selector matches random and even meaningless labels — so the picking step is not what limits results.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy climbs only as programs get used less
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: whole-task success and the share of tasks that actually run a program; higher accuracy is better. As program usage falls from 63% to 21%, success climbs from 38% to 75%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy rises only as the method leans on its direct-answer fallback and abandons the programs — the programs add no wins of their own.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks helped versus hurt against direct answering
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method count tasks made worse or better than direct answering, out of 24; fewer hurt and more helped is better. The helped bar is zero everywhere; hurt reaches 8 for the counterexample program and 9 for visible-only.
charts.chart_takeaway: No method helps a single task, while ungated programs break up to 9 of 24 — programs can only match or damage direct answering here.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families the programs break
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family give whole-task success for direct answering versus the counterexample program; taller is better. Direct solves most families cleanly but dips to 75% on DateTime and 33% on Number; the program drops City, EmergencyCall, Log, and Product to zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: Programs match direct answering only on Abbreviation, Name, and Phone and wipe out whole families direct solves cleanly — the failure is broad, not one odd task.

charts.chart_plain_title: Strict all-rows-correct scoring widens the gap
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: lenient per-row success versus strict whole-task success (every row right); taller is better. Batch drops most, from 74% of rows to 58% of tasks; the program methods trail on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring every row correct penalizes batch and program methods most, pushing them well below direct row-by-row answering.

charts.chart_plain_title: On broken tasks, almost no program even fits the examples
charts.chart_read: For the eight tasks the counterexample program hurt, one bar counts candidate programs generated (seven or eight each) and one counts how many fit the four visible examples; more passing is better. Seven tasks have zero passers.
charts.chart_takeaway: On 7 of 8 damaged tasks not one candidate fit even the visible rows, so no selection step could have rescued them — the failure is generation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-grading agreement does not predict whether a program works
charts.chart_read: Bars show the chosen program's self-grading agreement score for four solved and four failed unseen tasks; a useful signal would be high on solved and low on failed. Instead all four failed tasks score 100% and a solved one scores 0%.
charts.chart_takeaway: High agreement shows up on failing programs and low agreement on a working one — the selection signal is uncalibrated and misleading.

qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.verdict_tag: Asking row by row beats forcing one rule
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.concept_primer: You have four examples of how a column should be reformatted, say dates or phone numbers. Instead of transforming each new row directly, you hunt for one exact formula, invent extra test inputs, and let the model's own answers vote for which formula to trust.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_question: When a small model reformats data messily, can you distill its noisy guesses into one clean, reliable rule that works correctly on every new row?
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_answer: No. Distilling the model's messy guesses into one reliable formula solved only 20% of tasks completely, versus 50% when it simply transformed each row directly, a 30-point drop. For 29 of the 40 tasks no formula even fit the four examples, and the model's own guesses picked winners no better than randomly shuffled labels.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.why_it_matters: When you need a small model to reformat data reliably, don't reverse-engineer one formula from a few examples: its self-labeled guesses are too noisy to steer the search. Transform each row directly, and reserve a fixed rule only where a valid one provably exists.

charts.chart_takeaway: The three direct bars sit near 50% while the three rule bars flatten to 20%, and real guesses tie shuffled ones, so the rule approach threw away 30 points.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting most rows right is not solving the whole task
charts.chart_read: For each of five methods, one bar shows the share of individual rows answered correctly and a second shows the share of tasks solved completely; taller is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Direct methods get about 72% of rows right but only half of tasks fully right; the rule method collapses to roughly 21% and 20% on both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most tasks had no usable rule to choose from

charts.chart_takeaway: 29 of 40 tasks had zero valid rules, and every one of the 11 that had any had several, so on most tasks there was nothing to pick among.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixed rules collapse on the biggest task groups
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for five task types (addresses, cities, dates, numbers, phones) show three methods side by side; height is the share of tasks solved completely, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the two biggest groups, dates and numbers, the rule bars fall to near zero while direct answers hold 56% and 30%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row accuracy across the main task groups
charts.chart_read: The same five task types, three methods side by side; height is the share of individual rows answered correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule method drops to zero on several groups while direct answers stay high; voting among guesses nudges dates up but drags numbers down.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one task; height is how many valid fixed rules were found; of the 11 tasks that had any rule at all, 10 are shown.

charts.chart_plain_title: The two times the safeguard fired, it never helped
charts.chart_read: For the only two tasks where high guess-agreement triggered the safeguard, paired bars compare direct answers against the gated rule; height is the share of rows correct, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On both tasks the gate locked in a single constant answer; one task got worse and the other stayed at zero, so the safeguard never helped.

qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.verdict_tag: Contradictory examples teach a model to read its
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.concept_primer: Picture flashcards where the very same word demands a different answer in each deck, so memorizing never pays off — the only way to score is to first read that deck's example pairs. Training on such deliberately contradictory decks forces the model to read the prompt's examples.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_question: If you train a small model on made-up tasks where the same input can demand opposite answers, does it learn to actually read the examples instead of guessing?
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_answer: Yes. Untrained, a 4-billion-parameter model solved 23% of real text-transformation tasks perfectly; after this training, 57% — but only when it could see the prompt's examples. Scramble those examples and it fell to 17%, below where it started; remove them and it collapsed to about 3%. The entire gain lives in reading the examples, not memorizing a familiar transformation.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.why_it_matters: To make a small model obey in-context examples instead of falling back on a familiar guess, train it on example sets whose answers can only come from the examples — and always retest with the examples shuffled or removed; real conditioning makes accuracy crater.

key_numbers.label: Real tasks solved perfectly
key_numbers.sub: public text-transformation tasks, untrained vs trained (examples shown)

key_numbers.label: Made-up tasks solved perfectly
key_numbers.sub: unseen contradictory tasks, untrained vs trained

key_numbers.label: Trained, but examples scrambled
key_numbers.sub: real tasks — below the 23% untrained level

key_numbers.label: Trained, but examples removed
key_numbers.sub: real tasks collapse without any demonstrations

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task accuracy across four example conditions
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved completely correctly; taller is better. Four conditions along the bottom: untrained with examples, trained with examples, trained with scrambled examples, trained with no examples. One color is made-up unseen tasks, the other real public tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trained-with-examples towers over every other bar; scrambling or removing the examples crashes accuracy to near or below the untrained level — winning requires reading the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-line accuracy under the same four conditions
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of individual output lines matched exactly, not whole tasks; taller is better. Same four conditions and same two task groups — made-up unseen versus real public — as the whole-task chart.
charts.chart_takeaway: The looser per-line scoring shows the identical shape: big gains only when correct examples are present, confirming genuine example-reading rather than an artifact of strict all-or-nothing scoring.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error falls fast and stays low
charts.chart_read: A single line traces the model's training error (vertical) against training step from 1 to 120 (horizontal); lower is better. Only the model's answer tokens are scored during training, not the prompt examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Error plunges from about 7 to near zero within roughly 40 steps, then stays flat — the model masters the contradictory tasks quickly and cheaply.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training tasks lift transfer to real tasks
charts.chart_read: Whole-task accuracy bars; taller is better. Groups pair each task set — made-up unseen and real public — across a smaller 240-task run and the larger 800-task run. Three colors: untrained, trained with examples, trained with scrambled examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Going from 240 to 800 training tasks raises real-task accuracy from 38% to 57% and deepens the drop under scrambled examples — stronger, more genuine example-reading.

qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.verdict_tag: Cheap example drills triple accuracy; twist adds nothing
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.concept_primer: The training puzzles follow invented rules that contradict anything memorized — like a cipher whose key changes every round. The only way to solve one is to decode the worked examples shown in the prompt, forcing the model to read them instead of recalling a familiar format.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_question: Can cheap made-up puzzles that force a small model to copy its examples teach it to follow examples on real tasks — and is the special recipe why it works?
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_answer: Yes, but not for the reason expected. Tuning tripled whole-task accuracy on real text tasks, from 20% to about 61%, stable across three training runs — and the model genuinely reads its examples: remove them and it collapses to under 2%. But plain tuning on ordinary made-up puzzles reached 64%, so the clever counterintuitive twist added nothing. The lift is generic synthetic tuning.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.why_it_matters: Before crediting a clever data recipe for a small model's gains, run two cheap controls: tune on plain synthetic puzzles, and strip the examples at test time. Here both showed the win was generic synthetic tuning, not the advertised mechanism.

key_numbers.label: Public accuracy, before vs after tuning
key_numbers.sub: whole-task exact match on unseen real text-transformation tasks

key_numbers.label: Clever recipe vs plain synthetic tuning
key_numbers.sub: counterfactual tuning tied ordinary tuning, so the special ingredient is not isolated

key_numbers.label: Accuracy with the examples removed
key_numbers.sub: the tuned model collapses without in-context support examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Public accuracy: untuned model versus three tuned adapters
charts.chart_read: Bars give whole-task exact-match accuracy on public text tasks with examples intact; left to right: untuned base, the counterfactual adapter (mean of three seeds), the plain-synthetic adapter, and the shuffled-label adapter. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three tuned bars cluster near 60 to 64%, roughly tripling the untuned 20% and indistinguishable from each other, so the clever counterfactual ingredient adds nothing visible.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy depends on the examples in the prompt
charts.chart_read: For each adapter, bars show public whole-task accuracy under intact, shuffled, and removed support examples; colors mark the counterfactual, ordinary, and shuffled-label-trained adapters; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every adapter collapses to near zero once examples are removed, proving they read them, but the shuffled-label adapter still scores 51% on shuffled examples, hinting at learned formatting.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the training gains reach unseen public tasks?
charts.chart_read: Two evaluation splits — the synthetic training-style tasks and the unseen public tasks; two bars each, untuned base versus the counterfactual adapter, both with intact examples; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The adapter lifts both splits, from 38% to 93% on synthetic and 20% to 61% on public, so the skill transfers to genuinely unseen tasks, not just the generator.

charts.chart_plain_title: Improvement holds across three training runs
charts.chart_read: For each of three training seeds, one bar counts public tasks that improved and one counts those that worsened versus the untuned base, out of 45; more helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every seed improves about 20 of 45 tasks and worsens only one, so the gain is a stable effect rather than one lucky run.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which kinds of public task the tuning fixes
charts.chart_read: Ten public task families across the axis; paired bars give untuned base versus counterfactual-adapter whole-task accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Structured formats like codes, file paths and currency jump to 100%, dates stay hardest, and the single address task actually regresses to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: The shuffled-label adapter ignores whether examples are right
charts.chart_read: Four arms on the synthetic split — base and the counterfactual, ordinary, and shuffled-label adapters; two bars each for normal versus shuffled support examples; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most arms roughly halve under shuffled examples, but the shuffled-label adapter holds 92%, evidence it learned output formatting rather than truly reading the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Strict whole-task scoring lowers every arm's score
charts.chart_read: Four arms on the public split — base and the three adapters; two bars each, lenient per-row scoring versus strict whole-task scoring where every row must match; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring every row to match costs the adapters about 10 points but nearly halves the base model, so strict scoring widens the tuned-versus-untuned gap.

qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.verdict_tag: Self-grading yields running code, not correct code
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.concept_primer: The system turns each question into a small runnable program and generates dozens of candidate fixes. Choosing a winner with no answer key is like judging a room of working calculators by watching them run: you can tell which ones compute, not which one lands on the right number.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_question: Without an answer key, can a model learn to pick the best of its own attempts just by running each one and watching how it behaves?
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_answer: Barely. The self-grader learned to favor programs that run without crashing, but it identified the truly correct one only about 15% of the time, against a 41% best-possible ceiling. On fresh questions it picked worse than just trusting the model's first attempt. Given a real answer key, the same machinery hit 91%. The grader, not the compiler underneath, is the wall.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.why_it_matters: When you rank a model's own code fixes without ground truth, execution signals flag crashes but not wrong answers, so self-picked labels are mostly wrong. Filter picks by confidence to raise precision before training on them.

charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bars nudge just above the starting system, but all three huddle near the floor while the answer-key ceiling towers far above them.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model's own picks do worse than its first attempt

charts.chart_takeaway: The self-graded pick sits at or below the first attempt everywhere, while the best-available bar stands far higher, so the headroom goes untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training examples the model picked itself are mostly wrong

charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bar reaches only about a tenth as high as the two answer-key bars, which are fully correct by construction.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-picked targets drop multi-try accuracy below the starting point
charts.chart_read: For the paired question set, one bar per training stage: starting system, answer-key targets, self-picked targets, and full answer-key training. Height is accuracy when allowed several tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-picked training dips below even the untrained start, while full training nearly maxes out, so self-grading actively hurts multi-try search.

charts.chart_plain_title: With an answer key the compiler nearly masters every question set
charts.chart_read: For four question sets, four bars: full answer-key training with one try and with several tries, and the starting system with one try and several. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Full-training bars cluster near the top across all sets while the starting bars sit near the floor, showing the task itself is very learnable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair training almost never reproduces the intended program
charts.chart_read: For four question sets, four bars: starting system, answer-key repair, self-picked repair, and full answer-key training. Height is how often the exact intended program is recovered; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every repair bar hugs zero while full training reaches roughly half to three-quarters, so repair barely changes the program the system writes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra clues helped the small pilot but the gain vanished at scale
charts.chart_read: For a validation set and the two hardest question sets, three bars: a bare pilot, a pilot given extra execution clues, and the full-size run. Height is how often the grader picked correctly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra-clue pilot roughly doubles the bare pilot, but the full-size run slips back toward the bare level on the hardest sets.

qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.verdict_tag: Showing the work barely helps, costs far more
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.concept_primer: Imagine training a clerk to run fixed procedures like currency conversions, date math, and table lookups. You can teach using only the final number, or using a full written worksheet showing every step. This tests whether the worksheet version actually produces more correct answers.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_question: When you teach a small model to run step-by-step procedures, does making it write out every step give more correct answers than just teaching the final answer?
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_answer: Barely. On familiar inputs, writing out each step scored 94% versus 92% for answer-only, a two-point edge that cost three to six times more generated text. On genuinely new combinations of steps the model had never seen, every format collapsed to roughly 30 to 40%, so the extra detail bought almost nothing where it mattered most. Answer-only was actually best on reworded prompts.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.why_it_matters: For deterministic, familiar tasks, answer-only supervision is cheaper and nearly as accurate. Verbose step-by-step traces do not buy real generalization to new step combinations, so reserve them for cases you have actually measured.

key_numbers.label: Steps vs just the answer, familiar problems
key_numbers.sub: best step-writing style versus answer-only on fresh values of trained tasks

key_numbers.label: Text needed to produce an answer
key_numbers.sub: answer-only versus the step-writing styles

key_numbers.label: Best score on brand-new step combinations
key_numbers.sub: every style collapsed here

key_numbers.label: Same model with no training

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each output style across three kinds of test problems
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for five output styles: answer-only, two simple step-by-step styles, a verbose structured-object style, and the untrained model. They are grouped by test type: familiar problems, reworded prompts, and brand-new step combinations.
charts.chart_takeaway: On familiar and reworded problems the three simple trained styles cluster high near 85 to 94%, but all crash to roughly a third correct on brand-new step combinations; the verbose structured style trails even on familiar problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much text each output style writes to answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the average amount of text the model writes to produce an answer on familiar problems (shorter is cheaper), one per output style. Answer-only sits far left near ten words, the verbose structured style far right near sixty-four.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-writing styles use three to six times more text than answer-only, a large cost for at most a two-point accuracy gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each style produces a usable answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of responses that yield a readable final value (taller is better) for each style across the three test types, contrasting the simple line-based styles with the verbose structured-object style.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three simple styles always produce a usable answer, while the verbose structured style fails to give one about a quarter of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types break on brand-new step combinations
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for the best step-writing style on brand-new step combinations, broken out by task type: text cleanup, unit conversion, table lookup, and date math.
charts.chart_takeaway: Unit conversion stays fully correct while text cleanup and date math drop to zero, showing failures cluster where a genuinely new combination of steps is required.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded-problem accuracy by task type and output style
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) on reworded prompts, grouped by task type, with one bar per output style: answer-only, two simple step styles, and the verbose structured-object style.
charts.chart_takeaway: Line-based styles stay above 83% on most task families (dipping as low as 67% on text cleanup), while the verbose structured style drops to zero on table lookup and date math, failing whole categories.

qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.verdict_tag: Step-by-step edits beat prompting but trail the expert
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole program as text, the model works like a mechanic making one small fix at a time: it reads a compressed dashboard of the machine's current state, turns exactly one knob, re-inspects, and repeats until it decides to stop.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_question: Can a small model learn to build a solution by making one edit at a time and re-checking, instead of writing the whole answer out as text?
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_answer: Mostly yes. Teaching the model to make one edit at a time, then correcting it on the messes it made, roughly doubled accuracy on mixed tasks (22% to 41%) and won on four of five task types. But it lost on ordinary tasks (41% versus 56% for plain prompting) and stayed far below an all-seeing expert solver reaching 81 to 97%. Useful, not a replacement.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.why_it_matters: The loop teaches good single moves but not good whole-plan sequences. If you build step-by-step agents, imitating one correct edit isn't enough: train pressure over complete runs, and guard the stop button, since quitting early kept capping the gains.

key_numbers.label: The expert solver's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: an all-seeing teacher; the learned loop topped out near 53%

key_numbers.label: Where plain prompting still won
key_numbers.sub: ordinary tasks: untrained model beat the trained loop
