charts.chart_plain_title: Trained edit loop versus prompting and a flawless solver
charts.chart_read: For each of five task types, four bars: an empty-machine floor, the model answering directly, the trained step-by-step agent, and a flawless all-seeing solver. Vertical axis is share of tasks solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained agent tops the direct-answer bars on four of five task types, yet every bar sits far under the flawless solver.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy changes with the number of edits allowed
charts.chart_read: One line per task type. Horizontal axis is the cap on how many edits the agent may make; vertical axis is share of tasks solved; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line collapses near zero at two to four edits, then jumps up at eight, meaning half-finished attempts hurt while enough room to finish pays off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Three editing skills across the training stages
charts.chart_read: For five training stages, three bars each: picking the right edit, filling in the right value, and knowing when to stop. Vertical axis is per-step correctness; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three skills climb across stages, but the fill-in-the-value bars stay shortest throughout, marking it the persistent weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy after each round of practice-on-its-own-attempts
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: after basic copying of the expert, then two rounds of correcting the agent on situations it reached itself. Vertical axis is share solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra correction rounds steadily lift the hardest composition tasks from about 19% to 31%, while the other task types just shuffle up and down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Quitting too early gets worse the more room you allow
charts.chart_read: One line per task type. Horizontal axis is the cap on edits allowed; vertical axis is how often the agent stops before the task is done; lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines climb steeply, so giving more room makes the agent quit early far more often, capping the payoff of longer runs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Two stopping rules trade small wins across task types
charts.chart_read: For each task type, two bars: letting the agent decide when to stop, versus a confidence check before it stops. Vertical axis is share of tasks solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two rules trade wins, the confidence check adding a few points on some types and losing a few on the hardest, so neither clearly wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full trained agent towers over two early prototypes
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: two small early prototypes and the full trained agent. Vertical axis is share of tasks solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full agent dwarfs both prototypes, and the earliest text-only version sits near zero, showing the design change and the added training were both essential.

qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.verdict_tag: Caution helped; the clever tie-breaker quiz did not
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.concept_primer: A small model writes several short programs to reformat messy text — dates, prices, names. Because the programs disagree on some inputs, you feed it those tricky cases and ask it to judge which output is right, hoping its verdicts reveal the single best program.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_question: When a small model writes several programs to reformat text, does quizzing it on the inputs where those programs disagree help pick the best one?
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_answer: No. The disagreement quiz picked the same programs whether its judge answers were real, randomly assigned, or skipped entirely — all three landed at 64% of tasks fully solved. The only genuine gain came from a plain caution rule: run a generated program only when it clearly fits, otherwise answer each item by hand. That rule lifted fully-solved tasks from 56% to 64%.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.why_it_matters: When a small model writes code to handle a task, invest in a conservative fallback that runs the code only when it clearly fits — not elaborate schemes to rank rival programs, which here changed no decisions at all.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved: plain answering vs adding caution
key_numbers.sub: share of 25 tasks solved exactly, before and after the cautious program gate

key_numbers.label: Extra tasks won by the disagreement quiz
key_numbers.sub: real-quiz, random-quiz, and no-quiz gates all tied at 64%

key_numbers.label: Tasks where the quiz changed the choice
key_numbers.sub: quiz-gated and no-quiz decisions were identical on every task

key_numbers.label: Where the gains landed
key_numbers.sub: number-formatting tasks solved exactly; every other category matched plain answering

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tasks each method fully solved
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved exactly right, one per method — plain by-hand answering, program only, and gates using real, random, no, or scrambled quiz answers; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-quiz, random-quiz, and no-quiz gates land at exactly the same height, so the disagreement quiz bought nothing — the caution gate, not the quiz, delivered the lift.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks improved versus tasks broken, compared to plain answering
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar counts tasks it improved over plain answering and another counts tasks it broke, out of 25; more improved and fewer broken is better. Raw programs broke six to fourteen tasks; both caution gates broke none.
charts.chart_takeaway: Running programs unguarded wrecks many tasks; the cautious gate keeps the two wins while breaking nothing — its whole value is restraint.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved by category: plain answering vs the quiz gate
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per text-format category compare plain answering against the disagreement-quiz gate; taller is better. Every category matches except number-formatting, where the gate rises from about 29% to about 57%.
charts.chart_takeaway: All of the gate's advantage comes from number-formatting tasks; everywhere else it simply equals plain answering.

charts.chart_plain_title: Item-by-item accuracy by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of individual items transformed exactly right, one per method; taller is better. All gated variants tie near 83%, plain answering trails slightly, and the ungated program arms fall to 45% or below.
charts.chart_takeaway: At the item level the gated methods tie again, while trusting raw programs roughly halves accuracy and scrambled answers collapse it to near nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a fitting program is actually trusted
charts.chart_read: Per method, bars show the share of tasks where a generated program passed the visible examples or was actually used — this measures supply of usable programs, not final accuracy. Programs fit on 52% of tasks; the cautious gates trust only 40 to 44%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Programs often fit the shown examples, yet the gate deliberately trusts fewer of them, trading coverage for safety; scrambled-answer quizzes trust none.

charts.chart_plain_title: Item accuracy by category: plain answering vs raw programs
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per category compare plain answering against raw unguarded programs at the item level; taller is better. They match on prices, emails, phones, and ratings, but programs collapse on file paths, billing codes, and especially dates and times.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw programs handle simple formats fine but fall apart on structured ones, explaining why running them unguarded is risky.

qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.verdict_tag: Tuned hidden hints do not beat plain prompting
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.concept_primer: A soft prompt is a handful of invisible primer tokens slipped in front of every request to a frozen model. Here each reformatting task gets its own tiny set, shaped by that task's four worked examples to steer output, while the model itself never changes.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_question: If you tune a small set of hidden primer tokens for each reformatting task, does a frozen small model nail more of those tasks perfectly than plain instructions?
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_answer: No. Tuning the primer tokens on each task's own examples solved exactly the same share of tasks perfectly as plain instructions: just over half either way. The tuning genuinely worked, beating a version trained on deliberately scrambled examples by more than 7 points, yet that edge was too small to finish even one extra task, and per-item accuracy inched up by only one point.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.why_it_matters: When adapting a frozen small model from just a few examples per task, a tuned hidden prefix is not worth the training cost here: plain instructions already capture what those few examples can teach. Test that gap before investing.

charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-instructions bar and the tuned-prefix bar reach identical height for whole tasks, so the tuning added nothing over asking the model directly.

charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned prefix's bar sits exactly on zero and every other method falls below it, so nothing beat plain instructions on fully solved tasks.

charts.chart_takeaway: The real-example line drops steadily and far lower, showing the prefix genuinely learned, so the flat end result is not a training failure.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks made better, worse, or unchanged versus plain instructions
charts.chart_read: For each of five methods, three bars count how many of the forty tasks it improved, worsened, or left unchanged compared with plain instructions. More improved and fewer worsened is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every task lands in the unchanged pile; the tuned prefix helped one task and hurt one, a wash overall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Change in single-answer accuracy versus plain instructions
charts.chart_read: Bars show each method's change in how often individual answers matched exactly, against plain instructions at the zero line; above zero is better. Both the tuned prefix and an untuned prefix rise slightly.
charts.chart_takeaway: An untuned prefix rises just as much as the tuned one, so the tiny gain comes from having any prefix, not from tuning it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Single-answer accuracy at two test-set sizes
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often single answers matched exactly for each of six methods, measured on a smaller and a larger set of test tasks; taller is better. The two sizes check whether the ranking holds.
charts.chart_takeaway: Method ordering barely shifts between the two sizes, and the tuned prefix's edge over plain instructions stays tiny either way.

qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.verdict_tag: Teach short chains, get long ones free
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.concept_primer: The model acts as a translator: it turns a plain-language request into a short program that a simple step-by-step machine then runs. It only ever practiced stringing together up to three steps, yet it correctly writes and runs sixteen-step programs far past its practice length.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_question: If you only train a small model to string together up to three steps, can it still correctly write and run procedures four to five times longer?
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_answer: Yes. A model trained only on procedures up to three steps long reliably writes correct sixteen-step procedures, with accuracy climbing from 61% under single-step training to a perfect 100%. Surprisingly, adding longer training examples out to eight steps added nothing and slightly hurt, slipping to 89%. Short practice generalizes several times deeper than you would expect.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.why_it_matters: When training a small model to emit runnable programs or tool chains, skip collecting long multi-step examples. Short compositional practice transfers to procedures several times deeper, so spend your data budget on task variety, not example length.

charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of long programs the model got right. The four groups along the bottom are training diets, from single-step-only up to eight-step examples, with separate bars for twelve-step and sixteen-step tests. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Success leaps between single-step and up-to-three-step training, reaching the top; the two deeper diets are no taller, so extra training depth added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as the test procedures get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line is one training diet. The horizontal axis is how many steps the test procedure has; the vertical axis is the share solved correctly. Higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the single-step-trained line droops as procedures lengthen; every line that saw even short compositions stays flat and high out to sixteen steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing freely versus writing with output-format guardrails
charts.chart_read: For sixteen-step tasks, paired bars per training diet compare the model writing freely against writing with output-format guardrails that keep the program well-formed. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bars tower over the free-writing bars everywhere, showing the model already knew the right steps and messy formatting, not reasoning, was the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: What the long-task answers looked like, right and wrong
charts.chart_read: For deep tasks, bars split every answer into exactly right, a valid alternative that still works, wrong step order or choice, and wrong number used, grouped by training diet. Taller correct bars and shorter error bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Single-step training gave wrong-step-order answers about a third of the time; any short compositional training shrank that to a sliver and made most answers exactly right.

qwen_fastweight_hook.verdict_tag: Extra silent thinking loops bought no accuracy
qwen_fastweight_hook.concept_primer: Instead of making the model reason out loud in words, we bolted a small loop inside its frozen network — a private scratchpad it can spin extra times before answering. More spins should mean more thinking. We tested whether extra silent spins actually buy accuracy.
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_question: If a small model quietly loops through extra thinking steps inside its network before answering, without writing anything down, does it get better at multi-step arithmetic?
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_answer: No. Adding more silent internal loops gave no reliable accuracy gain. On small 100-problem tests a few settings looked promising, but rechecking those best moments on 250 problems erased the edge — zero loops did as well or better. A control loop that never actually trained fluked similar bumps, proving the gains were noise, not real internal reasoning.
qwen_fastweight_hook.why_it_matters: Do not trust small accuracy bumps from a lightweight add-on measured on a few hundred examples. Always run an untrained control and a larger recheck — noise alone routinely fabricates convincing few-point gains that vanish under scrutiny.

charts.chart_plain_title: More invisible thinking steps did not raise accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on two problem sets — everyday problems and harder longer-chain problems — as the model runs zero up to eight extra invisible thinking steps before answering. Taller is better; the leftmost zero-step bar is the reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The zero-step bar is tallest on both sets, so adding invisible thinking steps left accuracy flat or slightly lower rather than climbing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early improvements vanished on a bigger test
charts.chart_read: Bars compare no extra thinking steps against the best extra-step setting on two problem sets, each scored first on 100 questions then rechecked on 250. Taller is better; the pairs show whether the early lead survives.
charts.chart_takeaway: Extra-step bars lead on the 100-question checks but fall level or behind on the 250-question rechecks — the early edge was luck of a small sample.

charts.chart_plain_title: The step advantage flickered and a do-nothing control bumped too
charts.chart_read: Lines track how many points the best extra-step setting beats zero steps by across training, for the real system and for a control that never trained the thinking loop. The flat zero line means no advantage; above it is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines wobble above and below zero, and the untrained control bumps up just as much — the occasional gains reflect noise, not learned computation.

qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.verdict_tag: A trained judge never beat the first answer
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.concept_primer: A model fills a table by answering each row twice. You stitch those tries into every possible complete table and train a judge to pick the one where the rows agree best, betting that internal agreement flags the fully-correct table.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_question: If a model answers each table row a few times, can a trained scorer pick the one complete table that gets every single row right?
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_answer: No. The trained scorer got every row right on half the tasks, exactly what you get by just keeping the model's first answer, and barely better than scoring tables at random. A flawless table was reachable on five more tasks, but the scorer picked none: it ranked good tables above bad ones well, yet that never became a single extra solve.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.why_it_matters: When you sample many drafts and rerank whole tables, the payoff hinges on the selector picking the winner. Here the winner was in the pile yet the scorer missed it, so fix selection before spending more on drafts.

charts.chart_takeaway: The ceiling bar stands well above the rest while the trained scorer sits level with plain first-answer picking, so the extra machinery added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Can the scorer tell good tables from bad: real versus scrambled labels
charts.chart_read: Across four data splits, one bar is how well the scorer ranks correct tables above wrong ones and the other is the same scorer trained on scrambled labels; higher is better, and about 50% means random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-label bars tower near 90% over the scrambled ones, proving the scorer ranks tables well, yet it still solves no extra tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types leave room to improve whole tables
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer whole-table accuracy and the other the best reachable whole-table accuracy; taller is better and a gap means untapped room.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only Number tables show a real gap, 30% versus 70%, so nearly all the missed opportunity lives in numeric tables.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types get more rows right from extra drafts
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer row accuracy and the other the best reachable row accuracy; taller is better and a gap means extra drafts recovered rows.
charts.chart_takeaway: Number rows jump from about 62% to 89% with better drafts while Address and City rows never move, so draft variety helps only some types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Five winnable tasks the scorer still got wrong

charts.chart_takeaway: The first-answer and scorer bars are identical and short on every task, so the scorer picked the same imperfect table and captured none of the five wins.

charts.chart_takeaway: The testing-side share of correct tables swings from about 7% up to 48% across splits, an instability that helps explain why a strong ranker still can't reliably pick winners.

qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.verdict_tag: Self-coaching made the program builder worse
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.concept_primer: Picture the model at the controls of a tiny computer, fixing a program one edit per full thinking-pass, never typing words, just pressing labeled buttons. It first learns by copying worked solutions, then gets rewarded for whole successful fixes so it can sharpen itself.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_question: Can a small model that fixes a tiny program step by step sharpen itself with reward feedback, beyond just copying worked solutions?
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_answer: No. Copying worked solutions alone solved about 10% of tasks; one round of reward-based self-coaching dropped that to 7-8%. The coaching produced encouraging signals — it could rank good fixes over bad ones 80% of the time — yet it scrambled the model's judgment of which edit to make and when to stop. Exhaustive search on the same tasks reaches 83%.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.why_it_matters: Encouraging training signals — a wide reward spread, high ranking accuracy — do not predict better deployment. For small models steering tools step by step, prove reward feedback helps as a move-scorer before letting it directly rewrite the acting policy.

qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.verdict_tag: Chasing right answers broke the model's reasoning
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model writes a hidden worksheet of steps that a fixed calculator runs to reach it. To improve it, we hunted for any nearby worksheet that lands on the right number and drilled those in — including lucky ones that got there for the wrong reasons.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_question: If a model solves tasks by writing a hidden worksheet of steps, can you make it more reliable by teaching it nearby worksheets that happen to reach the right answer?
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_answer: No. Feeding it nearby worksheets that merely land on the correct answer wrecked it. Plain step-by-step training scored 72% on the main mixed test; the same model after answer-chasing repair fell to 35%, and collapsed on longer problems, dropping to 9% at the hardest length versus 30% for plain training. The gradual length curriculum helped; the repair step actively hurt.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.why_it_matters: When folding verified solutions back into a model, select for correct reasoning, not just the correct final answer. A correct worksheet almost always sits nearby, so build a checker that picks it at answer time instead of retraining on patches.

qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.verdict_tag: Writing hidden programs beats guessing the answer
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.concept_primer: Instead of blurting out a number from intuition, the model quietly fills the slots of a tiny program — choosing the operations and copying in the numbers — that a fixed, reliable calculator then runs exactly, like dictating steps to a spreadsheet instead of eyeballing the total.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_question: Can a small model be taught to turn many kinds of everyday word problems into a hidden program that a reliable calculator runs, instead of guessing the answer?
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_answer: Yes. Guessing the answer directly worked only about 15% of the time across six kinds of problems — arithmetic, dates, unit conversions, list totals, yes/no thresholds, and lookups. Having the model instead write a hidden program for a fixed calculator to run hit 78%. Showing it the exact worked steps during training, not just final answers, also produced far cleaner programs — 64% exactly right versus 34%.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.why_it_matters: Don't ask a small model to compute arithmetic, dates, or lookups directly — have it write a structured program and run that on a fixed calculator. Train it on the full worked steps, not just final answers, for cleaner, inspectable programs.

qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.verdict_tag: Folding verified fixes into training changes nothing
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model secretly writes a step-by-step recipe that a fixed calculator runs to reach the result. A checker can spot a corrected recipe sitting right next to the model's. The test: does feeding those corrections back as training make the model write better recipes on its own?
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_question: When a checker can automatically repair a model's hidden step-by-step reasoning, does training the model on those repairs make it produce correct steps by itself?
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_answer: No. Training the model on automatically corrected recipes reached 61% on new tasks, versus 59% for plain training — a 2-point gap that is basically noise, and it left longer tasks no better. The corrections are genuinely valuable live: a checker searching nearby recipes hits 89%. But that help evaporates the moment you try to bake it into the model itself.
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.why_it_matters: If a checker can fix your model's outputs, use it live at answer time to pick or correct them. Feeding those fixes back into training recovered nothing here and slightly hurt accuracy on longer inputs.

qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.verdict_tag: Learned how programs run, not which ones work
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.concept_primer: A helper reads a frozen model's internal state, writes tiny programs, and runs each in a sandbox. It was also trained to foresee each program's fate: will it crash, how deep it runs, is its answer right. The hope was that this foresight would reveal which program to trust.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_question: If a system answers questions by writing little programs and running them, does teaching it to predict what happens when each program runs help it choose better answers?
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_answer: Only partly. It became excellent at predicting mechanical outcomes, like how deep a program runs (about 92% right), but stayed no better than a coin flip at judging which program is actually correct. So it could not rank its own guesses, and ranking did worse than plain answering on every test. First-try accuracy on paired problems stayed stuck near 9%, far below the 77% a fully-supervised version reached.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.why_it_matters: Teaching a model to simulate its own execution is not the same as teaching it correctness. To improve which answer you pick, train on near-identical attempts that differ only in whether they are right, emphasizing plausible-but-wrong ones.

qwen_iterative_repair_policy.verdict_tag: One-step edits nearly double correct answers
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.concept_primer: A small model turns each prompt into a 24-step arithmetic recipe, but usually botches a step or two. A separate checker reads what each near-miss recipe actually computes, then swaps in one better step at a time — targeted fixes, never full rewrites.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_question: When a small model turns a prompt into a step-by-step arithmetic program but gets a few steps wrong, can a tiny helper repair it by editing single steps?
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_answer: Yes. The frozen model alone got about 30% of programs exactly right; editing one step at a time lifted that to 53% on fresh problems, closing roughly 38% of the distance to the best a perfect fixer could reach (89%). The first edit did most of the work, and it slightly beat simply picking the single best candidate outright — with no written reasoning generated.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.why_it_matters: When a small model outputs a runnable program, don't force it to rewrite its reasoning in words. Score and edit the program directly against what it computes — cheap targeted edits rescued many near-misses without generating any extra full attempts.

qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.verdict_tag: Bigger toolbox is free; branching must be taught
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.concept_primer: A small model writes short programs by picking from a fixed menu of building-block operations, and a calculator-like machine runs them, scoring only the final answer. Enlarging the menu is like giving a cook more ingredients: fine for step-by-step recipes, but recipes that call sub-recipes are a separate skill.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_question: When a small model writes programs by picking from a fixed library of operations, does a four-times bigger library hurt it, and can it learn recipes that split into branches?
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_answer: Two things. Making the library four times larger, from 32 to 128 operations, did not hurt straight-line programs at all: both stayed perfect on 16-step chains. But branching is a separate skill. Models shown only straight-line programs got the hardest eight-branch tasks almost entirely wrong, about 13% correct, while adding just a handful of small two-to-three-branch examples lifted that to roughly 87%.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.why_it_matters: If you want a small model to compose tool calls, a bigger tool library alone will not teach structure. Straight-line chaining scales for free, but branching needs explicit examples, and even then reworded prompts can still break it.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every line hugs the top across all lengths, so quadrupling the operation library leaves long straight-line programs essentially perfect.

charts.chart_takeaway: For branch-trained models the guardrail bar is about double the free-writing bar, but no guardrail rescues models never shown branches.

charts.chart_takeaway: The best model keeps most of its score on reworded straight-line programs but drops sharply on reworded branching ones, so wording robustness is unsolved for branches.

qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.verdict_tag: Small model writes exact hidden programs
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of doing arithmetic out loud in words, the model punches a compact program — a start value and a list of operations — into fixed hidden slots, like holes in a player-piano roll that a separate built-in calculator plays back step by step.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model solve multi-step math not by writing the steps out in words, but by quietly filling hidden slots with a program a built-in calculator then runs?
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_answer: Yes, up to a point. It wrote exact programs that a fixed calculator ran perfectly at eight and twelve steps, and its answers matched those programs — so it truly computed rather than guessing, where chance is about one in a hundred. But at twenty-four steps it ran roughly 85% of the chain, then erred late. Producing four candidate programs and keeping the best stayed at chance.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.why_it_matters: Offloading multi-step reasoning to a fixed runtime works for short and medium chains and gives exact, checkable traces. Don't reach for candidate sampling to fix depth — picking the best of four is useless when none are correct. Grow depth gradually instead.

key_numbers.label: Deepest programs, best vs worst wording
key_numbers.sub: exact twenty-four-step recovery, reworded vs plain prompts

key_numbers.label: Picking the best of four candidate programs
key_numbers.sub: best-of-four never above 4.7%, near one in a hundred

charts.chart_plain_title: How exact program accuracy falls as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is program length in steps (8, 12, 24); vertical axis is the share of programs written exactly right, higher is better. One line is plain-worded prompts, the other reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines sit near 100% through twelve steps, then split hard at twenty-four — reworded holds near 69% while plain collapses to zero, exposing a depth wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Candidate search failed where one trained compiler succeeded
charts.chart_read: Bars group four training setups on twelve-step plain prompts. Two bars each: the picked answer and the best of all candidates. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both candidate-search setups sit at the floor even for their best candidate, while the two single compilers reach 88 to 100 percent — search never held a correct program.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long programs run mostly right but end wrong
charts.chart_read: Three twenty-four-step splits (plain, reworded, matched-pair prompts). Each has two bars: share of fully exact programs and share of the step-by-step trace matched before the first error. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trace bars all stand near 85% while exact-program bars sit at zero — the compiler executes most steps, then makes a late fatal error.

charts.chart_plain_title: Twenty-four-step wins arrive late in training and do not hold
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training step; vertical axis is answer accuracy on twenty-four-step splits, higher is better. Four lines cross two training runs with plain and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most lines hug zero; the mixed run's reworded line jumps to 69% only at the final step, and the paired plain line peaks near 23% mid-run then crashes to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the best of four candidate programs stays near chance
charts.chart_read: Six evaluation splits (twelve- and twenty-four-step across three prompt styles). Two bars each: the selected candidate and the best of four. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar hugs the floor, with best-of-four never topping about 5% — the four sampled programs held no correct one, so selection could not help.

charts.chart_plain_title: Balancing worded prompt pairs traded one win for another
charts.chart_read: Four twenty-four-step measures: plain and reworded answer accuracy, plain and reworded trace-prefix. Two bars each: mixed-template training versus balanced paired-template training. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Paired training lifted plain trace prefixes from 49% to 85% but dropped reworded answers from 69% to 3% — a swap with no net gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the first working compiler favored reworded prompts
charts.chart_read: Two program lengths (4 and 8 steps). Two bars each: plain template versus reworded template. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, which equals exact program recovery here, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At eight steps the plain bar drops to 78% while the reworded bar holds at 94.5% — prompt wording alone shifts reliability by about sixteen points.

qwen_learned_active_interrogation.verdict_tag: Handpicked examples tie just grabbing them in order
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.concept_primer: A worker retypes messy data into a clean format, like dates and phone numbers. Before the graded test they may open the answer key for a few practice rows, picked from a pile of six. Does choosing the most useful rows beat opening whichever sit on top?
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.plain_question: When a small model can unlock the true answers to a few extra practice examples before a task, do its own picks beat just taking the first few?
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.plain_answer: No. Unlocking four extra answer keys did raise fully-solved tasks from 63% to 70%, and scrambling those answers sank it to 47%, so real labels genuinely matter. But the model's deliberate picks scored exactly the same 70% as blindly taking the first four in order. A hidden best-possible choice reached 80%, so better picks existed; the model simply never found them.
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.why_it_matters: Before building a smart example-selector, confirm it beats trivial fixed-order and random baselines on your strict all-rows-correct score. Here the model picked by slot position, not content, so spend the budget on more true labels rather than clever selection.

key_numbers.label: Extra true answers help
key_numbers.sub: tasks fully solved: two starter examples vs four unlocked

key_numbers.label: Choosing vs taking in order
key_numbers.sub: model's four picks match blindly taking the first four

key_numbers.label: Scrambling the unlocked answers
key_numbers.sub: same rows revealed, corrupted instead of true labels

key_numbers.label: Best pick that existed
key_numbers.sub: hidden ceiling the model failed to reach

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved perfectly under each way of picking four extra examples
charts.chart_read: Bars give the share of tasks solved perfectly, every fresh row correct and higher is better, for eight strategies: no extra examples, model-chosen, fixed-order, random, diversity, entropy, model-chosen with scrambled answers, and the best-possible hidden pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: Model-chosen and fixed-order land level at 70%; scrambled answers sink to 47% and the best-possible pick reaches 80%, marking real headroom the selector missed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved perfectly as more examples are revealed
charts.chart_read: Lines track the share of tasks solved perfectly (higher is better) as revealed examples climb from zero to four, one line each for model-chosen, fixed-order, random, entropy, and diversity picking.
charts.chart_takeaway: No line leads throughout; only at four examples does model-chosen rise above its two-example start, and fixed-order arrives at the same 70% point.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where extra examples still fail: date tasks versus number tasks
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare no extra examples, model-chosen, fixed-order, and the best-possible pick (higher is better) on the two largest task types: date and time formatting and number formatting.
charts.chart_takeaway: Extra examples lift date and time tasks sharply, but model-chosen and fixed-order both slip on number tasks, where the best-possible bar shows those gains were available.

charts.chart_plain_title: More examples help date tasks but hurt number tasks
charts.chart_read: Lines plot the share of tasks solved perfectly (higher is better) against revealed examples, split into date-and-time tasks versus number tasks under two picking methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: Date and time lines climb steadily with more examples while number lines fall, so a blended average at four examples hides two opposite trends.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row-by-row scores look close; strict whole-task scores separate them
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per strategy show per-row accuracy (individual rows correct) beside whole-task accuracy (every row correct); both higher is better, shown across eight strategies.
charts.chart_takeaway: Strategies sit within about three points on per-row accuracy but spread ten points on whole-task accuracy, so the strict metric is what actually distinguishes them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types collapse when the unlocked answers are wrong
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task type compare four model-chosen examples with true answers versus scrambled answers (higher is better); the gap shows how much each type leans on real labels.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambled answers wipe out file-path tasks and halve city and number tasks, while phone, language, and user-agent tasks survive on the model's built-in knowledge alone.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model keeps picking the same pool position
charts.chart_read: Bars count how often each of the six pool slots, labeled index 0 through 5, was chosen across 210 selection steps; this is a behavior tally, not an accuracy score, and no slot is inherently more useful.
charts.chart_takeaway: One slot, index 3, is chosen 76 times while the last slot is taken just 5, revealing the selector follows position rather than the examples' content.

qwen_learned_repair_verifier.verdict_tag: The fix exists; picking it blind is hard
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.concept_primer: A wrong program sits in a pool of 1,299 candidate rewrites, itself included, and one of them is correct nearly nine times in ten. It is like a multiple-choice test where the right answer sits on the page most of the time, but you must circle it with no answer key.
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.plain_question: If a small model writes a wrong program, can a cheap judge that never sees the correct answer reliably pick a fixed version from many candidates?
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.plain_answer: Partly. Without ever seeing the true answer, the judge lifted correct execution from 30% to 47%. But a checker allowed to peek at the answer key found a correct fix already sitting in the candidate pile 88% of the time, so blind picking claimed only about a third of the fixable errors. Comparing two wordings of the same task and rewarding agreement did better, reaching 51%.
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.why_it_matters: When repairing small-model output, spend on the selector, not the candidate generator: the fix is almost always already in the pool. And if you can pose one task two ways, rewarding answers that agree beats a trained scorer alone.

key_numbers.label: Programs that run correctly, before vs after blind repair
key_numbers.sub: raw model vs a judge that never sees the answer

key_numbers.label: Fixes already sitting in the candidate pool
key_numbers.sub: share where a correct rewrite exists, found by a checker with the answer key

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from cross-wording agreement
key_numbers.sub: same task posed two ways, answers rewarded for agreeing

key_numbers.label: Rewrite candidates generated per program
key_numbers.sub: the candidate pool per program, with the original itself included

charts.chart_plain_title: Blind repair-picker lifts correct programs across three fresh splits
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three fresh test splits: standard, reworded, and paired-wordings. Each split shows three bars: the raw model near 29-30%, the answer-blind picker at 44-48%, and a checker peeking at the answer key near 87-91%. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The blind picker clears the raw model on every split but sits far below the peeking checker: a real gain, with most of the fixable headroom still unclaimed.

charts.chart_plain_title: On paired tasks, rewarding agreement beats the scorer alone
charts.chart_read: Four selection methods along the bottom: raw model, answer-blind picker, agreement reranker, answer-peeking checker. Two bars each: single-task accuracy and the stricter both-wordings-correct rate. Higher is better; the checker tops out near 88% and 86%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The agreement reranker beats the blind picker on both bars, and its jump is largest on both-wordings-correct: consistency repairs the disagreements the picker itself created.

qwen_lora_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: Small model shows its work but smudges the
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.concept_primer: Picture tapping into a model's hidden scratchpad. A light, cheap retraining installs a reader that turns its internal states into a written arithmetic recipe: a starting number, each operation, and the number each step uses. The steps come through crisp; the numbers stay blurry.
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.plain_question: Can you cheaply retrain a small model so its internal states can be read as a trustworthy, step-by-step arithmetic program you could run yourself?
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.plain_answer: Partly. With step-by-step coaching, a small four-billion-parameter model's hidden states became a readable program: it named the starting number every time and picked the right operation about 98 percent of the time, while ordinary answer-only training stayed near zero. But the actual numbers came through only about 80 percent of the time per step, so exact recipes fell to zero by twelve steps.
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: If you want a small model's reasoning to be machine-readable, explicit per-step coaching really does install the interface, but audit each channel on its own. One noisy field compounds across steps and quietly kills exact multi-step execution.

key_numbers.label: Right number decoded per step
key_numbers.sub: the value each step uses — the bottleneck that sinks long chains

key_numbers.label: Fully correct four-step recipe
key_numbers.sub: ordinary answer-only training versus step-by-step coaching

key_numbers.label: Fully correct twelve-step recipe

charts.chart_plain_title: Short problems solved exactly: coached models versus plain-training controls
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of four-step problems each training approach solves exactly; taller is better. Two colors mark standard versus reworded question wording. The two coached approaches on the left tower over the two plain-training controls on the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coached bars reach 33 to 41 percent while both controls sit near zero, so the readable recipe comes from the coaching, not from ordinary answer-fitting.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exact-answer rate collapses as problems get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks the share of problems solved exactly as length grows from four to twenty-four steps; higher is better. The two coached lines start well above the flat, near-floor answer-only control line, then slide down toward it.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both coached lines fall to near zero by twelve steps, showing tiny per-step errors pile up and wreck long problems even when short ones work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which parts of the recipe the model reads cleanly
charts.chart_read: Each line shows how accurately one piece of the recipe reads as problems lengthen; higher is better. The starting-number line stays pinned at the top, the operation line sits just below, and the step-number line trails lowest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-number line sits near 80 percent and keeps sliding while the starting-number line holds perfect and the operation line stays high, pinpointing number-reading as the weak link.

qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.verdict_tag: Worked recipes teach the skill, answer keys don't
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of just checking a small model's final answer, you teach it to write a short recipe of simple steps — push a number, add, compare, look up — that a fixed calculator-like machine runs to reach the answer. The lesson material is fully worked recipes.
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.plain_question: Can a small local model learn to turn a word problem into a short runnable recipe of steps that a fixed calculator machine runs to the right answer?
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.plain_answer: Yes — but the win came from the teaching material, not from adapting the model. Fed fully worked recipes, it wrote a runnable recipe that reached the right answer about 68% of the time, versus only 15% when taught with final answers alone. Surprisingly, a completely un-adapted base model already hit 66%, so the extra tuning barely mattered.
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.why_it_matters: To make a small model emit runnable, checkable steps, invest in densely worked example programs, not more answer labels — answer keys teach almost nothing here. Letting it check a few nearby tries against the answer adds a further lift.

key_numbers.label: Answer keys versus fully worked recipes
key_numbers.sub: runnable recipes that reach the right answer, on fresh prompts

key_numbers.label: Adapting the model itself barely helped
key_numbers.sub: un-adapted base model versus the tuned one, both taught with recipes

key_numbers.label: Checking a few nearby tries against the answer
key_numbers.sub: first try versus keeping whichever nearby recipe matches

key_numbers.label: The hardest, longest problems
key_numbers.sub: direct success on the toughest problem split

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-recipe success by how the model was taught
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one teaching method; height is the share of fresh prompts where the model's recipe ran to the correct answer. Taller is better. The two recipe-taught bars stand tall; the answer-only bar collapses to lowest.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipe-trained methods tower over the answer-only bar, and the un-adapted and tuned recipe bars are nearly level — the teaching material, not the tuning, drives the win.

charts.chart_plain_title: First try versus keeping the best answer-checked recipe
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type from easy to hard; one color is the model's first recipe, the other keeps whichever nearby recipe matches the known answer. Higher is better; the checked color leads in every group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The checked color sits 13 to 20 points above first-try everywhere, so a correct recipe is usually just a small edit away.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-taught rounds climb but never reach recipe teaching
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is each self-teaching round; the rising line is the self-taught model, the flat line marks full recipe-teaching. Higher is better; the flat reference stays on top throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rising line more than doubles from 22% to 48% but never reaches the flat 68%, so self-made examples under-teach.

charts.chart_plain_title: Quality of the model's self-made practice recipes over rounds
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the self-teaching round; one line is how often a verified recipe was found, the other how often it overwrote the model's own guess. Rising found-rate and falling rewrite-rate mean the model is settling.
charts.chart_takeaway: Found-rate rises while rewrite-rate falls, a sign the model is converging on its own answers rather than being corrected.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-recipe success across prompt types, three methods
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type from mixed and easy to hard; each color is a teaching method. Higher is better; every method dips most on the hardest group at the far right.
charts.chart_takeaway: All methods sag on the hardest prompts, but recipe teaching leads on every prompt type while self-teaching trails everywhere.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-recipe success as training progresses
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step; the vertical axis is the share of runnable correct answers; each line is a method. Higher and earlier is better. The un-adapted-base line rises first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned recipe line surges late, from 31% to 66%, while the self-teaching line stays low the whole way.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many worked recipes it takes to work at all
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type; one color trained on 128 worked recipes, the other on 512. Higher is better; the 512 color is far taller everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: Going from 128 to 512 recipes lifts the model off the floor to 55-71%, showing the method is hungry for worked examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model writes the exact intended recipe
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type; each color is a method. Height is how often the recipe exactly matched the intended one. Higher is better; recipe teaching leads.
charts.chart_takeaway: Exact-match rates sit well below correct-answer rates, so the model often writes a different recipe that still runs to the right answer.

qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Blind proofreader fixes some mistakes, not most
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.concept_primer: A frozen model answers each task by writing a hidden step-by-step recipe. A small proofreader that never sees the answer key rereads several near-copies of that recipe, each with one step tweaked, and bets on the version it trusts most.
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.plain_question: Can a small second model, without ever seeing the correct answer, rerank a frozen model's step-by-step solutions and reliably pick the better ones?
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Yes, partly. On fresh tasks the frozen model alone got 46% right; the proofreader lifted that to 57%, and a cross-check that compares reworded versions of the same task reached 61%. But a correct recipe was already among the candidates 92% of the time, so the proofreader captured only about a quarter of the reachable gain, leaving most of it untouched.
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: When you can cheaply generate variant solutions, a lightweight reranker reading only prompt and execution clues buys real accuracy with no retraining of your main model. But the ceiling is the reranker's judgment, not the size of the candidate pool.

key_numbers.sub: frozen model alone, then proofreader, then proofreader plus cross-check

key_numbers.label: Correct recipe already in the pile

charts.chart_plain_title: A blind proofreader and cross-check beat the model's own recipe pick

charts.chart_takeaway: The proofreader and cross-check bars rise well above the model's own pick, but the perfect-picker bar towers over all, showing most of the room stays unused.

charts.chart_plain_title: The proofreader improves accuracy on every task type, easy to hard
charts.chart_read: Three bars per task type — the frozen model alone, the learned proofreader, and a perfect picker — with task types ordered easy to hard. Higher is better; the perfect-picker bar marks each ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The proofreader bar sits above the frozen-model bar in every group, including longer tasks it never trained on, yet stays far under the ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: Cross-checking reworded prompts gets both wordings right more often
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows how often a task and its reworded twin are both solved at once: the frozen model, the cross-check proofreader, and the best-possible ceiling. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cross-check bar rises clearly above the frozen model, closing part of the gap to the ceiling for getting both wordings right together.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on mixed-length recipes recovers more of the available room
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type showing how much of the gap to a perfect picker each proofreader closes: one trained only on short recipes, one trained on mixed-length recipes. Higher means more room recovered.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mixed-length proofreader's bars are taller on the harder task types, but even the best bars recover only a minority of the room.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the learned proofreader beats the frozen model; simpler pickers do not
charts.chart_read: Bars for five ways to pick a recipe — no edit, a most-likely pick, a support-based pick, the learned proofreader, and a perfect picker — shown for two task types. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The most-likely pick just ties the frozen model and the support-based pick drops below it; only the learned proofreader rises above, though well short of the ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: The proofreader's practice accuracy is bumpy, so keep the best round
charts.chart_read: Wiggly lines track each proofreader's accuracy on a practice set across training rounds, with a flat line for the untrained frozen model. Higher is better; the peak is the round worth keeping.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned lines bounce up and down and can dip near the flat baseline, so the single best round must be chosen rather than just training longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on mixed-length recipes wins on most task types
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: a proofreader trained only on short recipes versus one trained on mixed lengths, across easy-to-hard task types. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mixed-length bar is taller on five of seven task types, notably the hard reworded one, at a small cost on the other two.

qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.verdict_tag: Distilling noisy guesses into one rule backfires
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.concept_primer: Picture a hasty clerk reformatting every entry in a list, typing each one a little differently each pass. This hunts for a single fixed recipe that fits the four entries you can check and best matches the clerk's noisy retypes on all the rest.
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.plain_question: If a model gives messy, inconsistent answers for each row of a text-cleanup task, can you boil those guesses into one clean rule that solves the whole task?
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.plain_answer: No. Keeping the model's direct per-row answers fully solved half of the 40 tasks, while distilling those noisy answers into one fixed rule solved just 22.5% — worse even than a scrambled comparison at 25%. And a flawless rule-picker tops out at 27.5%, because for most tasks no short rule both fits the visible samples and holds on the rest.
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.why_it_matters: For per-row text cleanup, trust and vote among the model's direct answers. A single rigid rule is all-or-nothing, and the narrow library of rules caps you below direct answering — reach for a rule only when a simple one truly exists.

charts.chart_takeaway: A fixed rule even exists for only 11 of 40 tasks, below the 20 the direct answers already solve, so the rule library is the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task accuracy by task type: direct answers versus one fixed rule

charts.chart_takeaway: On the two biggest types the rule collapses far below direct — date tasks fall from 56% to 13% — tying only on tiny, trivially rule-like types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the rule-picking methods beat their own scrambled version?
charts.chart_read: Two rule-picking methods on the axis; for each, one bar for the real signal and one for a scrambled control, taller is better. A genuine method should beat its scramble.
charts.chart_takeaway: One method beats its scramble (17.5% vs 0%), but the candidate-vote method ties or trails its scramble (22.5% vs 25%), so its signal is not real.

charts.chart_takeaway: At both sizes the rule ceiling trails direct answers, confirming the rule space itself caps performance rather than being a fluke of a small run.

qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.verdict_tag: Point to the numbers, don't recompute them
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of doing chained arithmetic in its head and reading the digits back, the four-billion-parameter model only points: which word is the starting number, which words are the operations, which are their arguments, and in what order. An exact calculator then copies those symbols and runs the math flawlessly.
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model reliably run multi-step arithmetic if it just locates the numbers and operations in the text and lets an exact calculator do the math?
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. When the model just points to where each number and operation sits and copies the exact symbols for a hidden calculator to run, it solves four-step problems about 90% of the time. A version trained to write the answer itself never left the floor, at most 3% at any length. A shortcut trained only on final answers aced four steps but crashed to zero at eight, never learning the ordered pointing that stretches to longer chains.
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.why_it_matters: For long step-by-step symbolic work, stop making a small model regenerate exact values from memory. Have it mark the right token positions and copy symbols verbatim into a real executor; reliability then hinges on locating slots, not doing arithmetic.

key_numbers.label: Short chains solved end to end
key_numbers.sub: writing the answer yourself vs pointing and copying, at four steps

key_numbers.label: Chains twice the practiced length
key_numbers.sub: still solved correctly at 24 steps, with training capped at 12

key_numbers.label: The final-answer-only shortcut
key_numbers.sub: perfect at four steps, total failure at eight

key_numbers.label: Where accuracy still leaks
key_numbers.sub: operations located almost perfectly, number slots slip at 24 steps

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method solves the whole chain, by length
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by chain length (4, 8, 12, and 24 steps); bar height is the share of chains a method solves correctly end to end, so taller is better. The four colors are four methods, from point-and-copy down to writing the answer directly.
charts.chart_takeaway: The point-and-copy bars tower over the rest at every length past four steps; at four steps the shortcut method spikes to a perfect score, just edging out point-and-copy, while writing the answer directly stays pinned near the floor throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: Staying accurate as chains grow past the practiced length
charts.chart_read: Lines track the share of chains solved correctly as they lengthen up to 24 steps, though practice stopped at 12; higher is better. Colors compare the point-and-copy model against a frozen-backbone version, each on plain and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The point-and-copy line on plain prompts holds near 20% at 24 steps, while the frozen version collapses to almost nothing there.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which part of the program the errors come from
charts.chart_read: Lines show the share correct for each program piece — the starting value, the operations, the number arguments, and the whole program together — as chains lengthen; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Starting values and operations stay pinned near perfect while the whole-program line sags, showing tiny slips in the number slots are what compound.

qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.verdict_tag: Self-correction works, but plain examples work equally
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.concept_primer: The model turns a plain request into a tiny arithmetic program a computer can run. It drafts one, an automatic checker tries small edits until a version runs correctly, then the model retrains on those fixed drafts, studying its own corrected work.
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.plain_question: If a small model writes buggy little arithmetic programs, can teaching it its own automatically-corrected drafts make it reliable on brand-new requests?
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.plain_answer: Yes, but the self-correction isn't the secret ingredient. On brand-new requests the model climbed from about 29% correct programs to 99% after training on its own corrected drafts. The catch: training on plain correct programs alone also reached 99%, and training on corrections alone hit 91%. So the corrections give a usable signal without any answer key, but they aren't uniquely responsible for the jump.
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.why_it_matters: A tiny dose of step-by-step fine-tuning turns a weak program generator near-perfect. Self-correction earns its keep only when you lack labeled answers; if you already have correct examples, they train just as well with less machinery.

key_numbers.label: Correct programs on new requests, before then after training
key_numbers.sub: the deployable model on fresh prompts

key_numbers.label: Trained only on plain correct examples
key_numbers.sub: matches the full method, so corrections weren't uniquely needed

key_numbers.label: Trained only on self-corrections
key_numbers.sub: a strong but weaker training signal

key_numbers.label: Both wordings of a task correct
key_numbers.sub: same request phrased two ways

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct programs before vs after training, across test sets
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set (validation plus three sets of brand-new requests). Height is the share of programs the model gets fully correct. Three bars per group: before training, after training, and the best any small automatic edit could reach. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After-training bars leap from under a third to almost the top, matching the best-reachable line, so training nearly closes the gap on new requests.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training signal drives the gain
charts.chart_read: One group per training recipe on brand-new requests. The main bar is the share of programs correct; a companion bar shows the best any small automatic edit could reach. Compares mixing plain examples with self-corrections, plain examples alone, and self-corrections alone. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain examples alone tie the mixed recipe near the top; self-corrections alone trail well behind, so the corrections help but aren't uniquely responsible.

charts.chart_plain_title: How reachable a correct answer was, before vs after training
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of cases where a small automatic edit could reach a fully correct program. Two bars per group, before and after training. Higher means more failures were fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: After training every remaining slip is fixable to correct, versus only about two-thirds before, because the model now starts much closer to right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Final accuracy across test sets, by training recipe
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of programs correct after training. Four bars per group, one per recipe: the mixed recipe, plain-examples-only, self-corrections-only, and a smaller-data run. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes using plain examples stay near the top everywhere, while self-corrections-only and the smaller run dip on reworded requests.

charts.chart_plain_title: Consistency when the same task is worded two ways
charts.chart_read: Each group is a way of checking pairs of requests that mean the same thing (both correct, same program, same answer, same steps). Height is the share of pairs that match. Two bars per group, before and after training. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After training, pairs correct on both wordings jump from about a quarter to nearly all, closing the wording-robustness gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Leftover fixable mistakes after training
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of programs a small automatic edit still had to change, meaning still wrong. Three bars per group: mixed recipe, self-corrections-only, and the smaller-data run. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Leftover mistakes are few overall but cluster on reworded requests, worst for the self-corrections-only recipe at about one in six.

qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.verdict_tag: A trained example-picker loses to grabbing variety
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.concept_primer: You are teaching a small model a text-formatting rule from two worked examples. Before it tackles fresh items, you may reveal the true answer for a few of six more practice cards. A trained advisor tries to rank which cards teach the rule fastest.
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.plain_question: When a small model can reveal the true answers to a few more examples before solving a task, does a trained picker choose better ones than simple rules of thumb?
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.plain_answer: No. The trained picker does read real signal: it beats revealing nothing (50 to 57 percent of tasks fully solved) and crushes a version fed scrambled answers (27 percent). But it lost to simply grabbing a varied spread of examples (67 percent), and even to picking at random (63 percent). Clever ranking underperformed cheap diversity at the same budget.
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.why_it_matters: When you can feed a small model a few extra labeled examples, spend the budget on coverage: choose a varied spread rather than training a scorer to rank which example is best. On small task pools, diversity is the stronger, cheaper bet.

key_numbers.label: Revealing nothing vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: tasks where all three fresh test rows are answered exactly

key_numbers.label: Varied spread vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: tasks fully solved at the same four-example budget

key_numbers.label: Random picks vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: random still beats the trained picker at the same budget

key_numbers.label: Picker fed scrambled answers
key_numbers.sub: control proving the revealed answers carry real signal

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tasks each way of picking examples fully solves
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one policy: revealing nothing, a scrambled-answer control, the trained picker, fixed order, the model's own choice, random, a varied spread, and a best-possible single pick chosen with hindsight. Height is the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker sits low, above only revealing nothing and the scrambled control; the varied spread and random both top it, so smarter selection did not pay off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does revealing more examples help each policy?
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per policy compare revealing one answer against four; height is the share of tasks fully solved, taller is better. Policies are the trained picker, fixed order, random, the varied spread, and the model's own choice.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every policy improves with four answers, but the varied spread leads at both budgets and the trained picker never takes the lead at either one.

charts.chart_plain_title: How reliably the picker ranks useful examples, group by group
charts.chart_read: One bar per data group shows how well the scorer ordered candidate examples by their true usefulness; 50 percent is a coin flip and higher is better. Four scorable groups are shown.
charts.chart_takeaway: Three groups rank well above chance, from 74 to 96 percent, but one falls to 44 percent, below a coin flip, so the skill is real yet unstable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the picker falls behind, by task type
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task type (dates, names, numbers, phones) compare revealing nothing, the trained picker, the varied spread, random, and the best single pick. Height is the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On number tasks the trained picker drops to zero while every other approach holds a third or more, so its losses cluster in a couple of task types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambling the revealed answers breaks even easy tasks
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task type compare the picker fed true revealed answers versus scrambled ones; height is the share of tasks fully solved, taller is better. Types run from easy (abbreviations, currency) to hard (dates).
charts.chart_takeaway: Five task types the true-answer picker solves completely collapse to zero when the answers are scrambled, proving the revealed answers carry real information the model uses.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single rows right versus solving the whole task
charts.chart_read: Two bars per policy: the share of individual test rows answered exactly, and the stricter share of tasks where all three rows are exact. Higher is better; eight policies span revealing nothing to the best single pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: A 10-to-18-point gap separates the lenient per-row score from the strict all-rows score, but the ranking of policies stays about the same on both.

qwen_pairwise_table_judge.verdict_tag: Table judge picks whichever comes first
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.concept_primer: Picture asking a small model to referee two filled-in spreadsheets and crown the more correct one. On the genuinely hard match-ups it stops reading the cells and behaves like a rigged coin that always lands on the top-listed contestant.
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.plain_question: When a small model produces several versions of a reformatted data table, can it reliably compare them side by side and pick the one that is actually more correct?
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.plain_answer: No. Letting the model run a knockout comparison among its own candidate tables did not help: fully-correct tables slipped from 50% (just trusting its first attempt) to 47.5%. On the hard cases where a correct table existed, it picked the right one 100% of the time when that table was shown first and 0% when shown second. It judged position, not content.
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.why_it_matters: If you let a small model choose among its own outputs, never trust a raw side-by-side vote. Randomize candidate order and require the pick to survive a swap; otherwise you are selecting by slot, not by quality.

key_numbers.label: Fully correct tables: first attempt vs judged pick
key_numbers.sub: the comparison step slightly hurt instead of helping

key_numbers.label: Which table it picked on hard cases, by position
key_numbers.sub: chose the correct table when shown first, never when shown second

key_numbers.label: Ceiling if it always kept the best candidate
key_numbers.sub: the best table already sitting in the pool, versus 47.5% achieved

key_numbers.label: Misleadingly rosy overall pick rate
key_numbers.sub: inflated by easy tasks where both tables were already identical

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method produced a fully correct table
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for four methods: the model's first table, its judged pick between candidates, the best table already in its pool, and a version fed the correct rows. Each shows whole-table and single-row correctness; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The judged-pick bars sit just below the first-try bars, so the comparison step slightly lowered accuracy, while the pool's best towers above both.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model just picks whichever table is shown first
charts.chart_read: Two bars for the hard tables where the first try was wrong: left, the correct table is placed second; right, placed first. Height is how often it picked the correct table; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rate jumps from none to all when the correct table moves to first position, meaning the model followed order, not content.

charts.chart_plain_title: Overall pick rate looks fine but is misleading
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per prompt style, showing how often the model picked the correct table across all comparisons; higher looks better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars sit near 80-90%, but that height comes from easy tables where both choices were identical, hiding the failure on hard ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-row accuracy versus whole-table accuracy by method
charts.chart_read: For each of the four methods, one bar for how many individual rows were right and one for how many whole tables were fully right; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method's whole-table bar drops well below its per-row bar, showing one wrong row is enough to sink an otherwise-good table.

charts.chart_plain_title: On fixable tasks a better table existed but went unpicked
charts.chart_read: Ten hard tasks along the bottom; for each, a bar for the first-try row accuracy and a bar for the best-available table's row accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar towers over the first-try bar on all ten, several reaching a perfect score, yet the comparison step never chose them.

charts.chart_plain_title: A small trial hid the result the full run revealed
charts.chart_read: Four methods, each with a bar from a small six-task trial and a bar from the full forty-task run, measuring how often a fully correct table appeared; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In the small trial the comparison step tied the first try, but the full run shows it falling below the first try.

charts.chart_plain_title: The single table the comparison step changed got worse

charts.chart_takeaway: The swapped table's bar is lower than the original, turning a perfect table into an imperfect one; the only swap it made hurt.

qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.verdict_tag: Finds the right program, can't pick it out
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.concept_primer: To answer each puzzle the system writes a tiny step-by-step program for a calculator-like machine, one instruction at a time. A judge model rates each half-written program for whether it still looks on-track, like a proofreader checking a derivation line by line, sharp at flagging detours but unsure which promising draft actually finishes right.
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.plain_question: Can a helper that scores half-finished programs steer the search toward a correct one, without ever peeking at the right answer while it decides?
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.plain_answer: Barely. The judge got genuinely good at telling promising partial programs from dead ends, yet it lifted first-try accuracy only a few points, with hard problems going from 41% to 44%. The real gap: on hard problems a correct program sat among the candidates 70% of the time but got chosen only 41%. Knowing good from bad is not the same as surfacing the winner.
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.why_it_matters: If you rerank partial solutions with a helper model, first confirm the right one is even in your candidate pool. When it is there but unranked, the fix is scoring whether a draft can still reach the answer, not whether it matches one textbook path.

key_numbers.label: Telling a promising partial program from a dead end
key_numbers.sub: how often the judge ranks a correct partial program above a wrong one

key_numbers.label: Hard problems solved on the first try
key_numbers.sub: plain first guess vs judge-guided search, no answer peeking

key_numbers.label: Correct program present but not chosen
key_numbers.sub: on hard problems, in the candidate set vs actually selected

key_numbers.label: Ceiling when answer-checking is allowed
key_numbers.sub: repair that peeks at the final answer, not a deployable setting
