charts.chart_takeaway: The judge-guided bar sits just above the plain first-guess bar, while the answer-checked and best-possible bars tower far higher, so most of the room went uncaptured.

charts.chart_plain_title: A little trust in the judge helps, heavy trust hurts
charts.chart_read: Left to right the judge's opinion counts more toward the choice (zero means ignore it); height is how often the final program is correct, one line per problem set, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line rises slightly at low weight then falls, so leaning lightly on the judge helps but leaning hard makes picks worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct programs sit in the candidate set but go unpicked
charts.chart_read: For each problem set, the lighter bar is how often the judge's chosen program is correct and the taller bar is how often the candidate set held a correct program at all; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The taller bar stands well above the chosen-program bar in every set, showing the right program is usually present but not selected.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training data helps far more than any picking trick
charts.chart_read: Bars compare a small and a large training set, for both the plain first guess and the answer-checked pick, across problem sets; height is accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars for the larger training set dwarf the smaller one, so adding training data lifts accuracy far more than any picking trick.

charts.chart_plain_title: The judge learned to rate partial programs, then plateaued
charts.chart_read: Left to right is more training passes; height is how well the judge separates promising partial programs from dead ends (higher is better), with one line for the large training set and one for a smaller pilot.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb then flatten near the top, so the judge became a strong rater, yet that skill never turned into better final picks.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model learns single instructions faster than whole programs
charts.chart_read: Left to right is training progress; height is accuracy, with one line for getting individual instructions right and one for getting the entire program exactly right, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The instruction line climbs well above the whole-program line, so the model nails pieces more often than complete programs, the gap the judge was meant to close.

qwen_program_only_executable_abi.verdict_tag: Run the code it writes, not its answer
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.concept_primer: Picture an accountant who writes flawless spreadsheet formulas but misreads the final total aloud. Here a small model writes a tiny program for each office task and a calculator runs it. Trust the run, not the spoken answer, and accuracy jumps.
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.plain_question: If a small model writes a little program to solve each task instead of just stating the answer, does it handle brand-new multi-step problems better?
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Teaching a small model to write a short runnable program lifted brand-new multi-step accuracy from about 44% (just stating an answer) to 73%. And when it wrote out its steps plus an answer, the steps ran correctly 94% of the time while its stated answer was right only 40%. But the gain swung wildly between training runs, so this is a promising lever, not a finished recipe.
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.why_it_matters: For tasks you can verify by running code — conversions, lookups, date math — have a small model write a short program and grade what it computes, not the answer it types. Expect big run-to-run swings and sensitivity to exact prompt wording.

key_numbers.label: Written steps run correctly vs stated answer
key_numbers.sub: same model on new tasks: running its steps beats its typed answer

key_numbers.label: Familiar tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: program-writing styles, vs about 90% for just stating the answer

key_numbers.label: Length of output

charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new multi-step tasks the program-writing cluster is clearly tallest, well above just stating the answer, so compiled programs compose new procedures better.

charts.chart_plain_title: The written steps are right far more often than the typed answer

charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new tasks the run-the-steps bar towers over the typed-answer bar, so the model builds correct steps but fumbles the final number.

charts.chart_plain_title: Different teaching styles win on different kinds of tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type (text patterns, unit conversion, table lookup, dates); height is the share of new combinations solved, taller is better, with one color per teaching style.
charts.chart_takeaway: The styles have opposite strengths: program-writing aces text and date tasks where answer styles score near zero, and loses on unit conversions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded prompts trip up the program-writers most
charts.chart_read: Two bars per teaching style; height is share solved, taller is better. One color is familiar wording, the other is the same tasks reworded.
charts.chart_takeaway: Program styles are perfect on familiar wording but fall hardest when prompts are reworded, while answer styles barely dip, so programs overfit surface wording.

charts.chart_read: One bar per teaching style; height is the average amount of text the model generates per task, so shorter is cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: The program-writing bars are roughly three times taller than the bare-answer bar, the price paid for the accuracy gains.

qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.verdict_tag: The right repair exists but goes unpicked
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.concept_primer: A frozen model turns each prompt into a small arithmetic program that is often slightly wrong. The system lists a handful of tiny edits that might fix it, then a separate small judge circles the one it thinks will run correctly, never seeing the true answer.
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.plain_question: If a small model's first attempt at a program is usually wrong, can a separate judge pick the correct fix from a short list of candidate edits?
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.plain_answer: Partly. The correct fix sits in the candidate list almost nine times in ten, yet the judge finds it only about half the time, lifting exactly-correct programs from 30% to 49% against the 88% a flawless chooser would reach. Generating candidate fixes is easy; reliably recognizing the right one is the real wall.
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.why_it_matters: When a repair pipeline stalls, measure two things separately: whether a correct fix exists among your candidates, and whether your selector picks it. Here candidates were fine, so invest in the chooser, not more edits, and skip easy warm-ups.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned selector beats the base program but stays far below the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three fresh test splits (standard, paraphrase, paired). Each split shows base compiler (low), learned selector (middle), and a flawless-chooser ceiling (high). Bar height is the share of programs that run to the exactly correct answer; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned selector's middle bar lands roughly halfway up in every split, clearly above the base near 30% but far below the roughly 90% ceiling, so most fixable errors stay unselected.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy climbs only once training reaches the full set of fixes
charts.chart_read: Line of validation accuracy per training epoch for the learned selector, with flat reference lines for the base compiler (bottom, near 32%) and the flawless chooser (top, near 85%). Epochs 1-3 use one-edit sets, 4-7 medium, 8-18 the full two-edit space; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line hugs the base rate flat through the small and medium stages, then jumps only after full-space training begins around epoch eight: the warm-up bought nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: On paired prompts, a cross-phrasing check beats the single-prompt judge
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars on the paired split for three measures: exact-run accuracy, both-phrasings-correct, and cross-phrasing consistency. Four bars each compare base compiler, learned selector, pair reranking, and the flawless-chooser ceiling; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Pair reranking tops the single-prompt selector on every measure and, unlike it, lifts consistency above the base rate rather than dropping it below (71% to 57%).

qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.verdict_tag: Frozen text-cleanup recipes miss most real tasks
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.concept_primer: Picture a fixed toolbox of preset text-reshaping tools: pull the domain from an email, take the first word, round a number. Freeze it, then hand it an outside pile of formatting jobs, keeping a tool only if it also works on rows it never saw.
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.plain_question: If you hand-build a fixed set of text-reshaping recipes, will they handle an independent, outside collection of everyday spreadsheet-cleanup tasks?
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.plain_answer: No. The frozen toolkit fully solved only 19% of the 309 outside tasks. In 77% of them no recipe fit even the worked examples, so the toolkit lacked that operation entirely; under 4% overfit. Yet a small four-billion-parameter model, given the same examples, answered a fresh case correctly 73% of the time, and two-thirds right even on tasks the toolkit missed.
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.why_it_matters: Building text normalization from a fixed rule library covers only a fraction of real formats, especially dates and numbers. Prefer letting a small model infer the transformation from examples, or add retrieval to pick domain-specific rules.

charts.chart_takeaway: The model still nails about two-thirds of the tasks the toolbox misses, showing the shortfall is the fixed recipes, not the transformations being impossible.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is a type of text operation; height is the share of those tasks the best toolbox solved. Left bars are simple surface edits, right bars are date and number rounding and ranges. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage falls from about two-in-five for plain substring edits to zero for date rounding and range tasks (number rounding barely clears one-in-twenty), exactly where the toolbox runs out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Structured formats work; dates and numbers barely do
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a task family such as dates, phone numbers, or emails; height is the share of that family the best toolbox solved. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Tidy families like shipping codes and emails reach half or more, while the two biggest families, dates and numbers, sit near the bottom and drag the total down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failures are missing recipes, not lucky-but-wrong ones
charts.chart_read: For each toolbox size, the bar splits all tasks into solved, a recipe matching the examples only by coincidence, and no recipe fitting at all. The thin coincidence slice is the point.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coincidence slice stays near 3% while 'no recipe fit' fills roughly four-fifths, so failures are genuinely missing tools, not overfit guesses.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model stumbles most on date tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a task family in the sampled set; height is how often the small model gave the exact right answer from examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Names and phone numbers hit every case while dates fall to half, the same family where the fixed recipes are weakest.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying vastly more recipes barely helps
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a toolbox size; height is how many candidate recipes it sifts through per task. A taller bar means far more work, not better results.
charts.chart_takeaway: The largest toolbox searches over 500 times more recipes than the smallest yet lifts tasks solved only from 13% to 19%, so search size is not the bottleneck.

qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.verdict_tag: Private thinking slots did no real thinking
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.concept_primer: The model runs a short arithmetic program using a fixed set of blank, silent hidden steps, then reports only the final answer with no work shown. The check: shuffle those steps. If scrambling their order changes nothing, no real step-by-step computing ever happened inside them.
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.plain_question: Can a small model learn to run little multi-step programs entirely in its head, using private silent thinking steps, instead of writing out its work as text?
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.plain_answer: No. The silent hidden steps never cleared single digits: about 8% at best on the simplest programs, 4.5% on unseen longer ones, barely above a zero untrained model and near the 3% you would get by guessing. The tell: scrambling the steps' order worked just as well, so no real step-by-step computing happened. Even visible written reasoning, which hit 62.5% on the shortest programs, crashed to 0% as they grew longer.
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.why_it_matters: Do not expect extra hidden thinking tokens to buy a small model real multi-step execution. Always run a scrambled-order control: if shuffling the hidden steps does not hurt accuracy, you added raw capacity, not reasoning. Test on unseen program lengths, not trained ones.

key_numbers.label: Written reasoning, short then long programs
key_numbers.sub: accuracy writing steps out: shortest programs versus longer ones

key_numbers.label: Silent thinking, simplest versus unseen-longer programs
key_numbers.sub: best case on trained-simple programs versus unseen longer ones

key_numbers.label: Scrambled versus ordered steps, longest programs
key_numbers.sub: shuffling the hidden steps beat keeping them ordered

key_numbers.label: Tokens spent per answer
key_numbers.sub: written-out reasoning versus silent hidden steps, per problem

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by program length: written steps versus silent hidden steps
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4, 8, 16, 24 steps). Height is final-answer accuracy; taller is better. Three bars per group: explicit written-out reasoning, silent ordered hidden steps, and silent shuffled-order hidden steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Written reasoning towers at 62.5% on the shortest programs then falls to zero by length 16, while both silent arms stay stuck in single digits everywhere, nearly identical.

charts.chart_plain_title: More hidden steps on unseen long programs: ordered versus scrambled
charts.chart_read: On unseen 32-step programs. The horizontal axis is the number of private hidden compute steps granted (4 to 32); the vertical axis is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. One line ordered, one scrambled-order.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines wander between about 2% and 5% with no upward trend as steps increase; the scrambled control even peaks highest at 4.9%, so ordered steps buy nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: More hidden steps on unfamiliar operation mixes does not help
charts.chart_read: On programs combining operations never mixed during training. The horizontal axis is the number of private hidden compute steps (4 to 32); the vertical axis is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Ordered versus scrambled-order steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines hover near 3% at every step count, dipping as low as 1%; piling on hidden compute never recovers execution when the operation mix is new.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much accuracy swings from the training random seed alone
charts.chart_read: Bars group by unseen split (length 32, composition 16, composition 24). Height is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Three bars per group, one for each of three training seeds.
charts.chart_takeaway: Just by changing the random seed, accuracy swings from about 1% to over 8% across these splits, and more than quadruples on a single split, a lottery as large as any effect the method produces.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained silent models barely clear a zero untrained baseline
charts.chart_read: Bars group by five unseen splits. Height is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Three bars per group: the untrained frozen model, the trained ordered arm, and the trained scrambled arm.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained baseline sits at exactly zero; both trained arms add only 3 to 5 points and match each other everywhere, so training bought almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tokens spent per answer: written steps versus silent hidden steps
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4, 8, 16, 24). Height is mean output tokens per problem; fewer is cheaper. Two bars: explicit written-out reasoning versus silent hidden-step execution.
charts.chart_takeaway: Written reasoning emits roughly 750 tokens per answer against 1 for silent, yet still hits 0% on the longer programs, so the cost bought no durable accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training loss never settles over 500 steps
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step (1 to 500); the vertical axis is total training loss, lower is better. Three lines, one per training seed. A learning model would trend steadily downward.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three curves stay high and jagged between 6 and 8 with no downward trend, showing the silent-execution objective was never actually learned.

qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.verdict_tag: Giving the model more detail made it worse
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.concept_primer: A repair tool hands you 64 candidate fixes for one broken program, each written as plain pseudocode with the answer it claims to produce. The test: can a small model, never told the real answer, spot the fix that actually works?
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.plain_question: If you show a small model several candidate code fixes written as readable pseudocode with their claimed outputs, can it reliably pick the one that actually works?
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.plain_answer: Barely. Training a picker on the model's reading of each fix's pseudocode plus its claimed output raised the share of tasks fixed correctly from 44% to 51%, closing only about 15% of the distance to a perfect picker's 90%. And piling on detail backfired: handing the model a step-by-step execution trace crashed accuracy from 50% down to 8%, far worse than doing nothing.
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.why_it_matters: When ranking code fixes with a small model, keep what it reads lean: plain pseudocode plus the claimed output works best, while full execution traces flood its attention and mislead. Add a safety fallback so weak picks default to leaving the program unchanged.

key_numbers.label: Doing nothing vs the best picker
key_numbers.sub: share of broken tasks fixed correctly

key_numbers.label: A perfect picker's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: if it always chose a working fix from the full pool

key_numbers.label: Adding a step-by-step execution trace
key_numbers.sub: accuracy once the trace was shown, not just pseudocode

key_numbers.label: Naive majority vote
key_numbers.sub: picking the answer most fixes agreed on

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method picks a working fix
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of broken programs each method fixes correctly; taller is better. Left to right: doing nothing, majority vote on claimed answers, plain reading of the two programs, the trained picker, then two perfect-choice ceilings (best in the shortlist, best in the full pool).
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-picker bar sits just above the do-nothing bar and far below both ceilings, so reading the fixes helps only a sliver.

charts.chart_plain_title: Giving the reader more detail, and its accuracy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the model's accuracy as it is fed more, from just the two programs on the left, through adding the claimed answer, a full execution trace, a scrambled trace, the answer alone, or the fix alone; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars fall off a cliff from about half correct to near zero, so extra detail distracts the model rather than informing it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained picker vs doing nothing vs perfect choice, across test sets
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for each test set: doing nothing, the trained picker, and the best-possible pick from the shortlist; taller is better. Compare the middle bar against the left one within each group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker meets or beats doing nothing in every set yet never reaches the perfect-choice bar, a small but consistent gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error: real answers vs scrambled answers
charts.chart_read: Lines track training error over repeated passes through the data; lower is better. Separate lines compare the reader trained on real labels against one trained on scrambled, meaningless labels.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-label lines slope downward while the scrambled line stays flat and high, showing the picker learns genuine signal rather than noise.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task batch you test on matters more than the picker
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three task-generator batches: doing nothing, the best pick from the shortlist, and the best pick from the full pool; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Do-nothing accuracy swings from zero to over 90% across batches, so nearly all the room to improve is concentrated in one batch.

charts.chart_plain_title: A safety fallback cuts damage while keeping most fixes
charts.chart_read: Paired bars for six pickers: one bar for how often it breaks an already-correct program, one for how often it fixes a broken one. Lower breakage and higher fixing are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding the fallback slashes breakage toward zero while the trained picker keeps most of its fixing, so gating buys safety cheaply.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambling the answers destroys the picker
charts.chart_read: Bars for doing nothing, the trained picker, and the same picker trained on scrambled answers, shown for two test sets; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Training on scrambled answers drops accuracy well below doing nothing on both sets, proving the picker relies on real signal.

qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.verdict_tag: A fixed toolkit solves the routine
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Picture a fixed box of LEGO bricks. The question is whether everyday builds can be snapped together from bricks already in the box, combining at most a couple, or whether some designs demand a specialty brick the box simply never contained.
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.plain_question: If you build one fixed set of small data-cleanup operations, can everyday office jobs be assembled from those parts alone, without adding anything new?
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.plain_answer: It depends, and the split is sharp. A frozen kit of reusable office operations, with no training at all, assembled 84% of realistic tasks from stored parts alone, far above the 21% a bare kit managed, and it fully solved even brand-new task types it had never seen built. But every task deliberately designed to need a missing part failed: zero solved.
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: Before investing in training around a reusable operation library, measure first what share of your real tasks can be built from existing parts. Routine cleanup covers well, but budget a path to add the missing operations.

key_numbers.label: Everyday tasks the full kit could build
key_numbers.sub: 32 of 38 realistic tasks, no training

key_numbers.label: The same tasks with only a bare kit
key_numbers.sub: most unsolvable without richer parts

key_numbers.label: Deliberately unusual tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: custom fiscal-year, discount, latest-row logic all failed

key_numbers.label: Common office cleanups solved
key_numbers.sub: 10 of 10 routine name, date, and price tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage climbs as the kit gets richer and combines more parts
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of 38 everyday tasks built from prebuilt parts, grouped left to right by how rich the kit is (bare, mid, full), with two shades for allowing one versus two combining steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars rise steadily rightward, from a stub for the bare kit to the tallest for the full kit, so a richer parts library plus more combining solves far more tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Common tasks fully solved, deliberately unusual tasks all fail
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one group of tasks; height is the share solved from prebuilt parts, so taller is better. The final bar holds tasks deliberately built to need parts the kit lacks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first four bars sit at or near full height while the final unusual-task bar drops to zero, so the kit handles familiar work but nothing beyond its parts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most tasks solved; the few failures split two ways
charts.chart_read: Three bars show what happened to all 38 tasks with the full kit: solved on fresh examples, matched only the shown examples, or never built at all. The tall solved bar is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: One tall bar dominates near 84%, with two small failure bars, so a few tasks matched only the shown examples and a few could not be built at all.

qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.verdict_tag: Step-by-step self-repair helps but most gains stay locked
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.concept_primer: Instead of a coder forced to write a flawless program in one shot, picture one who runs the code, reads the wrong output, changes a single line, runs it again, and repeats until satisfied. This trains a small model to debug its own answers that way.
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.plain_question: When a small model writes a tiny program to solve a task, does letting it run, inspect the result, and fix one line at a time beat writing it in one go?
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.plain_answer: Yes, but only partway. Letting the model run its program, read the output, and fix one line at a time roughly tripled accuracy, from about 11% to 34% on the main test and 16% to 40% on reworded prompts. But a perfect editor reaches near 100% and a fully trained program writer hits 68 to 84%, so most of the possible gain stays unclaimed.
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.why_it_matters: Guided self-repair genuinely lifts a small model's accuracy without retraining the base model, but budget the compute: the payoff is capped by how well the fixing skill is trained, not by the task being unreachable.

key_numbers.label: One-shot vs step-by-step repair
key_numbers.sub: share of answers correct on the main test set

key_numbers.label: On reworded prompts
key_numbers.sub: accuracy when the question is paraphrased

key_numbers.label: Learning from its own mistakes
key_numbers.sub: success rate across three rounds of self-correction practice

key_numbers.label: Room still left
key_numbers.sub: accuracy a perfect edit-picker reaches on the same tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step repair versus one-shot and the best-possible ceilings
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across five test sets; bar height is the share of answers correct, so taller is better. Within each set: one-shot answers, the learned step-by-step repair loop, a perfect fixer, and a fully trained program writer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The repair bar sits two to three times above the one-shot bar on every set, yet stays well below the perfect-fixer and fully-trained bars, so most possible gain is still unused.

charts.chart_plain_title: Practice rounds sharpen the model's own fixing
charts.chart_read: Three lines rising left to right across successive practice rounds; height is a success rate, so up is better. Lines track how often its own fixes worked, how often it got the numbers right, and overall answer accuracy.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines climb steadily, with getting-the-numbers-right rising most, showing that repeatedly correcting the model on the mistakes it actually makes is what drives the gains.

qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.verdict_tag: Trust the code only when it earns it
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.concept_primer: Give a model four before-and-after examples of a text cleanup and ask it to write one small reusable rule for the pattern, like a spreadsheet formula. Some rules truly generalize; others just hard-code the four examples. The trick is knowing which to trust.
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.plain_question: If a model turns a handful of examples into a small reusable rule, does running that rule beat just answering each new row directly?
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.plain_answer: Only when you check the rule first. On its own, a model writing and applying a reusable rule solved 40% of tasks perfectly versus 56% for plain row-by-row answering, and it broke six tasks direct answering had solved. Adding a gate that uses the rule only when it passes the shown examples and isn't a memorized lookup lifted success to 64%, fixing two tasks and breaking none.
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.why_it_matters: Don't deploy a self-written transformation rule blind. Run it against your known examples, reject rules that merely memorize those examples, and fall back to answering row-by-row otherwise. That gate turns a net loss into a clean gain.

key_numbers.label: Plain row-by-row answering
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks solved perfectly, every row right

key_numbers.label: Self-written rule, used blindly
key_numbers.sub: worse than answering directly; broke 6 tasks it had solved

key_numbers.label: Self-written rule with a trust gate
key_numbers.sub: fixed 2 tasks, broke none versus direct answering

key_numbers.label: Scrambled-example sanity check
key_numbers.sub: rules built from mismatched examples almost never solve a task

charts.chart_plain_title: Task success by method: the trust-gated rule wins

charts.chart_takeaway: The trust-gated rule is the tallest whole-task bar, edging out plain answering, while blind rules and the scrambled control fall far below both.

charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar counts tasks it newly solved and another counts tasks it broke that plain answering had solved, out of 25. More rescued and fewer broken is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Blind rules break six or seven tasks to win two, but the gated version keeps both wins with a zero-height broken bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task success by task type: where rules help or break
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task type such as numbers, dates, names, file paths, billing codes, and ratings; bars compare plain answering, blind rules, and the gated rule. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Blind rules genuinely lift number tasks yet flatten to zero on dates, file paths, and billing codes, while the gated bar matches whichever approach is best in every type.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting rows right versus getting whole tasks perfect
charts.chart_read: Each method has two bars: the share of individual rows correct and the share of whole tasks correct with no mistakes anywhere. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain answering loses a big chunk moving from rows to perfect whole tasks, while rule methods stay low on both because they tend to miss an entire task at once.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fitting the shown examples does not mean solving new rows
charts.chart_read: For each rule method, one bar shows how often a rule matched the few shown examples and the other how often it solved the whole task on new rows. A truly working rule would have both bars high.
charts.chart_takeaway: Blind rules match the shown examples about half the time but solve fewer new tasks, and the scrambled control fits examples while solving almost nothing, exposing false matches.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row accuracy by task type: where rules collapse
charts.chart_read: Each task type has two bars comparing plain answering with blind rules on the share of individual rows correct on new data. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Rules track plain answering on currency, email, phone, and rating tasks but collapse toward zero on dates, billing codes, and file paths even row by row.

qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.verdict_tag: One reusable recipe loses to solving cases fresh
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.concept_primer: Shown four worked examples of a text reformat — say turning "12 Jan 2002" into "2002-01-12" — a small model can tackle each new case fresh, or first write one fixed recipe and follow it blindly on every case. This tests whether committing to that recipe helps.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_question: If a small model writes one reusable rule from a few examples and applies it to every new case, does it stay more accurate than solving each case fresh?
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_answer: No. Forcing a 4-billion-parameter model to commit to one written recipe lowered accuracy: it got 59% of individual cases right versus 69% when answering each fresh, and 43% of whole tasks perfectly versus 47%. The recipes weren't nonsense — one borrowed from an unrelated task scored even lower — but a single fixed recipe still can't beat the model's case-by-case judgment.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.why_it_matters: Don't make a small model freeze one rule and apply it mechanically; its per-case reasoning beats any single rule it can articulate. And checking the rule against the practice examples cannot catch this — every failing rule fit the examples perfectly.

key_numbers.label: Individual cases right: fresh vs one fixed recipe
key_numbers.sub: new cases exactly correct when answering each directly vs committing to one written recipe

key_numbers.label: Whole tasks fully correct: fresh vs one fixed recipe
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where every new case is exact

key_numbers.label: Recipe borrowed from an unrelated task
key_numbers.sub: individual cases right — below both real approaches, so the recipes do carry genuine task signal, just not enough

key_numbers.label: Best-possible step breakdown vs flat formula
key_numbers.sub: top achievable whole-task accuracy from an automatic formula library, but the version actually picked from examples ties the flat one at 23%

charts.chart_plain_title: Committing to one recipe does not beat solving cases one at a time

charts.chart_takeaway: Solving fresh is tallest on both counts; committing to one recipe drops individual-case accuracy from 69% to 59%, and a borrowed recipe falls slightly lower still.

charts.chart_read: Individual-case accuracy for the same three approaches, taller better. Two bars each compare a first attempt using one recipe against a second attempt that writes three recipe styles and keeps the one best matching the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even keeping the best-matching recipe, the recipe approach stays below solving cases one at a time, so more recipe attempts did not help.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step formulas only help cleanly separated task types
charts.chart_read: For each type of task, two bars show the best-possible perfect-task rate, taller better: a one-shot formula versus a step-by-step formula.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-by-step jumps to 100% on neatly delimited types like logs and web addresses, while dates and numbers barely move.

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking a recipe from a few examples loses most of the step-by-step gain
charts.chart_read: For the ten largest task types, two bars: the best step-by-step recipe available versus the one actually chosen from the few examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Example-picking loses the entire gain on addresses and cities and a large share on names and phones, keeping full coverage only on emails.

charts.chart_plain_title: Locking a recipe rescues two tasks but wrecks several already solved
charts.chart_read: For each task where locking a recipe changed the outcome, two bars compare individual-case accuracy: solving fresh versus committing to one recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Locking rescues two date tasks but collapses number tasks the model had solved outright, dropping some from 100% to 17% or even 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Matching the practice examples cannot tell which recipes will fail
charts.chart_read: For the ten worst tasks, two bars: how perfectly the recipe matched the practice examples versus how well it then worked on new cases. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe matched the practice examples perfectly yet many scored zero on new cases, so fitting the examples cannot flag a recipe that will fail.

qwen_register_structured_runtime.verdict_tag: Nearly-right steps compound into wrong long answers
qwen_register_structured_runtime.concept_primer: The model never says the answer. It writes a step-by-step arithmetic program into a fixed row of hidden memory slots — a starting number, then one operation each — and a fixed calculator runs the slots in order, blindly, unable to notice or fix a wrong one.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_question: Can a small model secretly write a step-by-step math program into hidden memory slots and have a fixed calculator run it correctly, even for long chains?
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_answer: Only up to a point. For chains of four to twelve steps the hidden program runs flawlessly, at 100 percent. But at 24 steps exact execution collapses to 25 percent — versus about 1 percent from pure guessing. The catch: each individual step is 80 to 94 percent right, yet a single wrong step anywhere breaks the whole answer, so mostly-right steps still add up to a mostly-wrong long program.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.why_it_matters: If you route reasoning through hidden slots run by a fixed engine, expect a hard length cliff: 90-percent-per-step still fails most long chains. Measure whole-chain exactness, not per-step accuracy, and add a repair pass over long programs before trusting them.

key_numbers.label: Long-chain execution
key_numbers.sub: exact execution, going from 12 steps to 24 steps

key_numbers.label: Each step correct
key_numbers.sub: yet the whole 24-step program is right only 25% of the time

key_numbers.label: Boost from consistency training

charts.chart_takeaway: The starting number is perfect and each piece scores 80 to 94 percent, but full-program and final accuracy crash to 25 percent as small errors compound.

qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.verdict_tag: Almost every step right, whole program still wrong
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.concept_primer: The model writes a step-by-step calculation into a fixed row of blank slots — a starting number, then one operation and one number per step. A hidden calculator reads only those slots, never the original question, and runs them exactly.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model quietly encode a runnable step-by-step calculation into a fixed set of hidden slots, so a separate calculator can then execute it correctly?
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_answer: Only for short chains. Up to twelve steps it builds the correct hidden program about nine times in ten, while stripped-down versions trained on the final answer alone never find the interface and stay at chance. But at twenty-four steps whole-program accuracy crashes to 22 percent — each slot is still about 90 to 97 percent right, yet many near-misses multiply into failure.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.why_it_matters: When you route reasoning through predicted or latent steps, per-step accuracy flatters you — measure whole-chain correctness and agreement across rephrasings. For reliable long chains, train directly against full-trajectory errors, not just the final answer.

key_numbers.sub: long-chain collapse on standard prompts

key_numbers.label: Each program slot at twenty-four steps
key_numbers.sub: yet only 22% of full programs run correctly

key_numbers.label: Final-answer-only training

key_numbers.label: Two wordings of one task agree

charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines sit near 90 to 95 percent through twelve steps, then plunge at twenty-four — standard to 22 percent, reworded to 3 percent: short chains work, long ones break.

charts.chart_plain_title: At twenty-four steps, trained interface still beats both controls
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across three twenty-four-step splits (standard, reworded, paired). Each split has three bars: the step-by-step-trained compiler versus a direct-answer control and an answer-only control. Taller is better; guessing scores about 1 percent.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained run tops every split (22, 3, 13 percent) while both controls sit near chance (0 to 3 percent): step-by-step training, not final-answer training, built the interface.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every part is right, the whole is wrong
charts.chart_read: Bars for five measures at twenty-four steps — starting value, operation, argument, whole-program exact, and executed answer. Height is accuracy, higher is better. Two colors: standard prompts and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars stand near 90 to 97 percent, but whole-program and executed-answer bars crater to about 22 and 3 percent: all twenty-four parts must land at once.

charts.chart_plain_title: When each chain length starts working during training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step (1 to 600); the vertical axis is executed-answer accuracy, higher is better. Four lines track chain lengths 4, 8, 12, and 24 as the curriculum lengthens the chains.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines hug zero until step 300, then short and medium lengths leap together toward 90 percent while length twenty-four crawls, reaching only 22 percent by the end.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the final recipe moved long-chain accuracy off zero
charts.chart_read: One bar per development run in order — five earlier pilot configurations, then the main run. Height is standard twenty-four-step executed-answer accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Five pilots all stalled at 0 to 3 percent; only the final 600-step curriculum run with adapters and step-by-step supervision reached 22 percent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Two phrasings stop agreeing at twenty-four steps
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is chain length; the vertical axis, higher is better, shows two rates on paired prompts: how often both phrasings reach the same hidden program, and how often both are correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: Same-program agreement holds near 98 to 99 percent through twelve steps, while the both-correct rate runs lower and bumpier at 88 to 96 percent; at twenty-four both collapse near chance, about 3 and 2 percent: identical tasks now compile to different hidden programs.

qwen_register_trace_refiner.verdict_tag: Even a perfect picker can't rescue a too
qwen_register_trace_refiner.concept_primer: A small model rewrites each math word problem as a tiny step-by-step program a calculator runs exactly. When the program is wrong, a repair loop swaps one or two steps for runner-up guesses and keeps whichever runs cleanest, never seeing the answer. A badly wrong program needs more swaps than that.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_question: If a small model turns a math word problem into a little program and gets it wrong, can trying a batch of nearby edits and keeping the best one fix it?
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_answer: Rarely. Even a flawless picker that always grabbed the correct edit reached only 37% on plainly worded problems and 7% on reworded ones, because the correct program usually isn't among the roughly 1,300 nearby edits at all. The trained picker did worse, nudging 23% to 27% and leaving reworded problems stuck near 5%.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.why_it_matters: Before training a scorer to rescue a small model's outputs, first check whether a perfect chooser could even win: the correct answer must exist in your candidate set. If it rarely does, widen or steer the search instead of tuning the scorer.

key_numbers.label: Plainly worded problems, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: raw model vs the trained repair loop

key_numbers.label: Best possible with perfect picking
key_numbers.sub: ceiling on plain problems; the correct fix is present only this often

key_numbers.label: Reworded problems
key_numbers.sub: repair loop gave no lift; even a perfect picker reaches only 7%

key_numbers.label: Correct fix among the edits, reworded
key_numbers.sub: how often the right program appears among ~1,300 nearby edits

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy on fresh problems: raw model, repair loop, and best possible
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three problem types: plainly worded, reworded, and matched pairs. Each group shows three bars: the raw model, the trained repair loop, and a perfect picker. Bar height is the share of problems solved correctly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The repair loop barely clears the raw model and adds nothing on reworded problems, while the perfect-picker bar towers over both, so the ceiling, not the scorer, is the wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the correct program even exists among the tried edits
charts.chart_read: One bar per split: training, validation, fresh plainly worded, fresh reworded, fresh matched pairs. Height is the share of problems whose correct program appears somewhere among the roughly 1,300 nearby edits; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even on plainly worded problems the correct fix is present only about a third of the time, and on reworded ones almost never (7%), which caps any picker.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of the reachable gain the repair loop actually captures
charts.chart_read: One bar per fresh split: plainly worded, reworded, matched pairs. Height is the fraction of the gap between raw accuracy and perfect-picker accuracy that the trained repair loop closed; higher means more of the reachable gain captured.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even where good candidates exist, the loop captures under a quarter of the reachable gain on plainly worded problems, less on pairs, and none on reworded ones.

qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.verdict_tag: Perfect one-step fixes still won't teach planning
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.concept_primer: Picture a repair apprentice fixing a program one instruction at a time: it edits, runs the code, reads the result, edits again. Handed the correct single fix at every dead-end, it learns only local corrections, never a whole plan that reaches working code.
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_question: If you show a code-editing model the verified correct fix for every mistake it makes, will it learn to reliably solve whole tasks?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_answer: No. An automatic search found a verified correct fix for 98% of the dead-ends the model wandered into, yet retraining on those single fixes matched or trailed the simpler training on four of five test sets. The model could pick the right next edit 60% of the time but solved at most 37.5% of whole tasks. Local fixes never became a winning plan.
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.why_it_matters: If you build a step-by-step editing agent, don't expect verified next-step fixes to teach it. The hard part is planning a full sequence that ends in a passing result: reward whole runs, not isolated single edits.

key_numbers.label: Verified fixes found for nearly every dead-end
key_numbers.sub: of stuck states got a correct repair label, so labels were never the bottleneck

key_numbers.label: Picking the next edit versus solving the whole task
key_numbers.sub: right-next-edit rate versus best whole-task solve rate, the planning gap it could not close

key_numbers.label: What a perfect edit sequence reaches on these tasks
key_numbers.sub: solve rate of an all-knowing teacher, proving the tasks are solvable with the right sequence

key_numbers.label: Edit-and-run beat one-shot answering on reworded tasks
key_numbers.sub: plain model versus the step-by-step editor, the one real win the fix-retraining then eroded

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task solve rate by test set, four solver versions
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one test set. Bars compare the plain model answering directly, the editor trained the normal way, the editor retrained on found fixes, and a perfect edit-sequence teacher. Taller means more tasks solved, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-retrained bars sit at or below the normally-trained bars on most test sets, while the perfect-teacher bars tower far above every trained version.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does allowing more edits raise the solve rate?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many edits the model is allowed; the vertical axis is the share of tasks solved. Each line is one test set, and a higher line means more solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only one test set climbs as more edits are allowed; the rest stay flat or drop, so extra editing does not reliably help.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-step correctness across the training stages
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training stage, from basic training epochs to fix-retraining epochs. Colors show three kinds of per-step correctness: picking the edit type, its details, and when to stop. Taller means more steps right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Picking the right edit peaked during basic training and fell after fix-retraining, and stronger per-step scores never produced better whole solutions.

qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.verdict_tag: Knowing the answer is reachable doesn't find it
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.concept_primer: The system writes a tiny program one instruction at a time, never allowed to see the target answer, like assembling a recipe blind and unable to taste it. A scorer rates each half-built program. Teaching it merely "this could still turn out right" proved too loose to pick the true winner.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_question: When a system builds a program step by step without seeing the answer, does scoring steps by whether the answer stays reachable beat exact-match scoring?
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_answer: No. Scoring each step by whether a correct answer is still reachable pushed the top pick to about 68 percent, level with plain confidence search and short of the 71 percent from scoring steps against the known correct program. The "still reachable" signal was genuinely learnable but far too permissive: about one step in three qualifies, so it cannot single out the real winner.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.why_it_matters: When guiding search without the answer, don't train the scorer on yes/no "could still succeed" labels, since too many steps qualify. Instead predict how good the best reachable finish would be, a graded score, so the ranker can isolate near-certain wins.

key_numbers.label: Right program already in the shortlist
key_numbers.sub: yet the top pick is correct only 69% of the time

key_numbers.label: Best no-answer scorer, exact match
key_numbers.sub: a small gain from scoring against the known correct program

key_numbers.label: Reachability scorer
key_numbers.sub: no gain over plain confidence search

key_numbers.label: Steps marked 'answer still reachable'
key_numbers.sub: vs 1 in 13 that exactly match — too loose a target

charts.chart_plain_title: Which way of picking a program gets the answer right most
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the final answer is correct (taller is better) across five ways of choosing a program, for everyday paired tasks and harder multi-step tasks. The last bar is allowed to peek at the answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only exact-match scoring nudges the everyday bar upward; reachability scoring sits level with plain confidence search, and every method trails far below the answer-peeking bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: The right program is usually generated but ranked too low
charts.chart_read: For everyday and harder tasks, three bars compare the first pick, the best program sitting anywhere in the candidate list, and an answer-verified pick. Taller is better; the gap between first pick and best-in-list is wasted potential.
charts.chart_takeaway: Best-in-list towers over first-pick, so the correct program is usually generated but ranked below wrong ones — a ranking failure, not a generation one.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each rule labels a program step as good
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of program steps each labeling rule marks as good; a taller bar is a broader, looser target. Exact matching is strict, raw reachability is loose, and filtered reachability sits in between.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw reachability marks most steps good, about seven times as many as exact matching, giving the scorer a much looser and harder-to-use target.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both scorers learn to tell good steps from bad
charts.chart_read: Bars rate each scorer's skill at sorting good program steps from bad on fresh data, from a coin-flip (half) up to perfect (one); taller is better. Left is exact-match scoring, right is reachability scoring.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit far above a coin-flip and the exact-match scorer is cleaner, yet even it converts to only a small gain on real tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tightening the reachability labels across trial runs
charts.chart_read: Across four successive trial runs, two bars each show the share of steps marked good by exact matching versus reachability. Lower reachability bars mean a tighter, less permissive target.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reachability bars shrink run over run, from most steps down to about a third, while the exact bars stay low and flat — the target was progressively tightened.

qwen_shared_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: Its hidden math is readable, but fragile
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.concept_primer: A language model silently reads a chain of wrap-around arithmetic steps. A small attached reader taps its internal activity, transcribes each step into a machine recipe — start value, operation, number — and a calculator runs the recipe. The model itself never writes the answer.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_question: When a language model quietly works through a multi-step math problem in its head, can a small add-on read its internal activity and rebuild the exact steps it took?
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_answer: Only when every step is taught directly. Given step-by-step labels, the add-on rebuilds short programs well — nearly 4 in 5 four-step problems run exactly right — but accuracy fades to 39% at twelve steps and under 1% at twenty-four. Reword the same problems and it collapses toward guessing. Training on final answers alone never finds this ability at all.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: To pull runnable, auditable structure out of a frozen model, supervise every intermediate step — rewarding only the final answer neither discovers nor preserves it. And expect brittleness: what works on the trained wording and length will not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rebuilding each step beats reading only the answer, until programs get long
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the problem has; vertical axis is how often the run gives the exactly correct answer, higher is better. One line teaches the reader each step; two lines read only the final answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-taught line starts high and slides down as programs lengthen, while both answer-reading lines stay flat at the bottom near random guessing throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same programs, new wording drops accuracy toward guessing
charts.chart_read: Both lines are the same step-taught reader on identical math programs; one uses the exact training wording, the other reworded prompts. Vertical axis is how often the answer is exactly right, higher is better; horizontal is program length.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reworded line lies flat near guessing at every length while the trained-wording line rides far above it — the reader keyed on phrasing, not meaning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra final-answer-only training erases the taught steps
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at each program length compare the working step-taught reader before extra training, after training on final answers only, and after the same training at a gentler pace. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After answer-only training the bars for longer programs fall to the floor; even the gentle version keeps only the four-step case standing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny per-step reading errors pile up across a long program
charts.chart_read: Three lines track the step-taught reader by program length: how often each operation is read right, how often each number is read right, and how often the whole program is exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Per-step reading stays high, but the whole-program line falls far faster — one wrong step anywhere spoils the answer, so small errors compound.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deeper internal notes and careful number-finding run longer programs
charts.chart_read: Each line is a different reader design, scored by how often the run gives the correct answer across program lengths, higher is better. Designs differ in how deep inside the model they read and how they locate each step's number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reading deeper internal activity and pinning each number beside its operation lift the lines highest, while the shallowest design dies past four steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence in the right answer falls to chance beyond four steps
charts.chart_read: Two lines by program length show how much probability the step-taught system versus a plain answer-reader places on the true answer. Higher is better; both settle onto a low floor marking random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-taught reader leads only at four steps, then drops to meet the answer-reader at random-guess level — past four steps its edge disappears.

qwen_slot_repair_distillation.verdict_tag: Fixable programs exist; the editor can't find them
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.concept_primer: A model writes short number-crunching programs that run correctly only about a quarter of the time. A fix almost always sits one or two small edits away. But a helper must spot which pieces are wrong from the program alone, with no answer key to check against.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_question: If a computer usually writes a slightly-wrong program, can a small model learn to spot and fix the few wrong pieces on its own, without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_answer: No. A correct program almost always sits one or two edits away — a search that peeks at the answer lifts solve rates from about a quarter to roughly 86%. But the blind helper couldn't pick which edits to make: on reworded problems it dropped accuracy from 25% to 19%, and on fresh problems it barely moved. It only helped on the exact problems it was tuned on.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.why_it_matters: Before bolting a learned fixer onto a model's output, test it on fresh and reworded inputs, and keep a do-nothing baseline. A high "a fix exists nearby" ceiling proves fixes are reachable — not that a blind one-shot patcher can find them.

key_numbers.label: Fixes are almost always reachable
key_numbers.sub: a search that checks the answer lifts solve rate this far above the base program

key_numbers.label: Reworded problems after editing
key_numbers.sub: the helper made paraphrased problems worse, not better

key_numbers.label: Tuning-set accuracy after editing
key_numbers.sub: the only split where editing helped, and only slightly

key_numbers.label: Same answer to both phrasings
key_numbers.sub: editing nearly halved agreement across two wordings of a problem

charts.chart_plain_title: Editor helps only on its tuning set, not on new problems
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars of how often programs run correctly across four problem sets — the tuning set plus three sets of fresh problems. Each set has three bars: the untouched base program, the trained editor's fix, and the answer-key repair ceiling. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor bar tops the base only on the tuning set; on every fresh set it ties or dips below, all far under the roughly 85% ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the fixable gap the editor actually closed
charts.chart_read: One bar per problem set showing the share of the base-to-ceiling gap the editor captured. Positive means it fixed programs; negative means it broke them; zero is doing nothing. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars are positive on the training and tuning sets but turn negative on all three fresh sets, worst at minus ten percent on rewordings — the skill doesn't transfer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editor only edges past the baseline on the final round
charts.chart_read: A line of the editor's tuning-set accuracy across 18 training rounds against a flat line for the untouched base program. Above the flat line is better; higher means more programs run correctly.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor line bounces between 13% and 35% and only clears the flat base line on the last round — the win is fragile, not a settled skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing makes answers more sensitive to wording
charts.chart_read: For pairs of prompts asking the same question two ways, bars for the base program, the answer-key repairs, and the editor. Two series: giving the same answer to both wordings, and getting both right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor's same-answer bar falls to about 38% from the base's 70%, while answer-key repairs lift it to 88% — the editor's patches make rewordings disagree.

charts.chart_plain_title: No editor recipe wins on both the tuning set and fresh problems
charts.chart_read: For six editor setups, paired bars compare tuning-set accuracy against fresh-problem accuracy. Higher is better; a good recipe would raise both bars together.
charts.chart_takeaway: No setup wins both — the main run tops tuning accuracy at 35% yet a smaller value-stabilized pilot beats it on fresh problems, 36% versus 28%.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model never reliably learns which parts to change
charts.chart_read: Two lines across 18 training rounds: how precisely the editor targets the parts to change (of edits it makes, how many were right) and how completely (of needed edits, how many it made). Higher and steadier is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Precision swings between 45% and 71% round to round and never settles — the choice of which few parts to edit stays unreliable throughout training.

qwen_slot_stability_compiler.verdict_tag: A bolted-on calculator beats teaching the model arithmetic
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.concept_primer: The small model never does the arithmetic in its head. A helper reads its internal signals, points at the exact starting number and each operation in the prompt, copies them out, and types them into a hidden calculator that runs every step exactly.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_question: Can you get reliable step-by-step arithmetic from a small model by pulling out the numbers and operations and running them through a calculator, instead of letting it answer directly?
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_answer: Yes, mostly. The point-and-compute helper solved about 91% of short problems and around half of medium ones, while training the same model to just emit the final answer never beat random guessing, about 1 in 60, at any length. But an extra rule forcing two rewordings of a problem to agree was a wash: it helped the longest reworded chains yet hurt agreement on medium ones.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.why_it_matters: For narrow step-by-step symbolic work, wire a small model to an exact calculator and supervise the extracted steps, rather than fine-tuning it to output answers. Don't expect a cross-wording agreement penalty to fix errors that compound over long chains.

charts.chart_read: Bars compare the two versions on medium-long reworded pairs, halfway through training and at the end. The left group is exact-answer accuracy, the right group is how often both wordings agree. One bar per group uses the rule, the other does not. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Halfway through, the rule's version agrees far more often, 36% versus 5%, but by the end both versions land together near 69%: the early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is problem length. Three upper lines track how often each piece is identified correctly, the starting number, the operations, and the argument numbers; the bottom line is getting the whole sequence exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Individual pieces stay near-perfect, but the whole-sequence line collapses to zero on the longest problems: one small slip anywhere ruins the entire chain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded-problem accuracy: most is gained in the second half of training
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem length, for reworded prompts. Each of the two methods is measured halfway through training and at the end. Height is how often the exact answer is right. Taller is better; compare halfway against end within each method.
charts.chart_takeaway: End-of-training bars tower over halfway bars at medium and long lengths: the second half of training is where the reworded problems actually get solved.

qwen_span_free_compiler.verdict_tag: Works only when you show it where to
qwen_span_free_compiler.concept_primer: A frozen language model silently reads a math word-problem and scribbles private notes across a long strip. A small add-on translator turns those notes into exact calculator steps, pointing out the starting number, each operation, and each amount, though nobody ever tells it where in the notes they sit.
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_question: Can a small add-on read a frozen model's internal notes and reconstruct the exact math steps of a problem, without being told where the numbers and operations sit?
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_answer: Only when it is first taught where to look. Fed just the frozen model's raw internal notes, a plain reader stayed near random guessing (about 1 in 97). Adding training that also highlighted which spots held the numbers and operations reached 99.6% fully-correct programs at the practiced length. But reword the prompt, or add more steps, and accuracy collapses back to chance.
qwen_span_free_compiler.why_it_matters: Frozen small models really do carry recoverable program structure in their internals, but only a narrow, brittle slice. Do not expect an extracted interface to survive reworded prompts or longer inputs unless training explicitly covers those cases.

Each line is one build of the system. The two trace-trained lines are high only at the practiced four-step length and crash toward zero beyond it, while the two control builds sit at random-guess level at every length.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two trace-trained lines plunge from near-perfect at four steps to near-zero at eight, twelve, and twenty-four steps, while the control lines never leave random-guess level, showing the skill is locked to one length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the problem breaks the translator's number-reading
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by what the translator had to read out: the starting number, each operation, each amount, and the whole sequence. Height is accuracy; taller is better. Practiced-wording bars sit near the top; reworded-prompt bars are much shorter except for the starting number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded bars stay tall only for the starting number and roughly halve for operations and amounts, dragging the whole-sequence bar down to almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Good step-by-step accuracy still fails the whole chain
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is accuracy; higher is better. Two lines track how often each single operation and each single amount is right; the third tracks solving the whole sequence. The per-step lines stay fairly high while the whole-sequence line sinks fast.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even with each step around 90% right at twelve steps, the whole-sequence line drops under 6%, because small per-step errors pile up.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only teaching the translator where to focus works
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of building the translator; height is how often it solved the whole practiced sequence exactly. Taller is better. The three simpler designs that just pool everything sit at the floor; the two that learn where to focus stand near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy jumps from random-guess level to near-perfect only for the two designs that learn where to point, marking focus as the key ingredient.

charts.chart_plain_title: No training schedule escapes the longer-chain cliff
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. Each line is a different training schedule. All lines collapse toward the floor by twelve steps, and some start low even at short chains.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every schedule falls to near-zero by twelve steps, and the fixed and mixed schedules give up much of their short-chain accuracy too.

charts.chart_plain_title: Robustness to rewording trades against raw accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training schedule, with one bar for practiced wording and one for reworded wording; height is whole-sequence accuracy, taller is better. High-accuracy schedules show a tall practiced bar beside a floor-level reworded bar; the milder schedules show two similar mid-height bars.
charts.chart_takeaway: The most accurate schedules drop from near-perfect to random-guess level under rewording, while milder schedules hold similar accuracy both ways but never climb as high.

charts.chart_plain_title: Translator locks onto practiced wording early, never learns rewording
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training progress; vertical axis is how often the whole sequence is solved; higher is better. One line is practiced wording, the other reworded wording. The practiced line shoots up early and flattens near the top; the reworded line stays pinned at the bottom throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The practiced-wording line reaches near-perfect within the first stretch and holds, while the reworded line never lifts off random-guess level.

qwen_state_ladder_compiler.verdict_tag: Easy-to-hard practice won; grading every step didn't
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.concept_primer: Picture a long chain of add, subtract, and multiply, always wrapping around at 97 like a clock. One slip anywhere ruins the answer. The test forced the model to show and be graded on its running total after every single step, not just the end.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_question: If you make a model show and get graded on its running total after every single step, does it follow long calculations more reliably?
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_answer: No. Grading the running total after every step never beat an identical model graded only on its final answer, and at full strength it collapsed on the hardest long programs. The real winner was the training schedule: starting on short chains and working up to 24-step ones lifted the hardest cases from near-random to about 1 in 5, and standard cases to about 2 in 5.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.why_it_matters: For long chains of exact steps, invest in a gradual difficulty ramp rather than dense per-step grading, which can overfit and turn brittle on reworded problems. And save the model that scores best on the hardest cases, not whichever version trained last.

key_numbers.label: Longest chains solved exactly
key_numbers.sub: 24-step programs: plain final-answer model versus the model trained short-to-long

key_numbers.label: Step-grading on the hardest split
key_numbers.sub: hardest 24-step test (same program in two wordings); step-grading did worse than no grading

key_numbers.label: Full-strength grading on reworded problems
key_numbers.sub: same model with full step-grading collapsed to zero; without grading it held near 16%

key_numbers.label: Guessing rate
key_numbers.sub: every answer is some number from 0 to 96

charts.chart_plain_title: Gradual practice lifts long chains; step-grading never beats the control
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three kinds of 24-step problems: standard wording, reworded, and both wordings paired. Bars compare a plain final-answer model, a gradual-practice model, and two step-grading strengths. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-model bars sit near random-guess level while gradual-practice towers over them; no step-grading bar tops the gradual-practice control, and full-strength grading vanishes on reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls as the programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Lines track how often the whole program is worked exactly right as length grows from 4 to 24 steps, one line per training method. Higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines start together high and slide down as chains lengthen, with the full-strength step-grading line dropping fastest to the lowest point at 24 steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best moment during training on the hardest chains
charts.chart_read: One bar per method showing its peak accuracy on the hardest 24-step problems during training, not the final saved model. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At its peak the lighter step-grading bar is tallest, but that moment was never saved, so it doesn't count as a real win.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same problem, two wordings: does the work match?
charts.chart_read: One bar per method showing how often the same 24-step problem, written two different ways, produced identical step-by-step work. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Full-strength step-grading nearly vanishes near zero while the no-grading control stands highest, showing heavy grading made the model inconsistent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-grading's early lead vanishes with more training
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare the step-grading model and the control on the hardest 24-step problems, first after short training then after long training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-grading leads at the short budget but slips below the control after longer training, so its early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full step-grading fails completely on reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars compare accuracy on standard versus reworded 24-step problems for three methods; taller is better, and a small gap between the two means the method handles new wording.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method dips on reworded problems, but full step-grading collapses to zero while lighter or no grading holds near one in seven.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-chain skill appears only in the final training stage
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy on the hardest 24-step problems across training steps for each method; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line hugs zero until the final long-chain stage, spikes around step 800, then dips, so the last saved model is not the best one.

charts.chart_plain_title: Near-perfect parts still add up to failed whole programs
charts.chart_read: Bars show how accurately each piece of a program is copied (starting value, operations, arguments) versus getting the entire 24-step program exactly right. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Per-piece bars sit near perfect while whole-program bars sink to about one in five, as tiny scattered argument slips compound over 24 steps.

qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.verdict_tag: Graded practice, not clever expansion, builds long programs
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.concept_primer: The model turns each problem into a 24-step recipe of clock-style arithmetic — numbers wrapping like hours on a dial — which a built-in calculator then runs. Like a pianist handed the whole score but drilled on easy passages first, it needs graded practice, not special wiring.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_question: What makes a small model write long, 24-step arithmetic programs: practicing easy-to-hard, or a trick that grows short programs by copying earlier steps into new ones?
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_answer: It's the practice schedule. Give the model its full size from the start, then feed examples easy-to-hard — 8 steps, then 16, then 24 — and it solves nearly every standard 24-step program (about 97%). The popular guess, growing short programs by copying earlier steps into the new ones, managed under 2%. And training only up to 16 steps, then testing at 24, got exactly zero.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.why_it_matters: To teach a small model a hard multi-step task, invest in an easy-to-hard training schedule at full capacity, not tricks that stitch long programs from copied pieces — and never assume it handles lengths beyond what you trained on.

key_numbers.label: Winning recipe on the hardest test
