key_numbers.label: Copy-and-grow recipe on the same test
key_numbers.sub: the popular explanation, refuted here

key_numbers.label: Trained only to 16 steps, tested at 24
key_numbers.sub: no ability to stretch past the trained length

key_numbers.label: Getting matched twin problems both right
key_numbers.sub: winning recipe versus every other recipe at 24 steps

charts.chart_plain_title: How each training approach does on the hardest 24-step programs
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training approach; the three bars show 24-step accuracy for standard wording, unfamiliar wording, and matched twin-prompt pairs. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-size easy-to-hard approach towers over all others on standard wording and twin-prompts; copy-and-grow only shows up on unfamiliar wording.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy stays high until programs reach full length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length from 8 to 24 steps; each line is one training approach; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every approach stays high (81% or better) at 8 and 16 steps, then all but the full-size easy-to-hard approach collapse toward zero at 24 steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: The winner's 24-step skill appears late in training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the lines track the winning approach's 24-step accuracy for standard wording, unfamiliar wording, and twin-prompt pairs; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines stay low, then jump sharply in the final stretch — the long-program skill emerges suddenly, not gradually.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded prompts flip which approaches look good
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a training approach; two bars compare 24-step accuracy on standard wording versus reworded-but-familiar prompts; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The copy-and-grow approach scores 75% on reworded prompts but under 2% on standard ones — wording, not just the approach, drives failures.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the winning approach answers twin prompts consistently
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a training approach; the bars show how often it gets both halves of a matched prompt pair right at 24 steps, for familiar and unfamiliar wording; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every approach except the winner drops to essentially zero — none of the others can reliably nail both twin prompts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failing approaches trace most steps but botch the final answer
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a training approach; one bar is final-answer accuracy, the other is the share of intermediate steps traced correctly, on standard 24-step programs; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Losing approaches keep 62-86% of intermediate steps right while final answers sink near zero — errors cluster in the last few steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Short-program skill dips mid-training as longer programs arrive
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the lines show accuracy on short, unfamiliar-wording programs for three approaches as longer programs enter training; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The winning approach sags mid-training as longer programs arrive, then rebounds to about 91%, while one control ends lower near 58%.

qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.concept_primer: Rather than writing steps out as text and picking the best-looking guess, the model fills a fixed form — a starting number plus a list of operations — that a calculator then runs. For longer problems, that filled form is copied into a bigger one with more blank rows.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_question: Can a model directly fill in a runnable calculation program instead of writing it out as text, and can that program be stretched to handle much longer calculations?
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_answer: Yes, mostly. The model fills fixed slots with operations a calculator runs — no text, no trying many guesses and picking one. After the learned short form was copied into bigger ones, it stayed perfectly correct on 8- and 16-step chains and hit about 83% on the hardest 24-step ones, where reworded versions of a problem still computed identically.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.why_it_matters: For structured or runnable outputs, let a small model fill fixed operation slots directly rather than generate text and rerank guesses — it is cheaper, repeatable, and you add capacity by copying weights into more slots. Expect some slippage on the longest chains.

key_numbers.label: Shortest chains, 8 and 16 steps

key_numbers.label: Longest chains, 24 steps
key_numbers.sub: accuracy on the hardest, longest problems after growing the slots

key_numbers.label: Reworded 24-step problems
key_numbers.sub: a differently phrased problem produces the same computation

key_numbers.label: Both of a reworded pair correct, 24 steps
key_numbers.sub: matched problem pairs where both answers came out right

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the grown solver got the right answer, by problem length
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the final grown solver reached the right answer for 8-, 16-, and 24-step problems, with three bars each for the plain, reworded, and paired phrasings. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars stay at full height for 8- and 16-step problems and dip only for the longest, so stretching the solver cost almost nothing on what it already handled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as longer problems were added in stages
charts.chart_read: Lines track answer accuracy over training for the plain phrasing, one line per problem length; each line begins when that length is first introduced. Higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each length's line holds near the top as longer ones are added — the 16-step line even climbs to perfect — so adding longer problems never dragged shorter ones down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do reworded versions of a problem solve the same way?
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show three checks — both rewordings correct, the same internal steps, and matching partial steps — for 8-, 16-, and 24-step problems. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Consistency holds at full height for 8- and 16-step problems and eases only to about seven-eighths for the longest, so rewordings mostly solve the same way.

qwen_structured_bridge.verdict_tag: Compile the steps, don't guess the answer
qwen_structured_bridge.concept_primer: Picture a frozen reader that understands the words but cannot do the math. A small trainable translator turns the reader's internal impressions into calculator instructions, and a separate pocket calculator actually crunches them. The reader stays frozen; only the translator learns.
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_question: Can a frozen small model's read of a word problem be turned into a runnable step-by-step program that beats simply asking it for the final answer?
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_answer: Yes, but only when you show it the individual steps during training. A tiny translator turning the frozen model's read into calculator instructions solved chains far longer than it trained on: 96% correct at twelve steps and about 88% at twenty-four, versus roughly 1 in 100 when the same model just names the final answer. Rewarding correct answers alone never found the trick.
qwen_structured_bridge.why_it_matters: For multi-step symbolic tasks, don't make a frozen small model blurt out the final answer. Train a light add-on to emit structured operations for a separate calculator. You gain big length generalization cheaply, but you must supply step-level supervision.

key_numbers.label: Solving twelve-step problems
key_numbers.sub: guessing the answer vs. compiling the steps

key_numbers.label: Trained short, tested long
key_numbers.sub: after training only on programs of up to four steps

key_numbers.label: Learning from the final answer alone
key_numbers.sub: never discovered the step interface

key_numbers.label: Reading the numbers correctly
key_numbers.sub: remaining slips were wrong operations, not misread numbers

charts.chart_plain_title: Compiling the steps wins; asking for the answer stays at guessing
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare three approaches at chains of 4, 8, and 12 operations (left to right); bar height is the share of answers fully correct, taller is better. Only the step-compiling bars rise near the top; the two answer-guessing versions sit on the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-trained translator towers near 100% across every chain length while both the direct-answer and reward-only versions flatline at roughly one-in-a-hundred guessing level.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy fades gently on chains far longer than training
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as chains grow from 4 to 24 operations, well past the up-to-four steps seen in training; higher and flatter is better. Two lines for share fully correct sit on top of each other; a third tracks the model's confidence in the right answer. All drift down only slightly toward the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy slopes down gradually to about 88% at 24 operations, six times the training depth, showing graceful decline rather than a collapse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-chain slips come from misreading an operation, not the numbers
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each piece of the compiled program is right at 24 operations: the starting number, the operation, its number, and the whole chain end to end; taller is better. The first three are near-perfect, the last is lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: Numbers are read essentially perfectly, so the roughly one-in-eight failed chains trace to the occasional wrong operation, whose errors pile up over many steps.

qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.verdict_tag: Models can ace tasks while ignoring your examples
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.concept_primer: Think of the worked examples in a prompt as a recipe card handed to a cook. One who memorized the dish cooks it even from a scrambled card: high score, card ignored. Contrastive training rewards only the cook who actually reads the card.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_question: When you fine-tune a small model to follow worked examples in its prompt, does it actually read them, or just recognize the task and answer from memory?
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_answer: Not by default. One tuned model scored 49% whether its worked examples were intact or scrambled, meaning it had memorized the task and ignored the examples entirely. A contrastive objective forced genuine reading: 50% with intact examples, collapsing to 7% when scrambled. The catch is this honesty cost a few points versus plain tuning's peak of 56%.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.why_it_matters: Before trusting a fine-tuned model to follow in-prompt examples, rerun it with those examples scrambled. If accuracy barely drops, it is ignoring your demonstrations and just pattern-matching the task family, and a high raw score hides exactly that.

key_numbers.label: Task accuracy, untrained vs contrastive-tuned
key_numbers.sub: share of public tasks fully correct, with real examples

key_numbers.label: Contrastive accuracy when examples are scrambled
key_numbers.sub: it collapses because it truly reads the examples

key_numbers.label: A plainly tuned rival, examples real vs scrambled
key_numbers.sub: identical score proves it ignores the examples

key_numbers.label: Best raw-accuracy recipe
key_numbers.sub: beats contrastive but does not depend on the examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Public task accuracy by training recipe and example condition
charts.chart_read: Five training recipes across the bottom; three bars each for full-task accuracy with real examples, scrambled examples, and no examples in the prompt. Taller is better; a big drop from real to scrambled means the recipe truly reads its examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The contrastive recipe's real-example bar near half towers over its scrambled bar at 7%, while the shuffled-labels recipe's two bars are equal, so it ignores examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much each recipe's accuracy depends on real examples
charts.chart_read: One pair of bars per recipe, public benchmark and synthetic tasks, showing accuracy with real examples minus accuracy with scrambled ones. Taller is better here: a bigger drop means the answer truly depends on the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive towers over every recipe on both sets (43 and 77 points); the shuffled-labels recipe sits at zero, confirming it disregards the demonstrations.

charts.chart_plain_title: Synthetic-task accuracy by recipe and example condition
charts.chart_read: Five recipes across the bottom on made-up transformation tasks; three bars each for real, scrambled, and no examples, as share of tasks fully correct. A high real bar next to a low scrambled bar signals the recipe reads its examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive collapses from 84% to 7% once examples are scrambled, while other recipes still score 37 to 83% on scrambled examples, the sharpest example-dependence.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by task family: base model versus contrastive-tuned
charts.chart_read: Ten task families (dates, numbers, phone, email, shipping codes and more) across the bottom; two bars each for the untrained base and the contrastive-tuned model, as share of tasks fully correct. A higher contrastive bar means the tuning helped.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive lifts most families, several from 0% to 100%; no family regresses, and only the billing-code family stays stuck at 0% (with zip codes already tied at 100%).

charts.chart_plain_title: Contrastive accuracy across three training runs
charts.chart_read: Three training runs with different random starts across the bottom; two bars each for public benchmark and synthetic tasks, both measured with real examples. Steady bar heights across runs mean the result is reliable, not luck.
charts.chart_takeaway: Public accuracy holds near half across all three runs; synthetic accuracy wobbles more, dipping to 77% on the third run.

charts.chart_plain_title: Public tasks helped, hurt, or unchanged by contrastive training
charts.chart_read: Three training runs across the bottom; three bars each counting, out of 45 public tasks, how many contrastive tuning helped, hurt, or left tied versus the untrained base. More helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every run helps 17 to 18 tasks and hurts exactly 3, so the gains are broad and repeatable, not a few lucky tasks.

qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.verdict_tag: The right fix existed; the picker missed it
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.concept_primer: A model writes 24-step number puzzles that usually run right until the final steps break. The system generates many rewrites of those last steps, then trains a judge to pick the winning rewrite from summary statistics alone, like a spellchecker choosing a correction without rereading the sentence.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_question: If a model's step-by-step answers are usually right until the last few steps, can a trained judge pick a corrected version without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_answer: No. A correct fix sat among the candidate rewrites for about nine in ten programs, but the trained judge could not tell which rewrite was right from summary statistics alone, so it played safe and edited nothing, staying at 45% correct, exactly the no-repair mark. A picker allowed to peek at the answer hit 91% from the very same fixes.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.why_it_matters: To repair a model's reasoning, the judge must actually read each candidate rewrite against the original prompt, not score it from aggregate confidence statistics about the generator. Those summary features simply cannot spot the correct fix, so the picker safely does nothing.

key_numbers.label: Correct programs, before and after the learned picker
key_numbers.sub: unchanged from the no-repair baseline

key_numbers.label: Same fixes, but the picker allowed to peek at the answer
key_numbers.sub: a perfect picker over the identical candidates

key_numbers.label: Weakest program source, with peeking allowed
key_numbers.sub: where nearly all the unclaimed gains sit

key_numbers.label: Programs the learned picker chose to edit
key_numbers.sub: it fell back to changing nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned repair picker never beats leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of choosing a fix; height is the share of 24-step programs that run correctly start to finish, so taller is better. Bars: no repair, two learned pickers, a scrambled-label control, and two pickers allowed to peek at the answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two learned-picker bars sit exactly on the no-repair bar while the peek-allowed answer bar towers far above, so a good fix exists but is never chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gap stays wide across every batch of programs
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs, three bars each: leave the program as-is, the learned picker, and a picker allowed to peek at the answer. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-picker bar is identical to leave-as-is on every batch, always sitting far below the peek-allowed bar it could have matched.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct fixes are plentiful, so having a fix is not the problem
charts.chart_read: For each batch, one bar is the share of programs where a correct ending-fix already exists among the tried edits; the other is the share already correct with no fix. Taller means more fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-exists bar sits near ninety percent everywhere, roughly double the already-correct bar, so the raw material for repair is abundant.

charts.chart_plain_title: Peeking would rescue mostly the weakest program source
charts.chart_read: Three source generators, two bars each: leaving programs as-is versus a picker allowed to peek at the answer. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The peek-allowed picker lifts the weakest source from near zero to about 78%, so nearly all the unclaimed gains sit on that one weak source.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled control edits and harms programs; the real picker touches nothing
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs. Bars show how often a scrambled-label control edits programs and how often that harms them, next to how often the real picker edits. Lower editing by the real picker means it stayed hands-off.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled control changes and damages roughly a third of programs while the real picker edits zero, showing its stillness is a deliberate retreat, not a broken pipeline.

qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.verdict_tag: Pointing to where the numbers sit didn't help
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.concept_primer: The model acts like a clerk working a 24-line arithmetic worksheet: each line, it lifts one number and one operation out of written wording and runs them in order. The extra training highlighted exactly where each number sat on the page. But the clerk already found them.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_question: If a model already knows which numbers to pull out of a written multi-step math problem, does training that points to exactly where each one sits make it more accurate?
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_answer: No. A model trained to copy numbers and operations out of text and run a 24-step calculation got 27% of final answers exactly right; adding the pointing signal landed at 28%, a tie. Worse, agreement between two rewordings of the same problem fell from 73% to 56%. A lighter version of the signal was clearly worse, at 18%.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.why_it_matters: When a system copies structured values out of text and chains them, do not add supervision on where values sit once the model already locates them over 90% of the time. Spend effort catching and repairing the small copy slips that compound across steps.

charts.chart_takeaway: The lighter-hint bar collapses from about a quarter correct to roughly one in nine when reworded, while the other two setups barely move.

qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.verdict_tag: Final answers keep a skill they cannot teach
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.concept_primer: The model turns each word problem into a tiny program — add 17, multiply by 3 — that a fixed calculator then runs. Show it the correct programs first; afterward, grading only the final answer keeps it writing correct programs.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_question: Once a small model learns to turn a problem into step-by-step instructions, can you keep training it on the final answer alone without breaking that skill?
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_answer: Yes. Once step-by-step labels install the skill, training on final answers alone preserves and even sharpens it: 97% of the longest 24-step problems solved exactly, versus about 1 in 100 — no better than guessing — when the same setup learns from final answers only from the start. Final-answer feedback can refine an installed skill but never discovers it.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.why_it_matters: Pay for step-level labels once to install a structured-reasoning skill in a small model. Afterward, cheaper final-answer feedback is enough to keep it and stretch it to problems three times longer than anything it trained on.

qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.verdict_tag: Writes recipes it cannot cook itself
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.concept_primer: Picture a cook who writes a flawless six-step recipe but burns the dish whenever he cooks it himself. Hand that written recipe to a machine and it follows the steps perfectly. This model wrote correct multi-step procedures yet could not carry them out in its own head.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_question: If you train a small model only on single-step operations, can it chain them into correct multi-step procedures it never saw during training?
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_answer: Yes. Trained only on single-step tasks, the 4-billion-parameter model wrote six-step procedures that ran correctly 63% of the time once a plain step-follower executed them, versus 0% when the model tried to state the final answer itself. Accuracy slid down gently as tasks lengthened, never collapsing. So it truly composes steps into working procedures, but cannot reliably run them in its own head. Treat it as a recipe-writer, not a calculator.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.why_it_matters: Have small models emit an explicit step list and run it with a deterministic step-follower rather than trusting their stated answer. The steps hold up on long tasks; the weak spot is reworded requests, so invest in language grounding.

key_numbers.label: Six-step tasks: direct answer vs written procedure
key_numbers.sub: answering in its head collapses; the machine-run procedure holds up

key_numbers.label: Correct procedure but wrong self-answer at six steps
key_numbers.sub: one setup wrote runnable six-step procedures yet its own final answer was never right

key_numbers.label: Six-step procedure when the task is reworded
key_numbers.sub: same task, only the phrasing changed

key_numbers.label: Single steps it was trained on
key_numbers.sub: accuracy on the operations seen during training

charts.chart_plain_title: Written procedures hold up as tasks get longer; direct answers collapse
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the task needs, from two to six; vertical axis is how often it was solved, higher is better. Three upper lines are machine-run procedures; the bottom line is the model answering directly in its head.
charts.chart_takeaway: The procedure lines slope down gently and stay high, while the direct-answer line falls all the way to zero by six steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: The procedure runs right even when the model's own answer is wrong
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is task length in steps; vertical axis is how often solved, higher is better. The top line is the model's written procedure run by a machine; the bottom line is the final answer the model wrote itself.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines split wide apart: at six steps the machine-run procedure is often right while the model's own answer is essentially never right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the same task lowers success at every length
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is task length in steps; vertical axis is how often the machine-run procedure solved it, higher is better. The upper line is the familiar wording, the lower line is the same tasks reworded.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reworded line sits below the familiar-wording line at every length, showing the wording of the request is the remaining hurdle.

charts.chart_plain_title: When a procedure fails, it usually fails to run at all
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one outcome type for the written procedures across all task lengths; taller means a bigger share. The first bar is fully correct; the rest are kinds of mistakes, taller meaning more common.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tallest failure bar is procedures that won't run at all, while picking the wrong steps is a tiny sliver, so parsing not reasoning is the main breakdown.

qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.verdict_tag: Self-checking helps, but worked examples win big
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.concept_primer: The model turns a word problem into a short program a calculator runs exactly. It generates many programs and keeps any that reach the correct final answer to learn from — like a student trusting any scratch work that matches the answer key, even lucky shortcuts.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_question: If a small model can check whether the programs it writes reach the correct final answer, can learning from those wins match learning from full worked-out solutions?
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_answer: It depends. Training the model only on its own attempts that landed on the correct final answer lifted unaided first-try accuracy from 62% to 73% on fresh problems — a real gain that sticks when it writes programs alone. But training on complete worked-out programs reached nearly 100%. Checking final answers helps for free, yet stalls far short of showing full solutions.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.why_it_matters: When you can score final answers but lack worked solutions, self-training on answer-verified attempts buys a real lift for free. But collect or synthesize complete worked solutions when you need near-perfect reliability, especially on longer multi-step problems.

key_numbers.label: Full worked-example ceiling

key_numbers.label: Same method on a real 4B model
key_numbers.sub: self-training on a frozen 4B model's readout, nearly tripling accuracy (17.6% to 50.4%)

key_numbers.label: Harder multi-step problems
key_numbers.sub: self-training versus full worked solutions on longer compositional problems

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-checking lifts accuracy; full worked examples nearly max it out
charts.chart_read: Three groups left to right on fresh problems: starting point, after self-training, full worked examples. Each has two bars — the lighter is the model answering unaided, the darker is it allowed to guess-and-check toward the right answer. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-trained unaided bar climbs from 62% to 73%, but the full worked-example bars tower near 100%, dwarfing the self-training gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Almost all the self-training gain arrives in the first round
charts.chart_read: Two rising lines track unaided accuracy across rounds of self-training, starting at round zero; higher is better. One line is ordinary fresh problems, the other is harder multi-step problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines jump at round one then flatten, and the harder-problems line stays well below, showing self-training stalls early and struggles with longer programs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same pattern when the front end is a real 4B model
charts.chart_read: Three groups on fresh problems: starting point, self-trained, full worked examples. Each has two bars — the light bar is answering unaided, the dark bar is with guess-and-check. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-training nearly triples unaided accuracy from 18% to 50%, but the full worked-example bar near 95% again far outreaches it.

charts.chart_plain_title: The self-training loop runs out of fresh lessons
charts.chart_read: Two lines across rounds; higher means more. One is the share of training prompts where guess-and-check found a correct program, the other is the share of those that were genuinely new fixes rather than already-known answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The found line climbs to 93% while the new-fix line collapses toward 5%, so later rounds mostly re-confirm what the model already knew.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guess-and-check stays ahead of unaided answering every round
charts.chart_read: Two lines across self-training rounds on fresh problems; higher is better. The lower line is the model answering unaided, the upper line is it allowed to guess-and-check toward the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guess-and-check line starts about 20 points above the unaided line at the seed round, then settles into a 14-16 point lead through the later rounds, meaning fixable mistakes remain in the model's first answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even full worked examples cannot fully crack harder problems
charts.chart_read: Four problem-type groups, each with two bars for the two model front ends after training on full worked examples; taller is better. The first three groups are ordinary problems, the last is harder multi-step ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both front ends near-max the first three groups but drop 15-20 points on the harder multi-step group, marking longer programs as the stubborn weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: The 4B version finds fewer, messier self-taught lessons
charts.chart_read: Lines across rounds; higher means more. Two lines are the 4B version's share of prompts solved and share needing heavy rewriting; one line is the simpler main version's solved share for comparison.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 4B version starts far lower and rewrites more heavily, but its solved line climbs toward the main version's, showing a slower version of the same trend.

qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.verdict_tag: Handing it a solved example did not help
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.concept_primer: Before a small model reformats messy data — dates, phone numbers, addresses — into a target style, it is handed a verified, already-solved example of a similar reformatting job, like glancing at the answer key for a related puzzle before tackling your own.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_question: When a small model reformats messy data like dates and phone numbers, does handing it a verified, already-solved example of a similar job make more batches come out perfectly right?
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_answer: No. Handing the model a matched, verified example made it slightly worse, not better: every value came out correct on 47.5% of jobs, versus 50% when it worked through each value alone with no example. The lookup itself worked — the fetched example was the same kind of task 77.5% of the time — yet across 40 jobs it improved none and hurt one. Relevant context was not the missing ingredient.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.why_it_matters: If you run a small model through repetitive data-cleanup, do not count on a library of similar solved examples to lift reliability. Here the gains came from clean one-value-at-a-time prompting, not from looking up past work — even accurate lookups added nothing.

key_numbers.label: Working alone vs given a matched example
key_numbers.sub: share of jobs where every value was exactly right

key_numbers.label: Fetched example was the right kind of task
key_numbers.sub: versus 20% for a random pick — the lookup worked

key_numbers.label: Jobs the matched example improved
key_numbers.sub: and it made one job worse

key_numbers.label: Given a random, unrelated example instead
key_numbers.sub: jobs fully correct — worse still

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method got a whole batch perfectly right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; taller means more jobs where every value was exactly right. The leftmost bar is the model working through values one at a time alone; the others add a looked-up example or process the whole batch at once. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain one-at-a-time bar is tallest; every version that adds a looked-up example or batches the job sits lower, so the extra example never paid off.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method got a single value right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; taller means more individual values exactly correct — a looser test than needing the whole batch perfect. The leftmost bar is the model working one value at a time alone. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Working alone stays tallest even under this gentler scoring; every added-example or batched version lands lower, confirming the pattern is not an artifact of strict grading.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many jobs each shortcut helped versus hurt
charts.chart_read: For each working style, two bars count the jobs it improved versus the jobs it worsened, against the model working one value at a time alone. Across 40 jobs, a taller help bar and a shorter hurt bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Help bars sit at or near zero everywhere while hurt bars climb; the matched example improved none of the 40 jobs and worsened one.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the lookup found a genuinely similar example
charts.chart_read: Two bars: the real lookup versus a random pick. Each shows how often the fetched example came from the same kind of task. Taller means the fetched example was more relevant to the job at hand.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real lookup fetched a genuinely similar example most of the time, far above the random pick, so the flat result is not a broken lookup.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny six-job trials swung wildly from run to run
charts.chart_read: Each group is one working style; the three bars within it are three separate tiny trials of only six jobs each. Height is the share of values correct. Watch how much the matched-example bars jump between trials.
charts.chart_takeaway: The matched-example bars swing from worst to best across the three tiny trials, showing six jobs are too few to trust and motivating the full 40-job test.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task type, examples did not rescue accuracy
charts.chart_read: Ten of the hardest number-formatting jobs run along the bottom. For each, one bar is the model working alone and the other is the model given a matched example. Height is the share of values correct; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On these number jobs the matched-example bars are mostly equal or shorter — shorter on five of the ten and taller on just two — so the failures are not about finding a relevant example.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only processing the whole batch at once broke the format
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; height is the share of jobs whose output could be read back cleanly. Taller is better. The bar worth watching is the one that processes the whole batch in a single pass.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every style produced clean, readable output except processing the whole batch at once, which garbled the format on a small slice of jobs.

charts.chart_plain_title: The single best-matched example is the most relevant
charts.chart_read: Two lines run across the first, second, and third best-matched examples. One tracks how similar the match is; the other tracks how often it is the same kind of task. Higher means more relevant.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward from the top match, so lower-ranked examples are less relevant — the reason only the single best example was ever handed to the model.

qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.verdict_tag: Most failed runs are one or two fixes
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.concept_primer: Think of a 24-step calculation where each step updates a running number. The model copies the recipe from memory, but a step or two comes out wrong and wrecks the final total. A checker that knows the correct running number at each step can pinpoint and swap the bad steps.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_question: When a small model builds a long 24-step calculation and gets it almost right, can checking each step let a few small edits fix the final answer?
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_answer: Yes, mostly, but with a catch. A small model copying 24-step calculations got only about 27% exactly right on its own. A checker that knows the correct running number after every step, allowed to swap one or two bad steps, lifted that to 91%; a single swap already reached about 70%. The catch: that step-by-step answer key isn't available in real use.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.why_it_matters: When a small model nearly solves a long task, its errors are usually a couple of local slips, not gibberish. A cheap search over small edits plus a strong step-by-step checker recovers most of it, so building that checker is the high-value move.

key_numbers.label: Exactly-correct 24-step runs, before vs after fixing
key_numbers.sub: share run perfectly, allowing one or two step swaps

key_numbers.label: Fixed by a single swap
key_numbers.sub: changing just one step already recovers most failures

key_numbers.label: True calculation rebuilt
key_numbers.sub: genuine fixes, not a lucky matching final answer

key_numbers.label: Accuracy as length grows to 24 steps (before fixing)
key_numbers.sub: why long chains fail: one bad step ruins the whole answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing one or two steps recovers most failed calculations
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how many steps the checker may swap: none, one, or two. In each group one bar counts runs with the right final answer, the other counts runs whose whole calculation matches the true one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb from about 27% with no fixing to 91% with two swaps, and the two heights stay matched, so it rebuilds the real calculation, not just a lucky answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer calculations fail far more, but fixing recovers most
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the calculation has; the vertical axis is the share of runs that come out exactly right. One line is before fixing, one after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Before fixing, accuracy slides from about 86% at four steps to 25% at 24 steps, while after fixing it stays near 82% even at the longest length.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gain holds across different prompt wordings
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by prompt style: plain wording, reworded, and matched pairs shown both ways. In each group one bar is before fixing, one after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every wording jumps from roughly a quarter of runs correct before fixing to about 86 to 91% after, so the gain is not a quirk of one phrasing.

qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.concept_primer: Picture an apprentice mechanic fixing an engine one adjustment at a time, checking the gauge after each move. Forcing them to also predict every gauge reading before they look makes them fluent at reading dials, but no better at choosing the right adjustment.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_question: If a small model fixes a tiny program by editing it and running it over several rounds, does also teaching it to predict each result help?
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_answer: Only the acting helped. Editing-and-running in a loop lifted the share of tasks solved from 10% at a blank start to 43%, beating a single one-shot guess near 37%. But adding a second job, predicting the program's output after each edit, dropped success to 36%. The model grew fluent at foreseeing results yet quit before finishing even more often.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.why_it_matters: When a small model acts in a loop, reward finishing the correct program, not narrating what the machine will show. Predicting the environment's responses builds fluency, not judgment, and here it made the model halt too early.

key_numbers.label: Editing in a loop vs a blank start
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks solved, action-trained model over eight rounds

key_numbers.label: Also predicting each output
key_numbers.sub: edit-only loop vs the same loop also foreseeing the result

key_numbers.label: Best reachable with a perfect guide
key_numbers.sub: an ideal step-by-step teacher's score, the room still to chase

key_numbers.label: Quit before finishing
key_numbers.sub: how often the model halted too early (54-60%)

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing in a loop beats a single guess; predicting outcomes adds nothing
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show the share of tasks solved across five test sets plus their average; taller is better. Each cluster has four bars: a single one-shot guess, the edit-and-run loop, that loop also predicting outputs, and a flawless step-by-step teacher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The loop's bars top the one-shot bars on average, the output-predicting bars are no taller on average and often shorter, and the teacher's bars tower over all, showing room still unclaimed.

qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.verdict_tag: Predicting a program's output barely improves writing one
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.concept_primer: This system rewrites each word problem as a tiny stack-machine program. The experiment added a side lesson: while the correct program runs, predict what the machine shows at every step. It is like memorizing how a finished dish looks mid-cook without learning to cook your own.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_question: If you train a program-writing model to also predict exactly what its target programs do when they run, does it start writing better programs itself?
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_answer: Mostly no. The model got far better at predicting execution, with reading a running program's top value climbing from under 1 percent correct to 43 percent, but that rarely improved the programs it wrote. First-try accuracy on standard problems even slipped from 85 to 81 percent. A few test-and-fix settings gained slightly, 93 to 96 percent, so it is a real limit, not a collapse.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.why_it_matters: If you bolt a predict-execution side objective onto a code generator, feed it programs the model itself proposed, not the ideal answer. Predicting a correct program's behavior barely transfers to writing or choosing better ones.

charts.chart_takeaway: The outcome-guessing bar leaps from near zero to about 43 percent and retries edge up, but first-try success is identical, so the new skill barely spills over.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two sit nearly level everywhere, and on the standard style the outcome-predicting bar is actually shorter, confirming no broad first-try gain.

charts.chart_read: Paired bars for two self-practice rounds show the share of problems where each helper found a working program to learn from; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars stay within a couple of points across both rounds, showing the extra training did not shrink the pool of examples to learn from.

real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.verdict_tag: Ready-made toolbox nails clean data, fumbles messy edges
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.concept_primer: Picture a fixed toolbox of ready-made data-cleaning functions. For each job you try every tool against a few input-output examples and keep whichever fits. The catch: with so few examples a wrong tool can fit by luck, so trick examples are added to unmask those coincidences.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_question: If you match ready-made transformation functions against a handful of input-output examples, do you get the truly correct function, or just one that happens to fit?
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_answer: It depends on how messy the data is. For clean, spreadsheet-style pipeline jobs the toolbox covered every one (100%) and held firm even against deliberately tricky examples. For irregular date, ID, and text cleanup, coverage slipped from 95% to 85% once trick examples exposed functions that only looked right. And a wrong function fit the few shown examples about a third of the time.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.why_it_matters: When auto-picking transformation functions from examples, never trust a match on a few cases: generate adversarial trick examples to catch lucky fits. Expect clean pipelines to work today; messy formatting needs richer functions or a human step.

key_numbers.sub: passed the few shown examples but was actually wrong

charts.chart_takeaway: Clean-pipeline bars stay at full height after trick examples while the messy group drops, so the fixed toolbox holds up only on tidy work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Planted decoy jobs caught by trick examples
charts.chart_read: Two bars for the same two deliberately deceptive tasks: coverage using the few shown examples versus coverage after trick examples are added. Here a drop toward zero is the intended, better outcome.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage falls from full to nothing, confirming trick examples removed every planted match that had fit the shown examples for the wrong reason.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many solved jobs used one tool versus two
charts.chart_read: Bars count solved tasks that needed a single tool versus two tools chained together. Lighter bars are before trick examples, darker after; height just shows how common each pattern is, not which is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-tool bar towers over the two-tool bar, so the toolbox mostly proves it can pick the right one tool, not chain several.

charts.chart_read: One bar per group (clean pipelines, messy work, overall) showing how often a function that passed the few shown examples still failed hidden checks. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near a third, showing thin visible examples routinely let wrong functions slip through and why the trick examples are needed.

rule_family_diversity_scaling.verdict_tag: Variety beats volume for fixing unseen code
rule_family_diversity_scaling.concept_primer: Give a mechanic the same 240 practice repairs. Spread across only 3 engine types, they just memorize those engines. Spread across 12 types, they instead learn to read the diagnostic printout itself, and can then fix an engine model they have never seen before.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_question: With a fixed budget of practice examples for teaching a small model to fix buggy code, does covering more kinds of problems help it fix brand-new kinds?
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_answer: Yes. Holding training at 240 examples but spreading them across 12 kinds of problems instead of 3 lifted fixes on completely unseen problem types from 0% to 29%, while fixes on familiar problems stayed high. But it only works because the model reads the failing-run evidence: strip that evidence and transfer drops to 0%. And the gain is lopsided, with one unseen type hitting 92% while two others never worked at all.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.why_it_matters: When fine-tuning a small model to generalize, spend a fixed example budget on breadth of problem types, not depth on a few. And feed it real execution-failure evidence at both training and answer time, or transfer to new types vanishes.

key_numbers.label: Fixing brand-new problem types
key_numbers.sub: 3 versus 12 problem types, same 240 examples

key_numbers.label: Remove the failing-run evidence
key_numbers.sub: same model, evidence stripped at answer time

key_numbers.label: Very uneven across new types
key_numbers.sub: best unseen type; two others never worked

charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many problem-type varieties were in training (3, 6, 12); vertical axis is the share of buggy tasks fixed, higher is better. One line tracks familiar problems, one the same problems in new formats, one brand-new problem types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new-type line barely lifts off zero, going 0% then just 4%, until 12 varieties, where it jumps to 29%, while familiar-problem fixes stay high throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: New-type fixes need real failing-run evidence at training and answer time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a setup's share of never-seen-type bugs fixed, taller is better. First bar is the full trained model with real evidence; the rest drop or scramble that evidence at answer or training time, or use the untouched base model.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full setup with real evidence reaches 29%; every stripped or scrambled variant collapses to 4% or below, so the evidence does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Almost all the transfer comes from a single new problem type
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one never-seen problem type, with 12 tasks each; height is the share fixed, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One type is nearly solved at 92% and another partly at 25%, while two get zero fixes, so the gain is narrow, not broad.

charts.chart_plain_title: On familiar problems, only one bug type wobbles as variety grows
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of familiar bugs fixed for three bug types, grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two bug types stay perfectly fixed at every variety level, and all the movement lands on the linear-formula bug type.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coping with new formats depends heavily on the bug type
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of same-problems-in-new-formats bugs fixed for three bug types, grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The threshold-labeling bug type stays near-perfect across formats, the linear-formula type is stuck near the bottom, and the text-slug type slips at the widest variety.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting the rule label right shows up before full fixes do
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training variety (3, 6, 12); vertical axis is a share on new-type problems, higher is better. One line is how often the model names the correct fix rule, the other how often it fully fixes the bug.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-label line climbs steadily while full fixes stay flat until 12 varieties, so partial understanding builds before complete repairs arrive.

charts.chart_plain_title: The transfer gain is not bought with extra thinking time
charts.chart_read: Bars show median seconds to produce one attempt on new-type problems for each setup; there is no better direction, this just checks whether some setups simply ran longer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained setup takes about eight seconds regardless of variety, so the widest-variety gain is not explained by more time per attempt.

sampled_query_filter_executor.verdict_tag: One sampled answer teaches the entire hidden world
sampled_query_filter_executor.concept_primer: Two unknown numbers sit locked a fixed distance apart. A short program nudges them and drops clues that rule out combinations. To answer any question the model must track every still-possible pair of numbers, though it is graded on just one guessed final answer per problem.
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_question: If you only ever grade a model on one possible answer per practice problem, can it still learn the full hidden set of possibilities behind every answer?
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_answer: Yes. Graded on just one sampled final answer per problem, the model rebuilt the entire set of still-possible number pairs it was never shown, capturing 94 to 98 percent of it, because holding that full set is the cheapest way to answer varied questions. Simpler models that tracked each number separately, or skipped step-by-step execution, stayed under 6 percent.
sampled_query_filter_executor.why_it_matters: When every answer in a task shares one hidden structure, you can supervise only final answers and let the model build that structure itself, but only if its design can hold the correlated whole rather than separate pieces, and you allow enough internal steps.

key_numbers.label: Hidden set of pairs recovered, never taught
key_numbers.sub: share of the never-shown set of possible number pairs the internal state captured

key_numbers.label: Same score for the best simpler model
key_numbers.sub: controls that track numbers separately or skip step-by-step execution

key_numbers.label: Stretch beyond training length
key_numbers.sub: trained on programs up to 8 steps, still about 95% correct at 24 steps
