charts.chart_plain_title: Answer accuracy on longer programs: full tracker versus two simpler models
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4 to 24; models trained only up to 8). Height is probability placed on the correct answer, higher is better. Three bars per length: the full joint tracker, a separate-numbers tracker, and a one-shot no-steps model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full tracker holds above 95 percent even at three times the training length, while both simpler models collapse toward 5 to 12 percent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy jumps exactly when internal steps reach program length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of internal thinking steps allowed; the vertical axis is probability on the correct answer, higher is better. Each line is one program length (4, 8, 12, 16, 24).
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat and low until the steps reach its own program length, then snaps up to 95 to 99 percent, the signature of genuine step-by-step execution.

charts.chart_plain_title: The never-shown hidden set of pairs is recovered anyway
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length; height is probability placed on the true set of still-possible number pairs, which was withheld from training entirely. Higher is better. Three bars each: full joint tracker, separate-numbers tracker, one-shot model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The joint tracker recovers 94 to 98 percent of a hidden set it was never taught, while both simpler models stay under 6 percent.

charts.chart_plain_title: All four question types clear the bar, including the combined ones
charts.chart_read: Bars group by unseen program length; height is answer accuracy once internal steps reach program length, higher is better. Four bars per length, one per question type: first number, second number, their sum, and their difference.
charts.chart_takeaway: No question type lags: even the harder sum and difference questions stay above 94 percent, so the model holds a genuinely joint state, not two separate ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden-state accuracy climbs during training while chasing the graded answer
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step; the vertical axis is probability on the correct target, higher is better. One line is the graded answer accuracy, the other the never-graded hidden set-of-pairs accuracy.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden line starts near 3 percent while the graded answer sits at 39 percent, then both climb and converge near 98 percent, hidden structure trailing the reward by roughly 200 steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: A separate smaller setup reproduces the same win
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length in an independent smaller-number version of the task; height is answer accuracy at a fixed internal-step budget, higher is better. Three bars each: full joint tracker and two simpler controls.
charts.chart_takeaway: The joint tracker holds near 99 percent at every length while both controls decay below 31 percent, so the result is not a fluke of one problem size.

sparse_support_memory_executor.verdict_tag: One memory slot per possibility recovers exact reasoning
sparse_support_memory_executor.concept_primer: Picture tracking a hidden pair of numbers as a page of suspects. Each program step either reshuffles every suspect the same way or crosses some off with a clue. Keep one line per suspect and the truth always survives; skimp on lines and you sometimes erase it.
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_question: When a small system tracks hidden numbers through a chain of clues and transformations, how much scratch memory does it need to never lose the true answer?
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_answer: Only when its scratch memory held one slot for every possible starting value. With that, it answered every question correctly through the longest 24-step programs. Cut the memory roughly in half and accuracy fell to about 69%, because whole batches of candidates got erased mid-program, and about 1 in 3 examples lost every candidate and fell back to a guess. A wider memory vector is not the fix; enough addressable slots is.
sparse_support_memory_executor.why_it_matters: For symbolic state-tracking, size scratch memory to the number of live possibilities the program must keep alive, not to a wider dense vector, and watch the rate of wiped-out cases as an early warning of undersized memory.

key_numbers.label: Full memory on the longest programs
key_numbers.sub: one slot per possible starting value, 24-step programs

key_numbers.label: Roughly half memory on the longest programs
key_numbers.sub: 16 of 31 possible slots kept, 24-step programs

key_numbers.label: Cases that lost every candidate at half memory
key_numbers.sub: fell back to a guess, 16 slots, 24-step programs

key_numbers.label: Two-thirds memory, larger number space
key_numbers.sub: 64 of 97 possible slots kept, 24-step programs

charts.chart_plain_title: More memory slots keep accuracy as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is program length in steps; vertical axis is how often the exact answer survives, higher is better. Each line is a memory size; the top line holds one slot per possible starting value.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full line stays flat at the top across all lengths; every smaller-memory line slopes downward, so missing slots cost accuracy on longer programs.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same pattern holds with far more possible answers
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is program length in steps; vertical axis is how often the exact answer survives, higher is better. Each line is a memory size, with the top line holding one slot per possible starting value.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even a memory holding two-thirds of the slots leaks accuracy on long programs; only the full-support line stays perfectly flat at the top.

structured_slot_initializer_ladder.verdict_tag: One slot per value beats a bigger network
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.concept_primer: Before the model computes, it must drop each possible hidden value into its own memory slot, like seating guests in numbered chairs. Left free, a plain network crowds several onto one chair and leaves others empty; a matching rule seats every value exactly once.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_question: Before a small model runs a step-by-step calculation, it loads its starting guesses into fixed memory slots. Does getting this right need a bigger network, or something else?
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_answer: Structure, not scale. A plain general-purpose network placed only 55.5% of its belief on the correct starting setup and gave barely half the possible values their own slot, doubling several onto the same one. A rule that forces each value into a distinct slot lifted that to 98.1%, essentially matching a flawless setup. Learning the underlying math alone was not the missing piece.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.why_it_matters: When a small model must hold a set of distinct possibilities in fixed memory before a later step can use them reliably, don't just enlarge the network. Add an explicit step that gives each possibility its own slot and leaves none uncovered.

key_numbers.label: Belief on the correct starting setup
key_numbers.sub: plain network vs a one-slot-per-value matching rule, on longer unseen programs

key_numbers.label: Values given their own slot
key_numbers.sub: plain network vs matching rule; the plain one doubled slots onto the same value

key_numbers.label: Knew the math but botched the filing
key_numbers.sub: the plain network got the value relationship right this often, yet covered only this many values

key_numbers.label: Gap to a flawless setup
key_numbers.sub: the matching-rule setup trails a perfect reference (98.1% vs 100%)

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the answer lands on the exact target, by setup style
charts.chart_read: Each group along the bottom is a setup style, from a flawless reference on the left to a plain general-purpose network on the right. Two bars per group show how much belief landed exactly right; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily as more structure is added; the matching-rule setup nearly touches the flawless reference while the plain network sits far below it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer quality stays flat as the task runs more steps
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is how many steps the exact calculator runs, up to 24. Height is how much belief stays on the exact right answer, higher being better. Each line is one setup style, flawless reference on top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line runs nearly flat across all lengths, so the calculator neither repairs nor spoils the setup; final quality is locked in at the very start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learning the rule is not enough without covering every value
charts.chart_read: Each group is a setup style. One bar is how often it got the value relationship right; the other is whether every value got its own slot. Taller is better on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several setups ace the relationship yet leave many values sharing slots; only the matching-rule setup scores full marks on giving each value its own slot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Matching-rule setup tracks the flawless reference as values multiply
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is the size of the pool of possible values, from small to largest. Height is how much belief lands on target, higher being better. One bar is the learned matching-rule setup, the other the flawless reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned setup stays within about two points of the flawless reference at every size, holding near-perfect even at the largest pool of values.

targeted_bridge_allocation.verdict_tag: Worked-out reasoning beats clever example targeting
targeted_bridge_allocation.concept_primer: A small model must fix code fusing two skills it only ever saw apart — say, sorting a list and reading a tuple, never both at once. Your worked examples are a fixed pool: spread them across every new fusion, or pile them onto the hardest?
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_question: With a fixed budget of worked examples for code patterns a model has never seen combined, should you spread them evenly or pile them onto the hardest patterns?
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_answer: Barely. Piling examples on the hardest never-seen combinations repaired 33% of test cases versus 28% for spreading them evenly — a three-case edge on sixty tests, and no better than the same budget spent on easy combinations. The real lever was not allocation: strip or scramble the step-by-step solution attached to each example and success crashed from 33% to under 10%.
targeted_bridge_allocation.why_it_matters: When you cannot add more training data, don't over-engineer which cases to target — the gain is marginal. Spend your effort attaching a correct step-by-step solution to every example; that pairing, not the allocation, is what carries the transfer.

key_numbers.label: Concentrating on hard cases vs spreading evenly
key_numbers.sub: never-seen combinations repaired: 17 of 60 vs 20 of 60

key_numbers.label: Hard cases vs same budget on easy cases
key_numbers.sub: 20 of 60 vs 19 of 60 — essentially tied

key_numbers.label: When the step-by-step solution is removed
key_numbers.sub: success collapses without correct reasoning attached

key_numbers.label: Untrained starting model
key_numbers.sub: 0 of 60 never-seen combinations fixed before training

charts.chart_plain_title: Where you spend a fixed set of worked examples changes repair
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of never-seen skill-combinations a trained model repairs; bars run left to right from the untrained model, through spreading examples evenly, to concentrating them on the hardest combinations. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concentrated hard-case bar is tallest at 33%, just five points above even spreading, yet every trained bar towers over the untrained model's 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training without a correct solution guts every kind of test
charts.chart_read: Three test types along the bottom — familiar problems, reworded versions, and never-seen combinations; colored bars are training examples that include a correct step-by-step solution, none, or a scrambled one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-solution bars dwarf the rest everywhere, falling from about 78% to under 30% even on familiar problems and from 33% to under 10% on new combinations.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples opens combinations even spreading leaves stuck
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by which never-seen skill-combination family; colored bars are spreading examples evenly, concentrating on the hardest families, and a control that concentrates on the easy families. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Concentrating on hard families lifts two families off zero that even spreading never cracks, while the already-easy families stay high — one pinned at 100%, the other holding around a third.

charts.chart_plain_title: Flooding one skill with examples is not the lever
charts.chart_read: Each bar is repair on never-seen combinations for spreading examples evenly versus pouring sixteen examples into a single skill family. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Overloading one family helps modestly at best — 33% for the remainder-math skill — and actually lowers repair for the length skill, so sheer volume is not what helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples costs nothing on familiar problems
charts.chart_read: One group of bars per training setup, with two colors: repair on familiar problems and on reworded versions of them; the leftmost frozen pair is the untrained model. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained setup clusters in a 69-83% band, so concentrating the worked examples on hard cases sacrifices nothing on familiar or reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: At test time the model still needs the worked solution in the prompt
charts.chart_read: Three test types along the bottom; colored bars give the model, inside its prompt, a correct step-by-step solution, none, or a scrambled one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing or scrambling the solution from the prompt crashes repair even on familiar problems, from 78% to under 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair depends heavily on the kind of task
charts.chart_read: One bar per task mechanism, sorted most to least repaired, for the hard-case model on never-seen combinations. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Repair splits sharply: step-through-a-sequence and text-cleanup tasks reach 100%, while remainder-arithmetic tasks stay flat at 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: The total number of examples does not predict repair
charts.chart_read: Each dot is a training setup; left to right is the total worked examples used, up is repair on never-seen combinations. A rising trend would mean more examples help; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Setups with the exact same example count scatter from 20% to 33%, so how examples are allocated matters more than how many.

trace_keyed_symbol_repair.verdict_tag: The fix was hiding in the error message
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.concept_primer: Give the code a wrong constant and its tests fail with a message that literally spells out the value it expected — a value found nowhere else, not in the source files, not in the bug report. The only way to fix the code is to read that failure message.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_question: When a program's tests fail and print the exact value they expected, can a small coding model learn to lift that value out and paste it into the fix?
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_answer: Yes. After light training, a small coding model fixed all 60 test cases — including value styles it never saw during training — by copying the correct value straight out of the failure message. Take that message away or scramble it and repair drops to none. An untrained model pulls the right value out only about 18 percent of the time, so this skill genuinely had to be trained in.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.why_it_matters: When building an auto-repair loop, feed the model the raw failure output, not a tidy summary — the answer often hides there. And test honestly: if scrambling that output does not hurt results, your model is pattern-matching, not reading the evidence.

key_numbers.label: Fixes before vs after training on the failure message
key_numbers.sub: same model, familiar value styles

key_numbers.label: Same trained model once the failure message is removed or scrambled
key_numbers.sub: repair collapses without the real message

key_numbers.label: Fixes on value formats never seen during training
key_numbers.sub: the skill carried over to brand-new styles

key_numbers.label: Untrained model pulling the right value out on its own
key_numbers.sub: rarely does it without training

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the model trained to read the failure message fixes the code
charts.chart_read: Each group is one training-and-inference setup; within it, two bars give the share of tasks fixed on familiar value styles and on brand-new styles. Taller is better. Only the rightmost setup — trained on the failure message and given it — rises off the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four setups sit flat at zero and the failure-message setup hits full on both styles, so reading the message is what does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Take the failure message away and the same model fixes nothing
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks the one trained model fixes under three conditions: given the correct failure message, given none, and given a mismatched one — split into familiar and new value styles. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the correct-message bars reach the top; removing or swapping the message drops repair to zero, so the answer lives in the message content itself.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each setup copies the correct value out of the message
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each setup pastes the exact correct value into its fix, split into familiar and new value styles. Taller is better. The untrained base shows a short bar, the trained model reaches the top, and the rest stay at zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model copies the right value only about 18 percent of the time and almost never on new styles, while training makes copying reliable every time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deleting the wrong value is easy; writing the right one is not
charts.chart_read: For each setup, one bar is how often it removes the wrong value and another is how often the code is actually fixed, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. Most setups clear the wrong-value bar but leave the fix bar flat.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every trained setup deletes the wrong value, but only the one reading the correct failure message writes the right replacement and passes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Well-formed edits are cheap; correct edits need the failure message
charts.chart_read: For each setup, bars show how often the edit applies cleanly, the code still runs, and the tests actually pass, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. The first two fill up for every trained setup; only the last separates them.
charts.chart_takeaway: All trained setups produce clean, runnable edits, but only the model that reads the failure message produces edits that pass the tests.

charts.chart_plain_title: Give the model enough room to write the whole fix
charts.chart_read: Two runs of the trained model differ only in how much output length it may produce. Bars show whether the edit applies and whether it fixes the code, on a small five-task pilot. Taller is better. The tight-limit run is flat; the roomier one fills up.
charts.chart_takeaway: With too little output room the model cannot finish a usable edit at all, but doubling the room flips it to fully fixing the pilot cases.

qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.concept_primer: Imagine a strong all-round apprentice and two specialists standing at the same half-finished job. The deep specialist is copied only when separate trial runs show that it finishes that exact job better than both the apprentice and the other specialist.
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.plain_question: Can carefully chosen lessons from the deep specialist improve one already blended small model without sacrificing its faster skills?
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.plain_answer: The teacher choice and the small safety update both passed. Deep won on two fresh groups, and five training steps changed unrelated logits only slightly while improving the lesson-matching loss. That authorizes the full four-round trial; no improved-capability result exists yet.
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.why_it_matters: The study has cleared the two common false starts: choosing a teacher that does not really help and making a weight edit that immediately spills everywhere. The remaining test is the important one: whether the full update creates one checkpoint that beats the specialists, runtime switching, controls, and eight tries.

key_numbers.label: Unrelated-logit drift after five steps
key_numbers.sub: well below the frozen 0.10 safety ceiling on 20 exact probes

key_numbers.label: Output uncertainty lost
key_numbers.sub: below the frozen 10% collapse ceiling

key_numbers.label: Exact lesson-matching loss
key_numbers.sub: improved rather than merely staying under the safety ceiling

key_numbers.label: Deep wins available online
key_numbers.sub: enough to freeze 60 lessons plus 20 reminders and 60 matched controls

charts.chart_plain_title: Five update steps barely moved unrelated logits
charts.chart_read: Two short bars show the average unrelated-logit movement and the relative loss of output uncertainty on 20 exact before-and-after probes. Smaller is safer; both are far below one tenth.
charts.chart_takeaway: The small pilot cleared its safety gate, and its exact lesson-matching loss also improved, so the full four-round test may proceed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deep wins on separate check runs in both fresh blocks
charts.chart_read: Each block has two positive bars: deep's score edge over the starting blend and over the quick specialist. Taller is better, but merely staying above zero in both untouched blocks is the key replication test.
charts.chart_takeaway: Deep's local teaching advantage repeated against both alternatives, so the experiment may now test whether that signal can be installed safely into one checkpoint.

charts.chart_plain_title: The proposed training and its two sharp controls receive identical lesson counts
charts.chart_read: Each group has a tall 60-unit capability bar and a shorter 20-unit reminder bar. Only the source of the 60 capability lessons changes: verified deep wins, matched places where deep did not win, or the wrong specialist on the verified-win states.
charts.chart_takeaway: Because all three arms get the same 80 lessons, a primary win can be attributed to choosing both the right state and the right specialist rather than to extra training.

qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.concept_primer: A training experiment needs room to improve. If the starting model already fixes every practice case, even a perfect trained model cannot prove that the new lessons helped.
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.plain_question: Can a tiny set of almost-correct failed-test revisions teach that exact distinction without erasing the coding loop already learned?
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.plain_answer: The test could not answer the question. Before the newly trained model was allowed to run, the starting model and the equal-size old-lesson control each fixed all 48 practice cases. The required gains were therefore impossible, so the run stopped before transfer or the external benchmark.
qwen35_4b_validation_policy_counterexamp.why_it_matters: Turning the old failure into a clear instruction plus an almost-correct program also made it easy. Future curricula must first prove that the starting model still fails the exact new practice distribution; failure forensics alone do not guarantee useful training headroom.

key_numbers.label: Starting model practice score
key_numbers.sub: no room for the required fifteen-point gain

key_numbers.label: Equal extra-training control
key_numbers.sub: no room for the required ten-point gain

key_numbers.label: Unrelated-behavior drift
key_numbers.sub: the trained candidate stayed below the 0.15 safety ceiling

key_numbers.label: External benchmark calls
key_numbers.sub: stopped before candidate behavior and transfer

charts.chart_plain_title: Both controls already sit at the maximum score
charts.chart_read: The first two bars show practice success for the starting model and the equal extra-training control; the third is the best score any candidate could possibly achieve. All bars reach the same ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: With both controls at 100 percent, the promised improvement cannot be measured and the candidate must remain unopened.

charts.chart_plain_title: The small update stayed inside its unrelated-behavior limit
charts.chart_read: The first bar is measured drift from the older reference model; the second is the largest allowed drift. Shorter is better, and the measured bar remains below the limit.
charts.chart_takeaway: The narrow training update was local enough, but locality cannot rescue an evaluation set the parent already solves perfectly.

qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.verdict_tag: The gap is before the failed test
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.concept_primer: A coding agent can know how to fix a clearly reported failure yet still patch too early when the requirement is ambiguous. The missing skill can be gathering the decisive evidence before writing code.
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.plain_question: Which malformed-input rules still challenge the transaction-trained model after a visible failure, and where in the coding loop does the remaining difficulty actually appear?
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.plain_answer: No rule produced a stable training target after a failed test: every one of 72 cases reached a correct patch. But before seeing test output, the model got zero of 54 ambiguous first patches fully right and never opened the visible tests first. The run formally stopped because answers also exceeded their length allowance too often.
qwen35_4b_semantic_policy_headroom_tourn.why_it_matters: More post-failure practice would teach behavior the model already has. The promising strategy is to teach it to inspect the evidence that distinguishes two plausible requirements, then make its first patch follow what it found.

key_numbers.label: Correct after failure
key_numbers.sub: every case reached a fully correct patch

key_numbers.label: Ambiguous first patches
key_numbers.sub: fully correct without test output

key_numbers.label: Tests read before patching
key_numbers.sub: in rejected-patch trajectories

key_numbers.label: Answer-limit contacts
key_numbers.sub: both blocks exceeded the 5% stop

charts.chart_plain_title: Most rules were already repaired after a visible failure
charts.chart_read: Each group is one hidden rule, with one bar for each fresh block. Taller means the model ended with the correct behavior more often after seeing the failed test.
charts.chart_takeaway: Negative and non-integer handling were perfect in both blocks; blank-resource misses moved between data shapes and did not form a stable lesson.

charts.chart_plain_title: The sharp gap appears before the first patch
charts.chart_read: The first bar asks whether a correct patch ever appeared after test output. The other bars ask whether the model got an ambiguous first patch right or read the tests before making it.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model can act on decisive failure evidence, but it does not seek that evidence before committing to a guess.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both blocks exceeded the answer-length stop
charts.chart_read: The first two bars are the share of tool turns that filled the answer allowance; the last bar is the maximum allowed rate. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The formal result remains an instrument failure even though most long answers contained a usable tool call.

qwen35_4b_confidence_policy.verdict_tag: Verifier-free SELECT + ABSTAIN work; selectively escalating hard tasks to more think budget is a null result at n=400
qwen35_4b_confidence_policy.concept_primer: Picture the model answering a coding problem several times. A cheap read of its own confidence (one logit: 'is this right?') picks the best try and flags the ones it is unsure about. A tempting extra step is to let the model think LONGER on the flagged-hard problems instead of drawing more tries. This experiment tests all three moves at matched compute — and powers up the shakiest one until the answer is trustworthy.
qwen35_4b_confidence_policy.plain_question: Can the model's own confidence be turned into one deployable policy — pick the best answer, abstain when unsure, and spend extra compute wisely — with no test-execution or grader?
qwen35_4b_confidence_policy.plain_answer: Partly. Picking the answer with the highest single-token P(True) confidence is the best grader-free selector at every compute level, and abstaining below a confidence threshold trades coverage for accuracy cleanly — both robust. But the appealing third move — spending compute on more THINKING for the flagged-hard tasks rather than more samples — showed no benefit once powered up to 400 tasks: an early small-sample win did not replicate (every 95% interval now includes zero). Thinking longer helps a little across the board, just not selectively on the hard tail — because these coding tasks nearly saturate the model's thinking budget already.
qwen35_4b_confidence_policy.why_it_matters: It gives a shippable, verifier-free recipe (select + abstain from one logit) AND a methodological lesson: a pre-registered, under-powered subset win was flagged as shaky and then REVERSED on the larger sample — power up before you claim. No grader, no bigger model, just the fixed 4B's own logits.

key_numbers.label: P(True)-select accuracy @k=9
key_numbers.sub: best verifier-free selector; majority-vote 0.742, mean-logprob 0.725, greedy 0.701

key_numbers.label: Abstention: accuracy at 68% coverage
key_numbers.sub: up from 0.757 at full coverage; solvability AUROC 0.72

key_numbers.label: Escalate vs breadth (n=400)
key_numbers.sub: esc-minus-breadth +0.004..+0.022 across abstain fractions — a null; the n=24-60 win did not replicate

key_numbers.label: MBPP tasks powered up to
key_numbers.sub: regenerated on vLLM (~10x faster than the initial HF pass) with bootstrap CIs

charts.chart_plain_title: P(True) confidence-select is the best verifier-free selector
charts.chart_read: Bars are MBPP accuracy at nine candidates for each selection rule. Single-token P(True)-select leads the verifier-free rules; the execution line and oracle need a grader.

charts.chart_plain_title: Escalating hard tasks to more think budget: a null result at n=400
charts.chart_read: Each group is the hardest X% of tasks by confidence. Escalating them to a bigger think budget (left) barely differs from spending the same compute on more samples (right) — every 95% interval on the difference includes zero.

qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.verdict_tag: Close weighting missed the local gate
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.concept_primer: The previous training recipe nearly learned to finish fresh puzzles, but a few answers ran out of space before the model stopped reasoning. This follow-up teaches the exact transition from reasoning to a final answer while keeping the amount of practice equal across comparisons.
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.plain_question: Does putting extra teaching weight on the moment reasoning ends help the model finish reliably, beyond simply adding targeted lessons or more replay practice?
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.plain_answer: No. On 26 fresh procedural cases, ordinary and close-weighted target training both produced 23 well-formed answers and three response-limit contacts. Close weighting scored 16 correct versus 15 for ordinary training, but only by gaining one unrelated abstention case; both remained zero for four targeted execution and rule-finding cases. The best arm missed each frozen numeric gate by one case or contact, so the benchmark stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_universal_close_weight_token_m.why_it_matters: The byte-identical comparison separates the effect of fresh target lessons from the effect of emphasizing the reasoning-to-answer marker. Fresh lessons improved answer emission, but extra marker weight did not. That retires close-weight tuning and redirects the search toward teaching a bounded search procedure itself.

key_numbers.label: Best fresh accuracy
key_numbers.sub: tied the immediate parent and missed the gate by one

qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.verdict_tag: Synthetic skills installed locally but displaced broad behavior
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.concept_primer: The curriculum teaches abstract procedures with invented symbols that do not resemble the benchmark. The crucial test is not whether the model learns those exercises, but whether the same update improves every unrelated family without erasing behavior it already had.
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.plain_question: Can carefully checked synthetic lessons add general procedures that improve every unseen benchmark family while preserving a strong starting policy?
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.plain_answer: Not with either tested training geometry. Continuing from the strong policy raised fresh synthetic accuracy from 50% to 69% and beat the untrained base overall, but it scored 31% aggregate versus 45% for the strong starting policy and fell below base on three of ten families. A second mixture trained from base with broad replay also reached 69% local accuracy, yet only 85% of its answers were well formed and it hit the response limit four times, so its broad test stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_universal_curriculum.why_it_matters: The lessons clearly changed the model, but local learning was not a universal gain. The result narrows the search toward gentler joint training that keeps mature broad behavior active during every update, with a replay-only comparison to separate designed-skill transfer from ordinary continued practice.

charts.chart_plain_title: Designed continuation trades broad gains across families

charts.chart_takeaway: The designed lessons create large wins in two families but regress three below base and lose overall to the strong starting policy.

qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.verdict_tag: Low-density doses failed the local gate
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.concept_primer: The previous designed mix may have been too dense, and its replay comparison also saw more tokens. This follow-up changes only a tiny number of rows while holding total training exposure exactly equal, so the later difference can be tied much more cleanly to curriculum content.
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.plain_question: Can a tiny dose of abstract skill lessons improve the replay-refreshed model without erasing any of its broad gains?
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.plain_answer: No. Neither 40 nor 80 abstract lessons passed the fresh local screen. The stronger 80-lesson stream matched the inherited anchor at 54% accuracy and parsed 62% of answers, but still hit the answer cap 10 times; broad evaluation stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_universal_low_density_token_ma.why_it_matters: Exact exposure removes the prior compute ambiguity: these gentle lesson doses were too small to install reliable concise execution. The next attempt must bridge the dose gap or target answer commitment directly, while keeping a replay-only comparison and fresh evaluation seeds.

charts.chart_takeaway: Adding 80 lessons modestly improves readable answers over replay alone, yet accuracy only ties the inherited anchor and answer-cap failures remain frequent.

qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.verdict_tag: 160 lessons nearly pass; benchmark stays sealed
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.concept_primer: Very small doses of abstract lessons did not install reliable execution, while a much larger earlier dose learned locally but displaced broad behavior. This experiment tests two doses between those endpoints while keeping training exposure exactly equal to replay-only practice.
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.plain_question: Can a middle-sized dose of abstract skill lessons cross the local installation threshold without sacrificing the replay practice that protects broad capability?
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.plain_answer: The 160-lesson stream improved fresh local accuracy from 17 to 19 of 26 cases, raised readable answers from 18 to 23, and cut answer-limit contacts from nine to three. It still missed the readability and length gates by one case each. Adding 80 more lessons erased the accuracy gain, so no model advanced to broad evaluation.
qwen35_4b_universal_mid_density_token_ma.why_it_matters: The middle dose moved the right behaviors and came close, while the larger dose moved backward. That narrows the next intervention to concise answer commitment at the 160-lesson mix instead of adding more generic lessons or weakening the gate.

charts.chart_takeaway: A middle dose improves correctness and concise completion, while 240 lessons reverse part of the gain; no arm is eligible for broad evaluation.

qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.verdict_tag: Designed mix loses; replay-only sets a stronger anchor
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.concept_primer: This experiment kept the model practicing broad tasks while adding a smaller set of abstract lessons. A replay-only comparison received the same number of updates, revealing whether any gain came from the designed lessons or simply from continuing broad practice.
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.plain_question: Can gentle joint practice add designed general skills without erasing the broad behavior the model already learned?
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.plain_answer: No at this dose. The designed mix passed its fresh synthetic screen but scored about 42% overall, below both the 44% mature policy and the 49% replay-only comparison. It also fell below base on one family. Replay-only was the useful surprise: it improved eight of ten families, tied the other two, and regressed none.
qwen35_4b_universal_replay_anchor.why_it_matters: Broad replay was not merely protecting old behavior; it was still adding capability. Future designed curricula must beat another replay continuation, not just the older starting policy. The next search should begin from this stronger replay-refreshed model, use far fewer designed rows, and match token exposure as well as update count.
